books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar

setups.name: High 2 / Low 2 Pullback
setups.one_liner: Two-legged (ABC) with-trend pullback to the moving average — Brooks' single most reliable with-trend day trade.
setups.context: An established trend pulls back in two legs (A = first leg, B = the High1/Low1 bounce, C = final leg) that usually breaks a minor trend line and typically ends at/near the 20-EMA (then called M2B in a bull, M2S in a bear). Requires that the prior High1/Low1 broke at least a minor trend line (shows the other side is active); bar count alone is not enough. A High 2 in a bear trend or a Low 2 in a bull trend is NOT a with-trend setup — it is a trap that should be expected to fail and traded the other way. Same structure after a climax = the common two-legged pullback that begins many trends.
setups.signal_bar: The bar ending the second leg (C), ideally a with-trend reversal or inside bar that touches/penetrates the moving average.
setups.entry: Buy stop 1 tick above the H2 signal-bar high (sell stop 1 tick below the L2 low). Aggressive traders use a limit at the prior bar's extreme.
setups.initial_stop: 1 tick beyond the signal bar; move to 1 tick beyond the entry bar once it closes as a trend bar. Do not tighten prematurely — the true stop is the signal-bar extreme (a bar may trade through the entry bar and trap you out).
setups.management: Scalp ~half at 1-2x risk (Emini ~4 ticks / 2 pts), move to breakeven, swing the remainder, add on subsequent H2/L2; if it fails it often evolves into an H3/wedge or H4 — re-enter larger on the stronger second signal.
setups.invalidation: Fails when taken countertrend without a prior strong trendline break — 'it will almost always fail and turn into a great with-trend entry' the other way; if the H2/L2 becomes an outside bar trapping counter-trend traders, expect the larger trend to run far.
setups.probability: 'At least 60%' in a trend (a Low 2 at the MA in a bear with a bear signal bar is ≥60% likely to succeed); ~80% that a Low 2/3 short in a strong bear (High 2/3 long in a strong bull) hits the stop beyond the higher low / lower high.
setups.emphasis: among the best / bread-and-butter with-trend setups
setups.grade_reason: Explicitly 'one of the most reliable with-trend setups,' 'High/Low 2 entries are the best,' and M2B/M2S at the EMA is 'probably the single best stock day trade' — Brooks' most-emphasized, highest-probability (60-80%) with-trend continuation trade.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: A high or low 2 is one of the most reliable with-trend setups.

In about 80 percent of cases, a low 2 or 3 short in a strong bear trend will hit the stops below the higher low... this is why low 2 and 3 shorts are so reliable in strong bear trends and high 2 and 3 longs are reliable in strong bull trends.
Entering on those pullbacks, especially if they are M2B or M2S setups, is probably the single best stock day trade that you can make.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks-A+ CONFIRMED by backtest (robust ES edge).

backtest.headline: M2B (pullback tags 20-EMA), held to EOD: +0.217R, PF 1.32, 5/6 yrs positive.
backtest.note: Real, robust edge on ES economics (~$6.7k/yr/contract). MES @ $5 RT nearly breakeven — the fixed cost eats a +0.1-0.2R edge. Higher timeframes did NOT improve it (lumpier, fewer trades).

setups.name: Trend from the Open (Trend from the First Bar)
setups.one_liner: The day's extreme forms in the first bar or two and the market trends all day — the strongest trend pattern.
setups.context: The day's high or low forms in the first bar or two (most common with a large gap; a large gap = one huge invisible trend bar) and the market trends toward the opposite extreme all day, closing at/near it. The setup is usually part of an imperfect double bottom (bull day) / double top (bear day), or a breakout of a small opening range (~25% of average range) that institutions enter on, or a first-bar trend-bar breakout. Strongest sub-type = the 'small-pullback trend day' (all pullbacks < ~20-30% of ADR), which forms once or twice a month.
setups.signal_bar: The strong first bar (short below / long above its extreme), or the first pullback / flag test 5-10 bars in (often a two-legged correction).
setups.entry: Enter early on the open breakout; if missed, sell/buy the FIRST pullback (high-probability even though you enter near the extreme of a big move); add on every with-trend pullback (H1/H2 to the MA). Stay on the 5-min chart and take every tiny entry.
setups.initial_stop: Often a breakeven stop (frequently hit); or beyond the signal/flag extreme.
setups.management: Swing most/all; move to breakeven quickly if very strong; expect a larger pullback (~2x the biggest prior pullback) after ~11:00 PST, then resumption into the close. One good swing trade can equal 10 scalps.
setups.invalidation: If the strong opening move reverses to a new opposite extreme, the day may trend the other way or become a Big Up/Big Down trading range; if the opening range resolves into two-sided reversals it is not a trend-from-open day.
setups.probability: Swing setups ~70%+ to an experienced trader (though they look ≤50% to a beginner); a large gap that goes sideways rather than reversing raises trend-day odds to ~60%+ (a large gap up: ~50% bull channel / 20% range / 30% reversal).
setups.emphasis: usually the strongest form of trend pattern; high-probability
setups.grade_reason: Brooks: 'This is usually the strongest form of trend pattern' and 'selling the first pullback is a high-probability trade' — one of his top day types to catch with a swing, hence A+.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: This is usually the strongest form of trend pattern. The market forms one extreme in the first bar or two and then trends all day, closing at or near the opposite extreme.
When there is a trend from the open, selling the first pullback is a high-probability trade, even though it is hard to take since you are selling near the low of a big move down.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks: 'This is usually the strongest form of trend pattern' and 'selling the first pullback is a high-probability trade' — one of his top day types to catch with a swing, hence A+.

setups.name: Opening Reversal
setups.one_liner: A sharp reversal in the first hour (or the start of the middle third) that reverses the open's move or yesterday's extreme and often forms the high or low of the day — Brooks' cornerstone swing trade.
setups.context: In the opening range the first move is fast and covers many points; institutions with order imbalances wait for a better price, creating a sell/buy vacuum that sucks price to a key level (EMA, prior swing high/low, yesterday's high/low, a trend/channel line, a trading-range breakout, or the same on another timeframe/Globex) where they finally act and reverse it. On most days the high or low of the day forms within the first hour; reversals here are frequent, abrupt, and often large. A related MIDDAY reversal often occurs at the start of the middle third (~8:00-9:30 PST).
setups.signal_bar: A strong reversal bar (often a two-bar reversal) at the opening-range extreme / key level; Brooks notes first-hour signal bars 'often look weak' even when the read is right; a second entry (e.g., a High 2 first pullback) adds confidence.
setups.entry: Stop 1 tick beyond the reversal bar (or the pullback high/low). Enter at market or on a small pullback once the always-in direction is clear.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the reversal bar / opening-range extreme (~3-pt price-action / 2-pt money stop cited for the Emini).
setups.management: Swing part (or all, if strong) since it can be the day's extreme; take partial (a quarter to a half) after ~2-3x initial risk, hold the rest with a breakeven stop for a swing to the other extreme; add at every with-trend setup (e.g., a two-legged EMA pullback).
setups.invalidation: On a true trend-from-the-open day there is no reversal — the market works toward the other extreme near the close. If the reversal fails within a bar or two and makes a new extreme, treat as a failed-breakout / breakout-pullback setup.
setups.probability: Days with an opening reversal / trend-from-open: 50-60% chance of ≥2x risk on the best setup. Earliest reversal ~30% to a 2:1 swing (many ~60% to 1:1). Waiting for a clear always-in move raises 'is it the high/low of day' to 60%+. First-hour reversals ~70% to a scalper's target but only ~40% to a swing target. High/low of day forms within the first hour or two on ~90% of days.
setups.emphasis: 'Most traders should work hard to become experts at spotting and trading the best opening reversals, and make these swing trades the cornerstone of their trading.'
setups.grade_reason: Brooks explicitly calls the best opening reversals the 'cornerstone' swing trade with the best trader's-equation combination — an A+ on his own emphasis.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: Most traders should work hard to become experts at spotting and trading the best opening reversals, and make these swing trades the cornerstone of their trading.
Either the high or low of the day usually forms within the first hour, so swing traders patiently wait for a setup that can be one of the extremes of the day.
If a trader waits for clear follow through and a clear always-in move after the reversal, the probability that it will be the high or low of the day is often more than 60 percent.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks explicitly calls the best opening reversals the 'cornerstone' swing trade with the best trader's-equation combination — an A+ on his own emphasis.

setups.name: Spike and Channel Trend (incl. Gap Spike and Channel, Stairs)
setups.one_liner: A strong spike followed by a tighter sloped channel — the most common trend structure, traded with-trend inside the channel.
setups.context: A strong spike (one or more large trend bars, little overlap — every large trend bar or gap is a spike/breakout/climax) moves out of a range; then after any pullback (even one bar) the trend resumes in a tighter, sloped channel that behaves like a flag on the higher time frame. A large gap can BE the spike (Gap Spike and Channel). A broad, overlapping channel that tests each prior push is a Stairs pattern (Shrinking Stairs = each breakout smaller = waning momentum). Present on almost every chart.
setups.signal_bar: The first pullback bar after the spike that reverses back with-trend (starts the channel); thereafter every minor pullback bar within the channel.
setups.entry: Buy above the reversal bar that follows the initial pullback from the spike; inside a bull channel, buy below the low of the prior bar on a limit (mirror for a bear); if shorting a bull channel at all, only sell above swing highs and primarily scalp.
setups.initial_stop: Structure-based, tight relative to the channel swings; trail below the most recent swing low (above swing high) as the channel advances.
setups.management: The channel usually ends on a measured-move target (often a third push); the start of the channel (the gap) usually gets tested within a day or two, often forming a double bottom bull flag with the channel's start. A with-trend breakout of the channel usually fails within ~5 bars; on an against-trend breakout, wait for the pullback/lower-high rather than entering on the break.
setups.invalidation: If the post-spike pullback is strong enough to make the always-in direction unclear, a trading range (not a channel) is more likely — look for scalps. A weak channel (much overlap, opposite-color trend bars, multi-bar pullbacks) warns of a coming reversal test.
setups.probability: High in the with-trend direction; tight channels are tradable ONLY with-trend because pullbacks are too small for a profitable countertrend scalp.
setups.emphasis: one of the most common and most important patterns on every chart
setups.grade_reason: Brooks: 'Spike and channel behavior is one of the most common and therefore most important things that you will see on every chart' — the primary framework for trend trading, hence A+.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: Spike and channel behavior is one of the most common and therefore most important things that you will see on every chart.
When the market is channeling, it is better to trade it like a trending trading range... in a bull channel, buy below the low of the prior bar and hold part of the trade for a swing.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks: 'Spike and channel behavior is one of the most common and therefore most important things that you will see on every chart' — the primary framework for trend trading, hence A+.

setups.name: Strong Breakout / Spike Entry (buy the spike)
aliases: buy calls during a bull spike
setups.one_liner: Enter at market / on the close / on a small pullback DURING a strong breakout spike (3+ big trend bars, little overlap), accepting a wide stop, because the measured-move math is strongly in your favor even if a pullback comes right after entry.
setups.context: A very strong always-in breakout is underway: three or more consecutive trend bars, small/no tails, little body overlap, sense of urgency, often high volume (10-20x average). No pullback is forming even though most traders are waiting for one. Often the opening spike of a spike-and-channel trend day (first hour / first few bars). Institutions enter here and don't wait for a pullback.
setups.signal_bar: Any strong trend bar within the spike (or the spike as a whole); a stop above the spike high also works.
setups.entry: Market order, at the close of a strong spike bar, on any 1-2 tick pullback, on a limit at/below the low of the prior bar (bull), or a stop above the spike high. Scale in with reduced size and add as the spike extends / as the stop can be tightened.
setups.initial_stop: Wide — worst case 1 tick below the low of the entire spike; tighten progressively (below the most recent strong trend bar, then inside a later micro measuring gap). Reduce size ('I don't care' / quarter size) to keep dollar risk normal given the wide stop.
setups.management: Target an approximate measured move equal to the spike height; take partials at resistance/measured-move levels and swing a runner. Spike phase ends when a bar fails to be a strong trend bar (inside/opposite/large tail) → transition to channel phase. Options variant: buy calls/call spreads (OTM) during a stock's bull spike, take partials after a few days, exit remainder on first reversal.
setups.invalidation: Next bar after the breakout is small/doji/inside/large-tailed/opposite trend bar → possible failure. If the opening breakout quickly reverses back through the open, treat as a failed breakout / trading-range day instead.
setups.probability: 'Once there is a strong spike, the probability of at least a measured move up based on the height of the spike is at least 60 percent and may even sometimes be 80 percent'; 'better than a 70 percent chance the market will make a measured move up equal to or greater than the height of the spike.' Trader's equation strongly favorable even at 40-60% win because reward is a multiple of risk.
setups.emphasis: Best / highest-math setup: 'Try to learn to trade breakouts because they have an excellent trader's equation'; 'this is what the institutions are doing, and so should you.' Emotionally hard and requires accepting a wide stop, so difficult for many traders.
setups.grade_reason: Brooks assigns it the best measured-move probabilities in the corpus (60-80%) and calls the trader's equation excellent; the only caveat is execution difficulty (wide stop, fast), not probability.
quotes: Once there is a strong spike in a breakout like this, the probability of at least a measured move up based on the height of the spike is at least 60 percent and may even sometimes be 80 percent.
Big traders don't hesitate to enter a trend during its spike phase, because they expect significant follow-through, even if there is a pullback immediately after their entry.
This is why it is so important to enter a trend early, even if that means entering during the spike phase and not waiting for a pullback.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks assigns it the best measured-move probabilities in the corpus (60-80%) and calls the trader's equation excellent; the only caveat is execution difficulty (wide stop, fast), not probability.

setups.name: Always-In / Trend-Bar Follow-Through Swing Entry
setups.one_liner: Two strong same-direction trend bars confirm the always-in direction — enter at the close and swing with a measured-move target.
setups.context: The market prints at least two consecutive strong trend bars (sometimes one average-range bar in the first hour) that flip/confirm the always-in direction; the follow-through bar's close is key — a with-trend close builds confidence, a doji or opposite-color close signals a weak trend and favors fading. Includes entering the break of a failed weak reversal bar (a one-bar bull/bear flag) that traps early counter-trend traders, with-trend outside bars that start two-legged moves, and buying/selling the test of a with-trend spike (spike pullback).
setups.signal_bar: The 2nd (or later) consecutive strong trend bar with a with-trend close; or the failed reversal bar / with-trend outside bar; or the retest of a with-trend spike.
setups.entry: Enter at the close of the strong follow-through bar (or on a 1-2 tick pullback if it is weak); for the one-bar flag, stop beyond the failed reversal bar's extreme.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the signal / spike / entry bar; size the position so total dollar risk stays constant (cut size when the stop is unusually far, e.g., 3-4x normal).
setups.management: Target at least an equidistant (measured) move; scale to breakeven after several favorable bars; hold through pullbacks that don't create a clear OPPOSITE always-in signal; scalp part, swing the rest.
setups.invalidation: A pullback that keeps growing without ever creating an opposite always-in signal — position likely unchanged, but honor a worst-case stop; a doji/opposite-color follow-through close favors fading rather than entering.
setups.probability: 'If a signal is clear enough to be an always-in reversal, assume it is at least 60 percent certain of reaching a profit target at least as large as the risk'; outside-bar breakouts in a strong trend usually give a two-legged move.
setups.emphasis: possibly the single most important concept in trading
setups.grade_reason: Brooks: 'This might be the single most important concept in trading' and one of the best ways to trade (especially for beginners) because the trader's-equation math keeps improving during a trend, with ≥60% assumed — clearly A+.
setups.pages: Trends p173-174; RPCBB p63-68; Reversals p88-89,165-175
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: This might be the single most important concept in trading.
If a signal is clear enough to be an always-in reversal, traders should assume that it is then at least 60 percent certain of reaching a profit target that is at least as large as the risk.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks: 'This might be the single most important concept in trading' and one of the best ways to trade (especially for beginners) because the trader's-equation math keeps improving during a trend, with ≥60% assumed — clearly A+.

setups.name: Breakout Pullback
aliases: breakout pullback without an actual breakout (near-miss)
setups.one_liner: After a breakout of a flag/range/trendline/swing, a small 1-5 bar pullback tests the breakout point and you enter with-trend on its resumption; it is a 'failed failure' (the breakout's failure itself fails).
setups.context: A genuine breakout has occurred (of a bull/bear flag, trading range, trendline, prior swing high/low, or yesterday's extreme) and you judge it will RESUME rather than fail. Almost all breakouts stall/pull back within a bar or two; a small pullback of about 1 to 5 bars forms within a few bars of the breakout. Mechanically it is a failed-failure / second entry (the failed breakout itself fails), hence 'cup and handle.' The pullback can be a lower high, double top, or higher high (mirror for bulls), and often the signal bar is an ii/ioi. Works best off a STRONG breakout (large trend body, little overlap) into a strong trend; the stronger the breakout bar vs. the pullback bar, the more the pullback resolves with-trend. It works even without a literal breakout: if a vertical move runs close to (or a micro-trendline within it breaks) an old extreme without exceeding it, trade the quiet pullback as if the breakout had happened ('close to a textbook pattern behaves like a textbook pattern').
setups.signal_bar: Reversal/continuation bar (often ii or ioi) at the end of the small pullback, resuming the breakout direction; ideally a trend bar closing back toward the breakout direction.
setups.entry: Stop entry 1 tick beyond the pullback signal bar's extreme in the original breakout direction (buy above / sell below). Aggressive variant: buy below the bull breakout bar's low on a limit anticipating the pullback (mirror for shorts).
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the pullback extreme; alternatively the stop you would have used entering the raw breakout (beyond the breakout bar / trendline).
setups.management: Breakout bar typically becomes a measuring gap → target an approximate measured move of the breakout leg. Scalp part, move to breakeven after follow-through (some instruments run stops a few ticks), let a runner go because trends run further than expected. It is a second entry, so 'excellent chance of a profitable trade' and 'unusual for the second entry to fail.'
setups.invalidation: If the pullback plunges back through the far side of the range/flag and erases the breakout bar, treat as a FAILED breakout and reverse. If the reversal bar off the breakout is clearly stronger than the breakout bar (and context favors reversal, e.g. a bull flag at the top of a range), take the reversal instead of the continuation. If a with-trend failure resumes against the breakout with force (no second failure), it is a genuine reversal, not a pullback.
setups.probability: 60% or better with-trend; 'the chance of any one breakout being successful is about 20 percent, but the chance the market will eventually break in the with-trend direction is about 60 percent'; specific 5-9 tick failed-breakout test case: 'better than 50% chance of making four or more points' risking 4-6 ticks. As a second entry / failed failure it has 'an excellent chance of setting up a profitable trade.'
setups.emphasis: Best / bread-and-butter with-trend continuation. 'One of the most reliable with-trend setups'; 'a pullback from the breakout of a flag is one of the most reliable setups'; 'only rarely is it best to enter on the breakout and it is almost always better to wait for a failure or a breakout pullback.'
setups.grade_reason: Explicitly named among Brooks' most reliable with-trend setups, 60%+ probability, second-entry math, and repeatedly cited as the preferred alternative to chasing a raw breakout. The single most frequently recurring setup in the corpus.
quotes: A breakout pullback is also referred to as a cup and handle, and it is one of the most reliable with-trend setups.
a failed failure is usually a reliable trade... Bar 7 set up a third entry on the failed failure, which is a very reliable Breakout Pullback long setup.

When there is a strong bull trend and a breakout of a bull flag and the breakout bar has a bull body, buying below its low is a reliable strategy... the pullback usually becomes a breakout pullback long setup.
setups.final_grade_reason: Positive but modest in backtest; downgraded from raw Brooks-grade.

backtest.headline: M2B breakout-pullback, EOD: +0.123R, PF 1.18, 5/6 yrs positive but ~half the money of H2/L2.
backtest.note: A weaker cousin of H2/L2 (same pullback geometry after a fresh breakout). Net-negative on MES.

setups.name: Final Flag Reversal
setups.one_liner: The last with-trend flag of a mature trend fails — instead of breaking out with-trend, price breaks the other way (or the with-trend breakout is quickly reversed), making that flag the trend's final flag.
setups.context: A flag/tight trading range forms late in a trend (after dozens of bars or a climax), often at the EMA or near yesterday's high/low. It shows two-sided trading (opposite trend bars, tails, >50% overlap) and both sides expect a correction. 'Every reversal follows some kind of flag and therefore is a type of final flag reversal.' Can be as small as a one/two-bar (micro) final flag after a spike, or an ii/doji pattern (needs a prior trend-line break to be a reversal). The final-flag breakout may make a new extreme (higher high/lower low reversal), a lower high/higher low, or barely break out at all.
setups.signal_bar: The reversal bar breaking OUT of the flag against the prior trend (a strong counter-trend trend bar closing several ticks beyond the flag), or a failed with-trend breakout bar of the flag; for micro final flags, the bar that fails the flag's with-trend breakout.
setups.entry: Stop entry 1 tick beyond the reversal bar (against the prior trend), or on the close of the strong counter-trend bar / fade of a failed micro-flag breakout.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the flag / signal-bar extreme.
setups.management: Expect at least a two-legged move; target ≥ a measured move of the flag's height. If the first leg extends beyond the flag, swing for windfall profits. Small final flags commonly evolve into larger final flags (wedges/triangles).
setups.invalidation: FAILED final flag: the reversal triggers but fails within a couple bars and the trend resumes → it becomes a breakout-pullback CONTINUATION trade. Micro/one-bar final flags are tradable for a scalp but do NOT reliably produce a full trend reversal, especially when momentum is very strong (e.g., a 4-bar flag that never breaks a significant trend line).
setups.probability: 'Final flags often provide high-probability reversal trades' and 'this is what makes final flag reversals so reliable.' A moving-average-gap-bar + ii-flag failed breakout is called 'a reliable signal.' Micro/one-bar versions: reliable for a scalp, not for a full reversal.
setups.emphasis: A core, frequently recurring, HIGH-probability reversal Brooks flags repeatedly; small final flags can lead to big reversals even after a strong spike.
setups.grade_reason: Explicitly and repeatedly called 'so reliable' / 'high-probability,' and the flag underlies essentially every reversal — a clear A; not A+ only because the smallest (one-bar/micro) versions are scalp-grade.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: This is what makes final flag reversals so reliable.
Final flags often provide high-probability reversal trades.
A one- or two-bar final flag can lead to a big reversal, even after a strong bull spike; small final flags commonly evolve into larger final flags.
setups.final_grade_reason: Explicitly and repeatedly called 'so reliable' / 'high-probability,' and the flag underlies essentially every reversal — a clear A; not A+ only because the smallest (one-bar/micro) versions are scalp-grade.

setups.name: Moving Average Gap Bar (incl. 20 Gap Bar / EMA Gap Bar)
aliases: MA Gap Bar / EMA Gap Bar
setups.one_liner: A bar that gaps away from the moving average in a strong trend, signalling a coming test of the trend's extreme.
setups.context: In a strong trend a bar forms entirely on one side of, and gapped away from, the 20-EMA (does not touch it). The first such gap bar — or the first MA touch after 20+ consecutive bars (~2 hours) away from it — usually precedes a test of the trend's extreme. If the first attempt to close the gap fails, the next attempt is a second MA/EMA gap bar (EMA Gap 2) and is especially reliable. Often after ~11:30 PST a countertrend spike traps traders and creates the gap, setting up a with-trend move into the close.
setups.signal_bar: The gap bar (bull: high below the EMA; bear: low above the EMA), entry on the following bar; or the reversal bar at the first MA touch.
setups.entry: Buy stop 1 tick above the gap bar (sell stop 1 tick below); on the 20-gap first touch, buy on a limit ~1 tick above the MA and scale in 1-3 pts lower in a strong trend. Prefer a price-action entry over a blind limit in case price overshoots the MA.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the gap bar / signal structure.
setups.management: Swing part (it may run to a new extreme and often into the close); target a test of the trend's extreme (~10 bars / two legs minimum). Note: a MA gap bar often marks the FINAL leg before a larger pullback/reversal — watch for exhaustion (double top / final flag) afterward. If the first entry fails, take a second entry; do not take a third.
setups.invalidation: If it forms after a genuine trend reversal (not a pullback) it will likely fail; without a strong underlying trend it is just a scalp back to the MA, not a swing.
setups.probability: 20-gap first touch is a 'high-probability scalp for a test of the trend's extreme'; the second gap-bar attempt: 'the odds are excellent that there will be a tradable rally off this setup.'
setups.emphasis: reliable trend-strength signal (but also a warning of a possible final leg)
setups.grade_reason: Repeatedly called reliable and high-probability ('MA/EMA tests are particularly reliable in stocks and often provide great entries all day long'); slightly below the H2/wedge because it is also a warning of trend exhaustion and can be a scalp rather than a swing.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: The first pullback in a strong trend that results in a moving average gap bar is usually followed by a test of the trend's extreme.
Whenever the market stays away from the moving average for 20 bars or more (a 20 gap bar buy setup), the trend is very strong and the odds are high that there will be buyers at the moving average.
setups.final_grade_reason: Repeatedly called reliable and high-probability ('MA/EMA tests are particularly reliable in stocks and often provide great entries all day long'); slightly below the H2/wedge because it is also a warning of trend exhaustion and can be a scalp rather than a swing.

setups.name: Wedge Reversal (Three-Push)
setups.one_liner: Three pushes to a new extreme with converging lines (usually overshooting a trend channel line) that reverse the trend; a countertrend setup best taken on the second signal.
setups.context: Three pushes in one direction, each extending further, with the trend line and trend channel line at least minimally convergent (shape can be a classic wedge, micro wedge, parabolic wedge, triangle, expanding triangle, triple top/bottom, or double-top/bottom pullback — 'the critical feature is the three pushes, not a perfect shape'). Most reliable when the third push OVERSHOOTS the trend channel line and there was a prior major trend-line break. Countertrend, so more caution than a with-trend wedge FLAG. A reversal usually needs ~20 bars and a clear trend channel line to be strong enough to reverse a trend. High 3 / Low 3 = a failed High 2 / Low 2 whose pullback takes on a wedge shape.
setups.signal_bar: Strong reversal bar at/after the third push (bear reversal bar at a wedge top, bull reversal bar at a wedge bottom), ideally overshooting the channel line; if the first reversal bar is weak, wait for a second signal (a lower high after a wedge top / higher low after a wedge bottom).
setups.entry: Because it is countertrend, usually wait for a breakout of the wedge then buy the pullback to a higher low (sell the pullback to a lower high) — take the SECOND signal unless the wedge is very strong; then buy/sell 1 tick beyond the reversal bar.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the wedge extreme (below the third-push low for a bottom, above the third-push high for a top).
setups.management: Expect at least a two-legged correction lasting ≥10 bars (roughly a third or more of the wedge's bar count). First target = the start/opposite end of the pattern; second = a measured move of the wedge's height. If the wedge triggers then fails by 1+ ticks, it often runs a quick measured move and may reverse again for a second, more protracted attempt.
setups.invalidation: A tight, overlapping-bar channel with no prior trend-line break and a weak signal bar is NOT a valid wedge (a wedge bottom in a bear channel without prior bull strength is not a buy). Micro wedges in strong trends usually fail — scalp only, and only in trading-range context. If price simply extends the trend beyond the wedge extreme, it becomes a wedge FLAG continuation.
setups.probability: When it overshoots the channel line, 'the majority of three-push patterns reverse.' On climactic three-push patterns odds are ~60%+ of at least a more complex two-legged correction. Parabolic-wedge first entry ~40% (with ≥2:1 reward), ≥60% after breakout confirmation.
setups.emphasis: 'The easiest reversal setup to spot'; well-recognized and reliable when it overshoots a channel line and follows a trend-line break. But 'it is a mistake to look for reversals in strong trends' — marginal/tight wedges usually fail; better to wait for the second entry.
setups.grade_reason: A recurring, well-recognized Brooks reversal with strong statistics when it overshoots the channel line, but downgraded from A+ because it is countertrend and he repeatedly urges waiting for the second entry.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: The easiest reversal setup to spot is a three-push pattern in a channel with a wedge shape, where the third push overshoots the trend channel line and reverses with a strong reversal bar, especially if there is a second entry.
A wedge reversal is an attempt to reverse a trend and it is therefore a countertrend setup; in general it is better to wait for a second entry.
The majority of three-push patterns reverse after overshooting a trend channel line, and that alone can be a reason to enter, even if the actual shape is not a wedge.
setups.final_grade_reason: A recurring, well-recognized Brooks reversal with strong statistics when it overshoots the channel line, but downgraded from A+ because it is countertrend and he repeatedly urges waiting for the second entry.

setups.name: Major Trend Reversal (Trend-Line Break + Test of Extreme)
aliases: Countertrend Reversal at Trendline Break + Test
setups.one_liner: The master reversal structure: a trend line is broken with strong momentum, then price tests the old extreme and a strong reversal bar (ideally a 2nd attempt) triggers a countertrend swing.
setups.context: An established, mature trend (running at least a couple hours, often days). Brooks' four requirements: (1) a trend on the chart; (2) a countertrend move strong enough to BREAK the trend line and usually the moving average (a mere divergence without a momentum break is 'a losing strategy'); (3) the trend resumes and TESTS the old extreme (higher low/double bottom/lower low at a bottom; lower high/double top/higher high at a top), usually with two legs, then a second reversal there; (4) the second reversal goes far enough for consensus that the trend has flipped. Absolute prerequisite = the trend-line break must come FIRST; you do not fade a strong trend without the countertrend side having already shown control. If the countertrend break is weak (doesn't even reach the MA), the trend is still strong — stay with-trend only.
setups.signal_bar: The bar that breaks the trend line with momentum is the alert; the ENTRY signal is a strong reversal bar on the test of the old extreme — large body, small/no tails, opens near/beyond prior close and closes beyond it, little overlap with prior bars. A 2nd/3rd attempt (second entry) is more reliable than the first. Wait for the bar to fully close before acting since bars can flip in the final seconds.
setups.entry: Buy stop 1 tick above a strong bull reversal bar testing the old bear low (or sell stop 1 tick below a bear reversal bar testing the old bull high). Earliest entries (right at the test) look weak but offer the greatest reward.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the signal bar / test extreme, or for a full swing stop beyond the start of the most recent countertrend spike (below the last higher low / above the last lower high); ~2 points in the Emini in worked examples.
setups.management: Trade it like a swing/trend once underway. First target = the high/low of the prior opposing trend bar; second = a measured move of that bar's height, then trend-line / measured-move objectives. Swing most of the position if a genuine new trend develops; shift to scalping if it stalls into a trading range. Move stop to breakeven after a several-bar move beyond the MA.
setups.invalidation: No prior trend-line break, or the break was weak/two-sided → not valid, treat market as two-sided/with-trend. If the test makes a new extreme and runs 5+ bars beyond the old one instead of stalling, the trend has RESUMED — start over and wait for a new trend-line break. If the test is in too tight/strong a channel, another with-trend leg is more likely than a reversal.
setups.probability: Earliest signals often <50% probability but reward many times the risk → positive trader's equation. Average setup: ~40% profitable swing, ~30% small loss, ~30% small profit (≈70% chance of at least a small profit). Strong setups (all components present) ≥60% chance of a profitable swing, only 30-40% chance of the stop being hit. Waiting for a clear always-in flip raises probability to 60%+ but shrinks reward. 'Most trend reversal attempts fail.'
setups.emphasis: Core reversal framework of the whole catalog (entire chapters devoted to it). Best risk/reward of any trade type, but explicitly a low base-rate, SELECTIVE setup — 'there are no reliable (high-probability) reversal patterns'; most attempts fail and become continuation flags.
setups.grade_reason: Brooks' signature reversal and the highest-reward trade type, but he repeatedly stresses it is low-probability at the earliest entry and most attempts fail — an A on emphasis, held off A+ because it is explicitly selective/not bread-and-butter.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: Trend reversals offer the best risk/reward ratios of any type of trade, but since most fail, traders need to be selective.

Four things are needed for a major trend reversal: a trend, a countertrend move strong enough to break the trend line and usually the moving average, a test of the trend's extreme and then a second reversal, and the second reversal going far enough for a consensus that the trend has reversed.

When considering a countertrend trade in a strong trend, you must wait for a trend line to be broken and then a strong reversal bar to form on the test of the extreme, or else the chances of a profitable trade are too small.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks' signature reversal and the highest-reward trade type, but he repeatedly stresses it is low-probability at the earliest entry and most attempts fail — an A on emphasis, held off A+ because it is explicitly selective/not bread-and-butter.

setups.name: Wedge Bull / Bear Flag (High 3 / Low 3)
setups.one_liner: With-trend pullback that extends to a third converging push, entered on the first signal as the trend resumes.
setups.context: A with-trend pullback that extends to a third push/leg against the trend, forming a converging wedge (three pushes, each a new extreme with less momentum) — a High 3 in a bull, Low 3 in a bear. Because it is with-trend, enter on the FIRST signal (unlike a countertrend wedge reversal, which needs a second signal). More reliable than a plain H2 inside a tight channel, because channels often reverse after a third push. Wedge flags usually last ~10-20 bars and need not be perfectly shaped (many are subtle but have three countertrend pushes).
setups.signal_bar: Reversal bar completing/reversing the third push, often at the wedge trend channel line or the moving average.
setups.entry: Buy above (sell below) the third-push signal bar; better, buy above a reversal bar that tested the wedge's trend channel line.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the wedge tip / third-push extreme.
setups.management: Expect resumption for at least a measured move / two legs; first target = the start of the wedge. Some traders buy half size at the H2 in case it evolves into the H3, then add on the confirmed wedge.
setups.invalidation: If the wedge is steep or momentum into it is strong, wait for a second entry rather than the first push; if it fails to reverse it becomes a Failed Wedge → measured move the other way.
setups.probability: 60% or better; listed among the 'single most reliable day trades' with the H2 at the moving average.
setups.emphasis: reliable with-trend; among the best trades
setups.grade_reason: Brooks groups the wedge bull/bear flag with the H2-at-MA as 'probably the single most reliable day trades a trader can make' and says to enter on the first signal — high-probability with-trend, a notch below the H2 in his emphasis.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: A wedge-shaped or three-push pullback in a trend, such as a high 3 in a bull trend or a low 3 in a bear trend... Since it is a with-trend setup, enter on the first signal.
A high 3 is a more reliable setup in a tight channel than a high 2, because channels often reverse up after a third push down.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks groups the wedge bull/bear flag with the H2-at-MA as 'probably the single most reliable day trades a trader can make' and says to enter on the first signal — high-probability with-trend, a notch below the H2 in his emphasis.

setups.name: Failed Breakout Reversal (Failed Failure, One-Tick, Barbwire, Micro-Channel)
setups.one_liner: Most breakouts fail — fade a weak breakout beyond a range/level, and if the fade itself fails, reverse with the original breakout (a failed failure / breakout pullback).
setups.context: The market is not in a strong trend (overlapping bars, tails, no directional strength) and a trend bar attempts to break a trading range, prior swing, yesterday's high/low, a trend line, or the EMA. ~80% of attempts to break out of a trading range fail; one-tick false breakouts are especially common. Barbwire = a tight 3+ bar overlapping range with a doji near the EMA in the middle third — never enter on its raw breakout; fade the first bar that closes clearly outside. Micro-channel: the first attempt to break a very tight ~10-bar micro channel usually fails. A failed FAILURE (the fade fails and price resumes the original breakout) is a more reliable second signal (breakout pullback).
setups.signal_bar: The breakout bar that closes clearly beyond the level then stalls, or the reversal bar / small inside bar back inside it; for one-tick failures, the bar that pokes exactly 1-2 ticks beyond a swing and reverses.
setups.entry: Fade: sell 1 tick below the bull-breakout bar's low (buy 1 tick above a bear-breakout bar's high), at/on the close of the breakout bar or on the next bar. If the fade fails, reverse 1 tick beyond that entry bar's extreme (the failed failure / breakout pullback), often doubling size.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the breakout extreme.
setups.management: For barbwire, if the fade also fails, the second reversal (breakout pullback in the original direction) is 'unusual to fail.' Expect at least a scalp, often two legs, as trapped breakout traders exit.
setups.invalidation: Do NOT fade if the breakout is an unusually large bar with small/no tails, has follow-through, or is two or more consecutive strong trend bars — that increases the odds the breakout is real. Once 2-3 bars pass, a later failure just sets up a breakout pullback in the new trend, not a reversal.
setups.probability: 'About 80 percent of attempts to break out of a trading range fail'; '80 percent of attempts to break below the bottom of a channel fail.' A one-tick failure is 'a reliable sign that the market is going the other way.'
setups.emphasis: A core, structural Brooks edge ('one of the most important concepts in trading is that most breakouts fail') and a frequent source of the best failed-breakout/failed-failure entries.
setups.grade_reason: Anchored to the ~80%-of-breakouts-fail principle Brooks calls one of the most important in trading, with the failed-failure/second-entry adding reliability — a strong A.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: One of the most important concepts in trading is that most breakouts fail; about 80 percent of attempts to break out of a trading range fail.
The cardinal rule for trading in barbwire is to never enter on a breakout; instead, wait for a trend bar to break out of the pattern and place an order to go short one tick below its low.
A one-tick failure is a reliable sign that the market is going the other way, so look for setups that allow you to enter.
setups.final_grade_reason: Anchored to the ~80%-of-breakouts-fail principle Brooks calls one of the most important in trading, with the failed-failure/second-entry adding reliability — a strong A.

setups.name: Breakout Pullback (incl. First Pullback After Trendline Break)
aliases: First Higher Low / First Lower High (new trend)
setups.one_liner: After a breakout/trendline break the market pulls back without reversing — enter with-trend on the pullback test.
setups.context: After a spike/breakout of a range, trend line, channel, swing point, or yesterday's extreme (a bull channel = a bear flag and vice versa), the market pulls back but does not retrace much of the breakout — or a counter-trend reversal fails (a 'failed failure'). The strongest instance is the FIRST pullback (first higher low / first lower high) after a significant trendline break — the best risk/reward of any trend entry. Brooks calls this 'the single most reliable countertrend trade' because it is actually with-trend on the larger time frame.
setups.signal_bar: The small pullback/test bar after the breakout (often a High1/2 or Low1/2 or MA-gap bar), or the failed counter-trend reversal bar (small bar/doji vs. a strong multi-bar spike).
setups.entry: Buy/sell stop 1 tick beyond the pullback signal bar in the breakout direction; for the failed-failure, buy on a limit at/below the failed reversal bar's low (mirror for shorts).
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the pullback swing point / bottom of the originating spike.
setups.management: Target at least a test of the major trend's extreme plus a measured move = spike height; the more momentum in the trendline break, the higher the odds; add on further with-trend pullbacks.
setups.invalidation: If price pulls all the way back through the breakout point and beyond, it is a FAILED breakout, not a breakout pullback — do not force the with-trend trade.
setups.probability: 'When the breakout is strong, the probability is usually 60 to 70 percent or more'; failed failures are second signals and 'have a high probability of success.'
setups.emphasis: high — 'the single most reliable countertrend trade'; best R:R on the first pullback
setups.grade_reason: Brooks calls it 'the single most reliable countertrend trade' (it is with-trend on the larger frame) and says 'the best risk reward ratio occurs when you enter on the first pullback after a trendline break,' with 60-70%+ follow-through — high-probability, just below his H2/spike-channel core.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: The single most reliable countertrend trade is actually a with-trend trade on a larger time frame... any small pullback to test this breakout is a great breakout pullback entry.
The best risk reward ratio occurs when you enter on the First Pullback after a trendline break, before there is a clearly established trend.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks calls it 'the single most reliable countertrend trade' (it is with-trend on the larger frame) and says 'the best risk reward ratio occurs when you enter on the first pullback after a trendline break,' with 60-70%+ follow-through — high-probability, just below his H2/spike-channel core.

setups.name: High 1 / Low 1 Pullback
aliases: First Higher Low / First Lower High
setups.one_liner: First-leg with-trend pullback (often a failed micro-trendline breakout) bought/sold on a stop in the direction of a strong trend.
setups.context: Valid with-trend ONLY in a clear, strong trend (spike phase is best) or a strong breakout — NOT in a trading range, not against the larger trend, and not right after a buy/sell climax. In a bull leg pulling back sideways/down, the first bar whose high exceeds the prior bar's high is a High 1 (ends leg 1); mirror Low 1 in a bear. The most reliable form is a false breakout of a micro trend line (2-10 bars, most bars touching), which appears only in the strongest trend segments; also strong as the first pullback within a bull/bear spike, or the first higher-low/lower-high of a new trend just after a trendline break. Most bear breakouts of a bull micro channel fail and become High 1 buys (mirror for Low 1).
setups.signal_bar: First bar to poke through the micro trend line and close back with-trend, or the first small pause/inside bar after a spike; in the strongest trends the signal bar is often weak (small or counter-colored), forcing traders to chase.
setups.entry: Buy stop 1 tick above the High 1 signal-bar high (sell stop 1 tick below the Low 1 low); aggressive traders buy on a limit at the prior bar's low anticipating the failed breakout.
setups.initial_stop: 1 tick beyond the opposite extreme of the signal bar/swing; widen to a money stop (up to ~8 ticks in the Emini) if the signal bar is a small doji.
setups.management: Swing in the strongest trends; treat as a scalp only when it is the 2nd/3rd consecutive H1/L1 (odds of a further swing decline). Brooks suggests a resting 'last-ditch' stop above the spike high in case the H1 is missed.
setups.invalidation: Do not take after a climax, at the edge of a trading range (usually a trap / final flag), or against the larger trend — a Low 1 in a bull or High 1 in a bear should be expected to FAIL and be traded as a with-trend breakout pullback the other way.
setups.probability: 'Most bear breakouts of bull micro channels in a bull trend fail and become high 1 buy setups'; with-trend micro-trendline entries are high-probability; the directional probability of an equidistant with-trend move is ~60% and 'unlikely to be much lower.'
setups.emphasis: high-prob in a strong trend / spike phase; scalp-only when repeated; trap in ranges or after a climax
setups.grade_reason: Brooks calls the micro-trendline false-breakout H1/L1 'the most reliable type of High 1/Low 1' and 'always a good buy in the spike phase of a strong bull trend' — high-probability with-trend, but only a scalp when repeated and an outright trap in ranges/after a climax, so it falls short of his bread-and-butter A+ setups.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: The most reliable high 1 and low 1 entries occur when there is a false breakout of a micro trend line in the spike phase of a trend, which is the strongest segment of the trend.
most bear breakouts of bull micro channels in a bull trend fail and become high 1 buy setups
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks calls the micro-trendline false-breakout H1/L1 'the most reliable type of High 1/Low 1' and 'always a good buy in the spike phase of a strong bull trend' — high-probability with-trend, but only a scalp when repeated and an outright trap in ranges/after a climax, so it falls short of his bread-and-butter A+ setups.

setups.name: Double Bottom / Double Top Pullback
setups.one_liner: A double bottom (top) forms, then a deep pullback makes a higher low (lower high); buying/selling that second-attempt structure is one of Brooks' most reliable reversals.
setups.context: A double bottom (current low ≈ a prior swing low, 1 to 20+ bars earlier, need not be the day's low) forms after a sell-off but before the reversal takes off, then a deep pullback (typically >50%, often almost all the way back to the double bottom) forms a distinctly HIGHER low; mirror for a double top → lower high. Often a rounded/accumulation bottom, or a triple-bottom / triangle where the third push down lacks sellers. Always at a support/resistance level. Distinct from a double-bottom BULL FLAG (which is a with-trend continuation); this is a REVERSAL. Best after a prior trend-line break / climactic move.
setups.signal_bar: The higher-low bar (bull) / lower-high bar (bear) completing the pullback — ideally a strong bull/bear reversal bar or an ii/breakout-pullback bar; strongest when the final of the three bottoms is a strong reversal bar.
setups.entry: Buy stop 1 tick above the higher-low signal bar (or sell stop 1 tick below the lower-high bar). The pullback is itself a breakout pullback from the move off the double bottom.
setups.initial_stop: Below the double-bottom low / pullback low (above the double-top high).
setups.management: Extended trend often ensues; the new trend usually takes out the highs/lows of the opposing signal bars formed during the prior leg. Scale/swing if shape and reversal bars are strong.
setups.invalidation: If the second bottom/top does not at least reach the first, risk of only a two-legged sideways correction → prefer scalp over swing. In a very strong trend without a prior countertrend trend-line break, a slightly higher low can be just a continuation — context-dependent, NOT automatic. If price falls through the double-bottom level, it fails.
setups.probability: Most double bottom pullbacks succeed; among Brooks' ≥60% setups when the setup is strong and there was a prior trend-line break.
setups.emphasis: 'One of the most reliable buy setups' when the shape is good and the reversal bars are strong. Brooks explicitly prefers this term to 'head and shoulders' because H&S mostly fail while double bottom pullbacks mostly succeed.
setups.grade_reason: Brooks calls it 'one of the most reliable buy setups' and contrasts it favorably against H&S; a genuine high-probability reversal, just short of A+ because it still needs supportive context.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: When the market forms a double bottom pullback and the shape is good and the bull reversal bars for the three bottoms are strong, especially if the final one is strong, the pattern is one of the most reliable buy setups.

Most double bottom pullbacks are head and shoulders bottoms, but since most head and shoulders patterns fail and most double bottom pullbacks succeed, you should distinguish them and never bother with the term head and shoulders.
Double bottom pullbacks have a pullback that typically extends more than 50 percent and often almost the entire way to the double bottom.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks calls it 'one of the most reliable buy setups' and contrasts it favorably against H&S; a genuine high-probability reversal, just short of A+ because it still needs supportive context.

setups.name: Double Bottom Bull Flag / Double Top Bear Flag
setups.one_liner: A pause/flag with two spikes to about the same price that resumes the trend — a particularly reliable High 2 / Low 2.
setups.context: A pause/flag within an established trend that makes two spikes down (up) to about the same price — bulls (bears) defending stops beyond the most recent swing — then resumes; 'all double bottoms are high 2 patterns.' This is a CONTINUATION pattern that forms after the trend has already begun, distinct from the double-bottom/top PULLBACK, which is a reversal at the end of a trend. The first higher low of a new bull often takes this form (first lower high of a new bear).
setups.signal_bar: The bar of the second bottom/top, often an outside-up (bull) or outside-down (bear) bar.
setups.entry: Buy stop 1 tick above the signal-bar high (double bottom); sell stop 1 tick below the signal-bar low (double top).
setups.initial_stop: 1 tick beyond the double-bottom low / double-top high; move to beyond the entry bar once it closes as a trend bar.
setups.management: Minimum target = a measured move of the flag's height / at least a scalp.
setups.invalidation: A one-tick stop-run break below the double bottom is acceptable only if it does not hold; if the low doesn't hold, bulls stop buying, bears turn aggressive, and the pattern fails (becomes a wedge/reversal).
setups.probability: 'Two failed attempts to break a level usually results in the market doing the opposite, especially when that opposite direction is with-trend.'
setups.emphasis: particularly reliable variant of the H2/L2; reliable for at least a scalp
setups.grade_reason: Brooks calls it 'a particularly reliable type of high 2 buy setup' and 'a reliable setup for at least a scalp' that often launches a trend day — a strong, specific instance of his best continuation family.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: It is a particularly reliable type of high 2 buy setup, so I generally refer to it as a double bottom to distinguish it from other high 2 patterns.
In a trend, most reversal patterns fail and most continuation patterns succeed.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks calls it 'a particularly reliable type of high 2 buy setup' and 'a reliable setup for at least a scalp' that often launches a trend day — a strong, specific instance of his best continuation family.

setups.name: Two-Bar Reversal
aliases: two-bar reversal off a trend channel line
setups.one_liner: A strong bar one way immediately followed by a strong opposite-color bar; enter beyond the extreme of the pair — one of the most common, important reversal triggers.
setups.context: Two (often but not necessarily consecutive) bars of similar size make a strong move one way then a strong move the opposite way. Applies when a reversal bar overlaps >75% of the prior bar (treat as a two-bar reversal, not a single reversal bar). All two-bar reversals are opposite climaxes, usually small. Common after a spike, at trading-range extremes, off a trend-channel-line overshoot, after a gap, or trapping the opposite side after a failed setup. An 'ii' variant (two bull-close bars fading a bear trend) is desirable confirmation.
setups.signal_bar: The two-bar combination (bear bar then bull bar for a long; bull then bear for a short); entry off the second bar / the pair's extreme.
setups.entry: Stop 1 tick beyond the extreme of BOTH bars combined (not just the second bar) when overlap is large.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the opposite extreme of the two-bar combination.
setups.management: Add on a breakout test that runs breakeven stops then reverses back in the new direction. Expect at least two legs when it comes off a climax/channel-line test.
setups.invalidation: In a strong trend, a two-bar reversal AGAINST the trend is usually a trap/one-bar flag → trade it as CONTINUATION, not reversal. Only fade it as a reversal when there is a specific reason (climax, channel-line overshoot, range extreme).
setups.probability: 'The reversal up at the bar 10 two-bar reversal was a setup with about a 60 percent chance of making a swing profit — a great setup for all traders, including beginners.' (Note: a 5-min two-bar reversal shows on the 10-min chart only ~50% of the time.)
setups.emphasis: 'One of the most common reversal setups and therefore very important'; at good locations it is a ~60% beginner-appropriate swing.
setups.grade_reason: Brooks explicitly quantifies a well-located two-bar reversal at ~60% swing probability and calls it beginner-appropriate and 'one of the most common and important' — earns A despite being a trap in strong trends.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: Two-bar reversals are one of the most common reversal setups and therefore are very important.
The reversal up at the bar 10 two-bar reversal was an example of a setup with about a 60 percent chance of making a swing profit, and therefore was a great setup for all traders, including beginners.
All two-bar reversals are opposite climaxes, albeit usually small.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks explicitly quantifies a well-located two-bar reversal at ~60% swing probability and calls it beginner-appropriate and 'one of the most common and important' — earns A despite being a trap in strong trends.

setups.name: Fade the Trading Range Extremes (Buy Low, Sell High Scalp)
aliases: buy low sell high in trading range
do the opposite of a trend day
setups.one_liner: In an established two-sided range, buy near the bottom and short near the top, taking the opposite of trend-day setups and scalping toward the other side.
setups.context: Market is confirmed to be in a trading range: overlapping bars, tails at both extremes, failed breakouts in both directions, no clear trend, roughly 50-60% two-sided odds. Rallies near the top behave like bear flags, sell-offs near the bottom like bull flags. Once the day is read as a trading-range day, standard with-trend signals (high 1/high 2 near the highs, low 1/low 2 near the lows) should be FADED rather than taken. On a trading-range day any leg lasting more than about five bars is watched for reversal.
setups.signal_bar: A reversal bar or reversal pattern at the extreme: high 1/high 2 (fade short) at the top, low 1/low 2 (fade long) at the bottom, weak reversal bar, small bar, or a strong trend bar poking to the extreme. Best signal bars are second entries (a high 2 at the top to short, a low 2 at the bottom to buy) that are not too large and do not overlap too much.
setups.entry: Sell above bars/resistance near the top of the range (including above strong bull trend bars); buy below bars/support near the bottom (including below strong bear trend bars). Stop entry one tick beyond the signal bar, or a limit order at the extreme. Options traders buy puts / exit calls at the top and buy calls / exit puts at the bottom.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the signal bar / beyond the range extreme just traded.
setups.management: Scalp: target the opposite side of the range or a fixed profit (about 1-2 points on the Emini, roughly one-third of the average daily range). Take profits, do not hold for a breakout, and be willing to sit through a pullback. On a trading-range day, watch any leg over five bars for the reverse.
setups.invalidation: Range too tight (about 3 points or less on typical ES) — do not enter on stops; wait for limit-order-only experienced-trader setups (see Barbwire/Tight TR). If clear strong with-trend continuation resumes (large trend-day characteristics reappear), stop fading and revert to trend rules.
setups.probability: Most trades in a range are only about 55% certain; near the extremes the directional probability of an equidistant move rises to 60% or higher (up to 70-80% following a strong trend). Second entries improve the odds.
setups.emphasis: The core, most reliable way to trade a range — 'Buy low, sell high, and scalp.' Doing the opposite of a trend day, especially with scaling, is effective.
setups.grade_reason: Brooks presents this as the fundamental, bread-and-butter method for range markets, repeated across all four books; probability is only moderate (~55-60%) but it is his default range tactic and second entries are explicitly reliable.
quotes: Whenever the market is in a trading range, traders should immediately think, 'Buy low, sell high, and scalp.'

In general, doing the opposite of what you would do on a trend day is effective, especially if you can scale in. Instead of looking to buy a high 1 or high 2 near the high, it is better to short above those signal bars.
Selling at the top of a trading range and buying at the bottom of a trading range is a reasonable approach, especially when a trader is entering on a second signal.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks presents this as the fundamental, bread-and-butter method for range markets, repeated across all four books; probability is only moderate (~55-60%) but it is his default range tactic and second entries are explicitly reliable.

setups.name: Failed Swing High/Low Breakout Fade (Higher High / Lower Low Reversal)
aliases: minor reversal scalps at new swing extremes
setups.one_liner: Fade a poke beyond a prior swing high/low (horizontal S/R) that fails, shorting the failed higher high or buying the failed lower low.
setups.context: Most days are trading-range days; horizontal lines drawn across prior swing highs/lows (and other tests: trend lines, measured-move targets, entry/signal-bar extremes, yesterday's high/low/close/open) act as barriers. Expect breakouts above swing highs and below swing lows to fail and reverse. Applies when momentum through the level is not too strong.
setups.signal_bar: A reversal bar at/after the failed breakout of the swing extreme, ideally closing strongly in the fade direction. Second entries and wedges at the new extreme are the strongest signal bars.
setups.entry: Short one tick below the low of the prior bar on a stop when price makes a failed higher high (mirror: buy one tick above the prior bar's high on a failed lower low). If not filled by the bar's close, trail the order bar-by-bar in the fade direction until momentum forces waiting for a second entry or pullback.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the new (failed) swing extreme.
setups.management: If the failure itself fails (breakout pullback) and the market makes a second, more extreme higher high/lower low, fade that second extreme — it is even more likely to succeed. On non-trend days a small target (often just one point in the Emini) is enough; high probability plus liquidity supports frequent small scalps.
setups.invalidation: On trend days do NOT fade extremes outright — use horizontal lines only to enter with-trend pullbacks (double top bear flag / double bottom bull flag), unless there was first a clear trend-line break. If momentum is too strong for the trailing-fade, wait for a trend-line break first. Beginners should restrict to fades where the signal bar itself closes strongly.
setups.probability: High on trading-range days; 'second signals are the best' and second higher-high/lower-low fades are 'even more likely to be successful.' Very high probability of making one point in the Emini on the best of these.
setups.emphasis: Strongly emphasized: 'Second signals are the best.' Most days offer many such entries.
setups.grade_reason: Brooks calls second-attempt swing-extreme fades among the best trading-range trades and notes most days provide many of them; repeated across Trends, RPCBB and Reversals.
quotes: Expect swing high breakouts to fail and form higher high reversal setups, and swing low breakouts to fail and form lower low reversal setups.

A fade of a second higher high or lower low setup is even more likely to be successful, because they are second attempts to reverse the market and second signals are good setups on trading range days.
When the price goes above a prior swing high and the momentum is not too strong, place an order to short the Higher High at one tick below the low of the prior bar on a stop.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks calls second-attempt swing-extreme fades among the best trading-range trades and notes most days provide many of them; repeated across Trends, RPCBB and Reversals.

setups.name: Breakout Test (breakeven / breakout-point retest)
setups.one_liner: A breakout pullback that comes back to within a few ticks of the original entry / breakout price (testing a breakeven stop) and then resumes; a particularly reliable, high-R/R version of the breakout pullback.
setups.context: After a breakout and its follow-through, price returns close to the exact breakout price / original signal-bar extreme / breakout gap (support-resistance flip), often to run traders' breakeven stops, without truly reversing the trend. Can occur within a bar or two of entry or 20+ bars (hours) later. Institutions routinely have resting limit orders at that level. May overshoot or undershoot by a few ticks.
setups.signal_bar: The test bar that stalls at/near the breakout price and reverses back in the breakout direction.
setups.entry: Stop 1 tick beyond the test bar in the trend direction; limit-order variant: buy/sell as price returns to within a tick or two of the original signal-bar extreme.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the test bar (4-6 ticks typical); on instruments known to run stops, risk a fixed small amount to avoid repeated whipsaw.
setups.management: Move stop just beyond the test bar once the trend resumes. A perfect/near-perfect breakout test (pullback low = breakout bar extreme exactly) implies very high odds of an approximate measured move from that pullback.
setups.invalidation: If the test bar breaks well beyond the breakout point (more than the instrument's typical tolerance), the breakout may be failing.
setups.probability: 'Better than 50 percent chance of a 4+ point move' with 4-6 tick risk; 'whenever there is a perfect or near-perfect breakout test, the odds are very high the market will make an approximate measured move.'
setups.emphasis: 'It is a particularly reliable breakout pullback setup'; 'an excellent risk/reward ratio for traders.'
setups.grade_reason: Brooks singles it out as 'particularly reliable' with an excellent R/R and >50% for a multi-point move; a hair below A+ only because it is the specific breakeven-retest subtype of the breakout pullback.
setups.pages: Trends p15-16; Ranges p53-57,196; RPCBB p363,384,397,401,405,416; Reversals p10
quotes: breakout test A breakout pullback that comes close to the original entry price to test a breakeven stop.
It is a particularly reliable breakout pullback setup.
it is common for the market to test down to within a tick or two of the high of that bull signal bar... it provides an excellent risk/reward ratio for traders.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks singles it out as 'particularly reliable' with an excellent R/R and >50% for a multi-point move; a hair below A+ only because it is the specific breakeven-retest subtype of the breakout pullback.

setups.name: Trending Trading Range Day
setups.one_liner: A day built from a series of (usually two) trading ranges separated by brief breakouts; trade each range by fading its extremes, take breakout pullbacks between ranges, and expect a reliable end-of-day reversal back through a prior range.
setups.context: Opening range is about a third to half the recent average daily range; after 1-3 hours the market breaks out, runs roughly a measured move, then forms a second trading range. On the daily chart it looks like a clean trend, but intraday it is two-sided within each range. About twice as common as spike-and-channel trend days. Note: in about a third of cases the initial range simply doubles with no sustained trend, so blindly entering on the initial-range breakout loses (the market has inertia).
setups.signal_bar: Range-edge reversal bars within each sub-range; breakout/breakout-pullback bars on the transitions between ranges; a large climax trend bar near the measured-move target of a new range for the countertrend fade. Also a high 2 / higher low with a bull body after three small pushes down.
setups.entry: Buy near the bottom of every range, short near the top of every range while each range holds; take with-trend breakout-pullback entries on transitions. Aggressive traders fade the close of (or just beyond) a large climax trend bar in the area of the new range's measured-move target, anticipating a test back into the breakout gap and the prior range.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the range extreme / beyond the climax bar; a wide stop roughly equidistant to the target is acceptable when fading breakouts (about 50 cents below in a ~$1-target example).
setups.management: Expect the day to reverse back through at least one prior range in the final hour or two — a reliable countertrend trade, because the market usually forms a breakout test; first target is the earlier countertrend signal-bar extreme in the prior range. Take partial profit at breakeven on the first scale-in, hold for the gap test; if the day closes beyond the new range it becomes a reversal day.
setups.invalidation: If the breakout is very strong (spike-like, little overlap, small tails), the day is more likely a genuine spike-and-channel trend day — avoid countertrend fading and favor with-trend trades. Do not simply enter on the initial-range breakout.
setups.probability: At least a 60% chance of making the equidistant target before the equidistant stop whenever there is a perceived imbalance; 'even if the market goes higher, the odds are very strong that the market will come back below their entry price' for the measured-move fade.
setups.emphasis: 'Trending trading range days... are about twice as common as spike and channel trend days'; the end-of-day reversal is 'a reliable countertrend trade.' Weaker/less predictable than trend-from-the-open.
setups.grade_reason: Brooks highlights this as the most common day structure with a quantified ~60% imbalance edge and a specifically 'reliable' end-of-day countertrend trade.
quotes: Trading ranges are more common than strong trends, and trending trading range days, in particular, are about twice as common as spike and channel trend days.
It is usually safe to buy near the bottom of every range in Trending Trading Ranges and short near the top.
whenever you feel that there is an imbalance, you should assume that it is at least 60 percent... there is a 60 percent chance that you would make a dollar before you lost a dollar.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks highlights this as the most common day structure with a quantified ~60% imbalance edge and a specifically 'reliable' end-of-day countertrend trade.

setups.name: Micro Trendline Breakout / False Breakout (High 1 / Low 1)
setups.one_liner: A micro trendline across 2-10 tightly-hugging bars gets a false breakout through it; the failure of that false breakout is a with-trend High 1 / Low 1 entry.
setups.context: Within an established with-trend context, a small trendline is drawn across 2-10 bars where most bars touch or hug it. One bar makes a false breakout through the line (countertrend), which quickly fails and reverses back in the trend direction.
setups.signal_bar: The false-breakout bar (or the bar where the micro-trendline breakout fails and reverses back with-trend).
setups.entry: Stop 1 tick beyond the false-breakout signal bar, with-trend (High 1 long in a bull, Low 1 short in a bear).
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the false-breakout extreme / signal bar.
setups.management: If this High 1/Low 1 entry itself fails within a bar or two, that failure-of-a-failed-breakout becomes a breakout pullback — another reliable with-trend setup (and often a countertrend trade off the trendline break).
setups.invalidation: If the with-trend entry fails within a bar or two, expect a countertrend trade instead (breakout pullback from the break beyond the trendline).
setups.emphasis: 'Micro trendline failed breakouts form High 1 long entries in a bull and Low 1 short entries in a bear and are also excellent with-trend setups.'
setups.grade_reason: Called 'excellent with-trend setups' and it chains into a reliable breakout pullback on failure; graded A rather than A+ only because Brooks gives no explicit probability.
quotes: Micro Trendline failed breakouts form High 1 long entries in a bull and Low 1 short entries in bear and are also excellent With Trend setups.
This false breakout sets up a with-trend entry. If it fails within a bar or two, then there is usually a countertrend trade.
setups.final_grade_reason: Called 'excellent with-trend setups' and it chains into a reliable breakout pullback on failure; graded A rather than A+ only because Brooks gives no explicit probability.

setups.name: Failed Final Flag / Failed Wedge → Measured Move Continuation
setups.one_liner: A final flag or wedge flag breaks out countertrend and appears to reverse, but the reversal fails within a bar or two; the original trend resumes as a breakout pullback and typically runs an approximate measured move of the flag/wedge height.
setups.context: The market forms a potential final flag (often barbwire/ii after a long trend) or a wedge flag (countertrend three-push) within a larger trend. It breaks out countertrend and looks like it is reversing, but the reversal triggers and then fails within a couple of bars (extends 1+ ticks beyond the wedge/flag extreme and stalls). This is a failed failure → resumption of the original trend.
setups.signal_bar: The strong reversal bar in the direction of the ORIGINAL trend appearing right after the brief countertrend breakout (or the bar/tick that breaks back beyond the wedge extreme after the reversal failed).
setups.entry: Stop entry with-trend beyond that reversal bar (in the direction the flag/wedge failed toward).
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the extreme of the brief countertrend move / failure point.
setups.management: Expect a quick run to an approximate measured move equal to the height of the flag/wedge from the breakout point; if price exceeds that, expect another leg after a pullback. If the move goes far enough it escapes the flag's magnetic pull. If it stalls after only 1-2 bars, it can set up a second-entry countertrend trade off the original final flag.
setups.invalidation: If the countertrend reversal instead succeeds and continues beyond the flag/wedge, the original final-flag/wedge-reversal read was correct — take the reversal, not the continuation.
setups.emphasis: Reliable measured-move tool: 'a failed wedge often leads to a measured move'; 'as is the case with all failed wedges, the market typically breaks out and runs for about the same number of points as there are from the bottom to the top of the wedge.'
setups.grade_reason: Brooks calls failed wedges/final flags reliable with a dependable measured-move projection; a specific, high-emphasis with-trend continuation, graded A (no explicit % given).
quotes: A failed wedge often leads to a measured move down, and the sell-off into the close came close to the target.
As is the case with all failed wedges, the market typically breaks out and runs for about the same number of points as there are from the bottom to the top of the wedge.
When a final flag setup fails to reverse the market, it becomes a failed final flag, which is a breakout pullback setup.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks calls failed wedges/final flags reliable with a dependable measured-move projection; a specific, high-emphasis with-trend continuation, graded A (no explicit % given).

setups.name: Failed Trading-Range Breakout Fade (Magnetic Effect / Outside Bar)
setups.one_liner: When a breakout beyond the range boundary fails, fade it back into the range — tight ranges act as magnets that draw breakouts back to the middle.
setups.context: An outside bar or breakout occurs at the edge of (or in the middle of) a trading range; breakouts from tight ranges commonly fail and get pulled back by the 'magnetic effect' (both sides see better value at the opposite extreme). Breaking beyond any swing high or low, even from an earlier opposite trend, is a sign of strength — so not every poke should be faded; wait for the failure.
setups.signal_bar: A failure bar / small bar near the high or low after the failed breakout attempt, closing back into the range.
setups.entry: Short on a stop one tick below the failure bar near the top of the range; buy on a stop one tick above the failed breakout bar near the low of the range.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the failed breakout's extreme.
setups.management: Eventually there will be a successful breakout — only fade while failures keep occurring; do not fight it indefinitely.
setups.invalidation: If the range's bars are relatively large with big tails, breakout-failure odds are especially high (favor the fade); if trend context is strong (large-range bars, little overlap), the breakout may succeed — trade its direction instead.
