setups.probability: 'In trading range markets, fading breakouts is a good strategy'; better than 60% chance of success with a positive trader's equation in typical situations.
setups.emphasis: Explicitly endorsed: 'In Trading Range Markets, Fading Breakouts Is a Good Strategy.'
setups.grade_reason: Brooks explicitly names this a good strategy with a quantified >60% edge and positive trader's equation; core range concept repeated across books.
quotes: A series of overlapping bars is a tight trading range, which is a magnet, and breakouts often fail and the market is drawn back into the tight trading range.

In trading range markets, fading breakouts is a good strategy. For the shorts, enter on a stop at one tick below the failure bar near the top of the range, and for longs, buy on a stop at one tick above the failed breakout bar near the low of the range.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks explicitly names this a good strategy with a quantified >60% edge and positive trader's equation; core range concept repeated across books.

setups.name: Breakout / Failed Breakout / Breakout Pullback of Yesterday's Extremes (First Hour)
aliases: first-hour breakout pullback of prior-day pattern
setups.one_liner: In the first hour, breakouts, failed breakouts, and breakout pullbacks of yesterday's swing highs/lows, flags, trendlines, or trading ranges are the most reliable patterns of the session and usually lead to one extreme of the day.
setups.context: First hour of trading; price breaks (or fails to break, or pulls back after breaking) a prior-day level — yesterday's swing high/low, a large flag, a trendline, or a trading range from the prior close. On a trend day, a strong breakout of a range pulls back to the breakout level after a few bars.
setups.signal_bar: A strong reversal or pullback bar at/through the prior-day level; if 3+ largely overlapping bars, wait for the second entry.
setups.entry: Stop entry beyond the failed-breakout or breakout-pullback bar (buy above / sell below); take the first entry only on a strong reversal bar, otherwise wait for the second entry.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the signal bar / breakout level (stocks: rarely more than $1, usually under 60 cents).
setups.management: These setups usually lead to one extreme of the day → swing at least part of the position. Move to breakeven after ~50c-$1 (stocks), scale out at $1 and $2, let a quarter run with a breakeven stop.
setups.emphasis: 'The most reliable patterns in the first hour'; trading stocks this way in the first hour is 'one of the easiest ways to make money trading.'
setups.grade_reason: Brooks names these the most reliable first-hour patterns and 'one of the easiest ways to make money'; graded A (not A+) because he gives no explicit probability number.
quotes: Breakouts, Failed Breakouts, and Breakout Pullbacks from yesterday's swing highs and lows, large flags, trendlines, or trading ranges are the most reliable patterns in the first hour.
These setups usually lead to one extreme of the day and therefore are important swing setups for at least part of the position.
trading stocks in the first hour or so is one of easiest ways to make money trading. Focus on Failed Breakouts and Breakout Pullbacks from patterns from yesterday.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks names these the most reliable first-hour patterns and 'one of the easiest ways to make money'; graded A (not A+) because he gives no explicit probability number.

setups.name: Trend Line Pullback (Bounce off the Trend Line)
setups.one_liner: Every pullback to an established trend line is a with-trend entry until the line is decisively broken.
setups.context: Once a trend is established by a series of trending highs/lows (a real trend line), every pullback to the trend-line area — even with a small under- or overshoot — is a with-trend entry. The market usually needs to poke a little past the line to 'find information' before resuming. The most profitable trades are with the trend line until it is broken.
setups.signal_bar: A reversal bar off the trend line (or the bar that closes back through a minor counter-trend line for the break-and-test variant).
setups.entry: Enter with-trend as price reverses off the line — stop beyond the reversal bar; experienced traders use limit orders at/just past the line.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the trend-line extreme / the reversal bar.
setups.management: Expect a test of the trend extreme first; take partial profit at a trend channel line overshoot / vacuum test on the with-trend side.
setups.invalidation: A big, fast trend-line break signals stronger countertrend pressure and higher reversal odds — but usually still expect a test of the extreme first before fading.
setups.probability: With-trend, ≥60% context.
setups.emphasis: high — the most profitable trades are with the trend line until it breaks
setups.grade_reason: Brooks: 'the most profitable trades are in the direction of the trend line until the trend line is broken' and frames it as one of the most important reliabilities (most breakouts fail) — a high-emphasis with-trend entry.
quotes: Every time the market pulls back to the area around the trend line, even if it undershoots or overshoots the trend line, look for a reversal off of the trend line and then enter in the direction of the trend.
Once a trend has been established... the most profitable trades are in the direction of the trend line until the trend line is broken.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks: 'the most profitable trades are in the direction of the trend line until the trend line is broken' and frames it as one of the most important reliabilities (most breakouts fail) — a high-emphasis with-trend entry.

setups.name: Channel-as-Flag Breakout (bull channel = bear flag)
aliases: trading a channel as a flag
setups.one_liner: Any bull channel behaves like a bear flag (it will eventually break down) and any bear channel like a bull flag; trade the eventual breakout out of the channel in the flag-resolution direction for a measured move of the channel's height.
setups.context: Any bull channel, no matter how steep or long, functionally behaves like a bear flag because it will eventually have a downside breakout; any bear channel behaves like a bull flag. This holds even without a preceding opposite trend. A related firm rule: never fade/trade the FIRST breakout of a steep, tight channel — wait for the channel to break, then trade the breakout pullback.
setups.signal_bar: Breakout bar out of the channel in the flag-implied direction (e.g. a strong bear trend bar breaking a bull channel's bottom, or a bull bar breaking a bear channel's top such as an ii-pattern breakout); then a breakout-pullback signal bar.
setups.entry: Stop entry beyond the breakout bar in the flag-resolution direction; preferably wait for the pullback to a higher/lower high (or low) after the channel breaks and enter on the breakout-pullback signal bar.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the opposite side of the channel/flag / beyond the breakout-pullback signal bar.
setups.management: Target a measured move ~equal to the height of the channel; a strong breakout with follow-through can become an outright trend reversal, not just a flag resolution.
setups.invalidation: Sometimes the flag does NOT resolve as expected — a bear flag can break out to the upside and become the final flag of the bear trend (flipping always-in long), and vice versa. If the test of the extreme is in a steep, tight channel with little overlap, another leg in the channel direction is more likely than a reversal.
setups.emphasis: Stated as a rule to apply regardless of higher-timeframe context: 'because a bull channel behaves like a bear flag, it should be traded like a bear flag'; 'it is always better not to short the first breakout of a steep bull channel.'
setups.grade_reason: Presented as a firm, repeatedly-stated rule with a clean measured-move target and an explicit risk-management gate (wait for the channel break + pullback); graded A.
quotes: Because a bull channel behaves like a bear flag, it should be traded like a bear flag. Similarly, any bear channel should be viewed as a bull flag.
It is always better not to short the first breakout of a steep bull channel. Wait to see if there is a pullback to a higher high or lower high.
Not all bear flags break out to the downside. Some become the final flag of the bear trend, break out to the upside, and lead to a bull trend.
setups.final_grade_reason: Presented as a firm, repeatedly-stated rule with a clean measured-move target and an explicit risk-management gate (wait for the channel break + pullback); graded A.

setups.name: Second Entry After a Scheduled Report (Emotional-Day H2/L2)
setups.one_liner: After a scheduled report creates ~30 minutes of large, whippy bars, let the institutions' first 1-3 bars pass, then take the SECOND attempt to reverse the post-report breakout (H2/L2) — second entries are always worth taking and emotional days trend far.
setups.context: A scheduled report (e.g. FOMC) causes large bars, outside bars, and reversals for roughly 30 minutes. Wait for the computers'/institutions' speed edge in the first 1-3 bars to disappear before competing. A first breakout-reversal attempt fails, setting up a second entry.
setups.signal_bar: The second attempt to reverse the post-report breakout (e.g. a bull trend inside bar).
setups.entry: Buy above (sell below) the second-entry signal bar after the first breakout-reversal attempt fails.
setups.initial_stop: Initially ~middle of the signal bar; once the bar closes, 1 tick beyond its extreme; then trail beyond the prior bar toward breakeven.
setups.management: Swing part with a breakeven stop — emotional/report days can produce trends far larger than expected (example ran ~30 points / ~$1,500 per contract); one such swing beats many scalps.
setups.invalidation: Entering in the first 1-3 bars right after the report, while the computerized/institutional speed edge dominates.
setups.emphasis: High — 'Second entries are always worth taking, and a bull trend inside bar is as good as they are likely to get on an emotional day.'
setups.grade_reason: Brooks calls second entries always worth taking and emphasizes the outsized swing potential on emotional days; graded A (no explicit % but strong emphasis).
quotes: Second entries are always worth taking, and a bull trend inside bar is as good as they are likely to get on an emotional day.
Emotional days often lead to trends that carry much further than you would ever anticipate, and catching one like this is much more profitable than the combined profits from many, many scalps.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks calls second entries always worth taking and emphasizes the outsized swing potential on emotional days; graded A (no explicit % but strong emphasis).

setups.name: Climactic / Exhaustion Reversal (Climax-Bar Fade, V-Top / V-Bottom)
setups.one_liner: After a protracted trend an unusually large trend bar (or a spike immediately reversed by an opposite spike) marks exhaustion; both sides act at the extreme and the market reverses.
setups.context: A protracted trend (often 20-40+ bars with small pullbacks) at/near a support/resistance level or measured-move target produces one unusually large trend bar (a climax) — or a spike that is immediately met by an equally sharp opposite spike (V-bottom / inverted-V-top / two-bar reversal on a higher timeframe). Every large trend bar is a spike/breakout/climax. A vacuum effect: strong with-trend traders step aside expecting a better price, creating the spike; then counter-trend and profit-taking traders act at the extreme. Often the 2nd or 3rd consecutive climax. 'Big up + big down = confusion = trading range.'
setups.signal_bar: The oversized climax bar itself (fade at/near its close), or the opposite-direction spike/reversal bar that follows (sometimes a large tail on one bar, sometimes a two-bar reversal 1-2 bars later); ideally a strong opposite-close bar.
setups.entry: Fade at the close of the climax bar and again beyond its extreme (buy the close of a large bear bar in a downtrend; short the close of a large bull bar), or buy/sell 1 tick beyond the following reversal bar. Safer to wait for a test / higher-low (lower-high) second entry.
setups.initial_stop: About the height/range of the climax bar (e.g., ~4 pts / as many ticks as the bar is tall) if entering at its close; tighter (beyond the reversal bar) if waiting for a reversal bar.
setups.management: First target = the opposite end (high/low) of the climax bar; then a measured move. Expect the new direction to run ≥10 bars / two legs before considering countertrend again; press during the fast spike off the extreme.
setups.invalidation: Most climax/reversal attempts fail or must be TESTED first — spike tops and V-bottoms are usually retested no matter how emotional the reversal looks (unless the pullback into the spike was itself the deciding flag). A climax only means 'too far too fast,' not automatic reversal; may just pause. Two or more consecutive strong same-direction trend bars increase odds the move continues — don't fade those.
setups.probability: Aggressive fade of an exhaustive bear bar's close had 'at least a 60 percent chance' of testing back to at least the bar's high — a mathematically sound trade. Chance of a genuine climactic crash after 40 bars up + a huge bar is 'probably less than 30 percent.' Strong climactic reversals WITHOUT a test occur 'only a few times a month' on 5-min charts.
setups.emphasis: Central concept ('the market is always trying to break out, then make every breakout fail'), but explicitly advanced: 'only very experienced traders should ever attempt to fade a strong trend.' V-bottoms are rare but strong.
setups.grade_reason: Mathematically sound (~60% on the fade) and central to Brooks' philosophy, but he flags it as an advanced/experienced-only fade that usually needs a test first — B, not A.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: A climactic reversal is a climax that is soon followed by a sharp move in the opposite direction, and all are failed breakouts of something.
Strong climactic reversals without a significant test of the extreme occur only a few times a month on 5 minute charts.

Since most reversal attempts fail, traders often fade them; they probably had at least a 60 percent chance of the market testing to at least the high of the bar, so this was a mathematically sound trade.
setups.final_grade_reason: Mathematically sound (~60% on the fade) and central to Brooks' philosophy, but he flags it as an advanced/experienced-only fade that usually needs a test first — B, not A.

setups.name: ii / iii / ioi Inside-Bar Breakout Mode
setups.one_liner: Two or three consecutive inside bars (ii/iii), or inside-outside-inside (ioi), compress range into a breakout-mode coil that resolves as a with-trend flag continuation or a reversal; enter on the break of the pattern's extreme.
setups.context: At the end of a leg or during a trend, range compresses into ii (2 inside bars), iii (3 inside bars), or ioi (inside, outside, inside). It is a balance/breakout-mode decision point that can become a flag continuation OR a reversal. Reliability is context-dependent: strong when at the top of a genuine strong bull spike in a strong trend, or as a second entry with a directional reason (e.g. an MA test); weak/marginal in the middle of a range or inside barbwire, especially if the ioi's inside bar is large and centered in the outside bar.
setups.signal_bar: The final (smallest) inside bar of the ii/iii, or the inside bar of the ioi.
setups.entry: Stop 1 tick beyond the pattern's extreme in the breakout direction (place both a buy stop above and sell stop below the ioi/ii and take whichever triggers). Limit-order variant in a strong trend: buy 1 tick above the bottom of the ii (sell 1 tick below the top).
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the far/opposite extreme of BOTH bars in the pattern (not just the signal bar); the unfilled opposite stop-order becomes the protective stop; ~2 ticks for the limit-order variant.
setups.management: Tighten after the entry bar closes and consider reversing 1 tick beyond the entry bar on failure — failures are common soon after ii breakouts, especially mid-day-range. A with-trend breakout from an ii is often just a scalp (final-flag risk).
setups.invalidation: A with-trend ii breakout in the middle of the day's range is often a final flag that reverses. A failed ii pattern is itself a reliable reversal ('most failed ii patterns' reverse well). 'Bodies-only ii' (only bodies nested, ignoring tails) is a less reliable variant. A small reversal bar from a failed ii in a tight range is 'nothing to reverse.'
setups.probability: Limit-order entry in a strong trend 'has about a 60 percent probability' (risk 2 ticks to make 4+). At a range-breakout-attempt location: '60 percent chance the market trades back down and only 40 percent the breakout succeeds — 60-40 is a good rule of thumb for trading-range breakout attempts.' iii 'somewhat more reliable' than ii.
setups.emphasis: Standard breakout-mode signal-bar shape; solid as a limit entry in a strong trend or as a second entry, but marginal/avoid in barbwire or mid-range. Often better used as the signal-bar shape for a breakout pullback than traded blindly.
setups.grade_reason: Genuinely ~60% only in the right context (strong trend / second entry); explicitly marginal or a coin-flip (40% with-trend) in barbwire and mid-range, so it grades B rather than A despite being ubiquitous.
quotes: ii Consecutive inside bars... At the end of a leg, it is a breakout mode setup and can become a flag or a reversal setup. iii Three inside bars in a row, and a somewhat more reliable pattern than an ii.
Buy on a limit order at one tick above the bottom of an ii pattern in a strong bull trend, risking two ticks to make four or more ticks, since this has about a 60 percent probability.
The bar 8 ii pattern was a high 1 buy setup. However, it was not at the top of a strong bull spike in a strong bull trend and therefore it was not a good trade.
setups.final_grade_reason: Genuinely ~60% only in the right context (strong trend / second entry); explicitly marginal or a coin-flip (40% with-trend) in barbwire and mid-range, so it grades B rather than A despite being ubiquitous.

setups.name: Micro Double Bottom / Micro Double Top
setups.one_liner: Two consecutive (or near-consecutive) bars with matching lows (highs) form a small double bottom (top) — the seed of most minor reversals.
setups.context: Consecutive or nearly consecutive bars whose lows (highs) are at essentially the same price, often a bar's tail matching a low/high 1-2 bars earlier (need not be exact). 'Most tradable reversals, even small scalps, begin with a micro double bottom or top' (or a full double bottom/top or final flag). A micro double top = a failed High 1/High 2/triangle buy in a bull; a micro double bottom = a failed Low 1/Low 2/triangle sell. Context decides direction: within a bear spike, selling below a micro double bottom is a valid short; within a bull-spike pullback, buying above it is the play.
setups.signal_bar: The second bar forming the matching low/high (often a doji or small bar with a tail).
setups.entry: Buy 1 tick above the micro double bottom's high (sell 1 tick below the micro double top's low), in the direction context dictates. In a strong bull, two bars with identical highs = a two-bar double-top BUY setup (failed double top), buying 1 tick above.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the micro double bottom low / top high; rely on the 5-min (entry-timeframe) stop, not the 1-min chart.
setups.management: Often a low-probability trading-range trade — scalp and take profits quickly; can be held/added when it confirms a larger wedge or reversal (two legs likely).
setups.invalidation: Same shape can be a valid long in one context and a valid short in another — never both; context/trend must confirm. Just because a (micro) double bottom forms does not mean the reversal should be taken.
setups.probability: ~50%, occasionally ~60% in range context. 'Most tradable bottoms come from some form of micro double bottom, just as most tradable tops come from some form of micro double top' — a foundational CONDITION for taking any reversal.
setups.emphasis: Foundational trigger — 'most traders will not take any reversal trade, no matter how minor, unless there is at least a double top or bottom, a micro double top or bottom, or a final flag.'
setups.grade_reason: Ubiquitous and foundational (Brooks won't take a reversal without one), but as a standalone it is a ~50-60% often-scalp trigger that needs context — B, not A.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: Most tradable reversals on every chart, even small scalps, begin with a micro double bottom or top, and most traders will not place the reversal trade unless one is present.

A micro double bottom is consecutive or nearly consecutive bars with identical or nearly identical lows; if the lows are identical, consider selling one tick below the low (in a bear spike) or buying one tick above (in a bull pullback).
Most tradable bottoms come from some form of micro double bottom, just as most tradable tops come from some form of micro double top.
setups.final_grade_reason: Ubiquitous and foundational (Brooks won't take a reversal without one), but as a standalone it is a ~50-60% often-scalp trigger that needs context — B, not A.

setups.name: Failed Low 2 / High 2 (and Failed Low 1 / Low 4) Trap Reversal
setups.one_liner: A with-trend pullback signal (Low 1/2/4 or High 1/2/4) triggers but immediately fails, trapping those traders; you enter opposite where their stops sit.
setups.context: A Low 2 (High 2) — or Low 1/Low 4, High 1/High 4 — pullback setup appears to trigger but fails to follow through and reverses, trapping the traders who entered on the breakout of the pattern. Especially potent at a range extreme, near the EMA with large overlapping bars, after a gap/overdone open, or as the third of consecutive one-tick failures. 'When the market tries twice to do something and fails both times, it usually does the opposite.' A failed Low 1 (trap bar) forming a small higher low is a classic strong-trend starter.
setups.signal_bar: The bar that reverses back through the Low2/High2 (or Low1/Low4) trigger level — the failure/outside bar that runs the trapped traders' stops.
setups.entry: Enter opposite the failed breakout, placing your stop order exactly where the trapped traders' protective stops sit (buy above a failed Low 2 / short below a failed High 2).
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the recent swing extreme of the failed pattern; in the Emini place it more than 1 tick beyond the signal bar (≈3, up to ~8 ticks) to avoid whipsaw.
setups.management: Scalp part and move to breakeven in case this pattern also fails. A failed Low 2 is usually followed by one more push up + a wedge bear-flag short, or two more pushes (Low 4); a failed Low 4 usually leads to two legs up. A failed Low 1 outside-bar trap often starts a strong trend.
setups.invalidation: In a genuinely strong bear spike / clear bear trend the Low 1/2 will NOT reliably fail — Low 2/3 shorts succeed ~80% there; only fade after the trend context is flipping. A stop run inside a tight trading range is NOT a valid reversal.
setups.probability: 'Traps lots of shorts and is a great entry... usually results in two legs up.' Reliability of a scalp opposite a trapped side is high; failed-breakout stop-runs are 'as reliable a signal as seeing them trapped in a trade.'
setups.emphasis: A reliable trapped-trader reversal; 'some of the strongest trends come from these traps.'
setups.grade_reason: Genuinely reliable trapped-trader edge ('great entry,' 'two legs up'), but strongly context-dependent (fails in strong trends / tight ranges) and mostly scalp-to-swing rather than a marquee setup — B.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: Bar 20 formed a failed Low 2, which traps lots of shorts and is a great entry, especially when the up momentum has been good; when it occurs, it usually results in two legs up.
A failed low 2 usually is followed by either one more push up and a wedge bear flag short setup, or two more pushes up and a low 4 short.
Place your entry stop order at exactly the same place that these trapped traders are placing their protective stops, and you will get in where they will get out.
setups.final_grade_reason: Genuinely reliable trapped-trader edge ('great entry,' 'two legs up'), but strongly context-dependent (fails in strong trends / tight ranges) and mostly scalp-to-swing rather than a marquee setup — B.

setups.name: Barbwire / Tight Trading Range Fade
setups.one_liner: In barbwire (3+ overlapping bars with a doji, flat EMA), never buy the high or sell the low — fade small bars at the extremes or wait for a trend-bar breakout to fail, then fade with a second entry.
setups.context: Barbwire / tight trading range: three or more largely overlapping bars, one or more a doji, usually mid-day near a flat EMA; a two-sided, magnetic pattern that usually acts as a continuation pattern and often becomes the final flag before a bigger move (especially near a breakout of yesterday's high/low). No safe setups exist in the middle. A tight range within a strong trend makes any countertrend entry a scalp at best.
setups.signal_bar: Either (a) a small bar (ideally a reversal bar) right at the top or bottom of the pattern, or (b) the first clear trend bar that breaks out of the pattern by more than a couple of ticks and traps one side, then its failure/reversal bar. Use 3- and 5-minute bars, rarely if ever 1-minute.
setups.entry: Fade small bars at the extremes (sell one tick below a small bar near the high, buy one tick above a small bar near the low), OR wait for one side to be trapped by a trend-bar breakout and fade the breakout, preferring a second entry. If the pattern sits below the EMA look only for M2 shorts; if above the EMA look only for M2 buys.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the range/barbwire extreme (very tight — e.g. four ticks in a tight-range example).
setups.management: Scalp only, small size, requires patience. If the first fade fails, once the entry bar closes reverse to trade with the breakout (breakout pullback) one tick beyond the entry-bar extreme. If barbwire follows a third consecutive climax, expect a ~10-bar two-legged correction. Barbwire often becomes the final flag.
setups.invalidation: Never buy at the high or sell at the low of barbwire, and never buy a breakout from barbwire (especially below the EMA); never buy if bars are mostly below the EMA or sell if mostly above it. Do not enter in a tight range until either a trend bar breaks out by 3+ ticks and fails, or there is a small bar to fade at an extreme.
setups.probability: The first (with-breakout-direction) fade often fails, but the second/reversal entry is 'unusual' to fail; 3- and 5-minute bars give better winning percentages than 1-minute.
setups.emphasis: Cautionary/marginal — 'Don't touch Barb Wire, or you will be hurt.' Brooks calls the trend-bar-breakout-then-fade method 'the most important piece of information in this book' for avoiding losses; the EMA-direction rule 'will save you an enormous amount of money.'
setups.grade_reason: Extensively documented but explicitly a low-quality, patience-heavy, scalp-only environment Brooks tells traders to mostly avoid; only the second-entry-after-trapped-breakout is high-confidence.
quotes: This is barbwire, which is usually a continuation pattern and, like with any trading range, you should never buy at its high or sell at its low.

One good way to trade Barb Wire is to wait for a trend bar to break out of the pattern... as soon as the bull trend bar closes, place an order to go short at one tick below the low of that breakout bar. It is unusual for the second entry to fail.
never look to buy if the bars are mostly below the EMA, and never look to sell if the bars are mostly above the EMA.
setups.final_grade_reason: Extensively documented but explicitly a low-quality, patience-heavy, scalp-only environment Brooks tells traders to mostly avoid; only the second-entry-after-trapped-breakout is high-confidence.

setups.name: Trend Channel Line Overshoot and Reversal
setups.one_liner: Price overshoots (pokes beyond) a trend channel line in an overdone move and reverses back into the channel — Brooks treats this as functionally identical to a wedge reversal.
setups.context: An established trend channel line (parallel to the trend line, on the opposite side of price). Price overshoots it, typically an exhaustion/climactic poke; often the slope has become curved/parabolic. All climaxes, parabolas, and V tops/bottoms are fundamentally trend-channel-line overshoots. Most reliable on the SECOND penetration/overshoot, and when it coincides with a wedge, final flag, measured-move target, or climax. Micro version: overshoot of a micro trend channel line after a very tight ~10-bar micro-channel.
setups.signal_bar: A strong reversal bar right at/beyond the overshoot; best with a second entry. A pullback after a probe of the line is NOT enough — you need an actual reversal bar.
setups.entry: Enter countertrend on a stop 1 tick beyond the reversal bar, back into the channel; favor a second reversal of the overshoot.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the overshoot extreme / signal bar.
setups.management: Expect at least a two-legged correction; the first leg should break the trend line, and price often pokes through the opposite side of the channel. After a climax expect ≥10 bars / at least an hour on a 5-min chart.
setups.invalidation: Alone it is not sufficient without confluence (final flag, wedge, measured move, climax) or a strong reversal bar. Most overshoots reverse, but if momentum is too strong the breakout continues into a steeper trend — if you keep redrawing steeper channel lines you are on the wrong side; stop fading and trade with-trend.
setups.probability: 'The best reversals have large reversal bars and preferably a second entry.' It is more profitable to buy a higher low after a sell climax than the initial reversal, because too many first attempts fail; if a second entry fails, the old trend likely runs two more legs.
setups.emphasis: One of the few situations where only ONE reason is needed to enter (overshoot + good reversal bar). Brooks: 'should be viewed and traded as one and the same' as a wedge — a core recurring reversal.
setups.grade_reason: Reliable enough that Brooks allows a single-reason entry, but it is countertrend, frequently just a wedge component, and he insists on a strong reversal bar / second entry — solidly B.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: A trend channel line is a useful tool to fade a trend that has gone too far, too fast. Look for an overshoot that reverses, especially if it is a second reversal of the overshoot.
Trend channel line overshoots are closely related to wedges and should be viewed and traded as if they are one and the same.
A pullback after a probe below a trend channel line is not reason enough to buy. You need a bull signal bar.
setups.final_grade_reason: Reliable enough that Brooks allows a single-reason entry, but it is countertrend, frequently just a wedge component, and he insists on a strong reversal bar / second entry — solidly B.

setups.name: With-Trend Limit-Order Entry in a Channel / Quiet Flag
aliases: Buy Below the Prior Bar in a Bull Channel
Quiet-Flag Limit Entry at the MA
setups.one_liner: In a clear channel or quiet flag, enter with-trend on a limit (below the prior bar / fixed ticks / beyond the scalpers' target).
setups.context: The market is clearly always-in in a channel or a quiet flag at the moving average; counter-channel 'reversal' signals (low 1/2 in a bull channel) are not real reversals, just the start of small with-trend flags. Enter with-trend on a LIMIT rather than a stop.
setups.signal_bar: For beginners, the best form is a High 2 with a bull signal bar at the MA, not too close to the top of the channel (mirror for a bear).
setups.entry: Buy on a limit at/just below the low of the prior bar (or a fixed number of ticks/points down equal to or slightly less than the average prior pullback, e.g. 2-4 pts on a ~12-pt-ADR Emini), or at/below the prior bar in a quiet bull flag at the MA; fade countertrend scalpers with a limit 2-3 ticks beyond their profit target (they usually fail to reach a 1-pt scalp). Mirror for a bear channel.
setups.initial_stop: Below the most recent swing low used for entry (~3 ticks for the scalper-fade); can scale in on multiple limits lower.
setups.management: May exit the whole scaled position at the first entry price (breakeven-plus-scalp on earlier fills), or swing part in a strong trend; scale in only in the first two-thirds of the day.
setups.invalidation: Do not take counter-channel signals while always-in is with-trend; wait for a clear always-in flip (a strong opposing spike through the channel and MA with follow-through, then a lower high with a bear signal bar).
setups.probability: Implicit ~80% (most attempts to break the channel fail, so betting with the channel is favored).
setups.emphasis: 'the most reliable buy signal' (bull-channel H2 at the MA); limit/fade entries need experience
setups.grade_reason: The bull-channel H2-at-MA is called 'the most reliable buy signal,' but the broader family (limit fills, fixed-tick, fading scalpers, scaling in) is explicitly experience-dependent and easy for beginners to mishandle — net B.
setups.pages: Trends p253-255,273-280,321-322; Ranges p171-198; Reversals p247
quotes: The most reliable buy signal will be a high 2 with a bull signal bar at the moving average where the entry is not too close to the top of the channel.
In a strong bull trend, fade short scalps, since most will fail... expecting the sell-off to not reach the six ticks needed for the bears to make a one-point scalp.
setups.final_grade_reason: The bull-channel H2-at-MA is called 'the most reliable buy signal,' but the broader family (limit fills, fixed-tick, fading scalpers, scaling in) is explicitly experience-dependent and easy for beginners to mishandle — net B.

setups.name: Consecutive Buy / Sell Climax Reversal
setups.one_liner: Two or three large trend bars (climaxes) within a short span, separated only by brief pauses, exhaust the move and lead to at least a two-legged correction.
setups.context: A strong move produces two (sometimes three) climaxes in the same direction separated only by a 1-3 bar pause or small pullback, rather than a normal overlapping channel. Two consecutive buy climaxes effectively form a double top (even if the 2nd high is much higher); three consecutive form a wedge top (mirror for sell climaxes → double/wedge bottom). The pause bar between climaxes can be any non-trend bar and acts as a one-bar final flag. Increasingly steep/parabolic channel lines signal panic and exhaustion of willing buyers/sellers.
setups.signal_bar: The reversal/signal bar after the final (2nd or 3rd) climax push — a bear/bull reversal bar, ioi/inside bar at the extreme, or breakout of the final-flag pause bar. Higher-low (lower-high) pullback confirms.
setups.entry: After the final climax, fade beyond the reversal signal bar (aggressive: buy the ioi inside bar / close of the largest final climax bar, scaling in). Most traders wait for a higher low (second entry).
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the climax extreme / pause-bar high or low (proportional to the large bar when buying its close).
setups.management: Two consecutive climaxes usually → a two-legged, ≥10-bar correction that tests the pause after the first climax; three consecutive → a generally larger reversal.
setups.invalidation: Odds of a sharp reversal go DOWN if there was already a significant correction after each climax. A very large climax (e.g., a 4-bar spike) can reset into a strong new trend instead of correcting.
setups.probability: 'Usually when there are two consecutive climaxes there is at least a two-legged correction; when there are three, the reversal is generally even larger.' Aggressive buy of a 3rd sell-climax close ~60% certain of a test to the bar's high.
setups.emphasis: A reliable STRUCTURAL rule for expecting a two-legged correction, but fading it is experienced-only ('easy to misread and end up shorting a strong bull').
setups.grade_reason: A dependable structural expectation (2 legs / 10 bars) that Brooks states as a near-rule, but the actual fade is advanced and often just a correction rather than a full reversal — B.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: Usually when there are two consecutive climaxes, there is at least a two-legged correction. When there are three, the reversal is generally even larger.
Two consecutive buy climaxes create two pushes up and is effectively a double top, even though the second high is much higher; three consecutive buy climaxes is a wedge top.
After three consecutive sell climaxes, the market usually corrects sideways to up for at least 10 bars and with at least two legs.
setups.final_grade_reason: A dependable structural expectation (2 legs / 10 bars) that Brooks states as a near-rule, but the actual fade is advanced and often just a correction rather than a full reversal — B.

setups.name: Expanding Triangle Reversal
setups.one_liner: A broadening pattern of at least five swings, each more extreme than the last, that traps breakout traders on both sides until it reverses into a major trend reversal.
setups.context: A three-push pattern with at least 5 (sometimes 7, rarely 9) swings between two DIVERGING trend-channel lines (higher highs and lower lows expanding). Occurs at the end of a trend (reversal — always a variant of a major trend reversal, since the final leg breaks a significant trend line) or within a trend (continuation/final flag, a weaker trade). Deep pullbacks make higher highs/lower lows, trapping breakout traders progressively until capitulation. 'Expanding triangle bottoms are always major trend reversal setups' and usually evolve into expanding triangle tops and vice versa.
setups.signal_bar: The reversal bar at the 5th (or 7th/9th) leg extreme.
setups.entry: Buy stop 1 tick above the signal bar at the final low (bottom) / sell stop 1 tick below at the final high (top).
setups.initial_stop: Below the final low (long) / above the final high (short) — the protective stop belongs beyond the pattern's extreme.
setups.management: Each leg is scalpable (a new swing high/low); once the 5th leg completes, swing/scale part since a larger trend can develop. Initial target = breakout of the opposite side; then a measured move ≈ the last leg's height.
setups.invalidation: If the breakout beyond the opposite side fails and reverses again, it becomes a continuation (expanding-triangle flag) in the original trend. Too many bars between legs makes it 'lose its influence' → just a plain reversal from a new extreme.
setups.probability: Strong as a structural reversal signal, but per the major-trend-reversal caveat the follow-through is often just a swing/trading range rather than a full opposite trend.
setups.emphasis: 'All expanding triangles are variants of major trend reversals'; part of its strength is trapping traders on each new breakout.
setups.grade_reason: Brooks calls expanding-triangle bottoms 'always major trend reversal setups' (strong), but with realistic follow-through often only a swing and continuation versions weaker — B.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: All expanding triangles are variants of major trend reversals, because the final reversal always follows a strong leg.
Part of its strength comes from its trapping traders on each new breakout.
Expanding triangle bottoms are always major trend reversal setups.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks calls expanding-triangle bottoms 'always major trend reversal setups' (strong), but with realistic follow-through often only a swing and continuation versions weaker — B.

setups.name: Breakout Test / Breakeven-Stop Run (With-Trend Add-On)
aliases: Breakout Test of Prior Signal Bar
setups.one_liner: Price returns to run the breakeven stops of an earlier with-trend entry, then resumes — a swing add-on and strength tell.
setups.context: While trying for a swing, price returns to test/run the breakeven stops of an earlier with-trend entry, or tests the high/low of the signal bar that launched the leg, or makes a higher-high/lower-low that tests a prior extreme. A weak, essentially sideways pullback that fails to REACH the breakeven/signal-bar level is a sign of strength (it effectively forms a double bottom/top flag because the stops were never hit).
setups.signal_bar: The test bar at (or 1-2 ticks beyond, or just short of) the original signal-bar / breakeven level.
setups.entry: Buy/sell (or add) at the breakout test in the trend direction; the 'missed breakeven' version = enter on the bar that turns before reaching the stops.
setups.initial_stop: Breakeven from the earlier entry / beyond the test bar.
setups.management: Adds to an existing swing position; may coincide with a High 4 / Low 4 near the EMA.
setups.invalidation: If the retest actually exceeds the original signal bar and hits the stops, it becomes a reversal candidate, not a breakout test.
setups.probability: Not quantified; a with-trend continuation tell (a test that fails to reach the stops is 'a reliable with-trend setup').
setups.emphasis: standard swing add-on tool
setups.grade_reason: Presented as a useful add-on / continuation tell for swing traders rather than a primary high-probability entry, with no explicit probability — solid but secondary, hence B.
setups.pages: Trends p322; RPCBB p83,106,402; Reversals p131-134,247
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: When trying for a swing in a bull trend, buy or buy more on a breakout test, which is an attempt to run breakeven stops from an earlier long entry.
This failed attempt to reverse the market beyond the original entry price is a reliable with-trend setup... the test effectively forms a double bottom bull flag.
setups.final_grade_reason: Presented as a useful add-on / continuation tell for swing traders rather than a primary high-probability entry, with no explicit probability — solid but secondary, hence B.

setups.name: Trend Resumption / Late-Day Second Leg
aliases: Momentum Close / Trend Into the Close
setups.one_liner: A trend off the open pauses for hours, traps the counter-trend crowd, then resumes for a swing into the close.
setups.context: A strong trend off the open, then 2-3 hours of tight/sideways action (sometimes three lazy countertrend swings / a H&S flag that traps traders), then a breakout resuming the ORIGINAL trend. A false breakout commonly occurs ~11:00-noon PST, trapping traders the wrong way. Some of the strongest trends begin mid-day from a range breakout/reversal (runaway mode). A relentless trend in the last 30-60 minutes (risk-manager forced covering + momentum programs) never gives the awaited pullback.
setups.signal_bar: The failed midday breakout / stop-run bar (often an EMA gap-2 bar), the resumption breakout bar, or simply the current bar into the close when no pullback comes.
setups.entry: Fade the failed countertrend spike and enter with-trend (the morning direction); into the close, enter on the close of the current bar with a stop beyond the other end.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the trap / entry bar (or beyond the other end of the bar for the close entry).
setups.management: Hold as a swing into the close; expect resumption to a new extreme; can be a windfall. For two-sided closes, keep the initial stop until after the pullback, then tighten.
setups.invalidation: The three-push sideways pattern often looks like a new countertrend move but usually is not; if a genuine reversal (strong trendline break + test of the extreme) forms, treat accordingly.
setups.probability: 'a great setup for a swing trade into the close'; the first midday break usually fails.
setups.emphasis: high for late-day swings; don't get trapped out midday
setups.grade_reason: Brooks calls the resumption 'a great setup for a swing trade into the close' and notes some of the strongest trends start midday, but it is context/time-dependent and needs patience through chop — reliable but a notch below the core entries, hence B.
setups.pages: Trends p89; RPCBB p64,112-114,138-140; Reversals p152
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: Whenever there is a strong trend on the open and then a tight trading range for several hours, the chances for a trend resumption day are good. There is often a false breakout between approximately 11:00 a.m. and noon PST... that failed breakout is a great setup for a swing trade into the close.
Traders who want to enter on pullbacks get trapped out because a pullback never comes. If you see this, enter with the trend on the close of the current bar.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks calls the resumption 'a great setup for a swing trade into the close' and notes some of the strongest trends start midday, but it is context/time-dependent and needs patience through chop — reliable but a notch below the core entries, hence B.

setups.name: Double Top / Double Bottom Fade (incl. Twins)
setups.one_liner: Two attempts to break a level both fail, forming a double top/bottom (rarely exact); fade the second attempt for at least two legs the other way.
setups.context: The market makes a second test of a prior high (double top) or low (double bottom) and fails — need not be exact; a slightly higher high / lower low still counts. The high of the day usually begins with an (imperfect) double top and the low with an imperfect double bottom, typically within the first hour or two. Twins: two consecutive bars with identical highs in a strong bull = double-top twin (a failed double top = BUY setup); identical lows in a strong bear = double-bottom twin (SELL). Opposite twins (up-down / down-up) = a 10-min reversal bar, valid only when there's reason to expect a reversal.
setups.signal_bar: The reversal bar on the second attempt / second top or bottom (or the two-bar twin pattern itself).
setups.entry: Stop 1 tick beyond the second-attempt signal bar, opposite the tested direction (for twins, 1 tick beyond the twin, buying the failed double top / selling the failed double bottom).
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the double top/bottom (or twin) extreme.
setups.management: Expect at least two legs in the opposite direction. Twins are fueled by the resting stops of the traders who faded the double top/bottom.
setups.invalidation: A third push exceeding both prior extremes without reversing negates the read. Opposite twins are NOT reversals when they are just the first pause after a strong breakout (that's a breakout pullback) or inside an overlapping flag.
setups.probability: 'Two attempts failed, so the market should have at least two legs down.' A double top/bottom has ~40-50% chance of leading to a swing depending on context/extreme.
setups.emphasis: Standard, reliable structure that Brooks says 'often leads to a bear/bull trend day'; the general rule is to wait for a double top/bottom before looking for a reversal swing.
setups.grade_reason: Reliable and a required precondition Brooks emphasizes, but the plain fade is only ~40-50% to a swing and needs context/an extreme of the day — B.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: The buyers made two attempts to go above this area and failed both times, creating a double top, so the market should have at least two legs down.
This is a double top twin buy setup and is a double top on the 1-minute chart; place a stop to go long one tick above the high of the bars because you will be buying a failed double top.
The high of the day usually begins with some type of double top, although it is rarely exact; the low of the day usually begins with an imperfect double bottom.
setups.final_grade_reason: Reliable and a required precondition Brooks emphasizes, but the plain fade is only ~40-50% to a swing and needs context/an extreme of the day — B.

setups.name: Daily-Chart Capitulation / Options Fade of Extreme Move
setups.one_liner: On the daily/weekly chart, fade a runaway or capitulation extreme (huge-volume reversal bar, 30+ gap bars from the EMA) using defined-risk options rather than the underlying.
setups.context: A large, extended 'runaway' move on the daily/weekly chart shows exhaustion: multiple trend-channel-line breaks, a wedge at the extreme, weakening pullback bars, and a good chance of at least a test of the 20-period EMA or a major reversal. Includes a huge-volume day (5-10x normal, often a gap-down) suggesting capitulation, and extreme regression-to-the-mean conditions (30+ consecutive bars not touching the EMA). For day traders this is a rare, limited-risk exception to 'don't trade the daily chart.'
setups.signal_bar: A Low 2/High 2 or a bar closing beyond a prior swing at the exhaustion extreme; a huge-volume day closing as a strong bull/bear trend bar; a bear/bull reversal bar above/below a protracted channel.
setups.entry: Buy at-the-money front-month puts/calls (most liquid) on the reversal, or a risk reversal (long OTM call financed by short OTM put ≈ net zero) at a possible major-reversal low; for cash, buy the close of the strong-close huge-volume bar or above its high.
setups.initial_stop: Defined by option premium risked (e.g., risk ~$200 of a $570 call) plus a hard price invalidation (trade back beyond the day's extreme); for a risk reversal, being put the stock at the short strike (the price you wanted to buy).
setups.management: Don't fear time decay if a fast move is expected; scale out at prior-high test / double top; close after a windfall rather than holding into the next day (avoids distracting from day trading). Cash daily trades can run days to weeks; target at least a two-legged move to the EMA.
setups.invalidation: Climactic volume WITHOUT bullish price action (weak/no tail, close near the low) is NOT a fade. If the move keeps running (no exhaustion/reversal bar), stand aside.
setups.probability: >95% of the time with 30+ consecutive gap bars the market touches the EMA within ~10 bars; huge-volume strong-close bottoms are 'almost always followed by at least two legs up'; a risk reversal at a good double-bottom low ~70% to make money.
setups.emphasis: Explicitly a rare, limited-risk exception recommended for day traders; huge-volume capitulation reversals rated high, options fades framed as low-distraction windfalls.
setups.grade_reason: High-conviction where the daily statistics are strong (95%+ EMA touch, capitulation 'almost always two legs up'), but out-of-scope for intraday ES and framed as a rare/limited exception — nets B.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: When a stock is in a steep bear on the daily chart and then has a day with volume 5 to 10 times normal... if it has a strong bull close, the odds of a profitable long increase; this strength is almost always followed by at least two legs up.
Runaway markets often offer profitable fades using options, and this type of trading does not cause too much distraction for day traders because the risk is limited.
More than 95 percent of the time when there were 30 or more consecutive gap bars, the market came back and touched the moving average within 10 bars.
setups.final_grade_reason: High-conviction where the daily statistics are strong (95%+ EMA touch, capitulation 'almost always two legs up'), but out-of-scope for intraday ES and framed as a rare/limited exception — nets B.

setups.name: Five-Tick / Scalp-Target Failure Reversal
setups.one_liner: A move that stalls one tick short of the popular scalp target (e.g., only 5 ticks in the ES) and reverses signals loss of trend control — fade it.
setups.context: In the ES a popular scalp targets 4 ticks, which needs a 6-tick breakout beyond the signal bar; in SPY/QQQ ~10 ticks. A move that reaches but fails to fill common profit-target limits (5-tick, 9-tick/2pt, 17-tick/~4pt) and reverses one tick short leaves scalpers trapped without their fill. Common at the end of a protracted trend and often the FIRST sign of a reversal. A failed short scalp similarly becomes a buy signal.
setups.signal_bar: The bar that reaches just short of the target extension (e.g., 16 ticks above the signal bar) then the next bar trades back below it.
setups.entry: Once that bar closes, reverse 1 tick beyond its opposite end (short 1 tick below it if a long scalp failed); trapped scalpers exiting at breakeven add fuel.
setups.initial_stop: Move stop to breakeven when price hits the unfilled target; tight, based on recent bar structure.
setups.management: Usually good for at least a scalp in the opposite direction; often two legs as trapped scalpers cover.
setups.invalidation: If the market goes one more tick and fills the target orders, there is no failure signal. (If price already reached 16 ticks, ~80% chance it reaches 17-18, so don't pre-empt.)
setups.probability: A five-tick failure 'often indicates the trend traders have lost control and a pullback or reversal is imminent.' If it reaches 16 ticks, ~80% chance of 17-18 ticks.
setups.emphasis: 'Common at the end of a protracted trend and often the first sign of a reversal' — a useful, ES-specific tell.
setups.grade_reason: A reliable, precise Emini tell that Brooks flags as often the first reversal sign, but it is a scalp-grade tape read, not a swing reversal — B.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: A five-tick failed breakout is common at the end of a protracted trend and is often the first sign of a reversal.
Since a six-tick breakout is needed to make a four-tick scalp, a move that goes only five ticks and then reverses often indicates that the trend traders have lost control.
A scalp that just misses its profit target is often a reversal setup.
setups.final_grade_reason: A reliable, precise Emini tell that Brooks flags as often the first reversal sign, but it is a scalp-grade tape read, not a swing reversal — B.

setups.name: Opening Range Breakout (small range → breakout mode)
aliases: breakout mode after large gap + tight range
setups.one_liner: When the opening range (first 3-10 bars / 1-3 hours) is small vs. average daily range (roughly a third to a half), the market is in breakout mode; buy above / sell below the range expecting an approximate measured move that roughly doubles the day's range.
setups.context: Market forms a small trading range / expanding triangle in the first several bars of the day, with two or more reversals and no clear directional edge — 'in breakout mode.' Strongest signal when the opening range is under ~30-50% of the average daily range, and especially after a large gap open followed by ~30+ minutes of a tight range. A quiet, small early range makes a later range-expansion breakout highly likely.
setups.signal_bar: The bar that closes beyond the high or low of the opening range (or its subsequent breakout-pullback bar). Lower-risk alternative: a small bar fading the range extreme.
setups.entry: Buy stop 1 tick above the opening-range high; sell stop 1 tick below the opening-range low. Lower-risk alternatives: fade small bars at the top/bottom of the range, or wait for a failed breakout / breakout pullback after the initial break.
setups.initial_stop: Below the low (longs) / above the high (shorts) of the breakout bar, or beyond the opposite side of the opening range / most recent higher low.
setups.management: Target an approximate measured move equal to the opening-range height projected from the breakout point → the day's range tends to roughly double toward average daily range; expect a later retest of the breakout point. Once the measured move is reached, expect a second trading range. Two consecutive strong-bodied trend bars while the range is forming = buy the low of the prior bar for at least a scalp.
setups.invalidation: If the market breaks one side, extends only 'a little,' then reverses and breaks the opposite side (~1/3 of moderate cases), it becomes a quiet/small trading-range day. Consecutive breakout attempts producing only small trend bars with no prior strength do NOT confirm an always-in flip — don't chase; expect a pullback to the MA.
setups.probability: 'In 90 percent of the cases the range will increase before the close.' A moderate (~1/3-1/2 of average) opening range grows into a trend day ~two-thirds of the time; the other third becomes a small trading-range day or reverses. Any one raw breakout ~40-60% (breakout-mode 60-40 rule).
setups.emphasis: Called 'a reliable breakout mode situation'; strongest when the range is genuinely small vs. average and there was a large gap. Otherwise breakout-mode is two-sided uncertainty.
setups.grade_reason: High confidence the range WILL expand (90%) and a clean measured-move target, but direction is a near coin-flip on any one breakout (60-40), so as a directional entry it grades B; approaches A only when the range is very small and one-sided pressure is already visible.
quotes: Whenever the opening range (the first five to 10 bars or so) is under 30 percent of an average daily range, the day is in breakout mode and traders will buy a breakout above and short a breakout below.
Don't give up on the day... because in 90 percent of the cases the range will increase before the close.

At this point, the market had been in a tiny range for over 30 minutes, and when that happens, especially when there is a large gap opening, the market is in breakout mode... This was a reliable breakout mode situation.
setups.final_grade_reason: High confidence the range WILL expand (90%) and a clean measured-move target, but direction is a near coin-flip on any one breakout (60-40), so as a directional entry it grades B; approaches A only when the range is very small and one-sided pressure is already visible.

setups.name: Buy the Close of a Countertrend Trend Bar
aliases: Fade a Large Bear Bar in a Bull Trend
Buy the First Bear Bar Below the MA
setups.one_liner: In a strong trend, buy the close of a large countertrend trend bar (or the first bear bar below the MA), expecting it to fail.
setups.context: An established strong trend prints a large countertrend trend bar closing on its extreme, especially near the moving average; because most reversal attempts in a trend fail, it is expected to be just another failed countertrend attempt (a flag). Also includes buying the close of the FIRST bear trend bar that closes below the MA in a strong bull trend (mirror the first bull bar above the MA in a bear).
setups.signal_bar: The large countertrend trend bar (and the next bar if it has a with-trend body).
setups.entry: Buy the close of the bear bar (aggressive) and try to buy below its low; or buy the close / above the high of the following with-trend bar (standard, more conservative). Mirror for shorting a bull bar in a bear trend.
setups.initial_stop: About as many ticks as the countertrend bar is tall (or a standard ~2-pt Emini stop).
setups.management: First target = the high (low) of the countertrend bar; second = a measured move equal to that bar's height.
setups.invalidation: If the trend's signs of strength are absent, the bar may be the START of a genuine reversal rather than a buyable dip.
setups.probability: Framed as what 'most traders' correctly do in a strong trend (a high-probability failed-reversal-attempt fade).
setups.emphasis: a standard with-trend fade of a countertrend bar, contingent on real trend strength
setups.grade_reason: A standard, expected with-trend fade in a strong trend with a defined target, but it is an aggressive limit/close entry that is fully contingent on genuine trend strength (else it catches a reversal) — solid but secondary, hence B.
quotes: If there is a bull trend and it forms a large bear trend bar closing on its low, most traders will expect this reversal attempt to fail, and many will buy at the close of the bear bar... The first target is the high of the bear trend bar, and the next target is a measured move up.
In a strong bull trend, buy on the close of the first bear trend bar that has a close below the moving average.
setups.final_grade_reason: A standard, expected with-trend fade in a strong trend with a defined target, but it is an aggressive limit/close entry that is fully contingent on genuine trend strength (else it catches a reversal) — solid but secondary, hence B.

setups.name: Lower-Timeframe With-Trend Entry / Fade of the LTF Countertrend Signal
aliases: Stop Where the Countertrend Would Enter
setups.one_liner: Use the 1-/3-min chart to get a low-risk with-trend fill during a runaway 5-min trend — never to trade countertrend.
setups.context: During a strong 5-min trend (especially a no-pullback runaway), smaller timeframes constantly generate countertrend signals that nearly all fail. Rather than take the countertrend trade, use the LTF to obtain a with-trend entry.
setups.signal_bar: The 1-/3-min countertrend reversal bar (NOT traded), or a 1-min with-trend High 1/Low 1, or the first 1-min reversal bar after the 5-min MA is touched.
setups.entry: Place a with-trend stop at the price where the LTF countertrend trade's protective stop would sit (you get filled exactly as the countertrend scalpers are stopped out); or enter beyond the 1-min with-trend signal bar (aggressive: limit at the prior 1-min bar's extreme).
setups.initial_stop: The height of the 1-min signal bar (much smaller than the equivalent 5-min setup).
setups.management: Once filled, manage the trade on the 5-min chart; swing a large part since it is with-trend.
setups.invalidation: Never trade countertrend off the 1-/3-min chart unless there is also a 5-min setup — 'it will cost you money.'
setups.probability: 'a very high-probability with-trend scalp.'
setups.emphasis: powerful but situational; use only when the 5-min offers no pullback
setups.grade_reason: Called 'a very high-probability with-trend scalp,' but it is a situational rescue technique for experienced traders (multi-timeframe execution, easy to misuse as countertrend) rather than a primary setup — hence B.
setups.pages: Trends p288; RPCBB p204-205,223-226; Reversals p41,156-157
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: Wait for a 1 minute reversal to trigger a countertrend entry, which you do not take, and then determine where you would place a protective stop... Then, place a stop order to enter with trend at that price... This is a very high-probability with-trend scalp.
Trading countertrend on the 1 minute chart will cost you money.
setups.final_grade_reason: Called 'a very high-probability with-trend scalp,' but it is a situational rescue technique for experienced traders (multi-timeframe execution, easy to misuse as countertrend) rather than a primary setup — hence B.

setups.name: Dueling Lines Reversal
setups.one_liner: Fade the point where a trend line and an opposite-slope trend channel line intersect (or two significant lines coincide) — a confluence reversal, often also a double top + wedge flag.
setups.context: A trend channel line drawn in a pullback/leg intersects the channel's own trend line at about the same price and bar, OR a longer-term trend line is tested exactly where a shorter-term opposite-slope trend channel line lies. Often the pullback into the intersection also forms a small wedge flag and/or a double top with an earlier swing (two confluence reasons at once). 'The location is perfect, since it is a test of a longer trend line.'
setups.signal_bar: A reversal bar at the intersection — typically a failed overshoot of the trend channel line (bear reversal bar at a resistance confluence).
setups.entry: Fade at the confluence beyond the signal bar (short below a resistance-line convergence; buy above a support-line convergence). If the move into the intersection was steep, wait for a second entry (higher low / lower high).
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the confluence zone / signal-bar extreme.
setups.management: With the direction of the longer/dominant trend line once the shorter-line breakout fails; standard swing/scalp management.
setups.invalidation: If the longer trend line is convincingly broken (not just tested) the confluence fails.
setups.probability: 'The market reversed up at the intersection, as is common' — treated as a reliable confluence, and 'dueling lines' is named among Brooks' valid reasons to take a trade.
setups.emphasis: A recognized confluence setup (one of the required 'two reasons' to enter), often stacking a double top + wedge bear flag.
setups.grade_reason: A legitimate, named confluence reversal Brooks lists among reasons to trade, but situational (needs two lines to coincide) rather than a standalone high-probability edge — B.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
setups.pages: Trends p232-233,406-408; Ranges p187,198; RPCBB p94-96
quotes: This is where a trend channel line in a pullback or a leg of a channel intersects the channel's trend line.
It was a dueling lines short setup, forming both a double top with the earlier pullback and a wedge bear flag.
When a longer trend line is tested and the test occurs at a shorter-term trend channel line of opposite slope, look to enter in the direction of the trend if the test is successful.
setups.final_grade_reason: A legitimate, named confluence reversal Brooks lists among reasons to trade, but situational (needs two lines to coincide) rather than a standalone high-probability edge — B.

setups.name: Stairs / Broad Channel Fade
setups.one_liner: In a broad, mildly sloping channel (stairs), fade each breakout to a new extreme near the trend/trend-channel line, expecting a pullback into the prior stair.
setups.context: Market is in a broad channel (stairs) with at least three trending swings; each breakout to a new extreme is followed by a pullback that overlaps the prior range (goes back past the breakout point but not past the prior swing extreme). Each new leg is a trend bar closing beyond the prior stair.
setups.signal_bar: The close of a trend bar that exceeds the prior stair extreme (for the aggressive fade), or a doji/reversal bar at the new stair extreme.
setups.entry: Experienced traders scale in with limit orders a fixed distance beyond the most recent swing extreme (based on the size of prior legs, e.g. 3-4 points), expecting a same-size breakout; buy near the trend channel line, short near the trend line. Aggressively, fade the close of every strong trend-bar breakout beyond the prior stair for a scalp. Options traders buy puts at a new stair high / calls at a new stair low.
setups.initial_stop: Sized to prior legs (wide); for the safer method, enter on a stop as the market reverses instead of fading the close directly.
setups.management: Take profit around the distance the prior countertrend legs ran (e.g., 3 points), usually near the trend channel line / trend line; expect a pullback of at least a couple points into the prior stair. On trending-trading-range days and stairs, fading breakouts of roughly average-range size has better than 60% success (target = test of the breakout point, ~1:1).
setups.probability: 'This trade has better than a 60 percent chance of success and therefore has a positive trader's equation' for wide-stop breakout fades in stairs / trending TR days.
setups.emphasis: 'This style of trading is only for experienced traders. Beginners should restrict themselves to stop entries.'
setups.grade_reason: Quantified >60% edge but Brooks repeatedly restricts the limit/scale-in version to experienced traders and recommends the safer stop-entry alternative for everyone else.
quotes: Traders pay attention to how many ticks breakouts run past the most recent swing point, and then use that number to fade subsequent breakouts, expecting a breakout test.

Traders can use wide stops when they are fading breakouts in stairs patterns or on trending trading range days... this trade has better than a 60 percent chance of success and therefore has a positive trader's equation.
This style of trading is only for experienced traders. Beginners should restrict themselves to stop entries, so that the market is already going their way.
setups.final_grade_reason: Quantified >60% edge but Brooks repeatedly restricts the limit/scale-in version to experienced traders and recommends the safer stop-entry alternative for everyone else.

setups.name: Shrinking Stairs Reversal
setups.one_liner: In a stairs (broad-channel) trend each successive breakout leg is smaller than the last, signaling waning momentum and an imminent two-legged reversal.
setups.context: A stairs/broad-channel trend where each successive breakout extends LESS beyond the prior push (e.g., 2nd push 10 ticks beyond the 1st, 3rd only 7 ticks beyond the 2nd). A wedge variant emphasizing decreasing momentum. Also framed as buying/selling a third or fourth push in a bear/bull stairs pattern for a test of the prior push's extreme.
setups.signal_bar: The reversal bar following the smallest/shrinking final push.
setups.entry: Countertrend stop entry on the failure of the smallest push (buy above / sell below the reversal bar).
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the extreme of the shrinking push.
setups.management: Signals an imminent two-legged pullback in a strong trend; target a test of the prior push's extreme, reward ≥2x risk.
setups.invalidation: Three pushes / shrinking stairs does NOT guarantee reversal — if there are no large trend bars or strong climactic reversal bars, it fails and the trend resumes (wedge-flag continuation). If the final step is much larger and then reverses, that's a channel-line overshoot, not shrinking stairs.
setups.probability: 'Whenever shrinking stairs are present, the trade becomes more likely to be successful and usually signals an imminent two-legged pullback.' Third/fourth-push stairs trades ≈50-60% (among the 'best trades').
setups.emphasis: A reliability-BOOSTER for a wedge/three-push reversal; listed among Brooks' 'best trades' for the third/fourth push.
setups.grade_reason: Brooks says it increases the odds of a successful two-legged reversal and lists third/fourth-push versions among best trades, but it's a momentum-context modifier that can still fail — B.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
setups.pages: Trends p432,436,466; RPCBB p237-239; Reversals p96,99-100,246
quotes: If each breakout gets a little smaller than the prior one, then this is a shrinking stairs pattern and a sign of waning momentum, which can lead to a larger correction.
Whenever shrinking stairs are present, the trade becomes more likely to be successful and usually signals an imminent two-legged pullback in a strong trend.
Three pushes down does not guarantee a trend reversal.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks says it increases the odds of a successful two-legged reversal and lists third/fourth-push versions among best trades, but it's a momentum-context modifier that can still fail — B.

setups.name: Outside Bar Reversal / Trap
setups.one_liner: An outside bar as a second-entry reversal (or one that flips always-in by trapping failed High/Low 1-2 traders) — excellent in the right spot, but never trade a raw 5-min outside-bar breakout.
setups.context: An outside bar engulfs the prior bar. Best when the bar BEFORE it was a good signal bar, or when it occurs as the SECOND entry in a strong reversal from a trend-line break / trend-channel-line overshoot. Also a powerful trap when a looked-valid Low 1/Low 2 (or High 1/High 2) countertrend setup fails and the outside bar flips the market to always-in the opposite way — common at the start of a strong new trend.
setups.signal_bar: The outside bar itself (or the good signal bar preceding it, whose stop entry gets filled on the outside bar).
setups.entry: Leave the buy/sell stop at 1 tick beyond the prior signal bar's extreme so the fill occurs on the outside bar's reversal; for a failed-Low/High trap, buy above the outside-up bar (sell below the outside-down bar).
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the outside bar's opposite extreme.
setups.management: When it occurs in the first leg of a trend reversal after a strong prior trend, treat it like a strong trend bar — expect an extended two-legged move (trapped traders on both sides prevent normal pullbacks).
setups.invalidation: Do NOT enter a reversal on an outside bar unless the bar before it was a decent signal bar; an outside bar mid-range is meaningless unless followed by a small fade bar. 'It is almost always unwise to enter on a breakout of a 5-minute outside bar, especially if it is large,' because of the distant stop.
setups.probability: 'If an outside bar occurs as the second entry in a strong reversal from a trend line break or trend channel line overshoot, it can be an excellent entry bar'; an outside-up failed Low 1/2 trap is 'a great trade... a sign the trend will likely go far.'
setups.emphasis: Excellent as a second-entry / always-in-flip trap, but explicitly risky/situational otherwise — 'when uncertain, wait for more price action.'
setups.grade_reason: Can be an 'excellent'/'great' entry as a second-entry or trap, but Brooks brackets it with strong warnings against raw outside-bar breakouts and their large risk — nets to B.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
setups.pages: Trends p187-199,295-296; RPCBB p62-68; Reversals p197-198
quotes: If an outside bar occurs as the second entry in a strong reversal from a trend line break or trend channel line overshoot, it can be an excellent entry bar.
It is almost always unwise to enter on a breakout of a 5-minute outside bar, especially if the outside bar is large.
An outside up failed low 1 or low 2 trap like this is common at the start of a strong trend and is a sign that the trend will likely go far.
setups.final_grade_reason: Can be an 'excellent'/'great' entry as a second-entry or trap, but Brooks brackets it with strong warnings against raw outside-bar breakouts and their large risk — nets to B.

setups.name: Shaved Trend Bar Breakout / Continuation
setups.one_liner: A tail-less trend bar in a runaway trend signals one-sided pressure — enter with-trend on its breakout.
setups.context: A trend bar with no tail (or a 1-tick tail) at one or both ends, occurring in the MIDDLE of a strong, one-sided runaway/free-fall trend — one-sided pressure from open to close. NOT valid in a trading range or a quiet / low-volume market, where shaved bars are traps that test extremes repeatedly.
setups.signal_bar: The shaved trend bar itself (a shaved TOP on a bull bar in a runaway bull is the stronger version, since strength carried into the close).
setups.entry: 1 tick beyond the shaved extreme with-trend (often unfillable on a shaved top in a runaway bull because the next bar gaps above — may have to enter at market or on a 1-2 tick bounce).
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the opposite side of the shaved bar.
setups.management: Expect continuation in a runaway trend; a shaved top on a bull bar is a stronger long setup than a shaved bottom.
setups.invalidation: In a trading range do NOT buy above a shaved top / sell below a shaved bottom; in quiet, low-volume markets shaved bars are common and are traps, not strength.
setups.probability: A sign of strength / more to come — only in a strong-trend context.
setups.emphasis: good only in a runaway trend; misleading in ranges/quiet markets
setups.grade_reason: A genuine sign of strength but narrowly applicable (runaway trends only) and explicitly a trap elsewhere, with no stated probability and often unfillable in real time — useful but secondary, hence B.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: A bar with no tail at either end in a strong trend is a sign of strength, and traders should enter with trend on its breakout.
When the market is quiet and the volume is low, shaved tops and bottoms are sometimes common and are not a sign of strength... Each of those bars is a trap.
setups.final_grade_reason: A genuine sign of strength but narrowly applicable (runaway trends only) and explicitly a trap elsewhere, with no stated probability and often unfillable in real time — useful but secondary, hence B.

setups.name: Failed Wedge / Failed Climax Continuation
setups.one_liner: A wedge/climax reversal attempt stalls sideways instead of reversing — trade the with-trend break for a measured move.
setups.context: A wedge/climax reversal attempt completes (e.g., three pushes bouncing off a trend channel line) but the market goes sideways / shows no countertrend momentum instead of reversing — acceptance of the extreme rather than rejection. Wedges most often fail when traders fade a small three-push without a prior clear trendline break or a major trend-channel-line overshoot.
setups.signal_bar: The failed reversal attempt / the break of the wedge extreme in the with-trend direction, often an outside bar trapping counter-trend traders.
setups.entry: 1 tick beyond the break of the wedge extreme (with-trend); wait for the bar to close and the next bar to confirm — do not front-run.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the recent high/low formed during the failed reversal attempt.
setups.management: Expect an approximate measured move equal to the height of the wedge.
setups.invalidation: If a genuine higher-low / lower-high with a strong reversal bar forms, it is a valid wedge REVERSAL, not a failed wedge — don't fight it.
setups.probability: Three small pushes alone rarely reverse a trend absent a prior trendline break/overshoot; 'a failed failed wedge often leads to a strong reversal.'
setups.emphasis: reliable continuation once the reversal clearly fails
setups.grade_reason: A reliable measured-move continuation once the reversal has clearly failed, but it is a second-order 'failure of a pattern' read that requires confirmation and judgment, so B rather than a primary A entry.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: When a Wedge fails, the move will usually be an approximate Measured Move equal to the height of the Wedge.
When a climax pattern fails to form any Countertrend momentum, then assume that you read the market wrong and are looking to trade in the wrong direction.
setups.final_grade_reason: A reliable measured-move continuation once the reversal has clearly failed, but it is a second-order 'failure of a pattern' read that requires confirmation and judgment, so B rather than a primary A entry.

setups.name: Options Expression of a With-Trend Pullback
aliases: Buy Calls on a Pullback to the MA
Buy Puts After a Bear Spike Pullback
Sell a Put Below the Market
setups.one_liner: Express a with-trend pullback entry through options — buy calls/puts on the MA pullback, or sell a put below the market.
setups.context: A strong bull/bear trend on the daily or 60-min chart where ~80% of attempts to end the trend fail; express with-trend pullback entries via options to reduce overnight/whipsaw risk. Buy calls on each poke below the MA in a bull; buy puts on a two-legged pullback to the MA after a strong bear spike; or sell a put ~2% OTM (or ATM) below the market to get long lower or collect premium in a rising market.
setups.signal_bar: The reversal off the MA pullback (calls/puts) — the same location as the H2-at-MA or two-legged-pullback price-action setup.
setups.entry: Buy ~1-month calls/puts on the pullback close; or sell a ~1-month put at/below the market (e.g., ~2% OTM).
setups.initial_stop: Managed via the option (defined premium at risk for long options); the short put's risk is assignment at the strike.
setups.management: Take partial profits after a few days, exit the rest at the first reversal sign or on a pullback near the entry; on a sold put, keep the premium if price holds/rises, or get assigned (buy the stock) at a lower net cost if it falls below the strike.
