setups.invalidation: Same as the underlying price-action setup — a confirmed trend reversal (strong trendline break + test) negates it.
setups.probability: 'About 80 percent of attempts to end a strong trend fail.'
setups.emphasis: high-probability trend continuation expressed through options
setups.grade_reason: Rests on Brooks' high-probability trend-continuation logic (~80% of trend-ending attempts fail), but it is an options/instrument expression of the H2-at-MA and two-legged-pullback setups rather than a distinct price-action signal — B.
quotes: Buying calls on a pullback to the moving average in a bull trend... About 80 percent of attempts to end a strong trend fail.
If you are long a stock that is basing on the daily chart and you are willing to add on lower, you could sell a put at one strike below the market.
setups.final_grade_reason: Rests on Brooks' high-probability trend-continuation logic (~80% of trend-ending attempts fail), but it is an options/instrument expression of the H2-at-MA and two-legged-pullback setups rather than a distinct price-action signal — B.

setups.name: Triangle (Three-Push) — Fade Edges and Trade the Breakout
setups.one_liner: When both a double top and double bottom form in one range (a triangle / three-push pattern), the market is in breakout mode — scalp fades at the edges, and trade the eventual breakout in its direction.
setups.context: A mostly sideways range develops three or more pushes in one or both directions (a triangle). When both a double top and a double bottom form within the same range, this usually creates a triangle, meaning breakout mode: the market tends to return to the middle then test an extreme (the third push). In an overall tight bear channel, a bottoming-looking triangle is unlikely to be a reliable buy. A special three-push variant often lacks a wedge shape but is a reliable breakout setup.
setups.signal_bar: A reversal bar at a range edge (for the scalp fade); a bear/bull reversal bar forming the third push (closes past its midpoint even with a small opposite body); or the second minor breakout beyond a prior minor swing extreme being exceeded again (for the breakout).
setups.entry: Scalp shorts as the market reverses down at the top and longs as it reverses up at the bottom. For the breakout: sell stop below the reversal/signal bar on a downside triangle breakout (in a bear trend), or enter in the direction of the eventual strong breakout once the second minor breakout is exceeded.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the range edge / above the signal bar / triangle top.
setups.management: Range fades: ~60% for a reward at least as large as risk. If the range is too tight, swing or wait for the breakout; if the breakout is strong, trade its direction (often 70%+ for adequate reward); if it fails, trade the opposite direction. Breakout target: approximate measured move equal to the height of the pattern.
setups.invalidation: A small doji/bull body signal bar is weaker than one with a real bear body.
setups.probability: About 60% for range-edge fades (reward ≥ risk); about 70% or more for strong breakouts; the three-push breakout 'usually leads to a significant breakout.'
setups.emphasis: 'A reliable sell setup' once confirmed as a triangle in a bear trend; the three-push breakout is 'important because it is a reliable breakout setup.'
setups.grade_reason: Solid, quantified (~60% fades, ~70% breakouts) and called reliable, but it is a breakout-mode pattern with two-way ambiguity requiring correct context reading rather than a top-tier standalone edge.
quotes: Once the bar 9 bear reversal bar formed the third push up, traders were confident that the pattern was a triangle in a bear trend, and a reliable sell setup.
If the setup is good, the probability of success is about 60 percent for a reward that is at least as large as the risk.
If the market then breaks again beyond that second minor breakout, it usually leads to a significant breakout and an approximate measured move equal to the height of the pattern.
setups.final_grade_reason: Solid, quantified (~60% fades, ~70% breakouts) and called reliable, but it is a breakout-mode pattern with two-way ambiguity requiring correct context reading rather than a top-tier standalone edge.

setups.name: Weak High 1/2 or Low 1/2 Limit-Order Fade (Institutional Buy-Low/Sell-High)
setups.one_liner: At a range extreme, sell on a limit above a weak (doji/small) high 1 or high 2 signal bar, or buy on a limit below a weak low 1/low 2 — trading like the institutions do, since these with-trend signals are traps in a range.
setups.context: At the top of a trading range (or start of a possible new bear trend after a strong reversal down), a weak, small-bodied/doji high 1 or high 2 signal bar forms; mirror at the bottom for low 1/2. Typically the middle third of the day (~8:30-11:00/11:30 PST) when the market is two-sided in a tight range and stop entries are terrible. High 1 buys at the top and low 1 sells at the bottom are usually traps/vacuum moves, not real trend strength.
setups.signal_bar: A weak (doji or small-body) high 1 / high 2 at resistance, or a weak low 1 / low 2 at support.
setups.entry: Place a limit order to short at or above the high of the weak high 1/2 buy signal bar (or go long at or below the low of the weak low 1/2 short signal bar), fading the with-trend breakout.
setups.initial_stop: About 2-6 ticks beyond the signal bar (example: ~6 ticks), sized so target and stop are roughly symmetric.
setups.management: Scalp a target of similar size to the stop (e.g., a 6-tick limit) back toward the middle/other side of the range; mimic institutional buy-low/sell-high.
setups.invalidation: In a genuinely strong bull trend, low 1/low 2 setups 'do not exist and are only traps' — do not fade a weak high there; only fade when a range or credible reversal context is real. Do not fade in the spike phase of a strong new trend.
setups.probability: 'At least a 60 percent chance of making six ticks while risking six ticks' near the extremes; 40-60% overall depending on circumstances (reward ≥ 2x risk at the low end).
setups.emphasis: 'Often good fade setups'; 'the best way to make money... is to do what the institutions do' — but explicitly cautioned against for beginners.
setups.grade_reason: Quantified ~60% at the extremes and endorsed as the institutional way to trade a range, but limit-order fading is harder to evaluate and Brooks explicitly warns beginners off it — solidly B.
quotes: High 1 buy setups at the tops of strong bull spikes and low 1 short setups at the bottom of strong bear spikes are common in trading ranges and are usually traps. In fact, they are often good fade setups.
Experienced traders will sell on limit orders above weak high 1 and high 2 signal bars at the top, and buy on limit orders below weak low 1 and low 2 signal bars at the bottom.
in a strong bull trend, low 1 and low 2 signals do not exist and are only traps
setups.final_grade_reason: Quantified ~60% at the extremes and endorsed as the institutional way to trade a range, but limit-order fading is harder to evaluate and Brooks explicitly warns beginners off it — solidly B.

setups.name: With-Trend Swing-Point Breakout Entry (very strong trend, no pullback)
aliases: buy above prior swing high in a strong trend
setups.one_liner: In a very strong trend with no reachable pullbacks, enter on a stop beyond the prior swing high/low (or the top of an embedded trending range) rather than miss the move — a lower-probability, wider-stop fallback to buying pullbacks.
setups.context: The trend is very strong with few/no with-trend pullbacks you can enter on (steep, tight, one-sided). Includes a trending trading range where both swing highs and swing lows are already trending, indicating an embedded trend before the visible breakout. Also the substitute-for-pullback entries on days that rarely pull back (ii breakout, two-legged sideways-correction breakout, tight-range breakout as part of the first leg).
setups.signal_bar: The prior swing high/low bar, or the breakout bar above the trending-range top / brief sideways pause (ii, tight range).
setups.entry: Buy stop 1 tick above the prior swing high (sell 1 tick below the prior swing low); or buy the breakout of the embedded range / ii / tight-range pause; or buy at market / at closes of trend bars / at-or-below the prior bar low.
setups.initial_stop: Wide — worst case to the start of the spike; requires reduced size. Tighten once the market moves in favor.
setups.management: Trade smaller due to the wide stop; a pullback that cannot drop back below the breakout point is a sign of strength (bulls in control).
setups.probability: Lower probability / higher risk than buying pullbacks: 'buying pullbacks before the breakout generally offers more reward, smaller risk, and a higher probability of success.'
setups.emphasis: The fallback when the trend is too strong to offer pullbacks — necessary to avoid missing the trend, not a first-choice edge.
setups.grade_reason: Brooks endorses it as necessary in very strong trends but explicitly lower-probability and wider-risk than pullback entries; grades B as a context-gated fallback.
quotes: When a bull trend is very strong, buying on a stop above a prior swing high.
buying pullbacks before the breakout generally offers more reward, smaller risk, and a higher probability of success.
both the swing highs and the swing lows were trending upward, indicating that there was already a bull trend going on within the trading range.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks endorses it as necessary in very strong trends but explicitly lower-probability and wider-risk than pullback entries; grades B as a context-gated fallback.

setups.name: Late / Missed Entry — Enter at Market (Always-In)
aliases: It's Never Too Late to Enter a Strong Trend
setups.one_liner: Missed the entry but the trend is clearly always-in — enter at market at swing-portion size using the original stop.
setups.context: You missed the original with-trend entry, but the market is clearly always-in and you would still be holding the swing portion had you taken it (e.g., 4+ not-too-large trend bars underway).
setups.signal_bar: None — entry is at market, not on a signal-bar break.
setups.entry: Buy/sell at market, sized to only the swing-portion size you'd still be holding from the hypothetical original entry (smaller, to keep dollar risk constant).
setups.initial_stop: The SAME stop you'd be using had you taken the original entry (breakeven or beyond the original signal bar), then trail it.
setups.management: Add on at pauses/pullbacks (the next H2/L2); after adding, move the whole-position stop beyond the newest signal bar and trail.
setups.invalidation: Only valid if you genuinely believe you'd still be holding the swing portion — otherwise it is a discipline/emotional issue to resolve, not a chart trade.
setups.probability: 'the probability of making a profit is high' when the always-in direction is clear.
setups.emphasis: logically identical to holding the original swing position
setups.grade_reason: High-probability when always-in is clear, but it is a discretionary catch-up entry contingent on an honest self-assessment rather than a discrete chart signal — a management principle, hence B.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: If you look at any chart and think that if you had taken the original entry you would still be holding the swing portion of your trade, then you need to enter at the market.
Entering late while using the original stop is absolutely identical to being long the swing portion of the original position, using the same protective stop.
setups.final_grade_reason: High-probability when always-in is clear, but it is a discretionary catch-up entry contingent on an honest self-assessment rather than a discrete chart signal — a management principle, hence B.

setups.name: Head and Shoulders as a With-Trend Flag / Failed H&S
setups.one_liner: A H&S top in a bull trend (or bottom in a bear) is really a wedge flag that usually continues the trend, not a reversal.
setups.context: A head-and-shoulders TOP within a bull trend (or H&S bottom within a bear) is really a triangle / wedge flag (three pushes: left shoulder, head, right shoulder), NOT a reversal — most fail to reverse and instead continue the trend. The right shoulder is a lower-high breakout pullback after the bull trend line breaks. Most H&S reversal patterns fail and become two-legged pullback continuation patterns; the shape alone (no prior countertrend strength) is a low-probability reversal.
setups.signal_bar: Failure of the neckline breakout in the countertrend direction, or the breakout beyond the right-shoulder line resuming the trend.
setups.entry: Buy above the right shoulder / above the failed downside neckline break (in a bull); short below the right shoulder for an H&S bottom in a bear.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the head or right-shoulder extreme.
setups.management: Trade it as a continuation; target trend resumption.
setups.invalidation: Like all continuation patterns it occasionally fails into a genuine reversal — that happens mainly when there was prior clear countertrend strength.
setups.probability: 'Most reversal patterns fail and become two-legged pullback continuation patterns' / 'Most Head and Shoulder patterns fail and become With Trend setups.'
setups.emphasis: reliable when read as continuation; a costly trap when misread as a reversal
setups.grade_reason: Reliable as a continuation ('rarely fail'), but it is a shape-based pattern that has 'cost more beginning traders money than almost any other' when misread, and the reversal read needs prior strength — solid but requiring judgment, hence B.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: Head and shoulders tops are really triangles or wedge bull flags and are reliable buy setups.
Most Head and Shoulder patterns fail and become With Trend setups... The shape alone is not enough reason to place a Countertrend trade.
setups.final_grade_reason: Reliable as a continuation ('rarely fail'), but it is a shape-based pattern that has 'cost more beginning traders money than almost any other' when misread, and the reversal read needs prior strength — solid but requiring judgment, hence B.

setups.name: Deep Pullback Buy (60-70% Retracement; 75% Reclassification)
setups.one_liner: Buy a ~60-70% pullback in a trend on a limit, risking to a new extreme; a ≥75% retrace flips it to a new trend.
setups.context: An established trend with a deep pullback of about 60-70% of the current leg; buy on a limit at the retracement, risking to a lower low (higher high in a bear). If the pullback retraces 75% or more, the odds of tagging the prior extreme fall substantially — stop treating it as a pullback and treat it as the start of a new opposite trend.
setups.signal_bar: None specific — a measured-retracement limit entry (better with a reversal bar at the level).
setups.entry: Limit buy at the ~60-70% pullback level (mirror sell in a bear), risking to a lower low, taking profit at/above a new high.
setups.initial_stop: Below a potential lower low (above a higher high).
setups.management: Reward is about 2x the risk.
setups.invalidation: A pullback of ≥75% → no longer a with-trend pullback; treat it as a new trend in the opposite direction.
setups.probability: 'about 60 percent, reward is about twice the risk' — a strong, positive trader's equation.
setups.emphasis: explicitly a strong trader's-equation trade
setups.grade_reason: Brooks gives a clean, positive trader's equation (~60%, 2x reward), but it is a deep-pullback limit entry that risks catching a reversal (hence the 75% rule) — favorable but a secondary approach, so B.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: In a bull trend, buy a 60 to 70 percent pullback from the current high, risking to a lower low, and taking profit at or above a new high (the reward is about twice the risk, the probability is about 60 percent).
The odds that a pullback will get back to the prior trend's extreme fall substantially if the pullback retraces 75 percent or more.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks gives a clean, positive trader's equation (~60%, 2x reward), but it is a deep-pullback limit entry that risks catching a reversal (hence the 75% rule) — favorable but a secondary approach, so B.

setups.name: Barbwire With-Trend Second Entry
setups.one_liner: When barbwire forms as a pullback to the MA in a strong trend, take the with-trend second entry as it resolves.
setups.context: Barbwire (overlapping bars / dojis) forms as a pullback to the moving average within a strong trend and stays mostly on the with-trend side of the MA; as it transitions into bars with good bodies and small tails it resolves into a with-trend High 2 / Low 2. Take the SECOND entry in the trend direction even inside the barbwire.
setups.signal_bar: A with-trend reversal bar forming a High 2 / Low 2 inside the barbwire, ideally at the same price as an earlier failed signal (second entry).
setups.entry: Buy above the High 2 signal bar (sell below the Low 2) even though it is inside barbwire.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the signal bar.
setups.management: Leads to a two-bar spike then a tight channel; the opposing side steps aside for a couple of bars, making the move one-sided.
setups.invalidation: Never buy if the barbwire bars are mostly BELOW the MA; never sell if mostly ABOVE it — regardless of trend context.
setups.probability: With-trend second entry in a strong trend (reliable in that specific context).
setups.emphasis: take the with-trend second entry despite the barbwire
setups.grade_reason: Reliable in its narrow context ('you need to take the second entry in the direction of that strong trend'), but barbwire is inherently chop with a strict location filter, making it a conditional B rather than a clean high-probability entry.
quotes: If barbwire is at the end of a pullback to the moving average in a strong trend, you need to take the second entry in the direction of that strong trend.
Never look to buy if the bars are mostly below the moving average, and never look to sell if the bars are mostly above the moving average.
setups.final_grade_reason: Reliable in its narrow context ('you need to take the second entry in the direction of that strong trend'), but barbwire is inherently chop with a strict location filter, making it a conditional B rather than a clean high-probability entry.

setups.name: Measured-Move Projection & Breakout-Test Limit Fade
setups.one_liner: Project a target one channel/range height beyond a breakout to anticipate pauses and reversals, and (for experienced traders) fade the breakout on a limit order at the measured-move distance, expecting a test back to the breakout point.
setups.context: Any channel (trend channel, trading range, double top/bottom, wedge) with a successful breakout: measure the height at the point of breakout. Applies as a target/management tool always, and as an entry when a range breaks out but not too strongly.
setups.signal_bar: None required for the target tool; for the entry, price reaching approximately the measured-move distance beyond the range.
setups.entry: Not an entry pattern itself for targeting. For the fade: buy a bear breakout on a limit order about a measured-move distance below the range (mirror for a bull breakout), expecting a test back up to the breakout point.
setups.initial_stop: Equal to the measured-move distance risked.
setups.management: Take partial/full profit as price approaches the target; watch for a pause, pullback, or reversal exactly at the projected level. When several independent measured-move targets converge near the same price, the odds of a reaction there increase. If price runs well beyond the target, a new trend is likely underway.
setups.invalidation: Only for experienced traders on the limit-fade version; not for strong breakouts with follow-through.
setups.probability: 40 to 60 percent depending on circumstances for the breakout-test fade (reward must be ~2x risk when probability is only 40%); 'the market often hits them exactly.'
setups.emphasis: The target tool is a core, frequently-used management device; the limit fade is for 'only very experienced traders.'
setups.grade_reason: The measured-move target is a high-value core tool, but as a tradeable entry the breakout-test limit fade is 40-60% and restricted to very experienced traders, pulling the overall grade to B.
quotes: The measured move targets are only approximate, but the market often hits them exactly and then pauses, pulls back, or reverses. If the market goes much beyond the target, a new trend is likely underway.
Buying a bear breakout at around a measured move, if the breakout is not too strong... Only very experienced traders should consider this.
setups.final_grade_reason: The measured-move target is a high-value core tool, but as a tradeable entry the breakout-test limit fade is 40-60% and restricted to very experienced traders, pulling the overall grade to B.

setups.name: Double Bottom / Double Top Flag in a Range
setups.one_liner: Buy a double bottom (or short a double top) where a support/resistance line is tested to the tick and holds, often reversing a failed reciprocal flag.
setups.context: Price repeatedly tests a support/trend line to the tick during a tight range (often following a sell climax) and cannot break lower, indicating strong buyers; mirror for a double top after a buy climax. Commonly a double top bear flag fails and reverses into a double bottom bull flag (or vice versa), often in stocks in the first hour. Trading ranges after a strong prior move are usually continuation patterns.
setups.signal_bar: Inside bars and higher lows at the repeatedly defended support line (or lower highs at resistance); a reversal bar at the failed double top/bottom.
setups.entry: Buy above the inside bar / higher low at the defended support line (mirror short below the lower high at resistance); or enter beyond the failed double-top/bottom pattern bar.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the tested extreme / signal bar.
setups.management: Repeated reversals build a range or triangle; look for support at the EMA on pullbacks.
setups.emphasis: A recognized, repeatedly-illustrated reversal-within-range pattern; strong-buyer/strong-seller read.
setups.grade_reason: A clean, well-known Brooks pattern illustrated in multiple books, but presented without explicit probability figures and often as a range-continuation flag, so B rather than A.
quotes: Notice how the market repeatedly tested the support line at the bar 7 low to the tick and continued to find buyers. There were double bottom pullback long setups at the inside bar after bar 8, the inside bar after bar 9, and the higher lows at bars 9 and 11.
Bars 2 and 4... formed a Double Top Bear Flag that failed, and the market then formed a Bars 3 and 5 Double Bottom Bull Flag.
setups.final_grade_reason: A clean, well-known Brooks pattern illustrated in multiple books, but presented without explicit probability figures and often as a range-continuation flag, so B rather than A.

setups.name: Failed Reversal Bar (Reversal Bar Failure — Opposite Entry)
setups.one_liner: A reversal bar forms but price breaks the wrong (with-trend) side, trapping the early countertrend entrants; enter with-trend beyond the bar's opposite extreme.
setups.context: A countertrend reversal bar forms in an area where a reversal might reasonably occur, but either its entry never triggers (price doesn't trade 1 tick beyond it) or the next bar breaks out the OPPOSITE (with-trend) side — trapping the traders who entered early anticipating the reversal. 'A reversal bar alone is not enough reason to enter.'
setups.signal_bar: The failed reversal bar plus the bar that breaks its opposite (with-trend) extreme.
setups.entry: Stop 1 tick beyond the with-trend break of the reversal bar's opposite extreme (buy above a failed bear reversal bar in a bull trend; sell below a failed bull reversal bar in a bear trend), since the trapped early entrants must exit into the move.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the reversal bar's extreme on the trapped side.
setups.management: Trade with-trend; trapped-trader stops fuel the continuation.
setups.invalidation: If the reversal bar's own entry triggers and holds (the reversal succeeds), this setup does not apply.
setups.probability: 'Failed reversal bars are often setups in the opposite direction' — a reliable trapped-trader continuation.
setups.emphasis: Reliable trap entry; underscores that a reversal bar by itself is insufficient.
setups.grade_reason: A dependable trapped-trader continuation Brooks highlights, but a niche/opportunistic entry rather than a headline reversal — B.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: A reversal bar failure is the bar after a reversal bar that breaks out of the wrong side of the reversal.
Failed reversal bars are often setups in the opposite direction.
setups.final_grade_reason: A dependable trapped-trader continuation Brooks highlights, but a niche/opportunistic entry rather than a headline reversal — B.

setups.name: First-Hour Two-Sided Breakout Mode (Double Top → Double Bottom trap)
setups.one_liner: In the first hour it is common to see a double top form, fail, then a double bottom form (or vice versa), putting the market in two-sided breakout mode; take BOTH entries (short below one, long above the other) because a big move usually follows one of them.
setups.context: First hour, often after a large gap and an unusually small opening range (breakout mode). A trend-from-the-open attempt has its extreme tested/retested near the open (frequently on an early scheduled report), forming a double top or double bottom before the real trend begins. Two opposing signals form close together.
setups.signal_bar: The bar defining each flag (the two-bar reversal at each swing extreme; e.g. bar defining the double-top bear flag at the falling MA, and the double-bottom bull flag).
setups.entry: Take both: short 1 tick below the double-top bear-flag entry bar, and long 1 tick above the double-bottom bull-flag entry bar (traditional approach doubles size on the second/reversing side). Better approach: short below the two-bar reversal, scalp part, tighten, then reverse long on the next H2, scalp, tighten, and keep scalp-and-reversing until a clear trend confirms, then swing the balance into the close.
setups.initial_stop: 1 tick beyond the opposite spike extreme (traditional), or each scalp signal-bar low/high (aggressive).
setups.management: Swing part of the winning side because 'a big move commonly follows either the first or the second pattern.' One extreme usually forms in the first hour, then the market moves away from it for the next couple of hours (or all day on a trend day).
setups.emphasis: Common, tradeable first-hour pattern; 'take both entries.' The aggressive scalp-and-reverse approach is 'generally better' than the traditional double-stop approach.
setups.grade_reason: Brooks calls it common and tradeable and worth swinging part, but it is inherently two-sided (you expect to be wrong on one side and reverse), so it grades B rather than A.
quotes: In the first hour, it is common to see both a double top and a double bottom, putting the market in breakout mode... you should take both entries (short below bar 4 and then go long above bar 5) and swing part because a big move commonly follows either the first or the second pattern.
This is the traditional approach to this setup, but it is generally better to be more aggressive.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks calls it common and tradeable and worth swinging part, but it is inherently two-sided (you expect to be wrong on one side and reverse), so it grades B rather than A.

setups.name: First-Bar / Gap Trend Bar Breakout (Trend from the Open)
setups.one_liner: When the first bar (or two) of the day is a strong trend bar — especially on a gap open — trade the breakout of its high/low; if stopped on the next bar, reverse, because it can become a trend-from-the-open day.
setups.context: The first bar (or first couple of bars) of the day is a strong trend bar (small tail, good size). A gap open improves the odds (market more overdone, moves tend to be stronger).
setups.signal_bar: The first strong trend bar of the day (or, if it fails, the opposite strong trend bar that follows).
setups.entry: Stop entry in the direction of the trend bar (breakout of its high/low). If it fails, especially with an opposite trend bar, take the reversal in the other direction.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the opposite end of the signal/entry bar.
setups.management: Treat as at least a scalp, often a good swing (swing part with a breakeven stop). If your stop is hit on the next bar, reverse for a swing — the market will usually move more than the ticks you lost, and it can develop into a trend-from-the-open day.
setups.probability: Higher chance of success with a gap. 'It would be very unusual for back-to-back opposite trend bars on the open to both fail to yield at least a scalp.'
setups.emphasis: Good trade even without a gap, better with one; strong trend bar on the open 'usually leads to at least a tradable scalp and often a good swing.'
setups.grade_reason: Brooks calls it a good trade with reliable scalp odds and swing potential, but gives no high explicit probability and it is primarily a scalp-with-reverse; grades B.
quotes: If there is a gap open and the first bar is a strong (small tail, good-size bar) trend bar, trading its breakout in either direction is a good trade.
if you enter and your protective stop is hit on the next bar, reverse for a swing trade because the market will usually move more than the number of ticks that you lost on the first entry.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks calls it a good trade with reliable scalp odds and swing potential, but gives no high explicit probability and it is primarily a scalp-with-reverse; grades B.

setups.name: Fade the Middle of the Range (50% Setup, Reward >= 2x Risk)
setups.one_liner: With a very tight stop and a target twice the risk, fade a minor reversal in the middle of a range — a 'best trade' whose edge is entirely the reward:risk ratio, not the ~50% probability.
setups.context: Market is in the middle of a multi-day/session trading range with no clear directional edge (about 50% either way for an equidistant move); today's range is unusually small relative to the recent average, raising the odds of an expansion.
setups.signal_bar: Any reasonable reversal bar at/near a minor swing extreme within the range (e.g., after eight bars up with three failed attempts to break lower).
setups.entry: Stop entry in the fade direction just beyond the signal bar.
setups.initial_stop: Very tight, just beyond the signal bar (cited as low as 4 ticks in the ES example).
setups.management: Use an OCO limit target far larger than the risk (cited: risk 4 ticks to make 16 ticks); patiently hold through pullbacks because a 50% setup has material give-back risk.
setups.invalidation: None by design — accept the ~50% win rate; the edge comes entirely from reward:risk, not probability.
setups.probability: About 50% chance of success; profitable because reward is roughly twice (up to 4x in the example) the risk.
setups.emphasis: Explicitly labeled a 'best trade' despite only ~50% probability, purely via trader's-equation math.
setups.grade_reason: Brooks calls it a 'best trade,' but only because of the reward:risk math on an intentionally marginal ~50% probability — not a high-probability setup, so B.
quotes: Even though selling in the middle of a trading range might have only about a 50 percent chance of success, if the reward is twice the size of the risk, it can be a best trade.
The actual risk was only four ticks, so traders were risking four ticks to make 16 ticks and they had about a 50 percent chance of success.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks calls it a 'best trade,' but only because of the reward:risk math on an intentionally marginal ~50% probability — not a high-probability setup, so B.

setups.name: Two-Legged Spike to a New Extreme Fade
setups.one_liner: In a clearly defined range, fade a two-legged, two-trend-bar spike that pokes to a slightly new high (or low) in the middle of the day, especially if it is part of a wedge or trend-line test.
setups.context: Clearly defined trading range with multiple reversals; a two-legged, two-bull-trend-bar spike pokes to a slightly new high in the middle of the day (mirror bear spike to a new low in the bottom half of the range), often coinciding with a wedge top or trend-line test.
setups.signal_bar: The two-bar spike itself.
setups.entry: Sell at market near the new slightly-higher high (or buy at market near the new slightly-lower low); can scale in higher/lower.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the spike extreme.
setups.management: Especially good if part of another pattern (wedge, bull/bear trend-line test); scaling in improves it.
setups.emphasis: 'This is often a good signal to go short at the market, especially if you can scale in higher and especially if it is a part of another pattern.'
setups.grade_reason: Brooks calls it 'often a good signal' but gives no probability figure and conditions it on being part of another pattern and on scaling — a good but qualified aggressive fade.
quotes: this is often a good signal to go short at the market, especially if you can scale in higher and especially if it is a part of another pattern, like if it is at the top of a small wedge
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks calls it 'often a good signal' but gives no probability figure and conditions it on being part of another pattern and on scaling — a good but qualified aggressive fade.

setups.name: Climax Reversal into Trading Range Fade
setups.one_liner: After a spike up followed by a spike down (or vice versa), expect a trading range — fade the extremes rather than chase, and project a measured move equal to the spike height on the eventual breakout.
setups.context: Whenever the market has a spike up followed by a spike down (a climactic reversal), it tends to enter a trading range as bulls and bears fight for follow-through; the range can last one bar to dozens of bars.
setups.signal_bar: Reversal bars within the resulting range, especially two-bar reversals near the day's extreme.
setups.entry: Fade the extremes of the resulting range as usual (buy low, sell high) rather than take with-trend continuation.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the range extreme.
setups.management: The eventual breakout usually leads to a measured move approximating the height of the spike — target accordingly.
setups.emphasis: A general principle / context flag rather than a single precise setup.
setups.grade_reason: A useful context principle for anticipating a range after a climax, but given as guidance without a discrete signal bar or probability, so it grades B.
quotes: Whenever there is a spike up and then a spike down, or vice versa, the market has formed a climax reversal... This two-sided trading that usually takes place after a climax is a trading range... The eventual breakout will usually lead to a measured move that approximates the height of the spike.
setups.final_grade_reason: A useful context principle for anticipating a range after a climax, but given as guidance without a discrete signal bar or probability, so it grades B.

setups.name: Trading-Range Scaling Entry (Low 3 / Low 4, High 3 / High 4)
setups.one_liner: When price has run many bars toward one range edge, short the low 3 (or buy the high 3) with a wide stop and scale in on the low 4 / high 4 if the first attempt fails.
setups.context: Market has been moving toward one edge of the range for many bars (e.g., up eight bars, forming a low 3 below the top of the range) and a with-trend range trader wants a scalp reversal, but the first attempt may fail.
setups.signal_bar: The Nth (3rd) push toward the extreme; if it fails, the next (4th) push.
setups.entry: Short the low 3 setup with a wide stop; if it fails, add to the short (scale in) a fixed number of ticks above entry, above a prior swing high, or on a stop at the low 4 signal (mirror for high 3/high 4 longs).
setups.initial_stop: Wide, covering both entries (example: 4 points on the Emini with a ~10-point average range).
setups.management: Exit both positions near the bottom of the range, or at the first entry's price (breakeven on the first, profit on the second, higher-priced entry).
setups.probability: 'If the low 3 fails, it is likely that there will soon be a low 4 and that it will be successful.'
setups.emphasis: A recognized scaling technique in ranges for experienced traders.
setups.grade_reason: A legitimate documented scaling method with a stated likelihood the low 4 succeeds, but it requires wide stops and scaling into a losing position — an experienced-trader technique, hence B.
quotes: If the low 3 fails, it is likely that there will soon be a low 4 and that it will be successful.
setups.final_grade_reason: A legitimate documented scaling method with a stated likelihood the low 4 succeeds, but it requires wide stops and scaling into a losing position — an experienced-trader technique, hence B.

setups.name: Classic Reversal Bar (Bull / Bear)
setups.one_liner: The best-known signal bar: a bar that closes strongly counter to the prior move with a prominent tail and little overlap, taken at a swing extreme after a trend-line break.
setups.context: Occurs at the end of a trend leg / swing extreme, best when it also tests a prior extreme after a trend-line break. Must NOT overlap prior bars excessively (a bull reversal bar's midpoint should be above the prior bar's low; heavy overlap = trading range, not a reversal). A large doji is a one-bar trading range, not a signal.
setups.signal_bar: Bull: close above open/midpoint, opens near/below prior close and closes above open and above prior close, lower tail ~1/3-1/2 the bar's height with small/no upper tail, little overlap, closes above the highs/closes of more than one prior bar. Bear = mirror image.
setups.entry: Stop order 1 tick beyond the reversal bar's extreme (buy 1 tick above the high for a bull reversal bar).
setups.initial_stop: 1 tick beyond the opposite extreme of the signal bar (full bar range + 2 ticks in his 8-tick example ≈ 10 ticks risk).
setups.management: Best when the ENTRY bar is a strong trend bar (not a doji/inside bar). Target often several times the risk (e.g., 10-tick risk, 20+ tick target).
setups.invalidation: If the reversal bar largely overlaps prior bars (midpoint inside the prior range) it's part of a trading range; a doji/inside entry bar means lacking urgency → skip or manage for breakeven. In a bull/bear flag near the EMA a big overlapping reversal bar is often a TRAP.
setups.probability: 'About 60 percent of the time, a large reversal bar with excessive overlap in a bull/bear flag near the moving average is a trap and the market turns the other way soon after entry.'
setups.emphasis: 'One of the most reliable signal bars' when clean — but a reversal bar ALONE is not enough reason to enter; it needs the right location/context.
setups.grade_reason: Brooks calls it the best-known / most reliable signal bar, but warns a bar alone isn't a reason to enter and that ~60% of overlapping ones near the EMA are traps — a strong-but-conditional B.
quotes: The best-known signal bar is the reversal bar.
About 60 percent of the time, this is a bull trap and the market will turn down soon after entry.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks calls it the best-known / most reliable signal bar, but warns a bar alone isn't a reason to enter and that ~60% of overlapping ones near the EMA are traps — a strong-but-conditional B.

setups.name: Breakaway Gap Breakout Pullback (measured move)
setups.one_liner: A spike breaks a range and leaves a breakaway gap between the range edge and the breakout-pullback entry bar; the middle of that gap tends to mark the middle of the full move, giving a measured-move target.
setups.context: A spike breaks below/above an established trading range, creating a breakaway gap between the range edge and the breakout-pullback bar.
setups.signal_bar: The breakout-pullback entry bar just past the gap (e.g. bar 10 following the bar 9 spike).
setups.entry: Enter in the direction of the breakaway gap at the breakout-pullback bar.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the pullback extreme.
setups.management: Project a measured move: the middle of the breakaway gap tends to equal the middle of the full move (from the prior swing extreme to the eventual close/extreme).
setups.emphasis: Followed by 'a perfect measured move into the close' in the cited example.
setups.grade_reason: A reliable measured-move projection but only one instance and no explicit probability; it is a specialized measuring-gap variant of the breakout pullback, so B.
quotes: The bar 9 spike down that broke below the upper trading range was followed by a perfect measured move into the close. Bar 9 was a breakaway gap, the gap being between the bottom of the upper range and the bar 10 breakout pullback.
setups.final_grade_reason: A reliable measured-move projection but only one instance and no explicit probability; it is a specialized measuring-gap variant of the breakout pullback, so B.

setups.name: Gap Pullback / Opening Reversal Into the Gap
setups.one_liner: With a with-trend daily setup but a gap beyond yesterday's range, wait on the 5-min chart for a pullback that tests/closes the gap and then reverses, and enter on that failed pullback as the market resumes away from yesterday's range.
setups.context: There is a with-trend setup on the daily chart but the market gaps beyond yesterday's range (e.g. gaps above yesterday's high on a buy setup). Rather than risk a reversal day, wait intraday for a pullback that tests/closes the gap and reverses. Best only when you can watch the stock intraday; if you cannot watch and there is a gap open, probably best to pass.
setups.signal_bar: Reversal bar after the gap is tested/closed (e.g. an MA gap bar / trend-channel overshoot reversal).
setups.entry: Enter on the failed pullback into the gap; buy the opening reversal back up (mirror for shorts) as the market resumes away from yesterday's range.
setups.initial_stop: Below the low of today's reversal bar / below the bar that closed the gap; risk is less if the market opens within yesterday's range and trades through the stop 1 tick beyond that range.
setups.management: If the entry fails and the stop is hit, look elsewhere or give it only one more try on a second setup; do not keep re-buying the same name after a loss.
setups.invalidation: Entry fails and stop is hit.
setups.emphasis: 'A great long entry' in the cited case (62-cent risk → several-dollar gain), but conditional on being able to watch it intraday.
setups.grade_reason: A high-quality R/R entry when watchable, but Brooks conditions it heavily (pass if you can't watch) and gives no probability; grades B.
quotes: watch for a gap pullback and then enter on the failed pullback into the gap, as the market resumes its move away from yesterday's range.
This was a great long entry with a protective stop below bar 6. This 62 cent risk led to a several-dollar gain.
setups.final_grade_reason: A high-quality R/R entry when watchable, but Brooks conditions it heavily (pass if you can't watch) and gives no probability; grades B.

setups.name: Second Entry (High 2 / Low 2) Reversal
setups.one_liner: After a first reversal attempt fails, a second attempt in the same direction after a pullback — almost always more likely to succeed than the first.
setups.context: A reversal attempt against the trend/momentum fails once (first entry); price resumes for a bar or two, then a second reversal is attempted. Especially required when the prior move had ~4 consecutive trend bars or 2-3 large ones (too much momentum for the first attempt). At the top/bottom of a trading range, at support/resistance, or as a double bottom (= a High 2 buy) / double top (= a Low 2 sell). Most reliable with prior demonstrated countertrend strength (a trend-line break). On ~80% of (non-trending) days, the best 2-5 trades are second-entry reversals at new swing highs/lows.
setups.signal_bar: The second reversal bar/pattern (ideally a trend bar in the trade's direction; a higher low or lower high, or a two-bar reversal on the second attempt).
setups.entry: Stop order 1 tick beyond the second signal bar's extreme, in the reversal direction. Only one reason is needed to take it, since the first entry already existed.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the second signal bar / swing extreme.
setups.management: Manage as any signal-bar entry. Second entries are late/aggressive, so risk is often similar to the first, not better. If a second entry in a good setup fails, assume you are misreading the market — do not take a third (except a wedge).
setups.invalidation: If the second entry fills at a BETTER price than the first, be suspicious it is a trap (failed High/Low 2) — 'most good second entries are at the same price or worse.' Also invalid if the first move had too much momentum with no prior pause.
setups.probability: 'A second entry is almost always more likely to result in a profitable trade than a first entry'; among Brooks' ≥60% setups (buying/shorting a range extreme, especially as a second entry). 'High/Low 2 entries are the best.'
setups.emphasis: Among the most reliable and most-emphasized reversal entries in the entire body of work.
setups.grade_reason: Brooks calls second entries the best and 'almost always more likely to be profitable than a first entry,' and lists them among his ≥60% trades — this is the clearest A+ in the reversal family.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: A second entry is almost always more likely to result in a profitable trade than is a first entry.
High/Low 2 entries are the best.
If the second entry is letting you in at a better price than the first, be suspicious that it might be a trap; most good second entries are at the same price or worse.
setups.final_grade_reason: FAILED backtest — mechanically unprofitable; discretionary-only.

backtest.headline: Fading extension: -0.5R, ~10% win, NEGATIVE every year, every timeframe.
backtest.note: Mechanically fading an extended move in ES is a disaster — an extension is the START of momentum, not exhaustion. Confirms the project's prior 'do not fade 5m extremes' finding. Reversal setups need discretionary context (climax, higher-TF level) that this mechanization lacks.

setups.name: High 4 / Low 4 Pullback
setups.one_liner: A fourth push against the trend — a reliable buy/sell only when the earlier 1/2/3 legs already showed strength.
setups.context: A fourth push against the trend, forming after a failed High 3/Low 3 (or a strong-momentum failed High 2/Low 2) — usually a not-particularly-strong channel. REQUIRES prior demonstrated strength in the High 1/2/3 (or Low 1/2/3) sequence; a bar count alone is insufficient. Low 4 / High 4 setups often form in tight channels, and the entry is a breakout of the tight channel.
setups.signal_bar: The bar completing the fourth push, often a small doji at the channel edge.
setups.entry: Buy stop 1 tick above the H4 signal bar (sell stop 1 tick below the L4); may need a limit a few ticks beyond.
setups.initial_stop: 1 tick beyond the signal bar; if it is a small doji, use a money-management stop (~2 pts / up to 8 ticks in the Emini) since these often run 1 tick before reversing.
setups.management: Target a test of the prior push's extreme / the trend extreme.
setups.invalidation: Do not take a bar-count-only H4/L4 with no prior strength. If the H4/L4 FAILS (price breaks beyond it), the pullback is likely a real trend reversal, not a pullback — stop looking for with-trend trades in the old direction.
setups.probability: 'usually a reliable buy signal, however, in the absence of any bullish strength in the high 1, 2, or 3, you should not take the trade.'
setups.emphasis: marginal / conditional; needs experience — a caution setup
setups.grade_reason: Brooks flags it as conditional and cautionary ('you should not take the trade' without prior 1/2/3 strength) and a sign trend strength may be fading — a low-confidence, experience-dependent count, hence C.
setups.pages: Trends p171-178,212; Ranges p108-117,203; RPCBB p144-150; Reversals p33
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: Note that bar 7 on the Emini was a high 4, which is usually a reliable buy signal. However, in the absence of any bullish strength in the high 1, 2, or 3, you should not take the trade.
If the high 4 fails to resume the trend and the market falls below its low, it is likely that the market is no longer forming a pullback in a bull trend and instead is in a bear swing.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks flags it as conditional and cautionary ('you should not take the trade' without prior 1/2/3 strength) and a sign trend strength may be fading — a low-confidence, experience-dependent count, hence C.

setups.name: Measured-Move / Magnet Target Fade
setups.one_liner: Fade a strong move as it reaches a measured-move / magnet target (or a deep 50-67% retracement) — a low-probability, high-reward countertrend entry needing prior countertrend strength.
setups.context: A strong spike/trend approaches a projected target: a measured move (spike height, gap midpoint, leg1=leg2, or range height), a prior resistance/support magnet, or the price of an EARLIER failed reversal's signal bar (which acts as a strong magnet the real reversal travels to). Or a deep retracement of a swing (50%, 60%, 63%, 67%). Directional probability near the target reverts toward 50% and can tip to the counter side. Requires prior countertrend strength (a trend-line break) — 'just being in the area of a measured move is not enough reason for a countertrend entry.'
setups.signal_bar: A strong/weakening reversal bar at/near the projected target (or the deep-retracement price level itself).
setups.entry: With-trend traders take profits at the target; experienced countertrend traders enter beyond the reversal bar, or fade on a limit order at the level. For retracements, sell deeper (50→67%) as reward scales.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the overshoot of the target / the swing extreme being tested.
setups.management: Reward must scale with depth: at ≤50% probability, require reward ≥2x risk (e.g., short a 67% retracement so a test of the swing low is 2:1). Scaling into a losing countertrend fade at successive swings is experienced-only and never in a strong trend.
setups.invalidation: Not a countertrend reason on its own without prior countertrend strength; the magnet 'never has to be reached.' Buying trend-channel-line tests repeatedly in a strong trend hoping for a wedge is 'a losing strategy.' Fibonacci 61.8% is not meaningfully more precise than 60/63/67% in computerized markets.
setups.probability: Fading magnets: '40 percent or less' probability, so reward must be ≥2x risk. Directional probability of an equidistant move ~60%+ at a spike's start, decaying toward 50% mid-move and tipping counter at the target.
setups.emphasis: Explicitly a LOW-probability, experienced-only fade justified by reward, not by probability; a reliable profit-TAKING zone more than an entry.
setups.grade_reason: Brooks pegs the fade at ≤40% probability requiring ≥2:1 reward and 'not for beginners' — by his own probability language a C.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: Just being in the area of a measured move is not enough reason for a countertrend entry; you need some earlier countertrend strength.
The entry price of an early reversal that failed is often a magnet for a later, successful reversal.
Fading magnets... the probability is 40 percent or less, so the reward needs to be at least twice the risk.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks pegs the fade at ≤40% probability requiring ≥2:1 reward and 'not for beginners' — by his own probability language a C.

setups.name: Head and Shoulders Reversal / Failed H&S
setups.one_liner: A left-shoulder/head/right-shoulder shape confirmed on a neckline break — but Brooks notes most H&S patterns fail and become continuation flags.
setups.context: A classic H&S shape (or its inverse). Brooks stresses it is really a trading-range/continuation pattern most of the time: 'all head and shoulders tops and bottoms are really head and shoulders continuation patterns (flags).' Requires EARLIER countertrend strength (a trend-line break) before the pattern to be tradable — shape alone is not a reason. A trend channel line across the neckline dragged to the left shoulder roughly projects the right shoulder (minor tool).
setups.signal_bar: Right-shoulder reversal bar, or the neckline-breakout bar.
setups.entry: Stop beyond the right-shoulder signal bar / neckline break; or a breakout-pullback entry after the neckline break.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the right-shoulder extreme.
setups.management: Measured-move target = head-to-neckline distance (or right-shoulder height for a failed-H&S continuation). Move stop to breakeven quickly since most fail; if the breakeven stop is hit and the trend resumes, use the failure (breakout pullback) as a with-trend entry.
setups.invalidation: If price trades back above the right shoulder (bull case), the reversal has FAILED — trade the with-trend continuation for a measured move instead. Most H&S become bull/bear flag continuations, not reversals.
setups.probability: 'Most head and shoulders patterns fail at least 80 percent of the time' (they become continuation flags).
setups.emphasis: Explicitly de-emphasized — Brooks prefers 'double bottom/top pullback' and says never bother with the term; whole figures titled 'Most Head and Shoulders Patterns Fail.'
setups.grade_reason: Brooks actively disparages it (mostly fails, ~80%, prefers other terms) — a clear C on his own emphasis.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: All head and shoulders tops and bottoms are really head and shoulders continuation patterns (flags), and like all trading ranges are much more likely to break out in the direction of the trend.
Most head and shoulders patterns fail at least 80 percent of the time.
You always want to see some earlier countertrend strength prior to the reversal pattern; even then, there are no guarantees.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks actively disparages it (mostly fails, ~80%, prefers other terms) — a clear C on his own emphasis.

setups.name: Tight Trading Range / Barbwire Breakout Pullback
setups.one_liner: A tight, overlapping trading range (barbwire) resolves; base your entry on the FAILURE of the first breakout becoming a breakout pullback, not on the raw breakout — which is frequently not profitable.
setups.context: A tight trading range (2+ overlapping bars, range small vs. ADR, sometimes 20+ bars) or barbwire, often after a long trend (where it may become a final flag). In most cases the first breakout fails within a bar or two, and that failure itself fails, becoming a breakout pullback in the breakout direction. Barbwire can also form at the high/low of the day after a breakout (usually a with-trend continuation: H2/L2, failed breakout, or failed failure).
setups.signal_bar: The pullback bar after the failed breakout attempt (closing without re-entering the range); or an H2/L2 within/at the edge of the barbwire.
setups.entry: Wait for the breakout, then on the pullback buy 1 tick above the pullback bar high (bull) / sell 1 tick below (bear). Only fade the range extreme if the setup bar is small and near the top/bottom. If the pullback plunges through the opposite side, take the reversal instead.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the pullback bar / beyond the tight range.
setups.management: Because barbwire after a long trend is often a final flag, favor scalping the with-trend breakout rather than swinging; after a 3rd consecutive climax expect a two-legged sideways/reversal correction (10+ bars) and scale out.
setups.invalidation: The raw tight-range breakout is 'frequently not a particularly profitable trading pattern'; barbwire raises risk/uncertainty. A 3rd consecutive climax raises odds of a two-legged reversal — don't hold for a big target.
setups.probability: 'The odds of a profitable trade are higher if you base your entry on the failure rather than on the breakout.'
setups.emphasis: The sanctioned way to trade a barbwire/TTR resolution, but low-quality raw material: better to focus on internal setups (trendline breaks, H2/L2) than to trade the TTR breakout itself.
setups.grade_reason: Brooks says the raw tight-range breakout is frequently unprofitable and that traders should mostly avoid trading inside barbwire; even the sanctioned breakout-pullback version is hedged, so C.
setups.pages: Trends p175; Ranges p152; RPCBB p164-165,171
quotes: In most cases, there is a failure within a bar or two of the breakout, and the odds of a profitable trade are higher if you base your entry on the failure rather than on the breakout. In most cases, the failure fails and becomes a Breakout Pullback.
barbwire after a long trend often becomes a final flag.
Barb Wire can also form at the high or low of the day, after a breakout. These are usually With Trend setups... a failed failure, which is a Breakout Pullback, and a strong setup.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks says the raw tight-range breakout is frequently unprofitable and that traders should mostly avoid trading inside barbwire; even the sanctioned breakout-pullback version is hedged, so C.

setups.name: Moving Average Gap Bar Reversal
aliases: first MA gap bar of a leg
setups.one_liner: The first bar in a trend leg whose whole range gaps away from the EMA marks the final leg before a larger correction/reversal; often stacks with a final-flag or wedge signal.
setups.context: After a tight channel that hasn't touched the moving average for many bars, the first bar whose entire range gaps away from (does not touch) the EMA appears. The leg up/down to it almost always breaks the significant trend line, and the subsequent higher/lower-low test of the trend extreme usually leads to a protracted correction or reversal. Frequently coincides with an ii-flag failed breakout (final flag) — 'a reliable signal.'
setups.signal_bar: The moving-average gap bar itself (or the reversal bar at the higher/lower-low test that follows it); on daily/options examples, an MA-gap-bar top with a doji/strong bear reversal bar.
setups.entry: Trade the reversal at the higher/lower-low test after the MA gap bar (or fade the MA-gap-bar top/bottom beyond the signal bar).
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the signal-bar / gap-bar extreme.
setups.management: Expect a protracted correction or reversal; often precedes the final leg before a larger move.
setups.invalidation: Alone it is a context/location marker, not a standalone trigger — needs a reversal bar / confluence.
setups.probability: 'The first moving average gap bar in a bear trend often leads to the final leg down before there is a larger correction up'; when combined with a final-flag/ii pattern, called 'a reliable signal.'
setups.emphasis: A secondary confirmation/location tool that strengthens a final-flag or wedge reversal rather than a standalone edge.
setups.grade_reason: Brooks treats it mainly as a context/location marker that boosts other signals, not a standalone high-probability trigger — C leaning B when stacked with a final flag.
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
setups.pages: Trends p444,458; RPCBB p10; Reversals p240
quotes: The first moving average gap bar in a bear trend often leads to the final leg down before there is a larger correction up.
Here, it was a failed final flag (an ii flag) and an EMA gap bar short setup, and therefore a reliable signal.
Bar 14 was a strong bear reversal bar, a moving average gap bar, and the top of a wedge bear flag, so traders could have bought puts going into the close.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks treats it mainly as a context/location marker that boosts other signals, not a standalone high-probability trigger — C leaning B when stacked with a final flag.

setups.name: Triangle Breakout
setups.one_liner: A convergent, narrowing range with 3+ pushes one way (2 the other) is a triangle — a breakout-mode area of balance; don't trade the middle, trade the eventual breakout / breakout pullback (or fade the extremes only if the range is tall enough).
setups.context: A trading range with at least three pushes in one direction and two in the other, converging/narrowing. Bulls and bears are in rough balance, so it is an area of uncertainty that often becomes the final flag of the prior trend; a breakout is imminent and expected (but not guaranteed) in the trend direction. When triangle-leg counts are ambiguous, the market is simply in a tight range / breakout mode.
setups.signal_bar: Strong breakout bar (for a breakout/breakout-pullback entry), or a Low 2 / High 2 at the triangle edge, or a small reversal bar at the extreme (fade) if the range is tall.
setups.entry: If the breakout is strong: buy the close / above the prior bar / a small limit pullback, or (preferred) wait for the breakout pullback. Safer than fading the extreme directly: stop below the Low 2 (short) / above the High 2 (long) at the edge. If the range is tall enough, fade extremes: buy near the bottom, sell near the top.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the triangle extreme / beyond the breakout spike.
setups.management: A successful breakout usually reaches a measured move ~equal to the height of the triangle; beyond that the market is likely trending. Failed breakouts get drawn back into the range and often break the opposite side, forming a two-legged correction or reversal.
setups.invalidation: Don't buy/sell the first breakout of a steep/tight wedge blind — evaluate breakout strength first. If no pullback forms for several bars, treat as a strong always-in breakout rather than fading it.
setups.emphasis: Marginal on its own — 'not enough to place a trade on that basis alone'; 'it is better to wait for the breakout before placing a trade.'
setups.grade_reason: Brooks explicitly says the triangle alone is not enough to trade and to wait; it is breakout-mode uncertainty with frequent failed breakouts, so it grades C absent a strong breakout or a second-entry edge.
quotes: when the market is in a triangle, it is in breakout mode, meaning that a breakout is imminent.
A trader could have entered on a stop below the trading range low, but it was less risky to short the low 2 below bar 5.
Whenever there is confusion, the market is in a trading range and therefore in breakout mode, and most traders should wait for the breakout instead of trading within a tight trading range.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks explicitly says the triangle alone is not enough to trade and to wait; it is breakout-mode uncertainty with frequent failed breakouts, so it grades C absent a strong breakout or a second-entry edge.

setups.name: Raw Trading Range / Channel Breakout Entry (low-probability)
aliases: buying the breakout of a range
setups.one_liner: Entering on the initial breakout of a trading range or channel itself (not the pullback) — explicitly a low-probability trade Brooks says to avoid except in special circumstances.
setups.context: A horizontal trading range or channel is present and price breaks out; the trader is tempted to enter on the raw break of the range/channel line. Brooks flags this as low-probability because most first breakouts fail.
setups.signal_bar: The breakout bar out of the range/channel.
setups.entry: Stop 1 tick beyond the range/channel edge in the breakout direction (only sanctioned when the with-trend move has clearly already started to resume before the breakout, e.g. an embedded trend).
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the opposite side of the range/channel.
setups.management: If taken, target a measured move ~equal to the range/channel height; strongly prefer to instead wait for the breakout pullback.
setups.invalidation: First breakout of a tight/prolonged channel usually fails, then the trend resumes to a new extreme; entering on the raw breakout of any range is low-probability except in special circumstances.
setups.probability: 'The chance of any one breakout being successful is about 20 percent' (though ~60% the market eventually breaks with-trend). 'Entering on the breakout of any trading range is a low-probability trade, except in special circumstances.'
setups.emphasis: Explicitly discouraged: 'it is usually much better to enter on a breakout pullback.'
setups.grade_reason: Brooks assigns the raw breakout ~20% success and repeatedly says to prefer the pullback; captured as C to document the anti-pattern.
quotes: The chance of any one breakout being successful is about 20 percent, but the chance that the market will eventually break to the upside is about 60 percent.
Entering on the breakout of any trading range is a low-probability trade, except in special circumstances... It is usually much better to enter on a breakout pullback.
Usually the first breakout of a tight, prolonged channel will fail.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks assigns the raw breakout ~20% success and repeatedly says to prefer the pullback; captured as C to document the anti-pattern.

setups.name: Measured Move Projection (Spike-Based)
setups.one_liner: A target/hold tool: the market tends to travel a distance equal to the spike (or prior range) — not a standalone entry.
setups.context: After a strong spike (3+ strong trend bars, little overlap) the market usually travels a distance from the pullback equal to the spike's size (or the prior trading-range height); the space between the breakout and its test is a measuring gap (project from the open/high of the first spike bar to the middle/close of the gap). Multiple projections exist; confluence of targets increases confidence.
setups.signal_bar: N/A — a projection technique, not an entry pattern by itself.
setups.entry: Not a standalone entry — used as a target / hold objective for with-trend positions. Do NOT take a countertrend trade on a measured-move target alone (most fail); need additional confirmation (trend channel line overshoot, final flag reversal).
setups.initial_stop: N/A (used to size targets on other setups).
setups.management: Hold with-trend positions at least until the measured-move area; once the first target is missed, look for the next logical projection.
setups.invalidation: Price runs through the target without reacting → look for the next measured-move target.
setups.probability: 'Most fail' when the measured move is the only reason for a countertrend entry — 'the odds of success are too small.'
setups.emphasis: reliable context/target tool, not a signal
setups.grade_reason: Explicitly not a standalone entry — Brooks warns 'most fail' as a lone reason and even 'measured moves are just observations, you should not consider them while trading' as a trigger; a context tool, hence C.
setups.pages: Trends p167-175; Ranges p161; RPCBB p367-369
books: Reading Price Charts Bar by Bar
quotes: Whenever there is a strong spike down, it is usually followed by a measured move down based on some aspect of the spike.
You cannot take the countertrend trade on a measured move alone since most fail... The odds of success are too small.
setups.final_grade_reason: Explicitly not a standalone entry — Brooks warns 'most fail' as a lone reason and even 'measured moves are just observations, you should not consider them while trading' as a trigger; a context tool, hence C.

setups.name: Expanding Triangle Bull/Bear Flag (Continuation)
setups.one_liner: After an expanding-triangle reversal, a second opposite triangle acts as a with-trend flag; also 'dueling lines' pullbacks.
setups.context: After an expanding-triangle reversal reaches its new extreme, the market often forms a SECOND expanding triangle in the opposite direction that acts as a with-trend continuation flag (bull flag in a bull trend, bear flag in a bear trend). Related: a 'dueling lines' pullback — one contained by its own trend channel line that ends exactly at a higher-time-frame trend line / MA / measured move (two opposite-sloped lines intersecting) — is a reliable with-trend entry, most reliable when the pullback has a wedge/three-push shape.
setups.signal_bar: Reversal bar at the extreme of the new (flag) expanding triangle, or the bar poking through the larger line at the dueling-lines intersection.
setups.entry: With-trend stop entry at the flag's reversal extreme (or at the dueling-lines intersection; if the move into the line was steep, wait for a second entry rather than the first signal).
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the triangle-flag / signal-bar extreme.
setups.management: Target continuation of the larger trend; eventually one of these increasingly larger triangles fails and a trend runs.
setups.invalidation: If the flag triangle reverses instead of continuing, it becomes a larger (e.g., 7-leg) reversal pattern.
setups.probability: Dueling lines 'often results in a reliable trade in the direction of the larger trend.'
setups.emphasis: reliable but uncommon / subtle continuation pattern
setups.grade_reason: Genuinely named continuation patterns but relatively rare and subtle, discussed only briefly with no probability figure — a low-emphasis, hard-to-define entry, hence C.
quotes: An expanding triangle bottom in a bear trend often later tries to become an expanding triangle bear flag.

When a pullback is contained by a trend channel line and it ends at a higher time frame support or resistance line, this is a dueling lines pattern, and it often results in a reliable trade in the direction of the larger trend.
setups.final_grade_reason: Genuinely named continuation patterns but relatively rare and subtle, discussed only briefly with no probability figure — a low-emphasis, hard-to-define entry, hence C.

setups.name: Gap Reversal
setups.one_liner: After an opening gap, the current bar extends one tick beyond the prior bar back INTO the gap, signaling the gap is failing.
setups.context: An opening gap is in place (the first bar opens beyond the high/low of the prior day's last bar or the entire prior day). The formation occurs when the current bar extends one tick beyond the prior bar back into the gap (e.g., a gap-up open where the second bar trades one tick below the first bar's low).
setups.signal_bar: The bar that pokes one tick beyond the prior bar back into the gap.
setups.entry: Enter in the direction of the move back into the gap (short 1 tick below the prior bar after a gap-up; buy 1 tick above after a gap-down).
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the extreme just made / the gap side.
setups.management: Often ties into an opening reversal; manage as a first-hour reversal/swing.
setups.invalidation: If the gap holds and the trend continues in the gap direction, no reversal.
setups.probability: Not quantified in the raw material (given only as a definition/formation).
setups.emphasis: Presented mainly as a defined formation rather than an emphasized high-probability edge.
setups.grade_reason: Only a thin, definitional formation with no probability/emphasis attached in the source material — C.
quotes: Gap reversal: a formation in which the current bar extends one tick beyond the prior bar back into the gap.
setups.final_grade_reason: Only a thin, definitional formation with no probability/emphasis attached in the source material — C.

setups.name: Micro Measuring Gap Continuation Entry
aliases: measuring-gap limit buy on always-in flip
setups.one_liner: Inside a developing leg, a trend bar makes a new extreme and the next bar does not overlap it, forming a micro measuring gap; enter 1 tick beyond the prior bar for a tiny-risk, big-reward continuation.
setups.context: Within a developing bull (bear) leg, a trend bar breaks to a new high (low) and the following bar's low (high) does not overlap the prior bar's high (low), leaving a micro measuring gap — a sign of strength. Also set up when a not-particularly-large bull/bear trend bar flips the market always-in and leaves a gap versus the bar before it.
setups.signal_bar: The trend bar creating the micro gap (and the bar preceding it).
setups.entry: Buy stop 1 tick above the prior bar's high (sell below prior bar's low), ~3 tick risk; or limit 1 tick above the high of the bar before an always-in flip bar, anticipating the gap holds.
setups.initial_stop: ~3 ticks (below the gap / prior bar low).
setups.management: Aggressive small-size, small-risk continuation add — NOT a target-taking tool early in a trend, since the market usually goes far beyond the micro measured move. If price trades back through the gap, sign of weakness.
setups.invalidation: Price closes back through the micro gap (gap fills) → weakness / failure.
setups.probability: 'The chance of success might be only about 40 percent, but the reward is many times the risk, so the trader's equation is very favorable.'
setups.emphasis: Marginal win rate but favorable equation via asymmetric reward; a strength-confirmation / scale-in tool, not a standalone edge.
setups.grade_reason: Only ~40% win rate by Brooks' own words; positive expectancy comes purely from reward asymmetry, and it is presented as an aggressive add rather than a core signal.
quotes: The chance of success might be only about 40 percent, but the reward is many times the risk, so the trader's equation is very favorable.
When a bull trend bar that is not particularly large flips the market to always-in long, buy on a limit order at one tick above the high of the bar before it, anticipating a measuring gap.
setups.final_grade_reason: Only ~40% win rate by Brooks' own words; positive expectancy comes purely from reward asymmetry, and it is presented as an aggressive add rather than a core signal.

setups.name: Ledge Limit-Order Fade
setups.one_liner: In a small range with a flat bottom or top formed by two or more bars with identical lows/highs (a ledge), buy a tick or two above the ledge bottom, or sell a tick or two below the ledge top.
setups.context: A small trading range forms with a flat bottom (bull ledge) or flat top (bear ledge) created by two or more bars sharing identical lows or highs.
setups.signal_bar: The ledge itself (the repeated identical extremes).
setups.entry: Buy on a limit order a tick or two above the bottom of a bull ledge; sell on a limit order a tick or two below the top of a bear ledge.
setups.initial_stop: Just beyond the ledge extreme.
setups.emphasis: Minor, specific range micro-pattern; no strong probability claim.
setups.grade_reason: Appears once with no emphasis or probability given; a niche limit-order micro-setup rather than a core range tactic.
quotes: Buy with a limit order a tick or two above the bottom of a bull ledge (a small trading range with a bottom created by two or more bars with identical lows).
setups.final_grade_reason: Appears once with no emphasis or probability given; a niche limit-order micro-setup rather than a core range tactic.

setups.name: First-Hour Trend-Bar Pullback Scalp
setups.one_liner: In the opening range, after two or more consecutive strong trend bars, buy a limit at/below the low of the following doji (mirror for bear) to scalp a pullback in the trend-bar direction.
setups.context: Experienced scalpers in the first hour / opening range expect most breakout attempts to fail and look to buy low / sell high; consecutive strong trend bars in one direction bias them to scalp a pullback in that same direction, using limit orders at or beyond the most recent trend bar.
setups.signal_bar: A weak reversal bar (e.g., a doji) following two or more consecutive strong trend bars.
setups.entry: Buy a limit at or below the low of the doji/weak reversal bar following consecutive bull trend bars (mirror for bear).
setups.initial_stop: A tick or two beyond the signal-bar low/high; risk sized to roughly match the average bar size / reward (near 1:1).
setups.management: Requires a 60%+ win rate since risk approximates reward; scale position size down (to half or two-thirds) when bars/ranges are unusually large.
setups.probability: Needs 'a 60 percent or better chance' since risk ≈ reward.
setups.emphasis: 'Only very experienced traders should be fading breakouts of bars in the first hour, because the moves can be fast and go far.'
setups.grade_reason: Explicitly restricted to very experienced scalpers in a fast, volatile window with risk approximately equal to reward, requiring a hard-to-sustain 60%+ hit rate — a marginal, expert-only scalp.
quotes: if there are two consecutive strong bull trend bars and then a doji bar, they might place limit orders to buy at or below the low of the doji bar.
Only very experienced traders should be fading breakouts of bars in the first hour, because the moves can be fast and go far, and traders have to be very confident of their read.
setups.final_grade_reason: Explicitly restricted to very experienced scalpers in a fast, volatile window with risk approximately equal to reward, requiring a hard-to-sustain 60%+ hit rate — a marginal, expert-only scalp.

setups.name: Extreme Scalping (High-Frequency 1-2 Tick Scalps)
setups.one_liner: The theoretical practice of scalping nearly every bar for 1-2 ticks using price-action reasoning — shown to illustrate how much information is on the chart, but not recommended for essentially anyone.
setups.context: A 5-minute chart has ~81 bars a day and about half present some scalp setup (about a third for stop entries, two-thirds for limit entries); virtually every bar can theoretically be traded for a 1-2 tick scalp, but it demands extremely fast, accurate reading and order placement.
setups.signal_bar: Any bar offering a recognizable pattern (reversal bar, double top/bottom, ii/iii, failed breakout, etc.) at any point in the day, including on 1-minute or tick charts during fast report-driven moves.
setups.entry: Stop entry one tick beyond the signal bar (needs about a six-tick move to scalp four ticks with a limit), or a limit at/near a prior swing extreme.
setups.initial_stop: A few ticks (as tight as five ticks) beyond the signal or entry bar.
setups.management: Scalp a small fixed target (1-2 points / several ticks); some traders scale in against themselves at set intervals (e.g., every 4-8 ticks) and manage the whole clip as one position off the original stop.
setups.invalidation: Used opportunistically; not a setup most traders should attempt for a full session.
setups.probability: Because scalping usually requires risk greater than reward, the win rate must be at least 70% to be consistently profitable — 'not realistic for most traders, even for very successful traders.'
setups.emphasis: Marginal / not recommended — 'virtually no one should attempt to take the 30 to 40 scalps that are present on a 5 minute chart.' Shown to illustrate information density, not as a methodology.
setups.grade_reason: Brooks explicitly says virtually no one should trade this way because the required ~70% win rate on negative reward:risk is unrealistic — presented as illustration, not a recommended setup.
quotes: scalping usually requires the risk to be greater than the reward, the winning percentage has to be at least 70 percent to be consistently profitable... That is not realistic for most traders, even for very successful traders.
virtually no one should attempt to take the 30 to 40 scalps that are present on a 5 minute chart of an average day.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks explicitly says virtually no one should trade this way because the required ~70% win rate on negative reward:risk is unrealistic — presented as illustration, not a recommended setup.

setups.name: Three-Bar Reversal
