setups.one_liner: A two-bar reversal with an unremarkable small/doji middle bar; enter beyond the three-bar extreme, more reliable if the third bar closes a 15-min reversal bar.
setups.context: A variation of the two-bar reversal: three consecutive bars where the first and third form the two-bar reversal pair and the middle bar is small/doji/unremarkable. More reliable when the third bar closes at the same time as a 15-min bar, forming a clean 15-min reversal bar.
setups.signal_bar: First and third bars are the reversal pair; middle bar small/doji.
setups.entry: Stop 1 tick above/below the high/low of the three-bar combination (or earlier at the embedded two-bar reversal if available).
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the extreme of the three-bar pattern.
setups.management: If it also forms a clean 15-min reversal bar, swing more size / use a wider target; otherwise just treat it as a two-bar reversal.
setups.invalidation: Two of three times it won't align with the 15-min bar close and forms some other 15-min pattern; don't hunt for it at the cost of missing more common setups.
setups.probability: ~1-in-3 the third bar aligns with a 15-min bar close.
setups.emphasis: Secondary/minor pattern — 'do not worry about' whether one forms; occurs only a couple times a day.
setups.grade_reason: Brooks explicitly downgrades it as an infrequent, secondary variation of the two-bar reversal not worth hunting for — clearly C.
quotes: A three-bar reversal is just a variation of a two-bar reversal.
Traders should not spend time looking for three-bar reversals, because they occur only a couple of times a day.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks explicitly downgrades it as an infrequent, secondary variation of the two-bar reversal not worth hunting for — clearly C.

setups.name: Second-Bar-of-Day Breakout (scalp only)
setups.one_liner: Entering as the second bar of the day breaks beyond the first bar's high/low; commonly mistaken for a swing but the math only supports a scalp.
setups.context: The second bar of the day breaks beyond the high or low of the first bar. Traders often treat it as a swing setup, but the probability rarely supports one.
setups.signal_bar: The first bar of the day.
setups.entry: Buy above the first bar's high (sell below its low) as the second bar breaks it.
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the low/high of the first bar.
setups.management: Treat strictly as a scalp, not a swing, unless a genuinely higher-probability variant can be subjectively assessed (rare, ~once a month).
setups.probability: 'The first bar of the day has about a 20 percent chance of being the high or low of the day' (50%+ on large-gap days). Buying as the second bar breaks the first has '~20 percent chance of making eight points while risking a little more than two points — a losing trader's equation' for a swing.
setups.emphasis: Marginal — 'looking for a swing entry on the second bar of the day is rarely worthwhile.'
setups.grade_reason: Brooks explicitly calls the swing version a losing trader's equation and the scalp under 50%; it is a low-probability entry he warns against, so C.
quotes: the probability of a successful scalp is often under 50 percent, depending on the situation.
a trader who buys as the second bar breaks above the first bar has about a 20 percent chance of making eight points while risking a little more than two points. This is a losing trader's equation.
setups.final_grade_reason: Brooks explicitly calls the swing version a losing trader's equation and the scalp under 50%; it is a low-probability entry he warns against, so C.

setups.name: Gap 2 (second gap-close attempt)
setups.one_liner: When a first attempt to reverse/close the opening gap fails, a second attempt to close the gap is a Gap 2 — a second-entry breakout toward closing the gap.
setups.context: A gap reversal attempt occurs when price extends 1 tick beyond the prior bar into the opening gap. If this first gap reversal attempt fails, a second attempt to close the gap develops.
setups.signal_bar: The Gap 2 bar itself.
setups.entry: Stop entry in the direction of closing the gap (beyond the Gap 2 signal bar).
setups.initial_stop: Beyond the Gap 2 extreme.
setups.emphasis: A second-entry (Gap 2) variant; minor, mentioned in passing.
setups.grade_reason: Only a brief definitional mention with no probability or emphasis; a minor second-entry gap pattern, so C.
quotes: Gap 2: If the first gap reversal fails, and then a second attempt to close the gap develops, this second attempt is a Gap 2.
setups.final_grade_reason: Only a brief definitional mention with no probability or emphasis; a minor second-entry gap pattern, so C.

setups.name: Round Number Magnet / Buy Vacuum
setups.one_liner: A strongly-trending market a few percent below a significant round number gets vacuumed to it (bears stop shorting, bulls buy aggressively); it usually overshoots 5-10% and then pulls back below the number at least once.
setups.context: A stock in a strong trend is a few percent below a significant round number (e.g. FCX at 93 heading to 100, AAPL near $300). Bears stop shorting aggressively (they expect to short higher) and bulls buy aggressively expecting a move above the magnet; the absence of bears creates a vacuum that quickly pulls price to the magnet.
setups.entry: Bulls buy as price rallies into the magnetic field of the round number and take profits just above it; bears wait to short until price is above the number and take profits on the test back down; bulls buy again there for a scalp.
setups.management: Take profits just above the round number (bulls); the number usually gets penetrated back below at least once; typically goes 5-10% above before pulling back.
setups.emphasis: A contextual magnet trade, not a core bar-by-bar setup; mostly discussed on the daily chart.
setups.grade_reason: Contextual/behavioral magnet trade with no signal bar, no stop specifics, and no probability; useful color but not a testable core breakout setup, so C.
quotes: Since round numbers are magnets, they can set up trades.
Their absence caused the market to go up quickly, since the bulls had to push the market up higher to find enough traders to take the opposite side of their longs.
setups.final_grade_reason: Contextual/behavioral magnet trade with no signal bar, no stop specifics, and no probability; useful color but not a testable core breakout setup, so C.

market-structure.teaching: Countertrend trades and countertrend scalps are generally a losing strategy because risk is usually at least as large as reward while probability is rarely high enough to make the trader's equation favorable.
market-structure.quote: countertrend A trade or setup that is in the opposite direction from the current trend... This is a losing strategy for most traders since the risk is usually at least as large as the reward and the probability is rarely high enough to make the trader's equation favorable.

market-structure.teaching: Directional probability of an equidistant up/down move hovers around 50% most of the time — there's roughly a 50-50 chance price moves X ticks up before X ticks down, and vice versa, absent other edge.
market-structure.quote: directional probability The probability that the market will move either up or down any number of ticks before it reaches a certain number of ticks in the opposite direction... it hovers around 50 percent most of the time.

market-structure.teaching: Every price pattern is a fractal: any pattern is simultaneously a micro pattern on a higher time frame chart and a standard/traditional pattern on a smaller time frame chart.
market-structure.quote: fractal Every pattern is a fractal of a pattern on a higher time frame chart. This means that every pattern is a micro pattern on a higher time frame and every micro pattern is a standard pattern on a smaller time frame.

market-structure.teaching: The market cannot reach any price, including where your stop sits, unless institutions are actively willing to trade there — retail stops are not 'run'; the market only tests a price when institutions find it financially sound to trade at that level.
market-structure.quote: The market will not run your stops. The market might test the price where your protective stop is, but it has nothing to do with your stop. It will only test that price if one or more institutions believe that it is financially sound to sell there and other institutions believe that it is profitable to buy there.

market-structure.teaching: Whenever price goes up, the bulk of institutional/big money is net buying at that moment (and vice versa on down moves) — even pullbacks within a strong trend that stop out weak longs or trigger weak shorts are often where big money is buying (or selling), so don't fight the trend based on a scary-looking pullback.
market-structure.quote: sometimes when the market is working higher, a bar will trade below the low of the prior bar, yet the trend continues higher. You have to assume that the big money was buying at and below the low of that prior bar... If the market is going up, institutions are buying constantly, even at times when you think that you should stop yourself out of your long with a loss.

market-structure.teaching: Price action patterns are essentially unchanged across 80+ years of charts and across all markets/timeframes because they emerge from human behavioral 'DNA' operating within an efficient market of countless smart participants — no single group of price-action traders acting on published knowledge can materially change these patterns because institutions already know them intuitively.
market-structure.quote: Price action is a manifestation of human behavior and therefore actually has a genetic basis. Until we evolve, it will likely remain largely unchanged, just as it has been unchanged for the 80 years of charts that I have reviewed.

market-structure.teaching: A climax is a move that went too far too fast and reverses; most climaxes end with a trend-channel-line overshoot and reversal, but most of those reversals lead only to a trading range, not a full opposite trend — so don't assume a climax reversal means a new trend.
market-structure.quote: climax A move that has gone too far too fast and has now reversed direction to either a trading range or an opposite trend. Most climaxes end with trend channel overshoots and reversals, but most of those reversals result in trading ranges and not an opposite trend.

market-structure.teaching: A trading range is fundamentally a state of uncertainty where repeated breakout attempts in both directions will tend to fail until the range ultimately breaks out — usually in the direction of the larger trend — and is itself just a pullback when viewed on a higher time frame.
market-structure.quote: In other words, traders have become uncertain about the direction of the breakout in the short term, and the market will have repeated breakout attempts up and down that will fail. It will usually ultimately break out in the direction of the trend, and is a pullback on a higher time frame chart.

market-structure.teaching: The most useful price action tools are trend lines/channel lines, prior highs and lows, breakouts and failed breakouts, bar/candle body and tail size, and the relationship of the current bar to the prior several bars.
market-structure.quote: The most useful tools for understanding price action are trend lines and trend channel lines, prior highs and lows, breakouts and failed breakouts, the sizes of bodies and tails on candles...

market-structure.teaching: Reliable reversals typically first break a trend line with strong momentum, then pull back to test the extreme; trading divergences without a prior trend-line-breaking momentum surge is a losing strategy.
market-structure.quote: Placing a trade because of a divergence in the absence of a prior countertrend momentum surge that breaks a trend line is a losing strategy. Wait for the trend line break and then see if the test of the old extreme reverses or if the old trend resumes.

market-structure.teaching: Extended trends with little bar overlap (large trend bars, minimal pullback) that retrace less than ~75% after a pullback tend to resume and test/exceed the prior extreme; a pullback retracing 75%+ should instead be treated as a new trend in the opposite direction.
market-structure.quote: the odds that a pullback will get back to the prior trend's extreme fall substantially if the pullback retraces 75 percent or more. For a pullback in a bear trend, at that point, a trader is better off thinking of the pullback as a new bull trend...

market-structure.teaching: Pattern labels (triangle, head-and-shoulders, flag) are irrelevant — what matters is correctly reading whether price is trending or ranging and in which direction it is likely to move next; the same price structure can simultaneously be described multiple ways.
market-structure.quote: The name that you apply is irrelevant since the direction of the subsequent move will be the same if you read the bars correctly.

market-structure.teaching: Markets have inertia: trends tend to continue, and most breakout attempts of trading ranges fail, while most reversal attempts within a trend fail and become flags.
market-structure.quote: Markets tend to keep doing what they are doing... most breakout attempts of trading ranges fail... An important point to remember is that the market constantly exhibits inertia and tends to continue to do what is has just been doing. If it is in a trend, most attempts to reverse it will fail. If it is in a trading range, most attempts to break out into a trend will fail.

market-structure.teaching: Two-legged pullbacks (ABC) are common and reliable; Brooks prefers counting legs (high 1/2/3/4, low 1/2/3/4) over ABC/AB=CD or Elliott Wave labeling because pullbacks can extend to 3 or 4 legs.
market-structure.quote: Two-legged corrections are reliable setups for trades in the direction of the trend... Since measured moves are an important part of trading and the AB = CD terminology is inconsistent with the more commonly used ABC labeling, the AB = CD terminology should not be used.

market-structure.teaching: Anything over ~100 contracts (Emini) should be treated as institutional footprint, regardless of whether it's one big trader or many.
market-structure.quote: In general, anything over 100 contracts should be considered institutional in today's Emini market.

market-structure.teaching: All trends contain smaller trading ranges and all trading ranges contain smaller trends; most trends are pullbacks within a higher-time-frame trading range, and vice versa — even the 1987 and 2009 crashes were pullbacks to the monthly bull trend line.
market-structure.quote: All trends contain smaller trading ranges, and all trading ranges contain smaller trends. Also, most trends are just parts of trading ranges on higher time frame (HTF) charts, and most trading ranges are parts of trends on HTF charts.

market-structure.teaching: A trend bar means one side is in control (has a body); a doji/nontrend bar means bulls and bears are in equilibrium (a one-bar trading range). Subtyping small-bodied bars with candle names (hammer, hanging man, harami) adds little value.
market-structure.quote: For a trader, it is most useful to think of all bars as being either trend bars or nontrend (trading range) bars... Subdividing bars with small bodies into a variety of subtypes like hanging man, hammer, or harami adds little to a trader.

market-structure.teaching: Every trend bar simultaneously functions as (1) a spike, (2) a breakout, (3) a gap (all breakouts/trend bars are functionally gaps), and (4) part or all of a vacuum/climax.
market-structure.quote: Every trend bar is simultaneously (1) a spike; (2) a breakout; (3) a gap...; and (4) a part or all of a vacuum and a climax.

market-structure.teaching: Never compete against algorithms on speed — pick a timeframe (e.g., 5-min) where you have time to process information, rather than trying to read ultra-fast tick/smaller-timeframe reversals.
market-structure.quote: It is always a mistake to compete against an opponent who has an edge in a game where the margin for error is so small... they should pick a time frame, like a 5 minute chart, where they have time to carefully process the information.

market-structure.teaching: A climax is any trend bar/consecutive trend bars, ending with the first pause bar; a climax does not necessarily mean a reversal — 'climax' and 'reversal' are not synonyms. A climactic reversal requires both a climax AND a subsequent breakout in the opposite direction.
market-structure.quote: A trend bar is a critical component of a climax, and a climax is a critical component of a reversal, but traders often incorrectly use the term climax as synonymous with reversal... The reversal requires a buy climax and then a bear breakout, which is a strong bear trend bar.

market-structure.teaching: Every reversal, no matter what timeframe it appears on, is fundamentally a climactic reversal (spike + reversal) on some sufficiently small timeframe — but it's not worth hunting for the 'perfect' timeframe to see it.
market-structure.quote: In fact, every reversal at the top of a bull market is a climactic reversal, whether or not it looks like one on the chart in front of you... it is not worth looking for the perfect time frame just to see the perfect reversal bar.

market-structure.teaching: Signs of an ideal/strong trend bar: body ≥ median of last 5-10 bars, open near the low / close near the high (bull case), close at/above prior closes and highs, high above prior highs, small tails. An unusually large body after a protracted move can instead signal exhaustion/climax.
market-structure.quote: The bulls can demonstrate stronger control by having the body be about the same size as or larger than that of the median body size over the past five or 10 bars.

market-structure.teaching: Buying/selling pressure is cumulative: an increasing number/size of bull (or bear) bodies within a bear trend (or bull trend) signals building pressure that can eventually overwhelm the prevailing trend and reverse or range it.
market-structure.quote: Selling pressure is cumulative, and the more bear bodies and the larger the bodies, the more likely it is that the pressure will reach a critical point and overwhelm the bulls and drive the market down.

market-structure.teaching: When strong bulls buy dips but strong bears only sell rallies (not dips), the net effect is a trading range, not a trend reversal — weak traders do the opposite (chase and get stopped).
market-structure.quote: So when the strong bulls are buying at the bottom and strong bears are only willing to sell higher, what happens? The market enters a trading range.

market-structure.teaching: A series of dojis with trending closes/highs/lows (each higher/lower than the last) indicates an actual trend despite each individual bar looking indecisive.
market-structure.quote: Individual dojis mean that neither the bulls nor the bears are controlling the market, but trending dojis indicate a trend.

market-structure.teaching: A trend bar breaking out of a range does not guarantee a trend if there's no follow-through in the next bar or two; failed breakouts trap traders and cause reversals, and it typically takes multiple failed attempts before the opposite side capitulates.
market-structure.quote: Just like dojis don't always mean that the market is trendless, a trend bar does not always mean that the market is trending... An experienced trader would have needed follow-through in the form of a second or third bull trend bar before believing that the always-in trade had reversed to up.

market-structure.teaching: A large exhaustive trend bar after a prolonged (30+ bar) trend without a pullback, near a support/resistance area, is often the sign of a sell/buy vacuum and can mark or precede the end of the trend as both sides step aside then rush in.
market-structure.quote: Whenever a large bear trend bar forms in a bear trend that has gone on for 30 or more bars without much of a pullback, it often represents a sell vacuum and an exhaustive sell climax.

market-structure.teaching: A trend is a transition into a trading range once the market stops seeing new extremes as good with-trend entries and starts seeing them as good profit-taking/counter-entry levels (buying pressure accumulating in a bear trend, or selling pressure in a bull trend).
market-structure.quote: When bears begin to see a new low as a good place to take profits instead of as a great place to short for a new leg down, and bulls see it as a good price to begin to get long, the market is transitioning from a strong trend into more of a two-sided market.

market-structure.teaching: Every bar is simultaneously a potential signal bar for both longs (buy stop above it) and shorts (sell stop/limit below or above it) — the trader's edge lies in correctly reading which side has the imbalance in a given context, not in the pattern alone.
market-structure.quote: That means that every bar is a signal bar for both long and short trades, with both bulls and bears entering on the breakout of both the top and the bottom... Signal bars in the right context are times when there is such an imbalance.

market-structure.teaching: The strongest trends usually have terrible-looking (weak/countertrend-colored) signal bars for with-trend entries, because if a setup looked obvious the market would correct it immediately with a small fast move; conversely swing/reversal setups need to look 'perfect' because their base rate is only ~50% or less.
market-structure.quote: The very strongest trends usually have terrible-looking signal bars, but the trades are still excellent. If a setup is too obvious, the market will quickly correct the discrepancy... Swing trade setups, in contrast, usually have a probability of success of only 50 percent or less.

market-structure.teaching: Every trend bar is simultaneously a spike, a breakout, a gap, and a climax/exhaustion bar; context determines which property dominates at any given moment, and any bar can be read multiple ways depending on which lens you apply.
market-structure.quote: remember, all trend bars are simultaneously spikes, breakouts, and climaxes, but the context determines which property is dominant at the moment

market-structure.teaching: Every bar is technically a setup bar for both a long and short entry; a setup bar only becomes a signal bar if a trade is actually entered on the following bar. A setup bar alone (out of context of surrounding bars) is not a reason to trade.
market-structure.quote: A setup bar becomes a signal bar only if a trade is entered on the next bar, which becomes the entry bar. A setup bar in and of itself is not a reason to enter a trade.

market-structure.teaching: When the market attempts something (e.g., break a low) twice and fails both times, it will usually then try the opposite direction — this is the structural reason double tops/bottoms work and why traders wait for a test of the old extreme before believing in a reversal.
market-structure.quote: Whenever the market tries to do something twice and fails, it usually then tries the opposite. This is why double tops and bottoms work

market-structure.teaching: Most daily-chart reversal bars originate from 'trending trading range days' on intraday charts rather than pure climactic reversals; be alert that a trending trading-range day can end in a strong late-day reversal.
market-structure.quote: Most reversal bars on the daily chart come from trending trading range days...but a few come from climactic intraday reversals.

market-structure.teaching: Swing traders (often institutions) are the strongest market participants because they can tolerate pullbacks; once they give up on a position, there is no one left to hold the market up/down and it typically must move ~10 bars/two legs to reach a new support/resistance level before they reconsider entering again.
market-structure.quote: Swing traders are generally the strongest participants...Once these strongest bulls decide that the market will fall further, there is no one left to buy, and the market usually has to fall for about 10 bars and two legs

market-structure.teaching: Most trends end with at least a minor climax where the last late/weak traders finally enter and the last traders on the wrong side finally capitulate/exit; once no one is left on the wrong side, urgency disappears and the market typically enters a trading range.
market-structure.quote: Most trends eventually have at least a minor climax before a deeper correction comes...When no traders are left on the wrong side, the sense of urgency disappears and the market usually enters a trading range

market-structure.teaching: Big breakout trend bars often fail on the very next bar, trapping traders on the wrong side — this is especially common on quiet trading-range days where price repeatedly surges to the range extremes and fools naive breakout traders.
market-structure.quote: Big trend bars on breakouts often fail on the next bar, trapping traders into the wrong side of the market. This is especially common on quiet trading range days

market-structure.teaching: A climax (sell or buy) is usually followed by a two-legged correction lasting many bars — at least an hour on a 5-minute chart, or at least 10 bars.
market-structure.quote: A climax is usually followed by a two-legged correction that lasts for many bars (at least an hour on a 5 minute chart).

market-structure.teaching: When a trend is particularly strong it often will not correct until after a second sell/buy climax, and rarely after a third; a third consecutive climax is unusual and raises the odds of a real two-legged reversal correction.
market-structure.quote: When a trend is particularly strong, it often will not correct until after a second sell climax, and rarely after a third.

market-structure.teaching: A strong spike is usually followed by a channel, and spike-and-channel (or spike-and-climax) moves commonly test back to around the start of the channel within a day or two — unless a higher timeframe pattern is controlling the move.
market-structure.quote: Spike and climax bears (a type of spike and channel bear trend) usually test to around the start of the channel within a day or two...a higher time frame pattern might be controlling the market.

market-structure.teaching: A small inside bar (or any sideways pause) after a strong move can become a one-bar final flag before the trend resumes hard.
market-structure.quote: The small inside bar that formed two bars before bar 1 turned into a one-bar final flag.

market-structure.teaching: When a two-bar reversal sell setup forms just below the moving average with large overlapping bars, it is often better to buy at/below the low of the signal bar for a long scalp of the small range instead of shorting.
market-structure.quote: whenever there is a two-bar reversal sell setup just below the moving average and the bars are relatively large and overlap one or more other bars, you usually can buy at or below the low of the signal bar for a long scalp.

market-structure.teaching: Tight trading ranges act as magnets: breakouts often fail and get pulled back because both sides see better value at the opposite extreme; the losing side becomes more aggressive at the range's edges, but eventually a breakout does succeed.
market-structure.quote: A series of overlapping bars is a tight trading range, which is a magnet, and breakouts often fail and the market is drawn back into the tight trading range... However, there will always be a successful breakout eventually.

market-structure.teaching: A bar with no tail at either end (shaved) in a strong trend indicates one-sided pressure and is a sign of strength, but the same shaved appearance can be meaningless (low volume/two-sided) if it occurs in a trading range rather than a real trend — watch for clusters of shaved bars as a low-volume warning.
market-structure.quote: Shaved tops and bottoms are not always a sign of strength... in the second cluster, the market was in a trading range and they may just indicate that the volume was low.

market-structure.teaching: As a trend wears on, later pullbacks tend to be deeper (bulls/bears in a mature trend want more of a discount before reentering).
market-structure.quote: As a trend wears on, the bulls typically will want deeper pullbacks before looking to buy again.

market-structure.teaching: Doji bars are generally poor signal bars for shorting a strong bull trend, but are acceptable countertrend/pullback signal bars within trading ranges or as pullbacks within a strong trend, depending on context.
market-structure.quote: Doji bars are never good signal bars for shorts in strong bulls, but they are acceptable signal bars for shorts in trading ranges, depending on the context.

market-structure.teaching: When risk is greater than normal (large signal bars forcing entry far from a logical stop point), it is often better to skip the trade and wait for a strong, tighter-risk setup.
market-structure.quote: When the risk is greater, it is better to not take the trade and to wait for a strong setup.

market-structure.teaching: A tight trading range below the moving average usually breaks to the downside (and vice versa above), though this bias alone doesn't guarantee a reliable signal — sometimes it's better to wait rather than force a trade.
market-structure.quote: A tight trading range below the moving average usually breaks out to the downside, but there was no reliable short setup. Instead, traders should wait for a better setup.

market-structure.teaching: After a climax that follows a long trend, expect at least a two-legged sideways-to-down (or up) correction lasting at least 10 bars; a second consecutive climax strengthens this expectation to at least an hour and a penetration of the moving average.
market-structure.quote: When a climax occurs after a trend has been going on for many bars, the odds of a two-legged sideways to down correction lasting at least 10 bars increase... A second consecutive buy climax usually results in at least a two-legged correction that penetrates the moving average and lasts at least an hour.

market-structure.teaching: Second legs of a move (second push in the same direction) are often where reversals occur.
market-structure.quote: second legs are often reversals.

market-structure.teaching: A bear (bull) channel should be conceptually treated as a bull (bear) flag — i.e., expect a strong move in the opposite direction once the channel breaks.
market-structure.quote: The move down from bar 6 was in a channel, and a bear channel should be thought of as a bull flag, so there might be a strong move up before long.

market-structure.teaching: All breakouts are simultaneously both spikes and climaxes; a strong breakout is usually followed by a channel move before a tradable reversal develops, with bears/bulls aggressively fading every small pullback until a bottom/top at a measured-move target forms.
market-structure.quote: All breakouts are spikes and climaxes... A breakout spike often leads to a measured move down.

market-structure.teaching: Spike-and-channel patterns / channels following spikes often correct or end after three pushes (three-push exhaustion).
market-structure.quote: Spike and channel patterns often correct after three pushes up. The spike is the first push and then there are often two more spikes followed by a correction.

market-structure.teaching: A parabolic (accelerating) trend — where trend channel lines must be redrawn increasingly steep — is itself a form of buy/sell climax and raises correction risk even while momentum looks unstoppable.
market-structure.quote: This parabolic curvature is also a sign of climactic behavior and that makes the risk of a correction significant.

market-structure.teaching: A market can reverse at any time even amid strong momentum, especially in the first trading hour; it often races to an unseen magnet/target and then is 'free' to do anything once that target is tested.
market-structure.quote: traders should always be aware that the market can reverse at any time, especially in the first hour. The market often races to some magnet that often is not evident, and once it is tested, the market is then free to do anything, including reverse.

market-structure.teaching: Tight trading ranges (7+ bars horizontal) that occur after a trend has run 10+ bars often become final flags before trend resumption.
market-structure.quote: tight trading ranges after a trend has gone for 10 or more bars often become final flags.

market-structure.teaching: The first bar (and first several bars) of the trading day often foreshadow the character (trend day vs. trading-range day) of the entire session.
market-structure.quote: The first bar of the day and the first several bars often foreshadow what the next few hours, and often the entire day, will look like.

market-structure.teaching: Many institutional orders are based on the close (line charts / value), which is part of why the close of each bar carries extra weight.
market-structure.quote: many institutional traders place orders based on value and not price action, and when they look at charts, the charts are line charts, which are based on the close.

market-structure.teaching: Stop runs on smaller time frames at important reversals are common and are opportunities, not reasons to panic — they trap weak traders and force them to chase.
market-structure.quote: Stop runs on the 3 minute chart are much more common than on the 5 minute chart at important reversals, and smart traders look at them as great opportunities.

market-structure.teaching: If a chart's price action is unclear, switching representation (line/bar chart, tick/volume chart, another time frame, a correlated ETF, or even the inverse ETF) can clarify the pattern before committing to a trade.
market-structure.quote: Sometimes the price action becomes clearer if you change something about the chart.

market-structure.teaching: Most tops form from some type of micro double top and most bottoms from some type of micro double bottom.
market-structure.quote: most tops occur from some type of micro double top... and most bottoms occur from some type of micro double bottom

market-structure.teaching: Most breakouts fail — this is one of the most important realities a trader must accept; strong momentum toward a trend line makes beginners assume reversal, but experienced traders fade that momentum and buy/sell into the trend line expecting the established trend to resume.
market-structure.quote: most breakouts fail! The market repeatedly races toward a trend line with very strong momentum, and it is easy to get caught up in the strength of the bar and overlook what just took place over the past 20 bars.

market-structure.teaching: A trend line break is the single most important signal that the market is no longer controlled by one side only, and two-sided trading becomes much more likely; after each break, a new (typically flatter-sloped) trend line forms, and trend lines in the opposite direction gradually gain importance as control shifts.
market-structure.quote: The single most important point about a trend line break is that it is the first sign that the market is no longer being controlled by just one side (buyers or sellers) and the chances of further two-sided trading are now much greater.

market-structure.teaching: When a market repeatedly tests a trend line many times without pulling away, one of two things usually happens: it eventually breaks through and reverses, or the trend accelerates away from the line as counter-side traders give up.
market-structure.quote: If the market repeatedly tests a trend line many times in a relatively small number of bars and the market cannot drift far from that trend line, then either of two things will likely happen.

market-structure.teaching: Gap openings (especially large ones) often lead to trend days in either direction.
market-structure.quote: Large gap openings often lead to trend days in either direction.

market-structure.teaching: A trend channel is simply a sloping trading range: two-sided trading occurs even within a trend channel, and the tighter/steeper the channel, the more it behaves like a pure trend (trade only with-trend); the flatter/broader it is, the more it behaves like a trading range (tradeable both ways).
market-structure.quote: Even in a trend channel, there is two-sided trading taking place and this is trading range behavior. In fact, a trend channel can be thought of as a sloping trading range.

market-structure.teaching: A channel that is so tight it has no pullbacks or only one or two tiny ones is a 'micro channel' and functions like a single spike on a higher timeframe chart; a broader channel often follows it and can reach a measured-move target based on the height of that tight channel/spike.
market-structure.quote: When a channel is especially tight ... it is a sign that the trend is strong, and it can be a spike on a higher time frame chart. A broader channel may follow, which might reach a measured move target, based on the height of the tight channel.

market-structure.teaching: Bull/bear channels usually need at least two confirming legs before traders will draw the channel lines at all, and most channels end up with at least three pushes (three legs) before a breakout/reversal is seriously traded.
market-structure.quote: Since a bull channel needs at least those first two legs down to confirm the existence of the channel ... bull channels usually have at least three pushes up ... many bull channels end after a third push up. Likewise, many bear channels end after a third push down.

market-structure.teaching: The 'vacuum effect': as price nears a channel line, both sides withhold trading because they expect the line to be tested, creating an imbalance that makes price race toward the line quickly; once there, the waiting side floods in and reverses/contains it.
market-structure.quote: The current relative absence of selling creates a buy imbalance, and whenever there is any imbalance, the market moves quickly.

market-structure.teaching: The slope of a trend's final flag often approximates the slope of the subsequent new trend, though this is a minor, low-weight observation compared to other factors.
market-structure.quote: One final minor observation is that the slope of the final flag of a trend often provides an approximation of the slope of the new trend. This has limited value to a trader.

market-structure.teaching: When drawing trend/channel lines, prefer the widest/best-fit version that contains the most price action over the strict two-point line through only the most recent bars; trend lines are strongest when tested by a third leg.
market-structure.quote: The widest channel is the most certain.

market-structure.teaching: A doji bar signals the market is still genuinely two-sided/undecided — useful context for judging whether a channel/trend is about to continue or reverse.
market-structure.quote: The bar after bar 8 was a doji bar, which indicates that the market is still two-sided.

market-structure.teaching: A first breakout attempt out of a very tight, prolonged channel usually fails and the original trend resumes, often extending to a new extreme equal to a measured move based on the height of that failed initial breakout attempt.
market-structure.quote: Usually the first breakout of a tight, prolonged channel will fail. The trend will then resume and often break out to a new extreme and reach a measured move equal to about the height of that initial breakout.

market-structure.teaching: Consecutive climaxes (buy climax after buy climax, or several tight channels in a row each acting like a spike) usually lead to a larger correction than a single climax would.
market-structure.quote: This was the third or fourth consecutive buy climax (every spike, whether it is one bar or many bars, is a climax), and it followed a strong bear spike. Consecutive climaxes usually lead to a larger correction, as they did here.

market-structure.teaching: Reversal-looking chart patterns (head and shoulders tops, expanding triangle tops, wedge shapes) usually resolve as with-trend continuation flags rather than actual trend reversals — the base rate favors continuation.
market-structure.quote: Bars 3, 5, and 7 formed a head and shoulders top, and like most reversal patterns, became a large bull flag and not a reversal.

market-structure.teaching: A micro channel is a tight trend channel (2-10+ bars) with little to no pullback; the more bars and the stronger/less-tailed the trend bars, the stronger the channel and the less likely the first pullback reverses the trend — treat reversal attempts as likely-to-fail with-trend setups.
market-structure.quote: The more bars, the stronger the bars... and the smaller the tails, the stronger the micro channel, and the more likely that the first pullback will fail to reverse the trend.

market-structure.teaching: A micro channel often functions as a spike on a higher time-frame chart and can be followed by a broader channel, forming a spike-and-channel trend with a measured-move target based on the height of the tight micro channel.
market-structure.quote: When the micro channel is especially tight, it can be a spike on a higher time frame chart; a broader channel may follow, and it might reach a measured move target based on the height of the tight micro channel.

market-structure.teaching: Program/momentum trading reinforces micro channels: once a trend is underway, momentum programs detect and add to it, and breakout programs begin trading once resistance/support breaks, adding to trend strength.
market-structure.quote: Once the trend begins breaking above resistance levels, breakout programs will start trading. Some will trade in the direction of the trend and others will begin to scale in against the trend...

market-structure.teaching: When a micro trend line extends for about 10+ bars, this is climactic and unsustainable — expect a tradable reversal or at least a breakout pullback trade soon (second attempt to reverse).
market-structure.quote: When a micro trend line extends for about 10 or more bars, the odds increase substantially that there will soon be a tradable reversal. This type of trend is unsustainable and therefore a type of climax.

market-structure.teaching: Most days are trading-range days; on these, prior swing highs/lows act as magnet/barrier levels where breakouts commonly fail and reverse, and second failed-breakout attempts (second higher high/lower low) are especially good fade setups because the market's midpoint acts like a magnet.
market-structure.quote: Most days are trading range days or have a lot of trading range activity... horizontal lines across swing highs and lows often serve as barriers that result in failed breakouts and then reversals.

market-structure.teaching: On trend days, use horizontal support/resistance lines mainly to time with-trend pullback entries, not countertrend fades.
market-structure.quote: On trend days, horizontal lines should generally be used only to enter on pullbacks.

market-structure.teaching: A trend, swing, pullback, and leg are overlapping/relative classifications defined by time frame — a pullback on one chart can be a strong trend on a smaller time frame; a single bar is itself a trend on a smaller time frame.
market-structure.quote: A trend can be as short as a single bar (remember, a trend bar is made up of a trend on a smaller time frame) or longer than all of the bars on your screen.

market-structure.teaching: All trends, no matter how small, must first break a trend line (or a support/resistance line of a prior trading range) and then form trending swings (series of higher highs/lows or lower highs/lows); absent either, there is no trend.
market-structure.quote: All trends, no matter how small, must first break a trend line from the prior trend or a support or resistance line from a prior trading range, and then have trending swings... Absent either of these, there is no trend.

market-structure.teaching: Especially strong trend days tend to have follow-through over the next one or more days (close near the high, next day opens/closes above open), sometimes with sharp opening sell-offs that trap bulls out and bears in before finding support at a higher-timeframe moving average.
market-structure.quote: When a trend is especially strong, there is usually follow-through over the next one or more days... The opens often have sharp sell-offs that trap bulls out and bears in but usually find support at the 15 or 60 minute moving average.

market-structure.teaching: A trend is a series of brief spikes (odds > 50-50) alternating with trading ranges (odds back to ~50-50); the skill is anticipating and entering as the next spike begins, catching maybe 60% (or higher) odds of making X ticks before losing X ticks.
market-structure.quote: One of the best ways to trade a trend is to anticipate when the next spike will begin and to enter on a stop as it is starting. That way, you can catch a spike with maybe a 60 percent probability or even higher of making X ticks before losing X ticks.

market-structure.teaching: Bull trends on higher time-frame charts tend to rally in the final 30-60 minutes (buy-into-the-close program trading), pushing the close above the open and producing a bull trend bar on the daily chart; the mirror is true for bear trends selling off into the close.
market-structure.quote: Bull trends on higher time frame charts tend to have rallies in the final 30 to 60 minutes of trading on the 5 minute chart... When the market is in a bull trend, look for buy setups in the final hour.

market-structure.teaching: A break above a prior swing high leads to one of three outcomes — more buying/pressing (measured move follow-through), profit taking (most common), or shorting; read which is occurring from how far/fast price moves after the breakout.
market-structure.quote: A move above a prior swing high in a bull trend will lead to predominantly one of three outcomes: more buying, profit taking (by far the most common possibility), or shorting.

market-structure.teaching: Traders don't flip from extremely bullish to extremely bearish overnight — sentiment transitions gradually (bullish -> neutral -> bearish); once enough firms make the transition, the market reverses into a deeper correction or a new trend.
market-structure.quote: In the absence of some rare, dramatic news event, traders don't suddenly switch from extremely bullish to extremely bearish. There is a gradual transition.

market-structure.teaching: Most new highs (or lows) are simply profit-taking points and become bull-flag (or bear-flag) continuations, not tops — 'every new high is a potential top, but most reversal attempts fail.'
market-structure.quote: Most new highs are followed by profit taking. Every new high is a potential top, but most reversal attempts fail and become the beginnings of bull flags, only to be followed by another new high.

market-structure.teaching: A breakout above the first leg of a two-legged pullback within a strong bear trend is usually met by aggressive sellers (not fresh buyers), because most traders view it as a temporary pullback destined to fail — the same logic applies symmetrically to bear-trend pullbacks in a bull trend.
market-structure.quote: the dominant traders on the breakout will usually be aggressive sellers, instead of aggressive new buyers or profit-taking longs, and the minor bull trend will usually reverse back down into the direction of the major bear trend.

market-structure.teaching: As a trend matures, pullbacks grow larger and start behaving like trading ranges rather than flags, making new-high/new-low breakout attempts increasingly likely to fail — this is the transition zone before a full reversal or range.
market-structure.quote: Once a strong bull begins to have pullbacks that are relatively large, the pullbacks... behave more like trading ranges than bull flags... a new high is now a breakout attempt above a trading range, and the odds are that it will fail.

market-structure.teaching: A trend that has run 30+ bars often ends with one or two unusually large climactic trend bars as the strongest traders step aside (creating a 'vacuum'), then reverse hard — weak traders read the large bar as strength and pile in the wrong direction just as smart money reverses.
market-structure.quote: The absence of selling by the strongest traders results in a vacuum above the market, which creates one or two relatively large bull trend bars. This bull spike is just the sign that the strong traders have been waiting for... they appear as if out of nowhere and begin their selling.

market-structure.teaching: Reading a breakout to a new swing low: a strong continued breakdown means new selling dominates; an uncertain/choppy reaction means shorts are taking profit and buying is weak (expect a range); a strong reversal up means aggressive fresh buying dominates and the sell-off was likely just a big pullback in an ongoing bull trend.
market-structure.quote: If there is a strong breakout, then new selling is dominant. If the market's movement is uncertain, then profit taking by the shorts and weak buying by the bulls is taking place... If there is a strong reversal up, then aggressive buying by the longs is the most important factor.

market-structure.teaching: Most trend days begin within the first hour or so; recognizing a developing trend pattern early lets you force yourself into several high-probability with-trend trades — if no such pattern is present, treat the day as a trading range and fade new extremes instead.
market-structure.quote: Although a trend can begin with any bar during the day, the majority of trend days begin within the first hour or so. If you see a trend pattern setting up within the first hour or two of the day, there will likely be several high-probability with-trend trades that you can then make.

market-structure.teaching: During strong bear markets/reversals (e.g., 2008), selling becomes independent of fundamentals — driven by loss-averse investors, forced hedge-fund redemptions, and fund closures — producing outsized range days; adjust stop size down position size but keep trading standard price-action setups rather than fighting the emotional flow.
market-structure.quote: The huge ranges offer great price action day trading opportunities but you might have to increase your stop size and therefore reduce your position size... a good price action trader can do very well just looking for standard price action setups.

market-structure.teaching: Once a trend enters its channel phase it always looks like it's reversing; most reversal attempts (roughly 80%) just evolve into with-trend flags.
market-structure.quote: experienced traders know that 80 percent of reversal attempts fail and become bull ﬂags

market-structure.teaching: Easy, clear, high-probability setups tend to produce small, fast scalps; big trend moves require unclear, scary-looking setups precisely because that keeps most traders sidelined/chasing.
market-structure.quote: When a setup is easy and clear, meaning it has a high probability of success, the move is usually a small, fast scalp. If the move is going to go a long way, it has to be unclear and difﬁcult to take.

market-structure.teaching: A third consecutive push/buy climax in a spike-and-channel bull run is a classic place for at least a two-legged, 10+ bar correction back toward the moving average.
market-structure.quote: Bar 24 was the third push up and third consecutive buy climax after the spike up from bar 14... Both expected a correction to have at least two legs and 10 bars and to penetrate the moving average.

market-structure.teaching: In the strongest trends the with-trend pullback signal bars are often weak, which discourages most traders from taking them and forces late chasing entries — this weakness of signals is a feature, not a bug, of strong trends.
market-structure.quote: In the strongest trends, the pullbacks usually have weak signal bars, making many traders not take them, and forcing traders to chase the market.

market-structure.teaching: Numerous objective signs mark a strong trend: big gap open, trending highs/lows, mostly trend-color bars, minimal body overlap, bars with small/no tails, measuring/micro-measuring gaps, absence of big climaxes or large countertrend bars, no significant trend-channel-line overshoots, failed wedges/reversals, 20+ consecutive moving-average gap bars, small/infrequent sideways pullbacks, and repeated two-legged pullbacks.
market-structure.quote: Here are some characteristics that are commonly found in strong trends: ... There is a big gap opening on the day. ... There are small, infrequent, and mostly sideways pullbacks.

market-structure.teaching: Large early gaps that don't reverse usually mark the start of a strong trend day that closes at or near the extreme; quiet markets with small bars/dojis often produce the biggest trends, and institutions filling large orders in pieces (not dumping at once) is why trend days grind rather than spike-and-crash.
market-structure.quote: Quiet markets with lots of small bars, many of which are dojis, often lead to the biggest trends... they don't dump all of their buy orders onto the market at once.

market-structure.teaching: A trend day whose open is very close to its low (bull) tends to close near its high, with trend persisting into the close.
market-structure.quote: trend from the open days that open within a few ticks of the low of the day often close within a few ticks of the high of the day, and there is often a trend into the close.

market-structure.teaching: When a limit order to buy at the prior bar's close repeatedly fails to fill because the next bar's low never dips to that price, that non-fill itself is evidence the trend is strong.
market-structure.quote: When traders cannot get ﬁlled on a limit order to buy at the close of the prior bar, the trend is strong.

market-structure.teaching: A crowded one-sided channel (everyone who wanted to buy has already bought) sets up sharp reversals once the market starts to fall, because all trapped longs rush to exit simultaneously.
market-structure.quote: Everyone who wanted to buy had already bought and there was no one left to buy. As the market started to fall, all of the buyers in the channel were quickly holding losing positions and then everyone rushed to the exits.

market-structure.teaching: Markets tend to attempt everything twice (two legs): pullbacks, breakouts, and trend continuations regularly subdivide into two legs; success in doing something twice often leads to trend extension, while double failure often leads to the opposite move.
market-structure.quote: The market regularly tries to do something twice, and this is why all moves tend to subdivide into two smaller moves... If it fails in its two attempts, it will usually try to do the opposite. If it succeeds, it will often then extend the trend.

market-structure.teaching: A two-legged view of trend structure is more practically useful for trading than Elliott Wave theory, which Brooks calls essentially useless for most traders trying to make money.
market-structure.quote: This two-legged view of markets makes more sense to a trader since it offers sound logic and abundant trading opportunities, unlike Elliott Wave Theory, which is essentially useless to the vast majority of traders.

market-structure.teaching: Every larger trend leg / pullback is itself composed of smaller legs at lower timeframes; 'leg' just means the direction of movement changed by whatever criterion you use.
market-structure.quote: Every trend line break and every pullback is a leg, and each larger leg is made of smaller legs... The term leg is very general and simply means that the direction of movement has changed.

market-structure.teaching: Opening range size predicts day type: small opening range -> breakout day; opening range about half the recent-day range -> measured-move / trending-trading-range day; large opening range -> spike, likely spike-and-channel trend day.
market-structure.quote: if the opening range is small, it will be followed by a breakout... If the opening range is large, there is usually a spike and the day will likely become a spike and channel trend day.

market-structure.teaching: Every trend alternates between a spike phase (urgency, breakaway-gap feel, everyone believes there's further to go) and a channel phase (worry/uncertainty, two-sided trading that always looks like it's ending yet keeps extending) — this spike-and-channel duality underlies almost every trend.
market-structure.quote: First, there is a spike of one or more bars where the market is moving quickly... Next, there is a pullback... After the pullback, the trend converts into a channel, where there is less urgency and more a sense of worry and uncertainty. This is the wall of worry.

market-structure.teaching: After a spike, if the pullback grows beyond ~10 bars it becomes a trading range that will most often (but not always) break out in the original trend direction; roughly a third of the time it breaks out the opposite way instead.
market-structure.quote: The second possibility occurs if the pullback extends for more than 10 bars. It will then have grown into a trading range... In general, the odds always favor the trading range breakout to be in the same direction as the original trend... in about a third of cases the market will break out of the bottom of the trading range instead of the top.

market-structure.teaching: A channel, no matter how steep, is a flag in the opposite direction: a bull channel is a bear flag and a bear channel is a bull flag; it is also the first leg of a larger, still-unfolding trading range.
market-structure.quote: Remember that a channel, no matter how steep, is a flag in the opposite direction. A bull channel is a bear flag and a bear channel is a bull flag.

market-structure.teaching: A spike is a breakout/gap; the pullback to the start of the channel is therefore a test of that gap, i.e., a breakout test, and should be expected before too long even after a very strong trend.
market-structure.quote: A spike is a breakout, which means that there will be a gap between the breakout point and the first pullback, which is the start of the channel... The test to the bottom of the channel is a test of the gap and is a breakout test.

market-structure.teaching: Every large trend bar should be conceptually treated as simultaneously a spike, a breakout, a gap, and a climax — these are different lenses on the same event, not different phenomena.
market-structure.quote: Every large trend bar should be thought of as a spike, a breakout, a gap, and a climax.

market-structure.teaching: Consecutive climaxes occur because urgency drives both weak longs and weak shorts to capitulate/pile in at the market rather than wait for a pullback; once the weak side is fully exhausted on both legs, there's no one left to take the other side at that price, forcing at least a two-legged correction.
market-structure.quote: Once these weak longs are out and the weak bears are in, there is no one left to short at these low prices and this creates an imbalance to the buy side.

market-structure.teaching: Spikes are cumulative — an early, less-obvious spike can be more influential in defining where a channel actually begins than the final, more visually obvious spike that triggers the move; earlier countertrend spikes should not be dismissed even if price makes a new extreme afterward.
market-structure.quote: Just because that final spike broke below the wedge and clearly led to the bear leg does not mean that it turned the market all by itself. Those earlier spikes were also part of the transition and should not be overlooked.

market-structure.teaching: Any breakout resets trader mind-set and should be treated as the start of a new trend, even when it fails a reversal pattern (e.g., a wedge that breaks the 'wrong' way becomes the new dominant trend, typically stronger for having overrun the failed reversal).
market-structure.quote: The breakout always resets the mind-set and should be thought of as the start of a new trend.

market-structure.teaching: Spike-and-channel patterns are fractal/nested: a steep channel on one timeframe often IS just a spike on a higher timeframe chart, and vice versa; be open to reinterpreting the same price action at different scales.
market-structure.quote: When the channel is very steep, the spike and channel often together form just a spike on a higher time frame chart, and that spike will usually be followed by a channel on that higher time frame.

market-structure.teaching: A large trend bar late in a protracted trend is often a 'buy/sell vacuum,' not simply a fresh breakout leg — strong opposing and even same-side traders step aside near resistance, letting momentum buyers push price unopposed to the target, after which both profit-taking longs and fresh shorts pile in expecting a deeper corrective phase.
market-structure.quote: it was more likely a buy vacuum. The strong bulls and bears were waiting for a bar like this to sell around its close. It always forms just below one or more resistance levels, and as the market approaches the area, the bears and many bulls step aside.

market-structure.teaching: A wedge (three-push) pattern that is unusually large often has its correction subdivide into more than the usual two legs, since a short correction can't adequately absorb a large wedge.
market-structure.quote: A wedge usually has a two-legged correction, but when the wedge is large, the legs often subdivide.

market-structure.teaching: Trending trading range days: opening range about 1/3-1/2 of recent average daily range signals a coming breakout and roughly a doubling of the day's range, followed by a second trading range; multiple breakouts across a day should instead be treated/traded as a stronger trend day (with-trend focus only).
market-structure.quote: If the opening range is about a third to half of the size of the recent average daily range, then look for a breakout and an approximate doubling of the range of the day... when this happens, it is usually better to consider the day as a stronger type of trend day and trade only with trend.

market-structure.teaching: Each trading range, having hosted two-sided trading, becomes a 'comfort zone'/value area for both bulls and bears, creating a magnetic pull that tends to draw later breakouts back into the range for a test — this is why late-day reversals through a prior range are common and tradeable.
market-structure.quote: Since each trading range has two-sided trading, it is a comfort zone for bulls and bears, who both see the area as value. This creates a magnetic pull and tends to draw breakouts back into the range.

market-structure.teaching: A subtle early clue that a trading range is secretly trending: each swing high is a little higher than the prior one and each swing low is a little higher, even while the market still looks range-bound; once enough traders notice, a breakout follows.
market-structure.quote: If you see a trading range day forming, but each swing high is a little higher than the prior one and each swing low is higher than the prior one, the market may already be trending even though it is still in a trading range.

market-structure.teaching: Most reversal days begin their session as trending trading range days, so recognizing a TTR day should keep a trader alert to a possible large late-day reversal swing that can span the entire day's range.
market-structure.quote: Most reversal days begin as trending trading range days... they should always be prepared for a possible late reversal swing trade, which might become a large intraday trend trade that can cover the entire day's range.

market-structure.teaching: Trending trading range days are two-sided, so the trending force is weaker than other trend days; when a day is less strong it is less likely to close on its extreme, so look for reversal trades into the close.
market-structure.quote: When a day is less strong, it is less likely to close on its low. Traders know this and look for a reversal trade going into the close.

market-structure.teaching: When a trend begins more than an hour into the day, that first hour was by definition a trading range, so the day often becomes (or behaves like) a trending trading range day rather than a clean trend day.
market-structure.quote: Whenever a trend begins after an hour into the day, the day often becomes a trending trading range day

market-structure.teaching: Tight, horizontal areas of strong two-sided trading act as magnets that pull the market back in after a breakout.
market-structure.quote: This was an area of strong two-sided trading, and therefore a magnet that would tend to draw the market back in after a breakout.

market-structure.teaching: A trend-from-the-open day with too much sideways action in the first two hours is a weaker, less predictable variant (trending trading range) even though it opens/closes on its extremes; a true trend-from-the-open day has few tradable countertrend swings.
market-structure.quote: A trend from the open trend day usually doesn't have many signiﬁcant tradable countertrend swings, but a trending trading range does, and is a weaker, less predictable type of trend day.

market-structure.teaching: When the market fails twice at trying to do something (e.g., two failed attempts to make a new extreme), it usually then reverses and goes the other way.
market-structure.quote: When the market fails twice at trying to do something, it usually then goes in the opposite direction.

market-structure.teaching: Buying above a trading range in a bear trend (or shorting below one in a bull trend) is generally a losing strategy — smart traders instead fade at the extremes in the direction of the larger trend.
market-structure.quote: buying above a trading range in a bear trend is a losing strategy. Smart traders are doing just the opposite. They have limit orders to go short at and above the high of the prior bar

market-structure.teaching: A strong breakout bar after a trend establishes control usually leads to at least two more legs in that direction, often forming a wedge flag before the next leg continues.
market-structure.quote: When there is a strong bear breakout like this, there will usually be at least two more legs down in the form of a bear channel, and those two additional legs often create a wedge bear ﬂag.

market-structure.teaching: If the first bar or two of the day is a strong trend bar, there is usually follow-through in that direction, and an attempted reversal typically only produces a lower high (in a down move) or higher low (in an up move) rather than an actual reversal.
market-structure.quote: If the ﬁrst bar of the day is a strong trend bar (see thumbnail), there will usually be follow-through, and when the ﬁrst two bars are strong, an attempted reversal up usually becomes a lower high

market-structure.teaching: An inside bar following a large trend bar / climax creates a 'breakout mode' situation where, absent immediate continuation, the opposite side becomes aggressive (shorts buy back, bulls buy for a low-of-day) and the move can reverse.
market-structure.quote: Bar 10 was a large bear trend bar and therefore a sell climax. The next bar was an inside bar and this created a breakout mode situation.

market-structure.teaching: Trend-from-open days have urgency and conviction from the start and are usually the strongest trend type with the smallest pullbacks; a small-pullback trend day is the strongest variant, forming once or twice a month.
market-structure.quote: Trend from the open days have urgency and conviction from the outset and are usually the strongest trends and have the smallest pullbacks.

market-structure.teaching: A trend-from-open day develops only about 20 percent of days; buying/shorting the first bar's break expecting sustained trend is therefore inherently a low-probability bet, and first-hour reversals are far more common.
market-structure.quote: A trend from the open day is usually the strongest form of trend pattern, but it develops in only about 20 percent of days. That means that buying above the ﬁrst bar or shorting below it...is a low-probability trade.

market-structure.teaching: The high or low of the day forms within the first 5 bars roughly 50% of the time, but within the opening range (which can span a couple hours) about 90% of the time.
market-structure.quote: The high or low of the day forms within the ﬁrst ﬁve bars or so in about 50 percent of days. However, it forms within the opening range...in about 90 percent of days.

market-structure.teaching: A trend-from-open setup is more reliable when it forms at a strong magnet such as a trend channel line (wedge reversal) or as part of a reversal pattern like a final flag at the prior day's close.
market-structure.quote: The setup is more reliable if it forms at a strong magnet like a trend channel line...or if it is part of a reversal pattern, like a reversal from a ﬁnal ﬂag at yesterday's close.

market-structure.teaching: A strong trend-from-open day can carry follow-through into the first hour or two of the next day; the pullback on that next-day open is often just a higher-timeframe two-legged correction (e.g., to the 15-minute MA), even though it may look like a reversal.
market-structure.quote: This type of trend can be so strong that there can be follow-through in the ﬁrst hour or two of the next day...The pullback often is strong enough to make traders wonder if the market is reversing, but it is usually just a higher time frame, two-legged correction

market-structure.teaching: Unusually large consecutive trend bars breaking to a new extreme after a long-running trend usually signal climactic exhaustion rather than a new leg, and are typically followed by a roughly 10-bar, two-legged pullback.
market-structure.quote: when a trend has gone on for a long time and then has unusual further strength, it usually signals the end of the move for the time being and the start of a two-legged pullback that will last about 10 bars or so

market-structure.teaching: A spike (4+ bars with no pullback, or even two large trend bars) represents a price level both sides agree shouldn't be traded at, forcing the market to quickly move on; after a spike, the three typical outcomes are exhaustive reversal, extended sideways consolidation, or a trend channel (spike and channel day).
market-structure.quote: The spike is an area where both bulls and bears agree that very little trading should take place, and the market therefore needs to quickly move to another price level.

market-structure.teaching: The market has inertia: the first attempt to end a trend (first trend-line break) usually fails and often becomes a with-trend entry leading to a new trend extreme rather than a genuine reversal.
market-structure.quote: The market has inertia and the ﬁrst attempt to end the trend usually fails...the ﬁrst break of the trend line has high odds of setting up a with-trend entry that will lead to a second trend leg

market-structure.teaching: A tight trading range acts as a magnet even at the close of the day: after a breakout out of it, price often comes back and closes within that range.
market-structure.quote: Tight trading ranges are magnets and tend to pull breakouts back into them.

market-structure.teaching: Institutions filling large orders in pieces throughout the day (to avoid creating an exhaustive climax that could reverse against their fill price) is a mechanism behind quiet, small-pullback trend days.
market-structure.quote: The market moved up quietly all day because institutions had buy orders to ﬁll, and they ﬁlled them in pieces during the day because they were afraid that a pullback might not come and they needed to buy.

market-structure.teaching: When a market forms multiple trending trading ranges in a day, traders should focus almost exclusively on with-trend trades and treat range-edge reversal attempts (final flag) as lower priority/secondary.
market-structure.quote: When the day is forming multiple trending trading ranges...traders should concentrate on taking with-trend trades.

market-structure.teaching: A short is only acceptable against a strong bull channel if there is prior bear strength (e.g., a 5-10 bar pullback to the moving average) or a break of the bull trend line combined with an approach to the moving average; otherwise you are betting the market does something it hasn't done all day.
market-structure.quote: You could only short a second signal and only if there was some prior bear strength, like a five- to 10-bar pullback to the moving average. In the absence of that, any short is a bet that the market would do something that it had not done all day.

market-structure.teaching: On most strong trend days there is a sharp, brief countertrend spike between 11:00 a.m. and noon (news is usually blamed but is irrelevant) that shakes out weak with-trend traders and traps eager counter-trend traders; it typically resolves back into a with-trend opportunity.
market-structure.quote: On most strong trend days, there is usually a sharp, brief reversal between 11:00 a.m. and noon PST that shakes out weak longs and traps overly eager bears... it usually sets up a buying opportunity for those who do not get frightened by the spike down.

market-structure.teaching: A trend from the open often first has a double top or bottom that gets tested/broken on an early scheduled report before the real trend unleashes.
market-structure.quote: A trend from the open can have a double top or bottom before the strong trend begins... you always have to consider the possibility that the market was just waiting for the report before it unleashed its strength.

market-structure.teaching: When pullbacks stay unusually small all day, treat it as a very strong trend and stay biased to that side for most/all of the day; mislabeling such a day as a mere 'trending trading range day' will cause you to take counter-trend trades and miss with-trend ones.
market-structure.quote: When the pullbacks are this small, you are dealing with a very strong bear trend. When that is the case, it is better to make sure that you are short for most or all of the day. Thinking of this as a trending trading range day will make you take longs and often miss shorts.

market-structure.teaching: The first moving-average gap bar in a trend, after the trend has not touched the MA for a long stretch (e.g., ~20 bars) in a tight channel, tends to precede the final leg of that trend before a larger correction or reversal, because the approach to the MA almost always breaks the significant trend line.
market-structure.quote: The first moving average gap bar in a bear trend often leads to the final leg down before there is a larger correction up. Since the leg up to the moving average gap bar almost always breaks a significant bear trend line, the higher low or lower low test of the bear low usually leads to a protracted correction up or even a reversal.

market-structure.teaching: Experienced traders should expect a strong countertrend move on a trend day after 11 a.m. PST and be ready to fade its failure (e.g., a small double top) rather than be trapped by it.
market-structure.quote: experienced traders know that the market often has a strong countertrend move after 11 a.m. PST on a trend day and they would be ready to short the failure, like the small double top at bar 24.

market-structure.teaching: Watch for a pullback that begins with an unusually strong countertrend spike bar — that is the classic early warning that the pullback may grow into a full trend-channel reversal rather than remain a minor flag.
market-structure.quote: whenever you see a pullback that has a strong countertrend spike, be aware that the trend might be reversing... make sure to consider the possibility that the bar could be a bull spike that could be followed by a relentless bull channel instead of a small bear flag.

market-structure.teaching: Consecutive climax (spike) bars usually lead to at least a several-bar pause or two-legged pullback of roughly 10 bars; a third consecutive climax typically leads to an even larger correction than after two.
market-structure.quote: A pair of consecutive climaxes is usually followed by at least a several-bar pause or pullback... Bar 7 was then a third sell climax, and a third consecutive sell climax is usually followed by an even larger correction.

market-structure.teaching: The start of any channel (the point where it began) tends to get tested later, often forming a double top/bottom flag rather than a clean break.
market-structure.quote: the beginning of a channel usually gets tested... The test may break above that high but more often forms a double top bear flag, as it did here.

market-structure.teaching: During the midday range in a trend-resumption setup, both sides keep scaling in until most reach max size; once a breakout begins, the losing side can't add anymore, leaving the breakout unopposed and prone to acceleration as trapped counter-trend positions capitulate.
market-structure.quote: Once there is a breakout, the losing side cannot scale in anymore, and their only choices are hope and getting stopped out. ... As the market falls, it will often accelerate as more and more bulls give up and sell out of their losing longs, adding to the collapse into the close.

market-structure.teaching: Trend resumption patterns can play out over two or more days: a strong spike on one day followed by a multi-hour/multi-day trading range can resume the original trend at any point, even while intraday 5-minute swings look large; recognizing this justifies swinging a larger part of the position.
market-structure.quote: Trend resumption patterns often take place over two or more days... If you are aware of this, you will be more likely to be willing to swing a larger part of your position for what could be a big move.

market-structure.teaching: Any breakout is often followed by a measured move based on the height of the originating spike, typically measured from the open/low of the first spike bar to the close/high of the final spike bar.
market-structure.quote: Any breakout often is followed by a measured move based on the spike. It is usually based on the height from the open or low of the first bar of the spike to the close or high of the final bar.

market-structure.teaching: Stairs often function as pullbacks/flags on a higher timeframe — a broad channel over the final hour or two can be a large flag that breaks out at the next day's open in the higher-timeframe trend direction.
market-structure.quote: Stairs are often just pullbacks or flags in higher time frame trends... For example, a broad bull channel today might just be a large bear flag, and the bear trend could break out tomorrow.

market-structure.teaching: Three pushes down (or up) does not guarantee a trend reversal — if the countertrend move lacks large trend bars and strong climactic reversal bars, it won't attract enough opposing strength and the original trend resumes, often as a wedge-shaped flag.
market-structure.quote: Three pushes down does not guarantee a trend reversal. The move down to bar 7 had very little buying pressure.
