There were no large bull trend bars and no strong climactic reversals... This was not how strong reversals typically look, and because of that, it did not attract enough strong bulls to reverse the market.

market-structure.teaching: When a wedge top/bottom fails and the market accelerates instead of reversing, expect the breakout to run to roughly a measured move (an Andrew's Pitchfork-style projection: a parallel line as far from the middle line as the middle line is from the origin line).
market-structure.quote: The breakout should extend to about a measured move up to a parallel line that is about the same distance from the middle line as the middle line is from the bottom line (an Andrew's Pitchfork move), which it did.

market-structure.teaching: The market is perpetually attempting to break out, and then perpetually trying to make every breakout fail — this tension is the single most fundamental dynamic in trading.
market-structure.quote: The market is always trying to break out, and then the market tries to make every breakout fail. This is the most fundamental aspect of all trading and is at the heart of everything that we do.

market-structure.teaching: Institutions generate ~90%+ of volume in large markets and are (as a group) profitable; price can only reach a level where institutions are willing to trade there, so retail stops are never 'run' — they're only hit when institutions independently choose to trade at that price.
market-structure.quote: Ninety percent or more of all trading in large markets is done by institutions... The market will not run your stops. The market might test the price where your protective stop is, but it has nothing to do with your stop.

market-structure.teaching: Strong trends can have pullbacks driven by big-money buying at/below the low of the prior bar even as weak traders get stopped out there — don't assume a pullback below a prior low means the trend is reversing.
market-structure.quote: You have to assume that the big money was buying at and below the low of that prior bar, and that is also what many experienced traders were doing.

market-structure.teaching: Profit-taking by early, aggressive with-trend traders — not new counter-trend selling/buying — is the primary cause of the first pullback in any strong trend.
market-structure.quote: Profit taking is the cause of the first pullback in any strong trend.

market-structure.teaching: A deep pullback (roughly 75% or more retracement of the prior leg) meaningfully lowers the odds the market re-tests the prior trend extreme; at that point it's often better to treat it as a new opposite-direction trend rather than a pullback.
market-structure.quote: In general, the odds that a pullback will get back to the prior trend's extreme fall substantially if the pullback retraces 75 percent or more.

market-structure.teaching: Sustained trends transition into trading ranges as pullbacks grow larger — once pullbacks start behaving like trading ranges rather than flags, new-high/new-low breakout attempts become less reliable and are more likely to fail.
market-structure.quote: Once a strong bull trend begins to have pullbacks that are relatively large, the pullbacks... behave more like trading ranges than like bull flags... a new high is now a breakout attempt above a trading range, and the odds are that it will fail.

market-structure.teaching: A correction after a strong, mature trend leg typically needs at least about 10 bars and two legs to be considered a real (rather than minor) correction; three consecutive buy/sell climaxes without such a correction often precede one.
market-structure.quote: That usually requires at least 10 bars and two legs, and sometimes can result in a trend reversal.

market-structure.teaching: After a breakout resolves uncertainty into a fast directional move, the market searches for a new price where both bulls and bears again see value — meaning every successful breakout eventually leads back into renewed uncertainty/a new trading range.
market-structure.quote: A breakout is a search for a trading range, for uncertainty, and a 50 percent directional probability of an equidistant move.

market-structure.teaching: Crash-shaped price action (violent one-directional moves) is not a rare daily-chart phenomenon — the same 'crash' pattern shape occurs routinely and repeatedly on intraday charts and is simply a tradable bear (or bull) swing once emotion is stripped away.
market-structure.quote: If you remove the time and price axes from a chart and focus simply on the price action, there are market movements that occur frequently on intraday charts that are indistinguishable from the patterns in a classic crash.

market-structure.teaching: 'Close is close enough': a pattern that resembles a textbook setup but isn't perfect will likely behave similarly to the textbook version — trading well requires operating comfortably in this probabilistic gray zone rather than demanding exact pattern matches.
market-structure.quote: If a pattern is setting up and is not perfect but is reasonably similar to a reliable setup, it will likely behave similarly as well. Close is usually close enough.

market-structure.teaching: Most breakouts fail — trading-range breakout attempts fail roughly 80% of the time, trend reversal attempts also fail roughly 80% of the time, and even the strongest trend-continuation breakouts fail about 30% of the time.
market-structure.quote: about 80 percent of attempts to break out of a trading range fail...even the strongest breakouts fail about 30 percent of the time.

market-structure.teaching: Markets have inertia: trending markets tend to keep trending (most reversal attempts fail), and ranging markets tend to keep ranging (most breakout attempts fail).
market-structure.quote: The market has inertia and a strong propensity to continue what it has been doing.

market-structure.teaching: Support and resistance act as magnets; the closer price gets to a magnet, the stronger the pull (a vacuum effect), so traders should generally trade in the direction of a nearby magnet until it is reached, then reassess.
market-structure.quote: Support and resistance are magnets that draw the market toward them. When you become aware of a magnet not too far from the current price, trade only in the direction of the magnet until after it is reached.

market-structure.teaching: Unusually high volume (10-20x average) on a breakout increases the odds of follow-through, but volume is not reliable enough to actively trade on — the trend bars themselves already convey what volume would tell you, and focusing on volume tends to distract and hinder decision-making.
market-structure.quote: Volume is not reliable enough to warrant following it, and the large trend bars that comprise the spike will already tell you whether the breakout is likely to be successful.

market-structure.teaching: Once broken, support becomes resistance, and once exceeded, resistance becomes support.
market-structure.quote: Once the market falls through support, it usually becomes resistance; and once the market breaks above resistance, it usually becomes support.

market-structure.teaching: Any upward-sloping channel should be treated as a bear flag (and any downward-sloping channel as a bull flag) for trading purposes, regardless of the larger trend context, because its eventual breakout tends to behave like a flag breakout.
market-structure.quote: any upward-sloping channel should be thought of as a bear flag, even if it is part of a bull market...Likewise, any downward-sloping channel should be thought of as a bull flag.

market-structure.teaching: Every trend bar functions as an intraday equivalent of a gap; the popular saying 'all gaps get filled' is better restated as 'all prior prices get tested' — gaps and breakout points act as magnets that get retested even if not fully closed.
market-structure.quote: 'All gaps will get filled' is a saying that you sometimes hear, but this saying only rarely helps traders...the saying would be more precise if it were 'all prior prices get tested.'

market-structure.teaching: After a level (support/resistance, moving average, trend line) is repeatedly tested (roughly 4-5 times) without a strong reaction, the defending side loses conviction, stops defending, and starts taking profits, increasing the odds the level eventually breaks.
market-structure.quote: if the market tests an area of support four or five times, the likelihood of breaking through that support increases and, at some point, the breakout becomes more likely than not.

market-structure.teaching: Because computer algorithms generate roughly 70% of all trading volume (about 80% of institutional volume) and are based on mathematics, technical levels are known to everyone — there are no secret levels, which is why classic support/resistance and measured-move targets work reliably across many participants.
market-structure.quote: computer algorithms, which generate 70 percent of all trading volume and 80 percent of institutional volume, know where they are.

market-structure.teaching: Trend strength can be read by what happens immediately after price breaks a prior swing extreme: if the breakout finds more buyers (sellers) and continues far enough for a scalper's profit, the trend is strong; if it reverses within a bar or two, the trend may be weakening or transitioning into a range.
market-structure.quote: if the market moves far enough up for the breakout buyers to make at least a scalper's profit, then the breakout found more buyers than sellers. That is one of the hallmarks of a strong trend.

market-structure.teaching: All pullbacks are small trading ranges on the chart you're viewing, and all trading ranges are pullbacks on a higher time frame chart; most breakouts of a trading range fail, but most breakouts of a pullback succeed.
market-structure.quote: All pullbacks are small trading ranges on the chart that you are viewing, and all trading ranges are pullbacks on higher time frame charts.

market-structure.teaching: About 80 percent of trend reversal attempts (and trading-range breakout attempts) fail and become continuation flags — this is the statistical basis for fading first attempts and buying/selling pullbacks with the trend.
market-structure.quote: There will be repeated attempts by the bulls to resume the trend and by the bears to reverse the trend, and 80 percent of both fail.

market-structure.teaching: Pullbacks end at a cluster of support/resistance levels, and the closer price gets to a well-recognized level, the stronger the 'vacuum effect' pulling it there before a sharp reversal.
market-structure.quote: Once the market gets close to the key price, the vacuum effect often dominates... once the support is hit, the bulls come in and buy aggressively and relentlessly.

market-structure.teaching: A pullback that retraces 75% or more of the prior trend leg should generally be reconceived as the start of a new trend in the opposite direction rather than a pullback in the old trend.
market-structure.quote: the odds that a pullback will get back to the prior trend's extreme fall substantially if the pullback retraces 75 percent or more.

market-structure.teaching: Any move with two legs — with-trend or countertrend — should be treated like a pullback/flag, since two-legged moves are the market's default corrective structure at every scale (fractal).
market-structure.quote: Any move that has two legs should be traded as if it is a pullback, even if it is with the trend.

market-structure.teaching: There is a typical escalating sequence ('first pullback sequence') by which a trend weakens: smaller bodies → tails → overlap → doji → opposite-color bar → high1/low1 → high2/low2 → wedge (high3/low3) → minor trend line break → moving-average touch → major trend line break → moving-average gap bar → eventual trading range. Each first-of-its-kind pullback is usually followed by at least a test of the trend extreme.
market-structure.quote: Here is the general order in which signs of weakness in a bull trend develop... A bar has a bear body... There is a break of a minor bull trend line... There is a break of a major bull trend line.

market-structure.teaching: High 1/high 2/high 3/high 4 buy setups are only valid in a bull trend or trading range, never in a clear bear trend; symmetric rule for low 1-4 shorts, which are invalid in a clear bull trend.
market-structure.quote: If the market is in a clear bull trend, do not look for low 1 or low 2 shorts, because those are setups only in bear trends and trading ranges.

market-structure.teaching: Reversal setups (high 1/2/3 in a bear trend, low 1/2/3 in a bull trend) become good short/long-fade candidates when they are LOW probability, e.g. buying a high 1 in a bear trend has roughly a 40% chance of success, giving a ~60% edge to shorting above the same bar.
market-structure.quote: buying a high 1 in a bear trend is a low-probability trade. That means that if it has only about a 40 percent chance of being a successful long, it has about a 60 percent chance of hitting the protective stop before hitting a profit-taking limit order.

market-structure.teaching: You can sanity-check a signal by viewing an inversely correlated instrument (e.g., an inverse ETF): if you would not take the opposite trade on the inverse chart, you shouldn't take your trade on the primary chart.
market-structure.quote: if you would not buy on the SDS, you should not sell on the Emini.

market-structure.teaching: Trends and ranges have inertia: strong trends tend to keep trending and reversal attempts tend to fail; trading ranges tend to stay sideways and breakout attempts tend to fail.
market-structure.quote: Trends tend to keep trending and reversals usually fail; trading ranges keep going sideways and breakout attempts usually fail.

market-structure.teaching: Big trend-bar extremes act as price magnets because traders who entered on or after the bar commonly place protective stops just beyond it; the market often works its way to run those stops before reversing back in the trend direction.
market-structure.quote: It is fairly common for the market to work its way to those stops and then reverse back in the direction of the trend bar.

market-structure.teaching: The broadest, most useful definition of a trading range is any area of two-sided trading, from a single doji bar up to the whole chart; even trending channels are technically slow trading ranges since the trend side is merely 'more aggressive.'
market-structure.quote: The broadest and most useful definition of a trading range is that it is simply an area of two-sided trading. It can be as small as a single bar (a doji bar) or larger than all of the bars on your screen.

market-structure.teaching: A rough personal-use rule: if you believe there's ~60%+ certainty the trend resumes soon, treat the sideways action as a pullback (trade with-trend only); if less certain, treat it as a true trading range (trade both directions).
market-structure.quote: if you are about 60 percent or more certain that your trade will soon be followed by a resumption of the trend, then you believe that the pattern is a pullback.

market-structure.teaching: Every rally within a trading range functions as a bear flag and every sell-off functions as a bull flag — traders should therefore trade the top of a range exactly as they'd trade a bear flag in a bear trend, and the bottom exactly as a bull flag in a bull trend.
market-structure.quote: Every rally in a trading range is essentially a bear flag and every selloff is effectively a bull flag.

market-structure.teaching: The final leg of a trading range in a bull market is usually a bear channel (a last-ditch bear attempt that functions as a bull flag); the opposite (bull channel functioning as bear flag) is true for ranges in bear markets.
market-structure.quote: The final leg down in most trading ranges in bull markets is a bear channel, which is a last-ditch attempt by the bears to create a top and a new bear trend. A bear channel is a bull flag.

market-structure.teaching: All so-called reversal patterns (double tops/bottoms, head and shoulders) are actually continuation patterns that rarely fail; treating them as reversal setups leads most traders to net losses, so the better mental model is to always look first for a with-trend trade off the failed 'reversal.'
market-structure.quote: It is a mistake to see every top or bottom as a great reversal setup, because if you take all of those countertrend entries, the majority of your trades will be losers and your occasional wins will not be enough to offset your losses.

market-structure.teaching: Trading ranges have a magnetic pull toward their middle and toward their extremes simultaneously: the market races toward the top/bottom (because both sides want the best price before committing) yet gets pulled back toward the middle after breakouts fail — explaining why most breakouts reverse.
market-structure.quote: This is why trading ranges always race toward the top and toward the bottom and they always look like they are about to break out, only to reverse... The top and bottom of a trading range have a magnetic pull that gets greater as the market gets closer to the top or bottom.

market-structure.teaching: Trading ranges tend to break out away from the moving average, especially when adjacent to it; ranges far from the MA may instead be setting up a test back to it.
market-structure.quote: They also tend to break out away from the moving average. If they are below the moving average, they usually break to the downside, and if they are above it, they tend to break out to the upside.

market-structure.teaching: The longer a trading range persists, the higher the odds it becomes a genuine reversal (distribution in a bull range, accumulation in a bear range) because with-trend traders lose conviction and stop adding to positions.
market-structure.quote: the longer a trading range continues, the greater are the odds that it will become a reversal pattern (accumulation in a bear trading range, distribution in a bull trading range).

market-structure.teaching: The middle third of the trading day (roughly 8:30-10:30am PST) combined with price in the middle third of the range is the highest-risk, lowest-edge environment on most days — trade sparingly or not at all then.
market-structure.quote: A trader who is not yet successful should not trade when the market is in the middle of the day while it is also in the middle of the day's range. This change to one's trading alone can mean the difference between being a loser and a winner.

market-structure.teaching: A single reversal bar / two-bar reversal / spike-and-channel on a lower time frame can be the equivalent of a normal (non-alarming) pullback pattern on a higher time frame chart — always keep the higher-time-frame trend context in mind before treating lower-time-frame action as a reversal.
market-structure.quote: the pattern might look strongly bearish on the 5 minute chart, but it could be a simple high 2 or double bottom buy setup on the 60 minute chart.

market-structure.teaching: Naming a pattern (flag, pennant, wedge, triangle, head-and-shoulders) is irrelevant; the only thing that matters is recognizing two-sided trading is occurring and using that to look for trades in both directions.
market-structure.quote: Many traders use descriptive names like flags, pennants, or triangles, but the name is irrelevant. All that matters is that two-sided trading is taking place.

market-structure.teaching: Barbwire that forms after a sell-off often becomes the final flag of the bear trend because both bulls and bears agree there is value there, drawing the market back into that price area.
market-structure.quote: Since there is strong two-sided trading within barbwire, when barbwire forms after a sell-off it often becomes the final flag of the bear trend.

market-structure.teaching: Sell (buy) climaxes are emotional exhaustion events; once weak bulls (bears) are flushed out, the remaining side has a supply/demand imbalance favoring reversal.
market-structure.quote: Sell climaxes are emotional and usually indicate bulls desperate to get out at any price, even at the market, during a collapse.

market-structure.teaching: Any downward-sloping wedge/bear channel functions like a bull flag regardless of whether a larger bull trend is visible on screen; direction of prior swing off-screen is irrelevant to how the pattern behaves.
market-structure.quote: Any down wedge will behave like a large pullback in a bull trend, which means that it will behave like a bull flag.

market-structure.teaching: A three-push sloped pattern is not automatically a reversal signal — if the channel is tight, it will usually continue like a channel rather than reverse like a wedge.
market-structure.quote: if the three-push pattern is in a tight channel, the pattern will usually function like a channel and continue indefinitely, and not function like a wedge and reverse.

market-structure.teaching: All trading ranges with three or more pushes behave essentially like triangles regardless of exact shape; the specific triangle taxonomy (ascending/descending/symmetrical/expanding) doesn't change trading implications materially.
market-structure.quote: It does not matter what you call it, because all trading ranges behave similarly.

market-structure.teaching: Small first pullbacks in a strong one-sided spike are mostly profit-taking by with-trend traders, not new countertrend positioning by the opposite side — so fading the very first pullback in a strong spike has no sound mathematical basis.
market-structure.quote: there is usually no good way for the bears to make profits by shorting below the low of the prior bar as the first pullback is forming in a strong bull trend

market-structure.teaching: 'Failure of anything' — a breakout, flag breakout, trend-line-overshoot reversal, or failure to reach a profit target — is itself a valid, tradeable reason/setup in the opposite direction.
market-structure.quote: Failure of anything (the market reverses before going as far as expected)... A breakout of a prior high or low. A flag breakout... A reversal from an overshoot of a trend line or a trend channel line. A failure to reach a profit target

market-structure.teaching: Every breakout of the prior bar's high/low is fundamentally a test, not automatically the start of a big move — most breakout attempts fail and the market usually pulls back into the prior bar's range before deciding its next direction.
market-structure.quote: most attempts to break out fail. Unfortunately, beginning traders get caught up in the emotion of all breakouts and assume that the market is at the start of a big move.

market-structure.teaching: In a genuinely strong bull (bear) trend, tops (bottoms) become bull (bear) flags roughly 80% of the time rather than reversing — countertrend low1/low2 (high1/high2) signals in that context are traps, not valid setups.
market-structure.quote: Tops in strong bull trends become bull flags in about 80 percent of the cases.

market-structure.teaching: Institutions are not "running your stops" out of malice; they make up ~94% of market volume vs ~5% from individual retail traders, and a stop being hit simply means an institution wanted to trade at that price too.
market-structure.quote: Institutions could care less about us and are not out there running our stops, trying to devour the little guy. If your stop gets hit, it has nothing to do with you.

market-structure.teaching: Most trading-range breakout attempts fail; roughly 60% chance of failure vs 40% success is Brooks' rule-of-thumb baseline probability for an unconfirmed breakout attempt out of a range.
market-structure.quote: Since most trading range breakout attempts fail, there was a 60 percent chance that the market would trade down and only a 40 percent chance that the breakout would be successful...60-40 is a good rule of thumb when it comes to trading range breakout attempts.

market-structure.teaching: Pullbacks/reversals begin with profit-taking by the trend side, not new opposite-side aggression; watch for large trend bars followed by small bars/pullbacks as evidence that a breakout attempt has failed.
market-structure.quote: All pullbacks and reversals begin with profit taking...This is why so many large trend bars, which are breakout attempts, are followed by small bars and pullbacks, which means that the breakouts failed.

market-structure.teaching: The market frequently runs obvious round-number/logical protective-stop levels (e.g., a 2-3 point stop at a new high) to the exact tick before reversing — experienced scale-traders place their adds just inside where the crowd's stops sit.
market-structure.quote: The market often hits obvious protective stops to the tick and then reverses...an astute trader looking to scale in would have placed a limit order to short more...one tick below the three-point stop.

market-structure.teaching: Individual traders can get trapped into bad trades or out of good ones even though most volume is institutional/algorithmic, because institutional models operate on different logic (hedging, scaling, unrelated timeframes) and aren't concerned with small 5-minute-chart moves.
market-structure.quote: What appears on the chart is the distilled product of a huge number of traders who are basing their decisions on a huge number of different and unknowable reasons. The result is that individual traders sometimes get trapped into or out of the market.

market-structure.teaching: Price action is largely the visible footprint of institutional order flow (which controls the market), not the cause; individual/retail price-action trades are effectively piggyback scalps on institutional activity that tends to defend its position rather than reverse the market immediately after your entry.
market-structure.quote: The price action that traders see during the day is the result of institutional activity and much less the cause of the activity.

market-structure.teaching: Trades often fail off institutional entries near round-number tick counts (e.g., stalling at 5 ticks then surging past 6) because large (100+ contract) institutional orders are actively defending/adding at those levels — a practical tell for scalp continuation.
market-structure.quote: In general, anything over 100 contracts should be considered institutional in today's Emini market... almost certainly that 250 lot order was an institutional buy.

market-structure.teaching: Whenever the market tries something twice (e.g., pushing to a new extreme) and fails both times, it usually then tries the opposite direction — this is the underlying logic behind double tops/bottoms and why traders wait for a test of the old extreme before committing to a reversal.
market-structure.quote: Whenever the market tries to do something twice and fails, it usually then tries the opposite. This is why double tops and bottoms work

market-structure.teaching: Every bar is classifiable as either a trend bar (body present, one side in control) or a doji/nontrend bar (tiny/no body, bulls and bears in equilibrium); the distinction is relative to recent bar sizes and the specific market/timeframe, not absolute.
market-structure.quote: it is most useful to think of all bars as being either trend bars or nontrend (trading range) bars... it is simpler to refer to all nontrend bars as dojis

market-structure.teaching: A trend bar breakout does not guarantee a trend is starting, and conversely a series of dojis can itself display a trend if each has trending closes/highs/lows — a doji or trend bar's meaning is always contextual, not automatic.
market-structure.quote: Just like dojis don't always mean the market is trendless, a trend bar does not always mean that the market is trending.

market-structure.teaching: In a trend (even within a trading-range day), a small pullback bar can set up either a with-trend continuation entry or, at swing points with supporting factors (trendline break, High/Low 2, new swing extreme), a countertrend entry — but in a pure pullback it is only ever a with-trend setup.
market-structure.quote: In a trend, a small bar on a pullback is only a With Trend setup.

market-structure.teaching: Trend, swing, pullback, and leg are overlapping, relative classifications — what is a pullback on one timeframe can be a strong trend on a smaller one; the terms are guidelines, not rigid categories.
market-structure.quote: a pullback in a bull on a 60-minute chart might be a strong bear trend on a I-minute chart

market-structure.teaching: A trend cannot exist without both (1) a break of the trendline from the prior trend/range and (2) trending swings (e.g., series of Higher Highs and Higher Lows in a bull). Absent either, there is no trend.
market-structure.quote: All trends, no matter how small, must first break a trendline from the prior trend or trading range and then have trending swings...Absent either of these, there is no trend.

market-structure.teaching: Trend channel line overshoots reverse because sophisticated/institutional participants use the same technical extremes as exit/reversal points, not because retail traders finally capitulate — volume at turning points is too large to be explained by small traders alone.
market-structure.quote: Smart traders won't trade Countertrend until either there is a pullback from a strong trendline break or there is a reversal from a trend channel line over­shoot...Look at the volume at key turning points. It's huge.

market-structure.teaching: Checklist of signs of a strong trend: big gap opening; trending highs/lows; no climaxes/large bars (largest bars often countertrend, a trap); no significant trend-channel overshoots (minor ones only cause sideways corrections); failed Wedges; 2-hour-no-EMA-touch (2HM); few/no profitable Countertrend trades; small pullbacks relative to average range; sideways (not deep) corrections after trendline breaks; repeated High/Low 2 and M2B/M2S entries; no two consecutive closes on the opposite side of the EMA; bars with small/no tails.
market-structure.quote: Here are some characteristics of that are commonly found in strong trends: Big gap opening. Trending highs and lows (swings). No Climaxes and not many large bars...

market-structure.teaching: An outside bar or trap that appears to run stops and push traders out of a strong trend often precedes a strong continuation leg, as trapped traders are forced to chase back in.
market-structure.quote: An outside bar that traps traders out of a trend often leads to a strong trend leg as traders are forced to chase the market up.

market-structure.teaching: When a market retraces a strong trend leg, the first price target is typically the earlier Countertrend entry points (the signal-bar extremes of failed opposite-direction trades along the way).
market-structure.quote: When a market retraces a strong move, the first target is always the earlier Coun­tertrend entry points.

market-structure.teaching: Trendlines are most reliable/tradable when tested by a third touch/leg; a trendline built from only two points that are far apart, tested by a close third point, is a weaker basis for a trade.
market-structure.quote: Trendlines work best when they are tested by a third leg, and here Bars 5 and 6 were essentially still part of the same leg...

market-structure.teaching: The start (origin) of a Spike-and-Channel move typically gets tested within a day or two of the channel breaking, so Countertrend/target setups should be anticipated around that origin price on subsequent sessions.
market-structure.quote: since the start ofthe channel often gets tested within a day or two, make sure to con­sider Countertrend setups on the next day.

market-structure.teaching: The slope of the final flag of an old trend often approximates the slope of the new trend that follows the reversal, though this has limited standalone trading value.
market-structure.quote: One final observation is that the slope of the final flag of a trend often provides an approximation of the slope of the new trend.

market-structure.teaching: A day can shift between trend day and trading range day (or reverse trend direction) at any time; accept the regime change and adapt rather than fighting it.
market-structure.quote: a trend day can turn into a trading range day or a trend in the opposite direction at any time. When it happens, don't question it or be upset by it. Just accept it and trade it.

market-structure.teaching: A pullback is technically a bar moving at least a tick against the trend, but functionally any pause (inside bar, opposite trend bar, or sideways action) should be treated as a pullback. Like any trend, a pullback itself usually has at least two legs (an ABC correction).
market-structure.quote: Since a pullback itself is a trend... it will commonly have at least two legs.

market-structure.teaching: Any move with two legs should be traded as if it's a pullback, even if it's actually With Trend (e.g., the final leg of a trend can be a two-legged With Trend move to a new extreme).
market-structure.quote: Any move that has two legs should be traded as if it is a pullback, even if it is With Trend

market-structure.teaching: If a trend ends in a genuine climax (trend channel line overshoot and reversal, or a significant reversal pattern), stop looking for pullback entries in the old trend — it's over, at least for an hour or the rest of the day.
market-structure.quote: if there is a Wedge top or a Lower High after a break of the bull trendline, you should now be looking for shorts and not pullbacks in the old bull.

market-structure.teaching: Pullbacks in a trend progress through an escalating hierarchy — bar (High/Low 1), minor trendline, EMA, EMA Gap, major trendline — each being the 'First Pullback' of its type, and each First Pullback is usually followed by at least a test of the trend extreme (often a new extreme) until the major trendline finally breaks.
market-structure.quote: each type of \"first\" Countertrend move will therefore likely be followed by another leg in the trend.

market-structure.teaching: Higher-timeframe (15/30/60min, daily) chart points of significance matter, but it's not worth watching them directly while trading the 5-minute chart — the 5-minute pullbacks tend to naturally end at those higher-timeframe points anyway, and watching multiple charts is a distraction that causes missed 5-minute signals.
market-structure.quote: Although it is not worth the effort to pay attention to higher timeframe charts when trading on a 5-minute chart, it is likely that the larger 5-minute pullbacks end at 15, 30, or 60-minute... chart points of significance

market-structure.teaching: Double top/bottom flags are structurally reversal-looking patterns but function as With Trend continuation setups — by ending the pullback they 'reverse' it, but the net effect is trend continuation. In a trend, most reversal patterns fail and most continuation patterns succeed.
market-structure.quote: In a trend, most reversal patterns fail and most continuation patterns succeed.

market-structure.teaching: Recognizing any one of several overlapping/coexisting chart patterns (e.g., Double Bottom, Spike and Trading Range Reversal) is reason enough to take the trade — you don't need to correctly label which pattern it 'really' is.
market-structure.quote: Many patterns resemble one another and often coexist. All you need to do is recognize one of the patterns because by itself, it is a valid entry.

market-structure.teaching: A 'gap' in Brooks' broader usage includes any bar failing to reach the EMA (e.g., a bull bar's high remaining below the EMA); in trending/sideways markets these EMA gaps tend to fill, and a second attempt to fill an unfilled gap (EMA Gap 2 Bar) has excellent odds.
market-structure.quote: if the high of a bar is below the EMA, then there is a gap between that bar and the EMA... there is a good chance that the market will move to fill that gap

market-structure.teaching: After a climactic/parabolic move devolves into a Tight Trading Range, risk/reward is poor — better to trade elsewhere until a breakout with strong trend bars occurs, then look for a failed breakout or Breakout Pullback.
market-structure.quote: It is best to look to trade other stocks after a climax devolves into a Tight Trading Range, because the risk reward ratio is poor.

market-structure.teaching: After a climactic move, don't take With Trend EMA pullback entries unless the pullback has been long (roughly an hour or more) and the setup is strong — an unusually long, undisrupted run (e.g., 20 bull bars with only 1 pullback bar) is itself a climax and unsustainable.
market-structure.quote: unusual behavior is not sustainable and therefore a form of a climax... traders should not look to enter With Trend on an EMA pullback unless the pullback has been long

market-structure.teaching: Corrections don't always have exactly two legs — sometimes four (or three, as in Wedges); the underlying principle (traps, failed reversal attempts) still applies regardless of the exact count.
market-structure.quote: Corrections Sometimes Have Four Legs and Not Two

market-structure.teaching: A Wedge pullback typically has three pushes but functionally behaves as a two-legged pullback, with the second and third pushes being subdivisions of the second leg.
market-structure.quote: A Wedge Has Three Pushes But Is Often Two Legs

market-structure.teaching: Complex, hard-to-count corrections on the 5-minute chart often simplify into an obvious High/Low 1 or 2 on a higher timeframe chart, but it's not worth switching timeframes to check — trade what's evident on the 5-minute chart and avoid missing signals.
market-structure.quote: complex corrections on the 5-minute chart often appear as simple High/Low 1 or 2 corrections on higher timeframe charts. It is not worth looking at the higher timeframe charts

market-structure.teaching: When the market is clearly trending strongly in one direction, don't bother looking to fade with Low 1/Low 2 (in a bull) or High 1/High 2 (in a bear) setups against the dominant trend — only trade the with-trend counts.
market-structure.quote: Although many are labeled, when the market is clearly trending up, you should not be looking to sell most Low 1 and Low 2 setups.

market-structure.teaching: Three-legged corrections in trends are common and are often really just two legs where the second leg itself contains two smaller legs; when leg-counting gets too hard, stop analyzing and focus on trend direction and price relative to the EMA.
market-structure.quote: When it gets this hard to think about it, stop thinking about anything other than the trend and the market's inability to get much above the EMA.

market-structure.teaching: Trading ranges are generally continuation patterns — they more often break out in the direction of the trend that preceded them, and tend to break out away from the EMA (below EMA -> break down; above EMA -> break up), especially when adjacent to the EMA.
market-structure.quote: In general, all trading ranges are continuation patterns, meaning that they more often than not break out in the direction of the trend that preceded them. They also tend to break out away from the EMA.

market-structure.teaching: The longer a trading range persists, the greater the odds it becomes a reversal pattern (accumulation in a bear range, distribution in a bull range) rather than a simple continuation.
market-structure.quote: the longer a trading range continues, the greater are the odds that it will become a reversal pattern (accumulation in a bear trading range, distribution in a bull trading range)

market-structure.teaching: Weak-hand traders (who barely move the market on a small breakout attempt) are distinct from institutional traders (whose entries are followed by large, decisive trend bars); institutional conviction is what actually drives sustained moves.
market-structure.quote: The Bar 5 longs were institutional traders who bought with conviction, as evidenced by the large bull trend bars that followed.

market-structure.teaching: Two legs commonly lead to a reversal, and breaking yesterday's low/high while forming the second leg increases the chance of a reversal.
market-structure.quote: it is also the second leg down, and two legs commonly lead to a reversal. Also, it took out yesterday's low, so again there is an increased chance of a reversal upward.

market-structure.teaching: A big up move fully retraced by a big down move (or vice versa) usually forms a trading range, but may instead be the start of a new trend; the presence/absence of a clear reversal pattern at the end of the second leg is the deciding factor, and lacking one, an approximate first target for continuation is twice the height of the first leg.
market-structure.quote: If there is a reversal pattern at the end of the second move...there should be at least a reversal scalp. If not, the second leg may just keep trending, and an approximate first target is twice the height of the first leg.

market-structure.teaching: Spike-and-Channel moves, no matter how strong, are usually followed by a test of the start of the channel.
market-structure.quote: Spike and Channel bulls, no matter how strong they appear, are usually followed by a test of the beginning of the channel.

market-structure.teaching: Trend reversals commonly form out of the FINAL trading range of a Trending Trading Range day, in the last hour or two; reversal of the entire day is rare. Reversal-type small ranges at bottoms tend to have larger bars/tails than at tops (though this asymmetry is not itself actionable).
market-structure.quote: These are common in Trending Trading Range days, where there usually is a reversal of at least the final trading range in the final hour or two and rarely even a reversal of the entire day.

market-structure.teaching: A pair of big opposite-direction range bars, or one/more big-range dojis, at an extreme of the day signals two-sided trading; it is not prudent to buy the high of day or sell the low of day in that context — prefer to wait for a trap/small-bar setup.
market-structure.quote: when there are a pair of big range bars of opposite direction or one or more big range dojis at an extreme of the day, it is usually better to look for a reversal entry because...when there is two-sided trading, it is not prudent to be buying at the high of the day or selling at the low.

market-structure.teaching: If there was already a strong move/first leg earlier in the day, a later With Trend breakout is more likely to succeed and failures will likely fail-again into a Breakout Pullback; if the day has been mostly trendless with brief two-way breakouts, breakout failures are more likely to become genuine reversals.
market-structure.quote: If there was a strong move or a first leg of a trend earlier in the day, then a With Trend breakout later in the day is more likely to be successful...However, if most of the day has been trendless...the odds of a failure leading to a reversal are increased.

market-structure.teaching: Two strong trend legs (a completed Measured Move) can represent the end of a higher-timeframe correction rather than just the end of a single 5-min trend; expect at least a protracted countertrend move of two-plus legs (sometimes a full new opposite trend) that will likely retest the trading range at the middle of the Measured Move.
market-structure.quote: Two legs often complete a move and usually result in at least a protracted Countertrend move of at least two smaller legs, and sometimes a new, opposite trend.

market-structure.teaching: After a breakout, either a 'thin area' (no/minimal bar overlap from strong momentum) or a 'fat area' (small trading range) tends to form; the midpoint of either is a rough guide to the midpoint of the overall leg, usable for Measured Move projections.
market-structure.quote: A breakout often forms either a thin area...or a fat area...The midpoint of both often becomes the midpoint of the move.

market-structure.teaching: Protective stops clustered just beyond huge trend bars (small tails) are a common magnet — price commonly works its way to run those stops before reversing back in the trend's direction.
market-structure.quote: It is fairly common for the market to work its way to those stops and then reverse back in the direction of the trend bar.

market-structure.teaching: The single most important rule: never even think about trading against a trend until after a significant trendline has broken; even then, look for With-Trend trades first because the market usually goes back to test the old trend extreme before truly reversing.
market-structure.quote: The single most important rule in this book is that you should never even be thinking about trading against a trend until after there has been a break of a significant trendline.

market-structure.teaching: Whenever the market tries twice to do something and fails both times, it usually then tries the opposite (basis for trading the second failed test of an extreme).
market-structure.quote: whenever the market tries twice to do something and fails, it usually tries the opposite.

market-structure.teaching: A 'reversal' in practical trading terms need not meet strict technician criteria (a series of confirmed HH/HL or LH/LL); a trader believes a reversal has occurred as soon as they are willing to trade against the prior trend, e.g., buying in a bear believing price won't trade a tick lower.
market-structure.quote: Once a trader is initiating trades in the opposite direction to the trend, that trader believes that the trend has reversed even thought the strict criteria have not yet been met.

market-structure.teaching: Two technical requirements for a trend reversal: (1) absolute requirement — break of the prior trend's trendline; (2) usual (not required) — a successful test of the old trend's extreme after the break.
market-structure.quote: Most would agree on two requirements for a trend reversal. The first is an absolute requirement: the move has to break a trendline from the prior trend. The second requirement happens most of the time but is not required: after the trendline break, the market comes back and successfully tests the extreme of the old trend.

market-structure.teaching: Most trends end with a breakout of the trend channel line, either via an overshoot-then-reversal (typically followed by two legs down/up with the first pullback forming a Lower High/Higher Low), or by falling straight through the trendline without overshooting first (test can be Higher High or Lower High, occurring about equally often).
market-structure.quote: Either there is an overshoot of the bull trend channel line that fails and is followed by a reversal down and then a break below the bull trendline, or the market can fall through the bull trendline without first overshooting the trend channel line.

market-structure.teaching: Trends last much longer than most traders imagine; as a result most reversal patterns fail (some even become continuation setups) while most continuation patterns succeed.
market-structure.quote: An important point is that trends last much longer than most traders would ever imagine. Because of that, most reversal patterns fail (and some become continuation setups), and most continuation patterns succeed.

market-structure.teaching: A large share of extreme-tick volume in modern markets is computer/algorithm driven; some models keep buying right to the high tick or shorting to the very low tick because that maximizes their own profit equation (e.g., delta-neutral options spreads), not because they're 'wrong' about direction.
market-structure.quote: A large percentage and possibly the majority of the day's volume is driven by mathematical models, and some of those models will continue to buy until there is a clear trend change...

market-structure.teaching: Typically, entries in trend pullbacks look bad but are profitable, while entries in reversals look good but are losers; countertrend reversal trades should only be taken if the setup is clear and strong.
market-structure.quote: Typically, entries in trend pullbacks look bad but are profitable, and entries in reversals look good but are losers...you should never take a reversal trade unless it is clear and strong.

market-structure.teaching: Major reversals off strong bear markets are often volatile with large bars and multiple false starts ('Oops, I'm too early'), which is why many major reversals end with large-range bars and either a Failed Flag or a Three Push pattern.
market-structure.quote: This can happen several times before the final bottom is in and accounts for why so many major reversal end with large range bars and either a Failed Flag or a Three Push pattern.

market-structure.teaching: Signs of strength of a trendline break (first leg of a possible reversal): move covers many points; goes well past the EMA; extends beyond the last swing point of the old trend; lasts 10-20+ bars; occurs after prior strong trendline breaks; the test-back to the old extreme lacks momentum/has overlapping bars; the test fails at the EMA/old trendline without reaching the extreme.
market-structure.quote: Here are many characteristics of strong reversals: Strong reversal bar. Second entry signal. Trap Bar...Began as a reversal from an overshoot of a trend channel line...

market-structure.teaching: A test of a prior trend's extreme after a trendline break can overshoot (start of the FIRST leg of the new trend, e.g. a Lower Low in a new bull) or undershoot (start of the SECOND leg, e.g. a Higher Low in a new bull); this distinction tells you which leg count you're in.
market-structure.quote: Since a Countertrend move after a trendline break usually has at least two legs, if a bull reversal has a Higher Low for the test of the bear low, it will also be the start of the second leg...if it forms a Lower Low for the test, that low will be the start of the first leg...

market-structure.teaching: Most sharp momentum spikes get tested eventually even after deep, lengthy retracements — especially if the spike is in a trading range or in the direction of the larger trend; a spike that is the FIRST countertrend move within a strong trend usually does NOT get tested (it just traps the countertrend traders).
market-structure.quote: the extremes of most of those sharp momentum spikes that have deep, lengthy retracements still end up being tested, and most are exceeded...it is not likely if it is the first Countertrend move in a strong trend.

market-structure.teaching: Climax reversals (trend channel line overshoot/reversal, climactic Failed Final Flag) don't require a pullback test of the extreme; climaxes are far more common on smaller timeframes (several per day on a 1-minute chart vs. a couple per month on 5-minute).
market-structure.quote: Climaxes are much more common on smaller time frame charts. For example, a 1-minute chart often has several each day, whereas a 5-minute chart might only have a couple each month.

market-structure.teaching: Named chart patterns (Head and Shoulders, Triple Bottom/Top with strict tick-identical definitions, Measured Moves) are of minor reliability/importance; bar-by-bar price action close to the entry location deserves far more attention than the named pattern itself, and loose/approximate versions of reliable patterns still tend to trade like the reliable pattern.
market-structure.quote: Also, most Head and Shoulder patterns fail and are not nearly as reliable as the bar-by-bar price action...Traders will make much more money by using loose definitions. When something resembles a reliable pattern, it will likely trade like the reliable pattern.

market-structure.teaching: Measured Moves are minor and not terribly reliable and should generally be ignored as a primary trading signal; only worth considering as a minor confirming factor once a strong trend reaches that area AND there's still a good independent setup.
market-structure.quote: Measured Moves are of minor importance and not terribly reliable and in general should be ignored.

market-structure.teaching: In a Trending Trading Range Day, don't short near the bottom of the range (or buy near the top) unless there is a genuinely strong reversal setup — a weak Double Top/Bottom Pullback that doesn't retrace close enough to the pattern is unreliable there.
market-structure.quote: it is not wise to short near the bottom of a trading range unless there is a strong setup, and this is not.

market-structure.teaching: Any breakout beyond a swing high or low — even one that belongs to an earlier, opposite trend — is a sign of strength and a potential trade setup worth noting.
market-structure.quote: Breaking out beyond any swing high or low, even if it was part of an earlier and opposite trend, is a sign of strength and a potential trade setup.

market-structure.teaching: An Expanding Triangle traps traders on both sides progressively (each swing larger than the last) until all traders capitulate to the same side and a trend begins; after completing, it often reverses into an Expanding Triangle flag (continuation) in the opposite direction.
market-structure.quote: The salient point is that it is a series of progressively greater Higher Highs and Lower Lows that continue to trap breakout traders, and at some point they capitulate, and then all of the traders are on the same side, creating a trend.

market-structure.teaching: A trendline break signals a possible trend reversal; a trend-channel-line break signals the trend has gotten stronger/steeper — the two breaks have opposite implications and should be traded oppositely.
market-structure.quote: The break of a trendline means that a possible trend reversal is underway, but breaking a trend channel line means that the trend has increased in strength and is now steeper.

market-structure.teaching: If a trade only reaches a scalper's profit before reversing (rather than being stopped out), there is far less fuel for a countertrend move, because far fewer traders are truly trapped and many scalpers already exited at breakeven with tighter stops.
market-structure.quote: If a trade makes a scalper's profit...and then comes back and takes out the original signal or entry bar stops, there is much less fuel left in the market to drive the market in this new direction because there are far fewer traders who are trapped.

market-structure.teaching: The single most reliable minor reversal is one at the end of a pullback in a strong trend, especially near the EMA, since you enter with the larger trend and can expect at least a test of the prior trend extreme.
market-structure.quote: The single most reliable minor reversal is one that takes place at the end of a pullback in a strong trend, especially near the EMA, because you are then entering in the direction of a larger trend.

market-structure.teaching: One-tick (or a few tick) failed breakouts are extremely common, especially in the Eminis, because traders place protective stops one tick beyond signal/entry bars; when hit, the resulting move is usually strong and big enough for at least a scalper's profit in the opposite direction, since only one side (the trapped side) remains active.
market-structure.quote: A One-Tick Failure is a reliable sign that the market is going the other way, so look for setups that allow you to enter.

market-structure.teaching: Second tests of the EMA should normally be closer to or penetrate the EMA more than the first test; when a reversal begins before the pullback reaches the EMA, the setup is missing the fuel that a proper EMA-touching pullback would have provided.
market-structure.quote: Normally, second tests of the EMA are closer to the EMA or penetrate it more than the first...When the reversal begins before this happens, it will be missing the fuel that those shorts would have provided.

market-structure.teaching: On a small, sideways, range-bound day, betting on any single breakout becoming a trend is a low-probability bet; better to fade breakouts or trade strong Breakout Pullbacks instead.
market-structure.quote: This is always a low-probability bet on a small day, and it is much better to fade the breakouts or look for strong Breakout Pullbacks.

market-structure.teaching: A horizontal trading range/flag is a magnet — it represents balance between buyers and sellers, so any breakout tends to get pulled back to re-test that balance area before a real trend resumes.
market-structure.quote: A horizontal area is an area of two-sided trading where the buyers and sellers are evenly balanced, and therefore this area acts as a magnet that pulls each little move up or down back to the center or opposite side.

market-structure.teaching: Choose a market/instrument that can absorb your full desired size without slippage; the 5-minute ES/Emini is essentially unlimited in this regard, which is why it's the preferred instrument as size grows.
market-structure.quote: The 5-minute S&P Emini futures contract can handle any order size that an individual trader can place; you will never outgrow it.

market-structure.teaching: The 5-minute chart is the 'sweet spot' timeframe: enough trades (about a dozen a day), enough time to read and place orders, and a materially higher winning percentage than 1- or 3-minute charts, which tend to induce cherry-picking and missed high-quality trades.
market-structure.quote: The 5-minute is the sweet spot and it offers at least 10 trades a day and gives you time to think about the setups and to place your orders.

market-structure.teaching: Never trade Countertrend on the 1-minute chart — apparent 1-minute reversals against a strong 5-minute trend are usually just shorts/longs covering into the with-trend crowd's entries, and countertrend 1-minute trades lose money.
market-structure.quote: Trading Countertrend on the I-minute will cost you money. In fact, those three long setups are at exactly the price that the I-minute shorts would cover...

market-structure.teaching: Heavy bar overlap with the prior bar after a large reversal bar can indicate a trading range forming; don't buy at the top of that range — instead look for trapped traders, small bars, and second entries.
market-structure.quote: Bar 2 was a large bull reversal bar but whenever there is a lot of overlap with the prior bar, a trading range might be forming and you should never be looking to buy at the top of a range. Instead, look for trapped traders, small bars, and second entries.

market-structure.teaching: The market usually tries to test gaps, so traders should look for opportunities to trade toward the gap after a failed breakout.
market-structure.quote: a short on a failed breakout to a new high on a gap up day that could lead to a test of the gap (the market usually tries to test gaps so traders should look for opportunities to trade in that direction).

market-structure.teaching: Premarket/Globex extremes often get tested during the day session, but there is little benefit to watching Globex directly — day-session price action alone gives the same signals with less risk of confusion/mistakes.
market-structure.quote: Nothing was gained by watching both charts, and trying to do so increases the chances that you will not be quick enough to take all of the available trades and make mistakes in your order placement.

market-structure.teaching: An investor holds for fundamentals over months/years; a (position/swing) trader holds days off daily charts with partial profits and a breakeven stop; a day-trade scalper holds 1-15 minutes for one small leg with no tolerance for a pullback into a loss; a day-trade swing trader holds through pullbacks for 2-4 larger swings a day.
market-structure.quote: In the eyes of a trader or investor using daily to monthly time frames, all day trading is scalping.

market-structure.teaching: Entering on a stop is preferable because it means you are being carried into the trade by the market's own momentum, i.e. trading in the direction of at least a tiny trend.
market-structure.quote: One of the best ways to trade using price action is to enter on a stop because you are being carried into the trade by the market's momentum and therefore are trading in the direction of at least a tiny trend (at least one tick long).

market-structure.teaching: One-tick false breakouts in the first minute or two of a countertrend 5-minute entry bar are common and less reliable than breakouts occurring in the final minute of the bar, which carry momentum into the next bar.
market-structure.quote: Breakouts that occur in the final minute of the bar tend to be more reliable because you then have momentum right at the end of the bar.

market-structure.teaching: In a Tight Trading Range, do not enter in either direction until there is a large trend bar breaking out of the pattern by at least 3-4 ticks, then wait for that bar to fail — or fade a small bar right at the top/bottom of the range.
market-structure.quote: In a Tight Trading Range like this, you should never enter in either direction until after there is a large trend bar that breaks out of the pattern by at least 3 or 4 ticks, and then wait for that bar to fail, or unless there is a small bar that you can fade near the top or bottom of the trading range.

market-structure.teaching: Institutions defend their entries: price will often probe one tick shy of a heavily-populated stop level repeatedly rather than trigger it, because triggering it would change the market's character (turning a strong second entry into a failed one).
market-structure.quote: Everyone knows that there are many protective stops just one tick below that entry bar low . . . why isn't the smart money gunning for it? Because if those stops are hit, the character of the market will have changed.

market-structure.teaching: During the first hour, volatility is higher and patterns run further and reversals can look weak (two or three doji bars) before breaking out — don't scratch out of a correctly-read trade just because it looks weak initially.
market-structure.quote: It is common for reversals to look weak and have two or three doji bars before the trend breaks out. Realize that this is common, and if you entered and see this weakness, don't scratch out.

market-structure.teaching: Opening price is a brief equilibrium, not set deliberately by any firm; institutions with large orders to fill often prefer the market to move away from their desired price first (creating a false move) so they can fill aggressively at a better price once price reverses back toward value, which is one explanation for common Opening Reversals.
market-structure.quote: If the institutions have a large amount of buying planned for the day, why would the market ever open above yesterday's close and then selloff before rallying to a new high?

market-structure.teaching: The vast majority of opens relate to the final hour or so of the prior day's price action and take the form of a breakout, failed breakout, breakout pullback, or trend from the open — anticipate these setups before the open by reviewing yesterday's close.
market-structure.quote: The vast majority of opens are related to the price action of the final hour or so of the prior day and take the form of a breakout, a failed breakout, a breakout pullback, or a trend from the open, and any of these can move very fast.

market-structure.teaching: A two-legged countertrend move in the final hour or two of the prior day that breaks a major trendline commonly signals a trend reversal attempt on today's open (Higher Low or Lower Low).
market-structure.quote: A two-legged Countertrend move in the final hour or two that breaks a major trendline is a common pattern. It alerts you to look for a trend reversal on the open in the form of a Higher Low or a Lower Low.

market-structure.teaching: The EMA can be misleading right at the open when price opens far from it and then reverses strongly against the EMA's slope; price action should be trusted over the indicator in that situation.
market-structure.quote: Although the EMA is often helpful in the first hour, the price action is more important so do what the market is telling you and not what the indicator is saying.

market-structure.teaching: A trap of the crowd at the start of a strong trend (e.g., bulls trapped long right before a big up move) is common at trend starts and signals the trend will likely go far.
market-structure.quote: A trap like this is common at the start of a strong trend and is a sign that the trend will likely go far.

market-structure.teaching: After a strong move ends in Three Pushes and then sells off with less momentum (shallower slope, smaller bars, more tails), the high of the Three Pushes is likely to be exceeded shortly — it's probably a higher-time-frame Higher Low, not a real top.
market-structure.quote: the odds are high that the high of the Three Pushes will be exceeded shortly because this is likely a higher time frame Higher Low.

market-structure.teaching: A gap should conceptually be treated as one giant invisible trend bar for pattern-reading purposes (e.g., a gap up plus a channel rally = a Gap Spike and Channel Bull).
market-structure.quote: The gap should be looked at as if it were one huge, invisible trend bar.

market-structure.teaching: A Big-Up-Big-Down sequence (strong move one way fully reversed) usually ends in a trading range for the rest of the day.
market-structure.quote: Big Up (to Bar 7), Big Down usually ends in a trading range, as it did on this day.

market-structure.teaching: A market turning up from a failed Low 2 (or similarly a failed breakdown) often rallies for at least two more legs; since big gap days often become trend days, such a reversal can be the start of a large move.
market-structure.quote: A market that turns up from a Low 2 often rallies for at least two more legs, and since big gap days often become trend days, this could be, and was, the start of a big move.

market-structure.teaching: On strong trend days, countertrend Pullback Bars (Low 1, Low 2 against a bull, etc.) appearing right after entry indicate more bullish/bearish price action confirmation is still needed before the leg extends — traders should stay biased toward the dominant trend and largely ignore counter-trend buy/sell signals.
market-structure.quote: in strong bear trend days, it is often wise to ignore the buys to make sure that you catch the shorts.

market-structure.teaching: The third 5-minute bar of the day coincides with the close of the first 15-minute bar, so institutional traders watching 15-minute charts pay extra attention to it; don't trade it alone, but be aware of its added significance when other price action supports a trade there.
market-structure.quote: the first 15-minute bar closes at the close of the third 5-minute bar... trading the breakout of the third bar of the day often leads to a big move... Do not place a trade on the third bar alone.

market-structure.teaching: A strong trend off the open — even if later reversed — shows traders were willing to be aggressive in that direction and are likely to act aggressively again later in the day if a reversal falters.
market-structure.quote: A strong trend off the open, even if it is reversed, still means that there were traders willing to trade aggressively in that direction, and they will be quick to trade again in that direction later in the day if the reversal falters.

market-structure.teaching: A strong series of trend bars off the open followed by a partial pullback that gets retested 5-10 bars later forms a Double Top/Bottom Flag entry with a good chance of a new extreme and a trend.
market-structure.quote: If there is a series of strong trend bars off the open and then a partial pullback that gets tested 5 to 10 bars later... this forms a Double Bottom or Top Flag entry and has a good chance of leading to a new extreme on the day and often leads to a trend.

market-structure.teaching: Countertrend moves are often composed of exactly two legs; after a climactic move, expect a two-legged countertrend correction.
market-structure.quote: Countertrend moves are often made of two legs... after a climax... there is often a two-legged Countertrend move.

market-structure.teaching: In a strong trend, only the first pullback that actually breaks a meaningful trendline counts as 'the First Pullback'; minor sideways bars before that are still part of the first leg and should be traded With Trend, not treated as pullback/reversal risk.
market-structure.quote: Only after a trendline is broken has there been a significant pullback, and the first leg has likely ended... all the With Trend entries before then are simply part of the first leg of the trend.

market-structure.teaching: On very strong trend days with no clear pullback, it is reasonable to buy/sell full swing size at the market at almost any point, trusting the trend will make a new extreme before any pullback goes far — an unclear pullback means countertrend traders are weak.
market-structure.quote: on strong days like this you can just buy your swing size position at the market at any point... an unclear pullback means the Countertrend traders are very weak.

market-structure.teaching: Price action's underlying 'fingerprint' (the patterns and behaviors) has remained essentially unchanged across history and markets — the same setups appear on a 1933-34 Dow daily chart as on modern intraday charts.
market-structure.quote: Price action is the cumulative result of a large number of traders acting independently for countless reasons to make as much money as they can. Because of that, its fingerprint has remained unchanged.

market-structure.teaching: Two consecutive failed attempts by one side to push through a level is a strong signal that a big move in the opposite direction is coming.
market-structure.quote: When two attempts at something fail, expect a big move in the opposite direction.

market-structure.teaching: When a market has been so strong that it fails to come back and test the expected retracement levels of several prior patterns, the eventual correction, when it finally comes, tends to be much deeper and longer-lasting than anyone anticipates.
market-structure.quote: Whenever the market has so much strength that it does not come back to test the expected prices for several patterns as it did here... when the correction finally comes, it will usually last longer and be much deeper than anyone anticipates.

market-structure.teaching: Corrections that follow a channel move usually lead to a trading range rather than an immediate resumption or sharp reversal.
market-structure.quote: Corrections after channels usually lead to trading ranges, and since this is a weekly chart, we may go largely sideways for several more years.

market-structure.teaching: The term 'Head and Shoulders' should be avoided because most H&S patterns fail (become continuation, not reversal) while most Double Bottom/Top Pullbacks — which look similar — succeed; conflating the two terms hides the distinction that matters.
market-structure.quote: Most Double Bottom Pullbacks are Head and Shoulders Bottoms, but since most Head and Shoulder patterns fail... and most Double Bottom Pullbacks succeed, you should distinguish them and never bother with the term 'Head and Shoulders.'

market-structure.teaching: Volume should almost never be used on intraday (5-minute) charts because it has unreliable predictive value there; the 5-minute chart is the one you should trade exclusively.
market-structure.quote: You should rarely if ever look at volume on intraday charts because of its unreliable predictive value on the 5-minute chart, which is the chart that you should be using exclusively for trading.

market-structure.teaching: Strong trending conditions occur only about 20% of the time or less; most markets/most of the time are non-trending, which is why trend pullback setups (High2/Low2 at the EMA) are so valuable when present.
market-structure.quote: Most markets are in a strong trend maybe 20 percent of the time or less.

market-structure.teaching: Always be alert to buy the first Higher Low that forms after a possible major Lower Low (and symmetrically, short the first Lower High after a possible major Higher High) — these are the highest-value early confirmation entries of a reversal.
market-structure.quote: You always need to be looking to buy that first Higher Low after a possible major Lower Low.

market-structure.teaching: Always look to short the first Lower High that follows a Higher High which could be marking a major reversal (mirror-image extension of the 'first Higher Low' rule).
market-structure.quote: You always must look to short the first Lower High after a Higher High that could be a major reversal.

market-structure.teaching: Identify trend days as early as the first couple of hours (often obvious by the third bar); once identified, stop looking for countertrend entries and trade only with-trend for the rest of the day.
market-structure.quote: Every day, you should be examining the chart throughout the day and especially in the first couple of hours to determine if the day is a trend day... If it is, you should not be trading Countertrend.

market-structure.teaching: Every type of first pullback in a trend is usually followed by a test of the trend's extreme (the day's high or low); expect this test and don't be shaken out by it.
market-structure.quote: Every type of first pullback is usually followed by a test of the trend's extreme (here, the low of the day).

market-structure.teaching: When the market attempts something twice and fails both times, it is likely to do the opposite next.
market-structure.quote: When two attempts at anything fail, it is likely that the market will do the opposite (here, rally). It is also a Wedge.

market-structure.teaching: Every single bar is a potential signal bar in both directions; stay open to trend beginning on the very next bar without denial or hesitation when a surprise occurs.
market-structure.quote: Every bar is a signal bar for both directions and the market can begin a trend up or down on the next bar... when the surprise happens, don't question or deny it. Just read it and trade it.

market-structure.teaching: Beginners often trade too large a position when even a couple of winning trades feel like a small win — sizing to smaller instruments (e.g., SPY instead of Emini) can improve psychology and confidence while learning.
market-structure.quote: If you find that you did not take a couple Emini trades in a row and they worked, you are likely trading too large a position size.

market-structure.teaching: Buy low / sell high is the default rule EXCEPT in a clear, strong, established trend; you must be able to convincingly classify the day as one of the book's established trend patterns before buying high or selling low, otherwise assume trading-range behavior and fade the extremes instead.
market-structure.quote: Buy low, sell high, except in a clear and strong trend... However, the market is in a trading range for the vast majority of the time... if you cannot convince yourself that it is, don't buy high, even if the momentum looks great, since the odds are great that you will be trapped.

market-structure.teaching: Almost every chart segment can be classified under more than one named pattern simultaneously and the patterns typically point the same direction — you only need to recognize one of the overlapping labels to justify a trade.
market-structure.quote: Every segment of every chart commonly can be classified as more than one pattern, and almost always the patterns will point in the same direction. All you have to see is just one to place a trade.

market-structure.teaching: Good with-trend pullback entries deliberately look like terrible entries (following a mini-climax) in order to trap traders out of taking them, while genuine reversal setups look attractively clean specifically to trap countertrend traders in.
market-structure.quote: Trends are always forming pullbacks that look like terrible entries but are profitable and reversals that look good but are losers.

market-structure.teaching: The first 90 minutes is the easiest window to make money, with failed breakouts and breakout pullbacks of yesterday's patterns being especially easy to spot; beginners should avoid the middle of the day and the middle of the day's range.
market-structure.quote: The easiest time to make money is in the first 90 minutes, and some of the easiest trades to spot are failed breakouts and breakout pullbacks of patterns from the prior day. Beginners should avoid trading in the middle of the day and in the middle of the day's range.

market-structure.teaching: The best signal bars are trend bars in the direction of the trade; doji bars (one-bar trading ranges) are terrible signal bars, and buying above/selling below a doji against the trend tends to lose.
market-structure.quote: The best signal bars are trend bars in the direction of your trade. Doji bars are one bar trading ranges and therefore terrible signal bars. You will lose if you buy above a trading range in a bear or sell below one in a bull.

market-structure.teaching: Always look for two-legged moves; when the market fails twice at the same thing, expect success in the opposite direction on the next attempt.
market-structure.quote: Always look for two legs. Also, when the market tries to do something twice and fails both times, that is a reliable signal that it will likely succeed in doing the opposite.

market-structure.teaching: A '2HM' (market hasn't touched the EMA for two-plus hours, not necessarily the first two hours of the day) is a sign of a strong trend.
market-structure.quote: 2HM: The market has not touched the EMA for two or more hours. It is a sign of a strong trend, and it does not have to be the first two hours of the day.

market-structure.teaching: A climax is a move that has gone too far too fast and reverses into either a trading range or an opposite trend, most often ending with a trend-channel-line overshoot.
market-structure.quote: climax: A move that has gone too far too fast and has now reversed directions to either a trading range or an opposite trend. Most climaxes end with trend channel overshoots and reversals.

market-structure.teaching: A trading range is a pullback on a higher time frame chart and will usually ultimately break out in the direction of the (higher-timeframe) trend, even though on the current chart it looks like neither side is in control.
market-structure.quote: trading range ... It will usually ultimately break out in the direction of the trend, and is a pullback on a higher time frame chart.

market-structure.teaching: Every price-action pattern is a fractal: any pattern seen on the current chart is a micro version of the same pattern on a higher time frame, and every standard pattern is itself a micro pattern of a still-higher time frame — patterns repeat across all time frames.
market-structure.quote: fractal Every pattern is a fractal of a pattern on a higher time frame chart. This means that every pattern is a micro pattern on a higher time frame and every micro pattern is a standard pattern on a smaller time frame.

market-structure.teaching: Traditional gaps (breakout, measuring, exhaustion) seen on daily charts have intraday equivalents in the form of moving-average gaps — a bar failing to touch the EMA is functionally the same signal as a daily chart gap.
