psychology.quote: Reading these 570,000 words is like reading a detailed manual on how to do anything, such as playing golf or a violin. It takes a lot of hard work to turn the information into the ability to make a living as a trader.

psychology.teaching: Every bar, even a strong trend bar, is simultaneously a potential signal bar for a reversal in either direction on the next bar — stay open to the exact opposite of what you expect, and when a surprise happens, don't question or deny it, just read and trade it.
psychology.quote: Every bar, even a strong trend bar, is a signal bar for both directions, and the market can begin a trend up or down on the next bar. Be open to all possibilities, including the exact opposite of what you expect, and when the surprise happens, don't question or deny it. Just read it and trade it.

psychology.teaching: If you are positive a setup is good, don't take the trade — overconfidence means you're using unrealistically high probabilities and missing what the other side of the trade sees.
psychology.quote: Whenever you are positive that your setup is good, don't take the trade. You are missing something.

psychology.teaching: Feeling twisted inside because a pullback seems to be going too far usually means the trend has actually reversed, not that it's just a deep pullback.
psychology.quote: If ever you feel twisted inside because a pullback is going too far, you are likely mistakenly seeing a pullback when in fact the trend has reversed.

psychology.teaching: The market is not fair, has no concept of fairness, and is driven by relentlessly objective computer programs; a trader's job is to copy what the big/institutional orders are doing since they will keep entering after you and drive price to your profit.
psychology.quote: Computer programs control all market activity, and they have no concept of fairness... your job is to see what they are doing and then copy them.

psychology.teaching: Losers look for an external savior (gurus, TV experts, chat rooms, indicators, newsletters) to protect them from losses; none of these people are aligned with your interests, and you won't make money until you rely on your own analysis.
psychology.quote: Those who talk don't know and those who know don't talk. Don't watch TV or read any news.

psychology.teaching: Adopt an 'I don't care' mantra — trade small enough that a loss won't cloud your judgment, and ignore all outside noise (TV, other timeframes, indicators) in favor of the one chart in front of you and your own rules.
psychology.quote: "I don't care!" That is the most useful mantra.

psychology.teaching: Your goal is to make money, not to make trades; take only trades likely to make money and don't be upset about missing good ones — many great traders find trading lonely and boring but profitable.
psychology.quote: You don't have to trade. You goal as a trader is to make money, not to make trades.

psychology.teaching: Fear and greed are the dominant feelings for beginners and media; profitable traders instead feel uncertainty/confusion and urgency and use both to their advantage — certainty signals a strong trend, urgency/desperation for a pullback also signals a strong trend (entering a small position at market rather than waiting).
psychology.quote: The two most important feelings for the media and for beginners are fear and greed. Profitable traders feel neither.

psychology.teaching: If down on the day in the second half, don't modify your trading style or increase size/take lower-probability setups — stick to your rules into the close and you'll likely win back some losses; changing approach only costs more.
psychology.quote: The worst thing to do is to modify your trading style, which is probably why you are down on the day.

psychology.teaching: You won't make money long-term until you understand your own personality well enough to find a compatible trading style that you can follow with minimal stress, uncertainty, or anxiety.
psychology.quote: If you feel stress while trading, then you haven't yet found either your style or yourself.

psychology.teaching: You're competing against computers that have the edge of speed (avoid trading during news reports), lack of emotion (don't trade upset/distracted), and never getting tired (don't trade when worn out, e.g. end of day).
psychology.quote: You are competing against computers... it is usually best not to trade during a report.

psychology.teaching: After a loss on a stock, don't keep trying to make your money back on that same stock; the need to prove you were right is a sign of emotional weakness. Great traders accept losses and move on.
psychology.quote: There is a natural tendency to want to make back your money on the same stock after a loss, but this is a sign of your emotional weakness... Great traders accept their losses and move on

psychology.teaching: When a strong trend forms large trend bars after a long spike (10-20 bars), there is great risk of a protracted two-legged pullback, because strong with-trend traders only buy a significant pullback and strong countertrend traders enter at the market and scale in.
psychology.quote: there is a great risk of a protracted two-legged pullback because most strong bulls would only buy a significant pullback and most strong bears would short at the market and scale in higher

psychology.teaching: The best way to make money is to do what the smart money is doing (which is too big to hide) and read the chart, not to listen to TV pundits. A spike and parabolic climax tells you the smart bulls and bears are betting on the pullback.
psychology.quote: The best way to make money trading is by doing what the smart money is doing and not listening to experts on television

management.teaching: Take partial profits at the first target, move stop to breakeven, and let a runner ride — some trades each week will run to 4x or more of the initial target.
management.quote: Move your stop to breakeven and then let the remainder run. You will likely have at least a couple of trades each week that run to four or more times your initial target before setting up a reverse entry pattern.

management.teaching: When afraid of the risk on a fast, high-probability spike entry, take a smaller position just to be in the trade, then add/move to breakeven after confirmation and hold into the close.
management.quote: If you are afraid, just short a very small position to be sure to be in the trade. Once you have another strong bear trend bar, like bar 14, move your stop either to breakeven or to above the high of that bar and hold into the close.

management.teaching: When price fails to hit a breakeven stop by even a single tick before resuming with-trend, that's a sign of underlying strength and a new extreme is likely to follow.
management.quote: Bar 9 also tested the long entry above the bull bar that followed bar 7, and it missed the breakeven stop by a tick. When the bulls are able to prevent their breakeven stop from being hit, they are strong and a new high usually follows.

management.teaching: On failed breakout setups where opposing stop orders are used (buy above/sell below a small inside bar), if the with-trend order fills first, generally do not flip to reverse on the protective stop; but if it's a strongly opposing (e.g., prior bear trend) day, reversing may be warranted.
management.quote: if the trader was first filled on the buy (with trend) order, he usually should not reverse on the protective stop, but might if the day had been a bear trend day.

management.teaching: After a strong bull trend bar off a higher low confirms the new swing, move the protective stop up to below that higher low and hold the position into the close rather than exiting early.
management.quote: Once the market turned up from the higher low with a strong bull trend bar, they could move their stop up to below the higher low and hold long into the close.

management.teaching: Risk sizing should scale with bar size: if you want constant dollar risk across trades, trade fewer contracts/shares when the current bars (and thus your stop distance) are larger.
management.quote: If you wanted to keep your risk the same on all of your trades, you would have to trade fewer shares.

management.teaching: When a setup is 'almost' a reliable pattern (e.g., almost an outside bar), it will likely still produce the outcome typical of that reliable pattern.
management.quote: in trading if something is almost a reliable pattern, it will likely yield the reliable result.

management.teaching: Wait for the 5-minute (or any time frame) bar to close before entering; entering early to save a tick or two risks getting trapped by a late reversal in the final seconds of the bar, and the math (needing ~8 early entries to work for every 1 that fails) doesn't favor early entry except in a very strong trend.
management.quote: That means that you need about eight early entries to work as planned for every one that does not, and that simply won’t happen.

management.teaching: Watch the strength of the close of the entry bar and the following bar(s): a body that grows strong into the close argues for holding/swinging more size; a weak close argues for scalping out more.
management.quote: If the entry bar is six ticks tall, you would much prefer seeing the body suddenly increase from a body that was two ticks to one that was four ticks in the final seconds of the bar. You will then likely reduce the number of contracts that you will scalp out.

management.teaching: When scaling into countertrend positions within a with-trend channel (e.g., shorts in a bull channel), do most of that scaling in the first two-thirds of the day; being heavily positioned against a strong channel late in the day with a distant breakeven point is a management trap.
management.quote: It is best to do this only in the first two-thirds of the day. You do not want to find yourself heavily short in a bull channel with your breakeven entry so far below that you don't have enough time left to get out without a loss.

management.teaching: Straight trend lines and trend channel lines consistently give more reliable setups and profitable trades than curved channels/moving-average bands, despite the popularity of the latter.
management.quote: Although the moving average often functions as support or resistance in a strong trend and many traders create curved channels and bands based on the moving average and many other factors, straight trend lines and trend channel lines consistently provide more reliable setups and profitable trades.

management.teaching: Entering below the low of a single bear reversal bar (rather than below the low of both bars in a two-bar reversal) carries extra risk of a one-tick bear trap where price pokes one tick lower and then resumes; entering below the low of both bars of the two-bar reversal reduces that risk.
management.quote: The problem with entering below the low of the bar 7 bear bar is that the market often has a one-tick bear trap. ... This risk is less if you enter below the low of both bars.

management.teaching: Best risk/reward for a trend trade is entering on the first pullback after a trend line break, before the trend is clearly established.
management.quote: The best risk/reward ratio occurs when you enter on the first pullback after a trend line break, before there is a clearly established trend.

management.teaching: Even in the strongest trend, a pullback eventually forms because experienced early buyers take partial/full profits at resistance levels (measured moves, trend lines, channel lines, opposite side of a trading range) — not because bulls stop wanting to buy dips.
management.quote: It is because not everyone is looking to buy. Experienced traders who bought early on look for price levels to take partial profits... They always take their profits at some resistance level.

management.teaching: In wide-range/high-volatility days, reduce position size (to half or less) and widen stops/targets accordingly to accommodate the bigger swings.
management.quote: Many of the bars today had a range of over 6 to 8 points. It would be prudent to reduce your position size to half or less, and increase your stop to 4 points and your profit target to 2 points.

management.teaching: Trail your protective stop to just below the most recent swing low (bull) / above the most recent swing high (bear) as new trend extremes form; consider partial profits on new highs/lows.
management.quote: every time the market reaches a new high, traders should raise their protective stop to one tick below the most recent low. This is called trailing their stop.

management.teaching: After a pullback tests the original entry and the market makes a new extreme, most traders raise stops to breakeven or below the pullback low because they don't want a second test of entry.
management.quote: Once the market tests the original entry price and then goes to a new high, most traders would then raise their stops to at least their entry price.

management.teaching: On strong trend days, use a generous profit target and do not take any profit until price reaches at least twice your risk; tighten the stop as new confirming trend bars form to reduce dollar risk toward near-zero.
management.quote: On a strong trend day like this, it was far better to use a generous proﬁt target. Traders should not have tried to take any proﬁt until the market went to at least twice their risk.

management.teaching: When holding a large profit late in the day, it's often wise to exit ahead of any setup that could produce a larger pullback, then look to re-enter after a two-legged pullback completes.
management.quote: When you have a large proﬁt, it is usually wise to exit in the ﬁnal hour or so on any setup that could lead to a larger pullback and then maybe look to get long again once that two-legged pullback is complete.

management.teaching: A tight channel should only be traded in the with-trend direction because pullbacks won't extend far enough for a profitable countertrend trade; only channels with broad swings are tradable both ways, and even then trading countertrend prematurely against a channel is costly since channels can last far longer than expected.
management.quote: if the channel is fairly tight, it is tradable only in the with-trend direction because the pullbacks will not go far enough for a countertrend trade to be proﬁtable... it is still a trend and it is very costly to trade countertrend prematurely.

management.teaching: Measured-move, trend-line, and trend-channel-line targets mostly fail, especially in very strong trends, but are still worth tracking as profit-taking areas rather than reversal-entry triggers; take a reversal trade only when the setup itself is strong.
management.quote: In general, it is far better to look at measured move targets as areas to take profits than as locations to take reversal trades. Traders should take a reversal trade only when the setup is strong.

management.teaching: In a strong, non-climaxed trend, experienced traders buy small size all the way up (not waiting for pullbacks) with far-away stops, keeping position size small because risk per unit is larger, adding as the market rises.
management.quote: Whenever the market is trending strongly and has not yet had a climax, experienced traders are buying small positions all of the way up... their risk is greater so their position size is smaller, but they keep adding to their position as the market rises.

management.teaching: When shorting/buying a high1 or high2 (etc.) after a sharp/climactic spike, expect the first pullback-based signal to fail and be prepared for at least a two-legged move before the setup that finally works; keep protective stops at the extreme until the market shows a second leg.
management.quote: After a climax, especially after a final flag reversal, the market usually has a two-legged correction, which means that a high 1 and maybe a high 2 would fail... keep your protective stop above the bar 5 high until after the market has begun its second leg.

management.teaching: On trending trading range days, don't trail protective stops below swing lows (in an uptrend context) since they'll usually get hit; instead prefer exiting on strength (into the forming upper range) rather than on weakness — 'buy low, sell high' inside the range framework.
management.quote: Traders should not trail their protective stops below a swing low once they think that the market is in a trending trading range, because those stops will usually get hit. It makes more sense to look to exit on strength.

management.teaching: When retracing a strong move, the market's first target is always the nearest earlier countertrend entry point (the signal-bar extreme of the last opposing setup).
management.quote: When a market retraces a strong move, the ﬁrst target is always the earlier countertrend entry points.

management.teaching: In a tight-channel strong trend, pullbacks often come back and hit breakeven stops, trapping traders out; stops should instead be trailed below/above swing lows/highs rather than moved hastily to breakeven.
management.quote: Traders need to trail their stops below swing lows in a bull trend or above swing highs in a bear trend. If they are too eager to move their stops to breakeven, they will get trapped out.

management.teaching: Entering on the first pullback after a strong initial leg exploits the tendency of strong moves to have at least two legs testing the extreme; it's a reliable way to catch a missed trend-from-open entry.
management.quote: Most strong moves have at least two legs, so entering on the ﬁrst pullback has a very good chance of leading to a proﬁtable trade.

management.teaching: In tight-channel trends, price will typically return to the entry price within 5-10 bars before reaching a new extreme; fearful/inexperienced traders often exit at breakeven right before the next leg, getting trapped out of a strong trend.
management.quote: if there is a strong bull trend, the market will often come back to the entry price at the signal bar high in ﬁve to 10 bars, and will often dip a tick or two below it...They cannot take the pain anymore and exit with a small proﬁt or loss.

management.teaching: Once a bull trend makes a new high, raise the protective stop to just below the most recent swing low (expecting trending lows); if the market instead enters a trading range where pullbacks fall below prior higher lows, switch to trading-range management.
management.quote: Trends tend to have trending highs and lows, so once a bull trend makes a new high, traders will raise their protective stops to just below the most recent swing low.

management.teaching: In strong trends made of small, sideways, overlapping bars, price-action stops (below recent bar lows) get hit too often; a fixed money stop (e.g., about two points) is usually the better choice to stay in the trade.
management.quote: With so many small, sideways bars, price-action stops below the lows of prior bars get hit too often and it makes more sense to rely on the original two-point stop.

management.teaching: In strong trend channels, swinging positions is more profitable than scalping because frequent with-trend pullbacks run stops on scalps; trail the stop beyond the prior swing point instead.
management.quote: As with most strong trend channels, you would make more by swinging than by scalping because there are frequent pullbacks that would run stops on even the with-trend scalps... It is better to trail your stop above the prior swing high.

management.teaching: When a genuine breakout spike is underway and the wide required stop scares you off, deliberately trade a smaller ('I don't care') position size rather than skipping the trade — the math (60-80% chance of a measured move) still favors taking it.
management.quote: They have to get their position size down to the 'I don't care' size so that they can quickly get in the trade. The best way for them to take a scary trade is to automatically trade only a third or a quarter of their normal trade size and use the wide stop that is required.

management.teaching: When a strong breakout's risk feels too large to take at full size, trade a reduced ('I don't care') quarter-size position just to participate and gain experience rather than skipping the trade entirely.
management.quote: If you have to, simply trade only a quarter-size position ("I don't care" size), just to get experience.

management.teaching: Always be quicker to take profits on an existing trade than to initiate a new trade in the opposite direction — a countertrend entry requires a stronger setup than simply exiting a with-trend position.
management.quote: You should always be faster to take profits on a trade than you should be to look for an entry in the opposite direction.

management.teaching: When deciding whether to hold a swing position after scalping part of a trade on a breakout bar that hasn't closed, ask whether you would enter at the market right now with a full swing-size position and the swing stop; if not, exit the swing portion at the market.
management.quote: One way to help decide if you should continue to hold your swing position is to ask yourself what you would do at this moment if you were not holding a position.

management.teaching: In a very strong trend, it is often better to buy/sell at the market (or on tiny 1-2 tick dips) than to wait for a clean pullback, because the pullback you're waiting for may never come at a good price, or may come much higher/lower than the current price.
management.quote: When the trend is strong, it is often better to enter at the market than to wait for a pullback.

management.teaching: On small/doji signal bars for high4/low4 setups, use a wider money-management stop (roughly 3 ticks beyond the bar, capped at about 8 ticks total risk on the ES) rather than a tight price-action stop, since these bars commonly get run by a tick before the real move.
management.quote: to avoid a whipsaw it is often best to place your protective stop at more than one tick beyond the signal bar (maybe three ticks, but no more than a total of eight ticks from your entry in the Emini when the average range is about 10 points)

management.teaching: A concrete stop-trailing/measured-move example: buying a bull spike with a fixed initial risk (~12 pts), moving to breakeven after a few bars, then trailing below each new higher low as the trend extends, captured ~50 points of a spike-and-channel bull run for 12 points of initial risk.
management.quote: If they held until the market reversed down at bar 17, they would have made about 50 points with an initial risk of 12 points, and a breakeven risk after the first few bars following their entry.

management.teaching: Fibonacci retracement levels are essentially meaningless as a standalone reason to trade because virtually any pullback can be labeled 'close enough' to some Fibonacci ratio after the fact.
management.quote: Fibonacci traders would call the rally to bar 10 a 61.8 percent pullback and say that it was close enough, but every pullback is close enough to some Fibonacci number, making the Fibonacci numbers essentially meaningless most of the time.

management.teaching: OCO bracket orders (fixed stop and target) plus stepping away from the screen can fix the tendency to exit winners too early at breakeven.
management.quote: you might try putting in a one cancels the other (OCO) bracket order as soon as you get long... Then go for a walk and come back in about an hour.

management.teaching: Scaling to breakeven after partial profits, and tightening stops progressively (signal-bar extreme → entry-bar extreme → breakeven after a 5-tick move), lowers the win-rate required for profitability.
management.quote: If you move your stop from the signal bar extreme to the entry bar extreme (one tick beyond both) after the entry bar closes, and then to breakeven after a five-tick move, this further reduces the required winning percentage

management.teaching: Look for where scalpers' stops cluster (second-entry levels) and consider adding to a position there if the pattern is still valid.
management.quote: Always look for where scalpers will have their stops, and look to add on to your position at that location, if the pattern is still valid.

management.teaching: To decide whether to hold a swing trade, imagine you are flat: would you initiate this exact trade right now with the current stop distance? If not, exit immediately — holding is financially identical to a fresh entry.
management.quote: imagine that you are flat and think about whether you should enter at the market with a swing-size position and a protective stop exactly where a swing trader who is in the market would have his stop. If you would not take that trade, then you should exit your swing position immediately.

management.teaching: When scaling into a countertrend position, exit or even reverse to the with-trend direction on the second adverse signal against you (e.g., a high 2 near the moving average while scaling into shorts in a bull trend) — don't keep averaging indefinitely.
management.quote: you should exit or reverse to the with-trend direction on the second move against you... exit or even reverse to long on a high 2, especially if it is near the moving average.

management.teaching: Protective stops should always be an actual working order in the market, never just a mental stop, because traders ignore mental stops exactly when they're needed most.
management.quote: It is better to have the stop working, because many traders who use mental stops find too many reasons to ignore them when they are needed most.

management.teaching: Two main stop types exist — money-management stops (fixed ticks/dollars) and price-action stops (beyond a bar/level); pick one primary method and use it consistently so stop placement isn't a distraction from the entry decision.
management.quote: In general, using one method most or all of the time is best because then it is such a part of your routine that you will always have a protective stop working.

management.teaching: The default initial stop is one tick beyond the signal bar; once the entry bar closes as a strong trend bar, tighten to one/two ticks beyond the entry bar; if the entry bar is a doji, keep the original wider stop since a doji is a one-bar trading range.
management.quote: The initial price action stop for most trades is one tick beyond the signal bar until the entry bar closes, when it is tightened to one or two ticks beyond the entry bar if the entry bar is strong. If the entry bar is a doji, then rely on your original stop.

management.teaching: In a strong trend, trail the protective stop to just below (bull) or above (bear) the most recent swing low/high once the market makes a new swing extreme; once the market looks like it's entering a trading range, stop trailing and take profits instead, since pullbacks will start breaking prior swing points.
management.quote: In a strong bull trend, traders often trail their protective stops to below the most recent swing low just after the market makes a new swing high. Once the market looks like it will enter a trading range, traders should take some profits.

management.teaching: If the market forces you to endure an N-tick adverse move before turning in your favor, the market will typically reward you with roughly N ticks of profit — use the observed max drawdown to set both the trailing stop and a realistic profit target.
management.quote: As a general rule, the market will rally enough to equal the size of the stop that it required you to have to stay in the trade... The market often rewards traders with as many ticks of profit as it forces them to risk.

management.teaching: When bars are unusually large, switch to a proportional money-management stop (e.g., about 60-70% of the signal bar's height, near the 62% Fibonacci retracement) rather than a full price-action stop, and reduce position size to keep dollar risk constant.
management.quote: If the bars at the time of entry into any trade are too large, it is wiser to use a money management stop, like eight ticks on the Emini 5 minute chart, or about a 70 percent pullback...The size of the money management stop is in proportion to the size of the bars.

management.teaching: Reversing on a stop-out can be a good strategy when the context supports a genuine reversal (e.g., failed low 2), but a stop run inside a tight trading range is not a reversal signal — read context before flipping direction.
management.quote: When a protective stop is hit before making a scalper's profit, you were trapped into a bad trade, so reversing on the stop is occasionally a good strategy. This depends on the context...However, a stop run in a tight trading range is not a reversal.

management.teaching: Pay close attention to the maximum adverse excursion (stop size needed) on the first trade of the day — it often becomes the appropriate stop size to use for the rest of the day, and if it's larger than usual you can also widen profit targets proportionally.
management.quote: pay close attention to the maximum size stop required in the first hour, because this often becomes the best stop to use for the rest of the day.

management.teaching: Entering late into a large trend-bar spike theoretically requires a very distant stop (below the whole spike); most traders instead scalp with a smaller position/stop, planning to re-enter a swing position on the pullback with a tighter stop.
management.quote: The theoretical stop for all of their entries is below the bottom of the spike, which is far away...most traders would consider scalping a larger position and using a smaller stop when they enter late in the spike.

management.teaching: When taking a 5-minute-chart entry, manage the stop based on the 5-minute chart, not a lower timeframe — relying on a 1-minute chart to tighten stops causes excessive premature stop-outs.
management.quote: Rely on your stop and ignore the 1 minute chart. When taking a 5 minute entry, rely on a 5 minute stop, else you will lose too often.

management.teaching: In a strong trend, take partial profits (e.g., half) once price has moved roughly 2x the initial stop distance, then trail the stop and scale out further portions at 3x, letting a small remainder run to a strong reversal signal or the close.
management.quote: If you believe that the trend is strong, it is reasonable to take about half of your position off after the market has gone your way for a distance equal to about twice the size of your original protective stop.

management.teaching: A profit target is the flip side of a protective stop: it should be an actual working limit order that locks in gains while the trader's equation is still favorable, rather than something held only in your head and given back.
management.quote: Remember, a profit target is the flip side of a protective stop and is there to protect you from yourself...it is also better to use a profit-taking limit order that is always in the market.

management.teaching: The least risky form of "scaling in" is really just taking a fresh independent signal while an existing position is still open and still valid — treat each entry as its own trade with its own stop/target rather than a true averaged position; complexity beyond 2-3 entries becomes unmanageable for most traders.
management.quote: If traders are determined to try to scale into a trade, the least risky approach is to take a second entry if one appears while they are still in their trade and if their premise remains valid...at some point their position becomes too complicated to be worth the effort.

management.teaching: Scalp trade management differs fundamentally from swing management: scalps assume limited profit potential (no trend, or trading against trend) and use OCO stop/target brackets; countertrend scalping should be reserved for only the most experienced traders.
management.quote: Scalping in a trading range can be a profitable strategy, but only the most experienced traders should consider trading against the trend.

management.teaching: On trades with strong follow-through potential, take partial profits at a target, move the stop to breakeven, and let a runner ride — some weekly trades run 4x or more the initial target before a reverse setup appears.
management.quote: Move your stop to breakeven and then let the remainder run. You will likely have at least a couple of trades each week that run to four or more times your initial target

management.teaching: Wait for the entry bar (and signal bar) to fully close before acting — entering early risks being caught by a last-second reversal; conversely, moving a stop up based on an unclosed bar can get you stopped out right before the market reverses in your favor. Discipline means trusting the initial stop and plan until the relevant bar closes.
management.quote: Good entry, good plan, bad discipline. You just let yourself get trapped out of a good trade.

management.teaching: Watch the strength of the close of the entry bar and the following bars: a strengthening close (bigger body into the close) argues for holding more contracts/points; weak closes argue for scaling out sooner.
management.quote: If there are strong closes, you should be more willing to swing more contracts and hold them for more points than if these bars had weak closes.

management.teaching: In a very strong trend all trades should be With Trend swings (scalping part off); Countertrend scalps should not be taken at all no matter how tempting.
management.quote: In a very strong trend, all trades should be With Trend swings (scalping out part), and no trades should be Countertrend scalps, as tempting as they are.

management.teaching: In strong/choppy trends with small bars, a fixed-size money stop (e.g., a two-point stop on the Emini) can work better than a price-action-based stop, which gets hit too often by noise.
management.quote: None of these pullbacks in Figure 3.6 would have hit a two-point money stop, which is usually the best stop to use in a strong trend, unless the average daily range is more than about 15 points.

management.teaching: In a runaway trend, prefer the 3-minute chart over the 1-minute chart for extra With Trend entries — the 1-minute has too many mixed Countertrend setups and reading speed demands that add stress and hurt execution; stick to 3- or 5-minute charts.
management.quote: Since you need to be making sure that you catch every With Trend entry, it is best to trade only off the 3- or 5-minute charts in a runaway trend.

management.teaching: If you find you missed a With Trend entry, stop hunting for Countertrend scalps and refocus entirely on With Trend setups — missed entries are a signal to re-anchor on the dominant trend, not chase alternatives.
management.quote: If you find that you missed a With Trend entry, stop looking for Countertrend scalps and start trading only With Trend setups.

management.teaching: During a suspected Failed Final Flag (sideways bars after a protracted trend), it's fine to take a setup near the flag's edge in the trend direction, but avoid chasing a breakout of the far edge — the opposite side may be willing to fade a new extreme (bear trap / bull trap risk).
management.quote: be careful buying a breakout of the high of the trading range because bears might be willing to sell a new high and bulls might be beginning to take profits at the high.

management.teaching: On a '2 Hour Mode' (2HM) day, once you recognize price has stayed off the EMA for 2+ hours, mentally prepare to fade all EMA touches with-trend, but wait for an actual price-action setup rather than blindly fading contact.
management.quote: Once you become aware that a 2HM is present, look to fade all touches of the EMA, but wait for a setup off the EMA, and enter on a stop.

management.teaching: When trading countertrend (against the prevailing trend), scalp most or all of the position unless the reversal shows exceptional strength.
management.quote: When trading countertrend, you should scalp most or all of your position unless the reversal is strong.

management.teaching: After a big bar, always look to fade small bars that follow it rather than trading in the big bar's direction, since a trading range is likely forming.
management.quote: always look to fade small bars that occur after big bars, since a trading range is likely forming and you should only be selling near the high or buying near low.

management.teaching: When you miss the earliest entry of a strong breakout, wait for at least two strong trend bars (or 3-4 moderate ones), then enter a 1- or 3-min pullback using half/swing size, using the same stop you'd have used entering earlier; scalp half and move stop to breakeven, then add on subsequent pullbacks.
management.quote: It is best to wait for at least two strong trend bars...or three or four moderate-size trend bars, and enter the 1- or 3-minute pullback using half of your normal size...Scalp out half, and then move your stop to breakeven.

management.teaching: Breakeven stops are not necessarily exact — they should be set based on the instrument's typical stop-running behavior (some stocks/instruments consistently run a few cents/ticks past breakeven before reversing; know your instrument's tendency).
management.quote: GS, on the other hand, routinely runs the stops before the pullback ends, so a trader would have to be willing to risk a little if he is trying to hold on to his position.

management.teaching: The more momentum/strength signs present in a reversal's first leg, the more reliable the reversal — ideally many bars, breaking well beyond the EMA, mostly trend bars in the new direction, extending beyond swing points of the prior trend.
management.quote: The more signs of strength, the more reliable is the reversal. Ideally, the first leg of the reversal will extend for many bars, break well beyond the EMA, have the majority of the bars be trend bars in the direction of the new trend...

management.teaching: When two legs are expected/likely in a move, always swing part of the position rather than taking a full scalp exit, because sometimes what looks like a two-legged pullback turns into a protracted new trend.
management.quote: Always swing part of your position when two legs are likely because sometimes there will be a protracted new trend rather than just a two-legged pullback.

management.teaching: When the extreme of a signal/entry bar is tested but the protective-stop level is not exceeded, the trade will often be profitable; if just missed by one tick on a long, it effectively forms a Double Bottom Bull Flag with the entry/signal bar low as the first bottom.
management.quote: If the extreme of the bar is tested but not exceeded, the stops are tested but not hit, and the trade will often be profitable.

management.teaching: Beginners should restrict countertrend fade entries to setups where the signal bar closes strongly in the fade direction — waiting for a strong opposite-close reversal bar improves odds materially.
management.quote: It is far more reliable if you wait for a signal bar with a strong bear close and beginners should restrict themselves to this type of fade setup.

management.teaching: On a clear Trend-from-the-Open day, restrict yourself to With-Trend setups (e.g., M2S or simple Low 2/High 2) only — this keeps win rate high and preserves psychological stamina/focus for taking entries.
management.quote: On a clear Bear Trend from the Open day, the best approach is to only trade With Trend and look to sell M2S setups or simple Low 2s. Your winning percentage will be high, allowing you to have a healthy attitude and continue to take entries.

management.teaching: When a stock/instrument consistently just misses its usual scalp profit target on two attempts, reduce the profit target for the current session (e.g., from $1 to 50 cents) rather than continuing to fight for the old target.
management.quote: With the market largely sideways and just missing a one-dollar scalp, a trader would likely reduce his profit target to about 50 cents.

management.teaching: Always swing at least part of a position with a breakeven stop because a move can run much farther than seems reasonable, especially around reports and on emotional days.
management.quote: In general, always swing part of the position with a breakeven stop because sometimes a swing will go much farther than you could ever imagine.

management.teaching: Neither pure scalping nor pure swing trading is superior; both yield roughly the same profit over time, and traders should pick the style that suits their personality (e.g., scalp Eminis, swing stocks).
management.quote: Neither way is superior and traders choose the method that best suits their personalities and both yield about the same profit.

management.teaching: If your limit profit-target order gets filled at the exact extreme without the market trading through it, that signals unusual trend pressure and the market is likely to continue beyond the target — look to re-enter on any pullback.
management.quote: If you were long and the market was willing to buy your position back from you...at the very highest tick of the leg, the buyers are aggressive and will likely reemerge on any pullback. Look for opportunities to get long again.

management.teaching: Initial protective stop is one tick beyond the signal bar; once the entry bar closes, tighten to one/two ticks beyond the entry bar. If bars are too large, use a fixed price/money stop (e.g., 8 ticks on the Emini) or ~60% of the signal bar's height as an alternative.
management.quote: The initial stop is one tick beyond the signal bar until the entry bar closes when it is tightened to one or two ticks beyond the entry bar. If the bars are too large, it is wiser to use a price stop, like eight ticks on the Emini 5-minute chart, or a 60 percent pullback.

management.teaching: Size the position inversely to bar size/stop size to keep dollar risk constant (e.g., reduce size 75% for an 8-point stop day so a 4-point target is still tradable at similar or better daily P&L).
management.quote: You need to reduce your position size by 75 percent to keep your risk the same, but if you follow basic price action principles, you could make the same or even more than on a typical day.

management.teaching: On uncertain-range days, trade one contract for the first trade or two to discover the stop size actually needed, then calibrate size/target for the rest of the day (and revert if volatility contracts again).
management.quote: If you are uncertain if today will be a huge range day and will require large stops, trade just one contract for the first one or two trades and see how large a stop would have been needed to keep you in the trade.

management.teaching: As a rule of thumb, the market tends to rally/fall roughly as far beyond entry as the size of the stop that was required to stay in the trade (e.g., an 11-tick required stop implies roughly a 12-tick move in your favor).
management.quote: AB a general rule, the market will rally enough to equal the size of the stop that it required you to have to stay in the trade.

management.teaching: When an entry bar is a doji, it's usually safe to allow a one-tick pullback beyond it before worrying, since a doji is a one-bar trading range and buying/covering back into it makes little sense.
management.quote: Also, when the entry bar is a doji, it is usually safe to allow a one tick pullback. A doji bar is a one bar trading range and it is foolish to buy above a trading range, so don't buy back your short there.

management.teaching: Manage stops on the timeframe you entered on — rely on a 5-minute stop for a 5-minute entry even if a lower timeframe shows the trade testing your stop, or you'll be stopped out of many otherwise-good trades.
management.quote: When taking a 5-minute entry, rely on a 5-minute stop, else you will lose too often and be stopped out of a great many trades in your attempt to risk less per trade.

management.teaching: The largest stop that would have been needed to hold a trade in the first hour is usually a good stop to use for the rest of the day.
management.quote: pay close attention to the maximum size stop required in the first hour because this often becomes the best stop to use for the rest of the day.

management.teaching: A protective-stop hit before reaching a scalper's profit means you were trapped; reversing on the stop can be a good strategy, but only when the context supports it (e.g., failed pullback in a trend) — a stop run inside a Tight Trading Range is not a reversal signal.
management.quote: When a protective stop is hit before making a scalper's profit, you were trapped, so reversing on the stop is occasionally a good strategy. This depends on the context.

management.teaching: When bars are unusually large (e.g., first hour, volatile days), quickly cut position size (half to two-thirds) rather than doing exact math — speed of decision matters more than precision.
management.quote: Just quickly cut the position size in half or two-thirds. It doesn't matter what you do; what matters is that you do it quickly, stop thinking about it, and concentrate on what is much more important: reading correctly and placing your orders.

management.teaching: Because the high or low of the day often forms in the first hour, swing part of any trade that could be a major extreme, using a breakeven stop to capture potential windfall profit.
management.quote: A final important difference is that the high or low of the day occurs in the first hour on most days, so it is critical to swing part of any trade that could be a major high or low, and use a breakeven stop to allow yourself to collect the windfall profits that often happen off the open.

management.teaching: When the entry setup bar's protective stop is not hit, a smart trader stays with the trade rather than taking a small opposite scalp, especially on a strong trend day.
management.quote: smart traders are holding short on a bear trend day.

management.teaching: Always swing part of every trade in the first hour, even accepting some stop-outs on the swing portion, because one good swing trade can be worth as much as ten scalps.
management.quote: One good swing trade can be as profitable as 10 scalps, so don't give up until it is clear that the day will not trend.

management.teaching: On a trade that could mark the day's high/low, take partial profits (a third to a half) once price has run 2-3x the initial risk; if a strong trendline break pauses as if reversing into a test, trim a bit more (e.g. a quarter); hold the rest until a clear strong opposite signal or a breakeven stop hit. Add to the position at every With Trend setup and mostly scalp those adds.
management.quote: swing all of the position and take partial profits on a third to a half after the trade has run two to three times your initial risk... Look to add to your position at every With Trend setup, like a two-legged pullback to the EMA in a strong trend.

management.teaching: On any trade that could be a major reversal, it's imperative to swing part of the position with a breakeven stop after scalping out part of it.
management.quote: Whenever a trade could be a major reversal, it is imperative to swing part with a breakeven stop after scalping out of some of the position.

management.teaching: New traders/new approaches should start at no more than two contracts regardless of account size, and only scale up after several consecutive weeks of consistent profit.
management.quote: When he begins to trade, he should not trade more than two contracts, no matter how large his account is. Only after consistently making a profit for several weeks should he begin to increase his position size.

management.teaching: Always let at least a quarter of a position run with a breakeven stop (or until a clear strong opposite signal), because trends last much further than a trader expects; likewise, always swing part of a position after a major reversal — a single swing entry usually earns more than many small scalps, even accounting for occasional breakeven stop-outs.
management.quote: always let at least a quarter of your position run with a breakeven stop or until there is a clear and strong opposite signal because trends last much further than you would ever think possible.

management.teaching: On huge-range days, it is especially important to swing part of every position with a breakeven stop, since swings of 20+ points can occur and getting stopped at breakeven once or twice before the trend accelerates is normal and expected.
management.quote: on huge days like this, it is very important to swing part of your position and rely on a breakeven stop because there were many swings of 20 points or more... it is common for the market to hit breakeven stops a couple times before the trend accelerates upward.

management.teaching: Successful intraday stock trading can be reduced to three trade types: EMA pullbacks in strong trends, clear/strong reversals after major trendline breaks, and first-hour failed breakouts/breakout pullbacks of prior-day patterns.
management.quote: Trading stocks on a 5-minute chart is fairly easy if you limit yourself to three types of trades: EMA pullbacks in strong trends, clear and strong reversals after major trendline breaks, and first hour failed breakouts and breakout pullbacks of patterns from the prior day.

management.teaching: Don't move a protective stop to breakeven too early; wait until roughly 60-80 cents (scaled to instrument) of open profit has accrued so the trade has time to work.
management.quote: Typically, you should not move your stop to breakeven until there has been about 60 to 80 cents of open profit.

management.teaching: Standard execution template: enter on a stop on the 5-minute chart, scale out (all or some) at a limit-order profit target, then move the stop on any remainder to breakeven.
management.quote: Begin trading using a 5-minute chart and entering on a stop. Take some or all off on a limit order at a profit target, and then move the protective stop to breakeven on any remaining contracts.

management.teaching: Focus on growing position size on your proven best setups rather than increasing trade frequency or the variety of setups traded; even one point per day in the Emini scaled to 100 contracts is a seven-figure annual result.
management.quote: Work on increasing your position size rather than on the number of trades or the variety of setups that you use. You only need to make one point in the Eminis a day to do well (100 contracts at 1 point a day is seven figures a year).

management.teaching: A scalp trade, because the profit target is smaller than the risk (often <4 points in the Emini), mathematically requires winning at least ~70% of the time to be worthwhile — an unrealistic hit rate for most traders, so traders should generally only take trades where reward is at least as large as risk unless they are extremely skilled.
management.quote: Since the profit is often smaller than the risk, a trader has to win at least 70 percent of the time, which is an unrealistic goal for most traders. Traders should take trades only where the potential reward is at least as great as the risk unless they are extremely skilled.

management.teaching: A setup only qualifies as 'reasonable'/'tradable' if it has a favorable trader's equation and at least a reasonable chance of reaching a scalper's profit — not every pattern recognized on the chart is worth risking capital on.
management.quote: reasonable A setup with a favorable trader's equation. / tradable A setup that you believe has a reasonable chance of leading to at least a scalper's profit.

management.teaching: As a trend extends, traders should trail their protective stop progressively (e.g., raise it to just below the most recent higher low in a bull trend) to lock in more open profit as the trade works.
management.quote: trailing a stop As the trade becomes increasingly profitable, traders will often move, or trail, the protective stop to protect more of their open profit. For example, if they are long in a bull trend, every time the market moves to a new high, they might raise the protective stop to just below the most recent higher low.

management.teaching: A true reversal trade differs from a countertrend scalp: reversal trades justify swinging most of the position because the potential move is large and uncertain in advance, while range/pullback conditions favor scalping for consistent smaller profits.
management.quote: In a true trend reversal, the new trend can go a long way and traders should swing most of their position... in a trading range, traders will usually make more money if they look for scalps rather than swings.

management.teaching: The theoretically 'ideal' stop for a swing reversal trade must sit beyond the start of the most recent countertrend spike (below the most recent higher low in a bull, above the most recent lower high in a bear), which can be far away — explaining why high-reward reversal trades have poor apparent probability/risk at the outset.
management.quote: The theoretically ideal stop for a swing trade goes beyond the start of the most recent spike (below the most recent higher low in a bull or above the most recent lower high in a bear, which can be far away).

management.teaching: Consecutive buy climaxes (progressively shrinking bull bodies, little pullback) are unsustainable and typically require a protracted correction of at least two legs and 10+ bars; the third consecutive buy climax especially signals an impending correction (mirror for sell climaxes).
management.quote: Consecutive buy climaxes usually are followed by a protracted correction that has at least two legs... After a third consecutive buy climax, a correction lasting at least 10 bars and having at least two legs was likely.

management.teaching: Don't rely on volume or indicators for reversal confirmation — traditional 'volume should be higher on the reversal' rule is unreliable; price action alone (chart) is sufficient.
management.quote: I don't want the distraction and do not look at volume or any indicators when I am trading, because the chart tells me all that I need to know.

management.teaching: For a successful three-push reversal pattern, first target is a test of the beginning of the pattern; second target is a measured move based on the height of the pattern.
management.quote: The first target is the beginning of the pattern, and the next is a measured move based on the height of the pattern.

management.teaching: Fast, high-urgency reversals off a wedge extreme can be traded early: move stops to breakeven after the first strong leg down and the subsequent lower high, since urgency reversals move fast and traders may struggle to react quickly enough.
management.quote: These fast reversals that quickly cover a lot of points represent the urgency that everyone is feeling... The move down is often fast and they can move their stops to breakeven after the first strong leg down and the subsequent lower high.

management.teaching: If a wedge's breakout is weak (not a strong reversal), traders should assume it fails and instead trade in the direction of the wedge's channel (with-trend), entering where countertrend traders will be taking losses (e.g., short the low-1/low-2 created by a failed upside breakout of a bear wedge).
management.quote: If the breakout is weak, traders assume that it will fail and they look for the channel to continue... These smart traders will enter where the countertrend traders will be taking their losses.

management.teaching: Once a 5-leg expanding triangle completes and a larger trend can develop, it is wise to swing (hold) some or all of the position rather than scalping the whole thing out.
management.quote: ...once the fifth leg is complete, a larger trend can develop and it is wise to swing part of the position.

management.teaching: When a reversal's first leg extends beyond the final flag that preceded it, this is one of the best reversal signs and traders should swing some or all of their position for potential windfall profits.
management.quote: Some of the best reversals come when the first leg up from the reversal extends beyond the final flag, as it does here... it is important to swing some or all of your position because the odds are good that you could receive windfall profits.

management.teaching: Traders commonly place initial protective stops one tick beyond the signal bar; after the entry bar closes strong, many move the stop to one tick beyond the entry bar instead — this creates predictable stop clusters that price often runs before reversing.
management.quote: When traders enter a trade, many place a protective stop at one tick beyond the signal bar. After the entry bar closes, if it is strong, many move it to one tick beyond the entry bar.

management.teaching: In the ES, common intraday profit targets cluster at round tick counts (4-tick scalp needing a 6-tick breakout, 2-point/9-tick, 4-point/17-tick); once a limit order is 'exactly' at those targets and price falls just short and reverses, it's a strong reversal signal because trapped limit-order traders must chase or exit at a loss.
management.quote: In the Emini, a popular trade is a scalp for four ticks... this five-tick failure is a sign of a loss of momentum.

management.teaching: When a countertrend trade based on a reversal pattern (e.g., double bottom bull flag) fails and hits a breakeven stop, but the trader still believes the with-trend read is correct, they may re-enter on the breakout-pullback that follows the stop run — but if the market keeps selling off instead, that expected pullback entry becomes a losing idea; recognize when a stop-run keeps going instead of reversing.
management.quote: they would have moved their stops to breakeven, thinking that if the stops were hit but the trade was still good, the stop run would set up a breakout pullback long setup. Here, the stops were hit but the market kept selling off.

management.teaching: There is a mathematical sweet spot for timeframe selection based on personality and skill: more bars/day means more trades and smaller risk per trade, but also a higher chance of misreading the chart or missing the best (fastest-triggering) setups.
management.quote: There is a mathematical sweet spot that you will have to determine based on your personality and trading abilities.

management.teaching: A new trader should focus primarily on swinging only the best two to five entries a day — usually second entries (reversals at new swing highs/lows) on non-trending days and spikes/pullbacks on trend days.
management.quote: A new trader should focus primarily on swinging only the best two to five entries each day, which are usually second entries in the form of reversals at new swing highs and lows on nontrending days ... and spikes and pullbacks on trend days.

management.teaching: In a bull trend on the daily chart, most days will have closes above the opens with a rally attempt into the final 30-60 minutes; in a bear trend, most days close below the open with a sell-off into the close.
management.quote: If there is a strong bull trend on the daily chart, most days will have closes above the opens and the market will usually try to rally in the final 30 to 60 minutes.

management.teaching: A trader can be correct about the trend into the close and still lose money if late dojis/tails create two-sided price action; the correct response is to keep a wide-enough (whole-signal-bar or wider) initial stop and only tighten it after the pullback resolves.
management.quote: You can be right about the trend into the close and still lose money when the market has a couple of dojis before the close ... you have to trade it for the swing and allow for two-sided movement and use a wide stop.

management.teaching: Shorter timeframe charts (e.g., 1 or 3 minute) generate more trades and smaller stops per trade but a lower winning percentage; most traders cannot execute fast enough and end up cherry-picking, keeping the worst trades and missing the best (fastest, easiest-to-miss) setups.
management.quote: the smaller the time frame, the more good trades they miss, and the lower their winning percentage becomes ... Invariably, they will cherry-pick and tend not to pick the very best setups.

management.teaching: Traders who use tick- or volume-based charts often shrink their stops to minimize apparent risk, but this typically just lowers the winning percentage without improving profitability.
management.quote: There is also a tendency for traders who use charts based on ticks or volume to use smaller time frame charts. They do so to minimize their stop sizes, hoping that the reduced risk will result in profitable trading. Invariably, they ignore the trade-off of a lower winning percentage and end up losing money.

management.teaching: Once a with-trend swing has moved several bars in your favor without pulling back to your entry, move your stop to breakeven; if it later gets hit on a deeper pullback without a clear new setup, stay flat rather than force a trade.
management.quote: if there is a pullback that would have hit a breakeven stop and then the market resumes in your direction for several bars, it should not come back to your entry price again. Therefore, in most cases, you could then move your stop to breakeven.

management.teaching: A practical two-lot always-in swing routine: on each reversal signal, sell one contract to exit the prior position and the other two initiate the new opposite position; scalp one for 1-2x risk and swing the other.
management.quote: If your trading size is two contracts and you are long one when a reversal pattern triggers, sell three. One gets you out of your remaining long position and the other two start the process in this new, opposite direction. Scalp the first for one to two times the size of your risk and swing the second.

management.teaching: Institutional risk managers force discretionary traders to cut losing positions against the trend even when they are emotionally attached and hoping for a reversal; the trend tends to be reinforced into the close by momentum programs detecting and selling into ongoing momentum.
management.quote: The boss always wins, and the result was a bear trend into the close. The trend is enhanced by momentum sell programs that detect the strong trend and sell into it, as long as the momentum down continues.

management.teaching: When both a bull and a bear can rationally scalp at the exact same price/bar (e.g., a large trend bar seen by bulls as continuation and by bears as exhaustion), what determines profitability for either side is disciplined trade management, not who was 'right' about direction.
management.quote: Both the bull and the bear scalpers could therefore make a scalper's profit by entering at exactly the same time, but in opposite directions. The key is money management.

management.teaching: In fast, large-bar first-hour conditions, scalpers should quickly cut position size (half to two-thirds) rather than spend time doing exact risk math, since speed of correct reading and order placement matters more than precision sizing.
management.quote: Scalpers should just quickly cut the position size in half or by two-thirds, and concentrate on what is much more important: reading correctly and placing orders.

management.teaching: On a trade that could become the high or low of day, scale out a third to half of the position after it has run one to two times initial risk, hold the remainder for a further move with a breakeven stop, and continue adding scalp-sized pieces on with-trend pullbacks while still swinging some contracts.
management.quote: If the trade looks particularly strong, swing all of the position and take partial profits on a third to a half after the trade has run one to two times your initial risk...Look to add to your position at every with-trend setup, like a two-legged pullback to the moving average in a strong trend. For these additional contracts, scalp most or all of the position, but continue to swing some contracts.

management.teaching: For a swing/pullback trend trade, use a protective stop just beyond the signal bar until the entry bar closes, then move the stop to just beyond the entry bar; exit roughly half the position at a defined profit level, move remaining stop to breakeven, trail beyond swing lows/highs, and consider letting a final runner ride until stopped or a strong opposite signal appears.
management.quote: They should place a protective stop beyond the signal bar...until the entry bar closes, and then once there is a trend bar in their direction, they should move the stop to just beyond the entry bar...After they exit half or so of the trade on a limit order at one dollar of profit, they should rely on their breakeven stop...They should consider letting the final portion run until the stop is hit or a clear and strong opposite signal develops.

management.teaching: Concrete worked example of the trader's-equation-driven stop-tightening process: after a strong 2-bar reversal signal, tighten the stop to one tick beyond it (defining 'actual risk' vs original 'planned risk'); with a believed 60% probability of an equidistant move, the minimum required profit target to keep a positive equation equals that actual risk in ticks.
management.quote: At this point, the trader knew that the actual risk to stay in the trade was only 11 ticks...he believed that the probability of an equidistant move was at least 60 percent. When the probability was 60 percent, the minimum profit that he needed to create a positive trader's equation had to be at least as big as his actual risk of 11 ticks.

management.teaching: After a final-flag-type climax/exhaustion bar (e.g., a large trend bar after a protracted move and a small prior trading range), traders typically wait for at least a 10-bar, two-legged pullback to the moving average before re-entering with-trend, rather than chasing immediately.
management.quote: After a final flag, traders usually wait for at least a 10-bar, two-legged pullback to the moving average before buying again.

management.teaching: Buying a pullback rather than chasing the spike itself reduces required risk because the stop can go below the pullback low instead of below the entire spike's start, trading off some profit potential for materially lower risk.
management.quote: One of the reasons why many traders like to buy pullbacks is because the risk is smaller...If he instead waited to buy a pullback...his stop would be below the low of [the pullback] and he would be risking much less.

management.teaching: Do not move a stop to breakeven too early; typically wait until the trade has moved roughly halfway to target / accumulated meaningful open profit (example: 60-80 cents on a stock trade) before tightening the stop.
management.quote: Typically, traders should not move their stops to breakeven on a trade like this until there has been about 60 to 80 cents of open profit.

management.teaching: Some traders scale out half the position at a fixed profit target (e.g., $1.00) via limit order, then move the remaining stop to breakeven and hold for a bigger swing, exiting on trend reversal, stop, a reasonable late-day reversal, or the close.
management.quote: On bar 6, traders would have been able to exit half of their shares with $1.00 profit using a limit order ... At that point, they could move their stop to breakeven and hold until the always-in position reversed to long, until their stop was hit, until any reasonable reversal up in the final hour or so, or until the close of the day.

management.teaching: When shorting (or buying) what looks like a minor top (or bottom) with-trend, if the market instead forms a clean high-2 (or low-2) reversal against you — especially with the signal bar testing the moving average — exit or even consider reversing the position.
management.quote: if you short what you think is a minor top ... and the market then forms a high 2 as it did at bar 17, exit or even reverse to long. This is especially true if the high 2 signal bar is a bull bar that is testing the moving average.

management.teaching: When a move has an increased chance of falling just short of an obvious target, some traders deliberately set their profit-taking limit order one tick less than usual (since price often approaches but reverses just before hitting the 'obvious' round target).
management.quote: Whenever a move has an increased chance of coming up short, many traders will use a limit order that is one tick less than usual, knowing that the market often hits the obvious target price, but reverses instead of filling the profit-taking limit orders.

management.teaching: It's difficult to reverse a position; for most traders it's better to exit (even at a loss) and then look for a fresh setup in the new direction rather than flip the existing position.
management.quote: It is difficult to reverse a position.

management.teaching: Any reversal setup is a good reason to take partial or full profits on an existing with-trend position, but should only be used to initiate a countertrend trade if it's especially strong — treat each top as the start of a bull flag and each bottom as the start of a bear flag.
management.quote: Since 80 percent of reversals fail, it is far better to view each top as the start of a bull flag and each bottom as the start of a bear flag.

management.teaching: Experienced traders can scale into or out of trades to improve the overall trader's equation — an initial entry may have low probability but subsequent entries can have much higher probability, improving the equation for the whole position.
management.quote: Experienced traders can scale into (or out of) trades to improve their trader's equation.

management.teaching: If a trade isn't behaving as expected, decide whether to exit by pretending you're flat and asking whether you would enter this trade right now; if yes, stay in, if not, get out.
management.quote: Look at the market and pretend that you are flat. If you think that you would put that trade on at this moment, stay in your position. If not, get out.

management.teaching: Habitually converting swing trades into quick scalps (or exiting scalps early for less than the risk) tends to lose money; if you can't stick to your plan, rely on bracket orders and walk away for about an hour after entering.
management.quote: If you find that you frequently take swing trades, but quickly convert them to scalps, you will probably lose money.

management.teaching: On higher time frames the signal bars are large, so far fewer contracts can be traded for the same risk; overnight risk means reducing size further or using limited-risk option strategies.
management.quote: far fewer contracts can be traded if the risk is to be the same as for a day trade

management.teaching: Daily-chart pullbacks rarely have classic reversal bars, so there is more uncertainty and risk; use smaller size, take partial positions, and scale in as the price action unfolds.
management.quote: Uncertainty means risk, and when there is more risk, position sizes need to be smaller, and a trader will have to consider taking a partial position and adding to the position as the price action unfolds

management.teaching: Because option bid-ask spreads are often wide, mainly consider option trades on higher time frame (60 minute/daily) charts; any setup with a significantly positive trader's equation is reasonable.
management.quote: traders should usually only consider option trades on higher time frame charts. Any setup with a significantly positive trader's equation is reasonable

management.teaching: Option costs (bid-ask spread on each leg entering and exiting, double commissions on spreads, time decay) mean you must restrict yourself to only the very best setups, and only add options to day trading if it truly increases your bottom line.
management.quote: Because of these costs, traders have to restrict themselves to trading only the very best option setups

management.teaching: Protective stops in options often offer no protection: the stop is based on the last trade, and if no trades occur in your option the stop won't fill even as the option loses value; in a fast move against you, exit on a limit order as soon as you can.
management.quote: It is important to understand that protective stops in options often offer no protection, and if the market is moving quickly against you, you should try to exit on a limit order as soon as you can

management.teaching: In a huge free fall near limit down, broker systems can overload and orders may not fill or report for 30+ minutes; buying calls (rather than the Emini) caps your risk at a known premium even if a stop never triggers.
management.quote: You cannot afford to live with this kind of uncertainty for such a long time on a huge bear day when your broker's order systems are not working correctly

management.teaching: Avoid thinly traded options (open interest under 1,000 or recent daily volume under 300 for the strike) because bid-ask spreads can be unacceptably large; if you must trade them, always use limit orders. Active markets like the SPY front-month ATM have penny spreads.
management.quote: In general, if the open interest is less than 1,000 or the recent daily volume is less than 300 options for the strike that you are considering, you usually should not place the trade

management.teaching: During a strong spike, buy an option early; if you are late in the spike, wait for a pullback to reduce the risk of a brief VIX spike that overprices all options. A strong spike can spike the VIX and elevate your call's cost so its value won't rise even as the trend continues.
management.quote: if you are not buying early on in the spike, it is better to wait for a pullback to reduce the risk of buying during a brief spike up in the VIX

management.teaching: Buy a spread (vs. an outright) only when you plan to hold for several weeks into the final week, or expect a huge move beyond your short strike; time decay accelerates in the final week and hurts the more-OTM short leg less, so spreads reach full value only if price falls well beyond the lower strike or near expiration.
management.quote: Buying a spread is usually best when you plan to hold the trade for several weeks, especially if you can hold the position into the final week

management.teaching: OTM options gain value disproportionately during big spikes (volatility skew), so a spread can profit less than a beginner expects; prefer outright put/call purchases when expecting a move within a week or so, or leg out of a spread when the market moves your way.
management.quote: A dramatic increase in option volatility usually causes a disproportionate increase in price of the OTM options. This is called skew

management.teaching: Enter with-trend option trades near the close of a bar once you can confirm it will be a strong trend bar in your direction (buy puts on a likely strong bear bar, calls on a likely strong bull bar), taking profits below/above the most recent swing.
management.quote: be confident that the day would be a bear trend day... They took profits below the most recent swing low

trader's-equation.teaching: The trader's equation: take a trade only if (probability of success x reward) > (probability of failure x risk); you control reward/risk via target/stop placement, but probability can never be known with certainty. As a guideline, assume 50% if uncertain and 60%/40% if confident.
trader's-equation.quote: To take a trade, you must believe that the probability of success times the potential reward is greater than the probability of failure times the risk... if you are uncertain, assume that you have a 50 percent chance of winning or losing, and if you are confident, assume that you have a 60 percent chance of winning and a 40 percent chance of losing.

trader's-equation.teaching: During most of the trading day the market is close to a 50-50 directional coin flip for any reasonable target/stop distance; whenever you are uncertain, default assumption should be that the market is in a trading range.
trader's-equation.quote: When the market is 50–50, it is uncertain and you cannot rationally have an opinion about its direction. This is the hallmark of a trading range, so whenever you are uncertain, assume that the market is in a trading range.

trader's-equation.teaching: During strong trends the directional probability can briefly rise to 60-70% (occasionally 80-20 in very strong trends), but this cannot persist — the market gravitates back toward 50-50 uncertainty and toward areas of support/resistance (often measured moves) where uncertainty and a brief trading range return.
trader's-equation.quote: During a strong trend, it might be 60 or even 70 percent, but that cannot last long because it will gravitate toward uncertainty and a 50–50 market...

trader's-equation.teaching: Unlike coin flips (always 50-50), markets exhibit momentum/persistence: in a trend most reversal attempts fail, and in a trading range most breakout attempts fail — roughly 70% or better odds that the current state (trend or range) persists.
trader's-equation.quote: When a market is trending, most attempts to reverse fail. When it is in a trading range, most attempts to break out fail... In trading, the odds are more like 70 percent or better that what just happened will continue to happen again and again.

trader's-equation.teaching: A scalper using a stop larger than the profit target needs a ~70%+ win rate to be profitable, which is very hard to sustain; most traders should instead favor swing trades with reward at least equal to (ideally 2x) risk, at typically 40-60% win rate, though truly high-probability swing setups (~60%+) occur mainly during strong breakouts.
trader's-equation.quote: if a scalper is using a stop that is larger than his profit target, he has to win on 70 percent or more of their trades to be profitable... Most traders should start out looking for swing trades where the profit is at least twice as large as the risk.

trader's-equation.teaching: If risk equals reward, you must win well over 60% of the time to be profitable; many price-action patterns have a 70%+ success rate, and moving the stop to breakeven while holding a partial position adds further edge.
trader's-equation.quote: If the risk is the same as the reward, you have to win much more than 60 percent of the time to make the trade worthwhile. However, lots of patterns have a 70 percent or better success rate.

trader's-equation.teaching: A large non-overlapping multi-bar spike (e.g., 2 consecutive large bear bodies without overlap) implies high odds of a measured move to the open/high of the first bar minus the range, or similar — and is a high-probability with-trend continuation entry despite feeling late/risky.
trader's-equation.quote: The odds were high that the market would have some kind of measured move down from the high or open of the first bar to the close or low of the final bar of the spike... this was a very high-probability short.

trader's-equation.teaching: When multiple independent reasons (final flag, third consecutive climax, wedge bottom, measured move) align, the odds of a successful trade increase considerably — combine confluence factors before taking countertrend trades.
