trader's-equation.quote: In the context of a final flag, a third consecutive sell climax, a wedge bottom, and a measured move, it was a good setup for a rally.

trader's-equation.teaching: Smaller time-frame charts (e.g., 3-min vs 5-min) give smaller stops but a materially higher chance of being stopped out of a trade that the larger time frame would have kept you in — reducing overall profitability despite the smaller per-trade risk.
trader's-equation.quote: The risk is smaller but the probability of success also drops. Since there are more trades on a 3 minute chart, there is more risk of missing the best ones, and this can lead to the overall profitability of 3 minute trades being less for many traders.

trader's-equation.teaching: Every trade decision must weigh three things — reward, risk, AND probability of success — not just the reward being chased in the moment.
trader's-equation.quote: they are ignoring two other essential considerations for every trade: the risk and the probability of success. All three must be evaluated before placing a trade.

trader's-equation.teaching: Market direction is normally only about 50% certain (bulls/bears roughly balanced); in a strong, established trend, traders can be 60% or more certain of continued direction — this is Brooks' explicit probability framing for trend strength.
trader's-equation.quote: the market’s direction is usually only about 50 percent certain because the bulls and bears are in balance most of the time. However, when there is a strong trend, traders can often be 60 percent or more certain of the direction.

trader's-equation.teaching: Every trading firm's strategy loses on a large share of its trades (30-70%) yet remains profitable overall; this is why smart institutions still sell at the exact bottom tick of a bear market — don't read a single trade/tick as informative about 'who is right.'
trader's-equation.quote: Every firm uses strategies that they have carefully tested and found to be profitable, but all lose money on 30 to 70 percent of their trades.

trader's-equation.teaching: A short setup can have as low as ~40% win probability and still be a good trade if the reward is several multiples of the risk — the trader's equation (probability x reward vs. probability x risk) is what matters, not raw win rate.
trader's-equation.quote: The probability of a successful swing down might be only 40 percent. However, since the reward is several times the risk, the trader's equation is still very positive.

trader's-equation.teaching: Scalping against a strong spike usually loses because the trader's equation is weak (probability of the scalp profit times its size is smaller than probability of loss times stop size), and chasing the scalp causes traders to miss the much larger with-trend move that follows.
trader's-equation.quote: most traders who attempt this will lose, because most of the pullbacks do not fall far enough for a profit, or the trader's equation is weak... traders who take the short are hoping so much for their small profit that they invariably end up missing the much more profitable long that forms a few minutes later.

trader's-equation.teaching: Trade only when probability of success times potential reward is significantly greater than probability of failure times risk; in a good trend context assume at least 60% win probability, which makes a 2-point reward on a 2-point stop favorable, but a smaller reward loses money over time.
trader's-equation.quote: trade only when the chance of success times the potential reward is signiﬁcantly greater than the chance of failure times the risk

trader's-equation.teaching: Do not take profit before the market has moved at least twice your initial risk unless your probability of success is truly ~80%, which is rare; most traders should never risk more than their reward.
trader's-equation.quote: traders should not take any proﬁts until the trade has gone at least two points... the trader's equation begins to become favorable only when the reward is two points or more.

trader's-equation.teaching: Directional probability of an equidistant move is about 50% in the middle of a trading range; a trend is a move from one trading range (one perceived-value area) to another, and once directional probability falls back to ~50% a new trading range becomes evident.
trader's-equation.quote: the directional probability of an equidistant move is about 50 percent in the middle of the trading range... A trend is simply a move from one trading range to another.

trader's-equation.teaching: A large gap up leads to a bull trend day about 60% of the time and a bear trend day about 40% of the time (opposite for gap-down); the larger the gap, the more likely it becomes a trend day and the more likely the trend follows the gap direction.
trader's-equation.quote: When there is a large gap up and a trend from the open day forms, the day will be a bull trend day about 60 percent of the time and a bear trend day 40 percent of the time.

trader's-equation.teaching: Even to an experienced trader, most with-trend swing setups in a strong trend look like 50% or less probability, yet they actually succeed 70% or more of the time — this gap between appearance and reality is exactly what keeps most traders out of the best trades.
trader's-equation.quote: Most setups appear to have 50 percent or less probability of success. This makes traders not take the trades and forces them to chase the market, or miss the trend completely.

trader's-equation.teaching: Even the best price-action patterns fail to do what's expected about 40 percent of the time; if the market hasn't paused by the third or fourth bar of a move, it may have overextended and the odds of reversal instead of continuation increase.
trader's-equation.quote: Even the best patterns still fail to do what you expect about 40 percent of the time.

trader's-equation.teaching: You cannot take a trade simply because the risk is small — you must weigh the probability of success together with the size of the profit target (the trader's equation), not risk alone.
trader's-equation.quote: You cannot take a trade simply because the risk is small. You need to consider the probability of success and the size of the profit target as well.

trader's-equation.teaching: A one-point scalp risking 6-7 ticks requires roughly a 60% or higher win probability to be worth taking; if the market has proven it can't sustain a move of the needed size (e.g., the biggest pullback all day was only 9 ticks), the trade fails the equation and must be skipped.
trader's-equation.quote: You can scalp for one point and risk six or seven ticks only if the probability of success is 60 percent or higher, so you cannot short this market.

trader's-equation.teaching: Even when a setup looks like it's building perfectly toward a big move, there is always roughly a 40% chance the exact opposite happens — never treat a favored scenario as certain.
trader's-equation.quote: Even though the middle of the day was setting up perfectly for a big sell-off into the close, you can never be certain and there is always about a 40 percent chance that the exact opposite can happen.

trader's-equation.teaching: To take any trade you must believe probability-of-success × potential reward > probability-of-failure × risk; if uncertain, default to assuming 50% odds, and if confident, assume about 60/40.
trader's-equation.quote: To take a trade, you must believe that the probability of success times the potential reward is greater than the probability of failure times the risk.

trader's-equation.teaching: Buying/selling a with-trend pullback generally has a better trader's equation than chasing the raw breakout above/below a prior swing point: greater reward, smaller risk, higher probability.
trader's-equation.quote: In general, the reward is greater, the risk is smaller, and the probability of success is higher when entering on pullbacks. ... It is usually better to buy the pullback than the breakout.

trader's-equation.teaching: The trader's equation is what matters, not raw win rate: if reward is at least as large as risk, a strategy is only mathematically sound when win probability is meaningfully above 50%; institutions taking a trade at 1:1 risk/reward implicitly signal they believe probability is around 60% or better.
trader's-equation.quote: it is mathematically unwise to have your risk equal your reward if the chance of success is much less than 60 percent, and institutions would therefore not be taking this trade.

trader's-equation.teaching: Directional probability of an equidistant move oscillates: roughly 60%+ (or better) at the start of a strong spike/channel, decaying to about 50% near the middle of the move/range, then overshooting to favor the opposite side once the market reaches a significant support/resistance magnet — this cycle underlies why measured moves and reversals work.
trader's-equation.quote: When a bear channel begins, the directional probability of an equidistant move is at least 60 percent...In the middle of the trading range, there is uncertainty; but once the market hits the bottom...the directional probability favors the bulls.

trader's-equation.teaching: When trading with the trend, assume roughly a 60% directional probability of an equidistant move; set the profit target at least as large as the risk to get a positive trader's equation.
trader's-equation.quote: the probability is never known with certainty, but since they are making a trade in the direction of the trend, they can assume that the directional probability of an equidistant move is 60 percent

trader's-equation.teaching: In a trading range, the trader's equation forces scalping (small targets) but scalping requires very high win probability, which cannot persist long in an inherently uncertain trading range — this tension is 'the trading range dilemma.'
trader's-equation.quote: The dilemma is that you have to scalp, but scalping requires a very high probability of success, and that high degree of certainty cannot exist for long in a trading range, where by definition uncertainty prevails.

trader's-equation.teaching: A worked example: risking 2 points to make 2 points at the top of a 6+ point range with a second-entry signal gives roughly 60% win probability — a minimally but genuinely profitable trader's equation.
trader's-equation.quote: they have at least a 60 percent chance of making two points while risking two points. This results in a minimally profitable trader's equation and is therefore a successful strategy.

trader's-equation.teaching: Tightening the stop after partial profits can flip a lower-probability trade (even 40%) into a favorable equation because reward now vastly exceeds risk.
trader's-equation.quote: they are risking five ticks to make four points, and even if the chance of success is only 40 percent, they have a winning trader's equation.

trader's-equation.teaching: Two exact opposite scalps (same risk/reward) cannot both have >50% probability simultaneously; the person on the other side of a good scalp mathematically has to be a lower-probability, larger-reward swing trader.
trader's-equation.quote: the market cannot have a 60 percent chance of going up two points before going down two points at the same time that it has a 60 percent chance of going down two points before going up two points.

trader's-equation.teaching: Core mathematical rule of trading: probability of success × reward must exceed probability of failure × risk (the trader's equation); this, not a simple risk/reward ratio, is what actually determines profitability.
trader's-equation.quote: you must believe that the trader's equation is favorable: the probability of success times the reward has to be greater than the probability of failure times the risk.

trader's-equation.teaching: Edges are always small because for every trade one institution must profitably take each side; a trade with 80% probability, 6-point reward, and 2-point risk implies an institution knowingly accepting a 20% probability, 2-point reward, 6-point risk trade — virtually impossible, so overly favorable-looking setups are almost certainly mis-assessed.
trader's-equation.quote: if you think that you have an 80 percent chance of making six points while risking two points, then you believe that there are institutional traders willing to take the opposite side of your trade... Since that is virtually impossible in a huge market, you have to assume that your assessment of the variables is wrong.

trader's-equation.teaching: Because ~90%+ of market volume is institutional and institutions are net profitable, every filled trade — including the one that hits your stop — reflects a rational, profitable strategy on the other side; the market never 'runs your stop' out of malice, only because an institution found it profitable to trade there.
trader's-equation.quote: The market will not run your stops. The market might test the price where your protective stop is, but it has nothing to do with your stop. It will only test that price if one or more institutions believe that it is financially sound to sell there.

trader's-equation.teaching: Scalpers and swing traders can profitably take opposite sides of the same trade because their management (targets, stops, scaling) differs, not because either is 'wrong' — the scalper has a small target/high probability, the swing trader on the other side has a large target/lower probability but positive equation via wider stops or scaling.
trader's-equation.quote: Your entry is the opposite, but your management is not.

trader's-equation.teaching: High-probability (60%+) systems with reward ≥ risk have a positive expectation on every individual trade, so cherry-picking is mathematically fine for these — the opposite of low-probability systems.
trader's-equation.quote: If a system has a probability of 60 percent or higher and a reward that is at least as large as its risk, then every individual trade has a positive trader's equation... traders can cherry pick all they want and still make money.

trader's-equation.teaching: Directional probability of an equidistant move (chance of X favorable ticks before X adverse ticks) hovers near 50% most of the day, briefly rising above 50% during trends/spikes and at tested extremes of ranges — traders must act fast because these edges are fleeting and self-correcting.
trader's-equation.quote: the directional probability of an equidistant move of any size up versus down hovers around 50 percent for most of the day... it is briefly higher many times during the day, and traders should look for trades when that happens.

trader's-equation.teaching: As a spike/trend extends, the trader who entered early keeps the same fixed dollar risk (stop near spike start) while the reward (measured-move target) keeps growing — this is the core mathematical case for entering trends early rather than waiting for a pullback.
trader's-equation.quote: If the spike then grows to $4.00, there is still at least a 60 percent chance that AAPL will go up $4.00 more before it falls to below the bottom of the spike... you now have a 60 percent chance of making a total of $6.00 while just risking $2.00.

trader's-equation.teaching: Most trades are only about 60% certain at best; the other 40% is real and dangerous, so you must always plan for and respect the possibility of loss with a working protective stop.
trader's-equation.quote: Since most trades are only 60 percent certain at best, you always have to have a plan for that other 40 percent of the time.

trader's-equation.teaching: Once a swing trade moves roughly halfway to its profit target, move the stop to breakeven (or better) — this strengthens the trader's equation because probability of success rises while risk shrinks.
trader's-equation.quote: Once a swing trade reaches about halfway to the profit target, many traders will move their protective stop to break even.

trader's-equation.teaching: All trade decisions should be based on the trader's equation (probability x reward vs (1-probability) x risk); beginners should seek setups with ≥60% win probability and reward at least equal to (ideally ~2x) risk, though such strong setups occur only a couple times a day.
trader's-equation.quote: All trades should be made on the basis of the trader's equation, and beginning traders should look for trades where the probability of success is 60 percent or higher, and the reward is at least as large and preferably about twice the size of the risk.

trader's-equation.teaching: Random entries are a coin flip (50%); a trade is only worthwhile long-run if, for equal risk and reward, win rate exceeds 50%, and many valid price-action patterns run 70% or better.
trader's-equation.quote: without even looking at a chart, you can place a buy order and have a 50 percent chance of being right! ... lots of patterns have a 70 percent or better success rate

trader's-equation.teaching: A bar count/pattern label alone (e.g. High 4) is not sufficient to trade — you need prior demonstrated strength (a relatively strong move that broke at least a minor trendline) before acting on the count.
trader's-equation.quote: Note that Bar 7 on the Emini was a High 4, which is usually a reliable buy signal. However, in the absence of any bullish strength in the High 1, 2, or 3, you cannot take the trade. A bar count alone is not sufficient.

trader's-equation.teaching: A breakout implies strength and possible new trend; in a strong trend (strong-close breakout bar + subsequent bars with strong closes/trending highs-lows and no pullback), price is likely to be higher before the trend reverses back beyond the start of the move — but most breakouts fail, so fade failures or wait for the failure-of-failure (Breakout Pullback) rather than entering the raw breakout.
trader's-equation.quote: Fade it if it fails, as most breakouts do, and re-enter in the direction of the breakout if the failed breakout fails and therefore becomes a Breakout Pullback. Only rarely is it best to enter on the breakout.

trader's-equation.teaching: A rough magnet/target heuristic: expect price to run roughly double the stop distance required to avoid being stopped out (e.g., a 12-tick required stop implies roughly a 12-tick favorable excursion from entry).
trader's-equation.quote: Double the required stop (if an Emini trade required you to use a 12-tick stop to avoid getting stopped out, expect the move to extend about 12 ticks from your entry.

trader's-equation.teaching: Scalping the 5-minute Emini at a typical ~2-point risk for a 1-point target requires winning over 67% of trades just to break even; using breakeven stops on swung partial size, tightening stops progressively, or using a wider stop/target with adds all reduce the required win percentage.
trader's-equation.quote: To scalp the 5-minute Emini chart effectively, you usually have to risk about two points and your profit target is one point or more. This means that you have to win on over 67 percent of your trades just to breakeven.

trader's-equation.teaching: On a strong trend day, taking countertrend long scalps against the prevailing bear trend is a low-probability bet where most scalps fail and losses outweigh the rare winner — do the math before taking the trade.
trader's-equation.quote: Most long scalps will fail, and the amount you lose on each one will be too great to make up by the eventual winner or two.

trader's-equation.teaching: Countertrend trading in strong trends will often win individual trades and feel fun, but the math guarantees a slow account bleed over time — countertrend setups in strong trends almost always fail and become great with-trend trades instead.
trader's-equation.quote: If you trade Countertrend, you are gambling, and although you will often win and have fun, the math is against you, and you will slowly but surely go broke.

trader's-equation.teaching: Countertrend scalps and with-trend pullback avoidance are the two habits that most directly prevent consistent profitability; reversals should only be traded after a break of a significant trendline followed by a strong reversal bar on a test of the trend's extreme.
trader's-equation.quote: You will not make consistent money until you stop trading Countertrend scalps... You will not make money until you start trading With Trend pullbacks... You will not make money trading reversals until you wait for a break of a significant trendline and then for a strong reversal bar on a test of the trend's extreme.

trader's-equation.teaching: To take any trade you must believe the probability of success times the potential reward is greater than the probability of failure times the risk — this trader's equation, not pattern recognition alone, is the real basis for whether a setup is worth taking.
trader's-equation.quote: trader's equation To take a trade, you must believe that the probability of success times the potential reward is greater than the probability of failure times the risk. You set the reward and risk... The difficulty in solving the equation is assigning a value to the probability, which can never be known with certainty.

trader's-equation.teaching: As a practical guideline when uncertain, assume a 50% chance of winning or losing; when confident in a setup, assume 60% win / 40% loss — Brooks' working definitions of 'probably'/'likely'/'usually' (≥60%) versus 'risky' (≤50%) versus 'unlikely' (≤40%).
trader's-equation.quote: As a guideline, if you are uncertain, assume that you have a 50 percent chance of winning or losing, and if you are confident, assume that you have a 60 percent chance of winning and a 40 percent chance of losing. / likely At least 60 percent certain. / risky ... the probability of success for a trade is 50 percent or less... / unlikely At most 40 percent certain.

trader's-equation.teaching: Without looking at a chart, buying or selling any instrument with a symmetric bracket (OCO) gives roughly a 50% win rate — this is the baseline; a trading range is defined as a period where directional probability is genuinely near 50-50, and traders should default to assuming a trading range whenever uncertain.
trader's-equation.quote: The market gravitates toward uncertainty. During most of the day, every market has a directional probability of 50–50... When you are uncertain, assume that the market is in a trading range.

trader's-equation.teaching: During a strong trend the directional probability can briefly reach 60-70%, but it cannot persist because the market gravitates back toward 50-50 equilibrium and toward support/resistance (measured-move) areas where uncertainty returns.
trader's-equation.quote: During a strong trend, it might be 60 or even 70 percent, but that cannot last long because it will gravitate toward uncertainty and a 50–50 market.

trader's-equation.teaching: Reversal trades typically have positive trader's equations because even at a low win rate (40-50%, sometimes only 30-60%), the reward is several times the risk; math, not win rate alone, determines whether a setup is worth taking.
trader's-equation.quote: In general, when risk is held constant, a larger potential reward usually means a smaller probability of success... a trend reversal trade may have a reward that is several times larger than the risk, it can have a profitable trader's equation.

trader's-equation.teaching: Worked numeric example: on a 4-point swing reversal in the Emini (10-15pt avg range) with good context and a decent signal bar, probability of success ~40% (up to 50-60% if the setup is very strong); probability of a 2-point stop being hit before target/exit is only ~30%. Over 10 trades: 4 wins x 4pts = 16pts; remaining 6 trades average out near breakeven (3 losses ≤2pts, 3 small wins 1-3pts) → ~1.6 pts/trade average, 'good for a day trader.'
trader's-equation.quote: If traders win four points in four out of 10 trades, they have 16 points of profit... They then have about 16 points of profit on 10 trades, or an average of 1.6 points of profit per trade, which is good for a day trader.

trader's-equation.teaching: Most initial reversal entries have relatively low probability (~40-50%); waiting for strong follow-through and a clear always-in flip raises probability to ~60%+ but shrinks the reward. Both approaches are mathematically sound; choose based on personality.
trader's-equation.quote: Most initial entries have relatively low probability (about 40 to 50 percent) of success. Some traders prefer a higher probability, and they wait for strong follow-through and a clear always-in flip. The trade-off is a smaller reward for a higher chance of success.

trader's-equation.teaching: An average setup has ~40% chance of a profitable swing, ~30% chance of a small loss, ~30% chance of a small profit (70% chance of at least a small profit); the strongest setups have a 60%+ chance of a profitable swing.
trader's-equation.quote: An average setup has about a 40 percent chance of leading to a profitable swing, a 30 percent chance of a small loss, and a 30 percent chance of a small profit.

trader's-equation.teaching: Earlier, weaker-looking entries in a reversal have lower probability (often below 50%) but the greatest potential reward, and the trader's equation is usually still positive; waiting for a confirmed always-in reversal raises probability to 60%+ but the stop is further away, reducing reward.
trader's-equation.quote: The earliest entries in major trend reversals often do not look strong... the potential reward is at its greatest and the result usually is a positive trader's equation.

trader's-equation.teaching: After three consecutive climaxes without a significant break, the odds are 60 percent or better that the market will attempt a more complex correction (two legs, 10+ bars).
trader's-equation.quote: They know that the odds are 60 percent or better that the market will try to have a more complex correction, like two legs up, that lasts for about 10 or more bars.

trader's-equation.teaching: A trade with a low win-rate (e.g., ~40%) can still have a positive trader's equation if the reward targeted is at least twice the risk; conversely, waiting for confirmation trades off lower reward for higher win probability (~60%+), which is also positive if reward ≥ risk.
trader's-equation.quote: The traders who took the first entry knew that it had only about a 40 percent chance of success, but they were looking for a reversal down and a reward that was at least twice the size of their risk, and therefore had a positive trader's equation.

trader's-equation.teaching: A failure is any trade that does not reach its goal (smaller profit than expected, or a loss); since most good setups have about a 60% win rate, they have about a 40% failure rate as a baseline.
trader's-equation.quote: Since the most common good setups have about a 60 percent success rate, they have about a 40 percent failure rate.

trader's-equation.teaching: When a trade reaches a scalper's profit and then comes back and takes out the original signal/entry bar stops, the reversal move has less fuel because fewer traders are trapped (many already exited profitably or moved stops to breakeven), so it tends to be a smaller/less reliable reversal than a fresh failure.
trader's-equation.quote: If a trade makes a scalper's profit... and then comes back and takes out the original signal or entry bar stops, there is much less fuel left in the market to drive the market in this new, opposite direction because there are far fewer traders who are trapped in the trade.

trader's-equation.teaching: Once a profit target is missed by one tick, a disciplined trader should never let the trade turn into a loss because doing so risks far more (e.g., 8+ ticks) to make 1 tick — a terrible trader's equation.
trader's-equation.quote: Once it hits nine ticks, they should never allow the trade to turn into a loss, because they would then be risking eight or more ticks to make one tick, and this has a terrible trader's equation.

trader's-equation.teaching: Scalping requires a very high winning percentage to be profitable, which is difficult even for experienced traders; traders who are not yet consistently profitable should avoid making scalps their cornerstone strategy.
trader's-equation.quote: you should avoid scalps until you are an experienced trader, because you need a very high winning percentage to be profitable

trader's-equation.teaching: Around scheduled reports, slippage is common, risk is often larger and reward smaller, and probability is roughly only 50%, producing a bad trader's equation — wait for the market to become orderly before entering.
trader's-equation.quote: Whenever a report is released ... slippage is common ... the risk is often larger and the reward is often smaller. Also, the probability is only 50 percent. The result is a bad trader's equation.

trader's-equation.teaching: The minimum bar for taking any always-in style trade is that probability × reward must exceed (1-probability) × risk; in practice most qualifying always-in setups are at least 60% certain, well above the 51% breakeven minimum.
trader's-equation.quote: the minimum criterion for an always-in signal is that the market is more likely to go in the new direction than in the old one, which implies that all that is needed is 51 percent certainty, most such signals are 60 percent or higher.

trader's-equation.teaching: As a spike grows and the trader tightens the stop, the risk shrinks while the measured-move reward target grows, so the trade's math keeps improving as long as the spike continues (and probability stays at or above 60%).
trader's-equation.quote: As the spike continues, the risk remains constant, and the directional probability of an equidistant move will stay at 60 percent or better, but the reward grows and this makes the trade even better mathematically.

trader's-equation.teaching: Countertrend scalps against a clear always-in trend are mathematically poor: risk is roughly double the reward, requiring an unrealistic ~70% win rate, versus waiting to enter with the trend at the end of a pullback.
trader's-equation.quote: This is because they almost certainly would have to be scalps, which means that the risk would probably be about twice the reward and the chance of success would have to be an unrealistic 70 percent or greater.

trader's-equation.teaching: Scalps generally need a win rate of about 60% or more because the stop (risk) usually has to be roughly the same size as the target (reward); swings can succeed with a much lower win rate (even ~40%) if the reward is a multiple of the risk.
trader's-equation.quote: From the trader's equation, readers know that scalpers generally have to win on 60 percent or more of their trades, since the risk usually has to be about the size of the reward. Scalp setups are much more common than swing setups, but most scalp setups do not have a 60 percent probability of success and should be avoided.

trader's-equation.teaching: A trader must decide, before entering, whether a trade is being taken as a scalp or a swing, and manage it consistently with that premise; switching goals mid-trade (e.g., planning to swing but grabbing a scalp profit, or vice versa) produces a losing trader's equation over time.
trader's-equation.quote: You can't have one premise and then manage the trade as if it was based on a different premise, but it is common for traders to confuse scalps and swings in the first hour when everything happens so quickly and the bars are large and have big pullbacks within the bars as they are forming.

trader's-equation.teaching: For a reasonably good opening reversal setup, the probability of a swing hitting a target of at least twice the stop is often only ~40% (50-60% if the setup is very strong), but the probability of the stop being hit before target or a smaller-loss exit is only ~30%, making the trader's equation strongly favorable even at modest win rates.
trader's-equation.quote: This makes the trader's equation very favorable for this type of trade. If traders win four points in four out of 10 trades, they have 16 points of profit off of their swing trades...they will end up about breakeven on those trades...the trader then has about 16 points of profit on 10 trades, and averages 1.6 points of profit per trade, which is good for a day trader.

trader's-equation.teaching: A large gap that goes sideways rather than reversing sharply raises the probability of a trend day (Brooks estimates to 60%+ in his example), which can make the with-trend risk/reward math excellent (e.g., risking ~6-10 points to make ~15-20 points at 60% odds).
trader's-equation.quote: When there is a large gap up or down and no strong reversal, the trader's equation can be strong for the with-trend traders...they had a 60 percent chance of success, which is a great trade...this excellent math is why large gap openings can offer great trades.

trader's-equation.teaching: An edge (positive trader's equation) exists when the probability of reaching the profit target before the stop, times the reward, is greater than the probability of hitting the stop first, times the risk. This is the single mathematical criterion for whether a trade is worth taking.
trader's-equation.quote: An edge (positive trader's equation) exists if the probability of a trade reaching his profit target before hitting his protective stop is greater than the probability of the market hitting his stop before reaching his target.

trader's-equation.teaching: You can have a positive trader's equation even with probability under 50% if the reward is large enough relative to risk (e.g., a bear thinking a swing-down chance is 40% or less can still trade it if reward vastly exceeds risk).
trader's-equation.quote: They will short a reversal setup at a lower high, a double top, or a higher high, even though they realize that the chance of a swing down might be 40 percent or less. As long as the reward is much larger than the risk, they have a positive trader's equation.

trader's-equation.teaching: Position-size mismanagement on 'free' add-on contracts (e.g., a second contract scalped for 1 tick to cover commissions with an 8-tick stop) can secretly have a negative expectancy even while looking profitable overall, because a trader must consider the math of every leg of a position separately.
trader's-equation.quote: He was risking eight ticks to make one tick on his second contract. This requires the market to move three ticks beyond the signal bar before hitting his protective stop in 90 percent of his trades just to break even...his second contract is losing money on average, even if it makes money on it 80 percent of the time!

trader's-equation.teaching: Different legendary traders have different style preferences (Paul Tudor Jones: reversals; Richard Dennis: breakouts; Paul Rotter: scalping; John Henry: always-in basket approach) — any style can work if the trader's equation is positive; Brooks personally prefers high-percentage pullback entries and trading-range fades, and rates strong breakouts as having >70% follow-through probability.
trader's-equation.quote: I especially like breakouts because when they are strong the probability of follow-through is often more than 70 percent. I look less often for reversal trades, because most reversal attempts fail, but I will take a strong reversal setup.

trader's-equation.teaching: Best-trade setups cluster into two math profiles: 60-70% probability with reward roughly equal to risk, or 50-60% probability with reward at least twice the risk. Both are mathematically sound and beginners should primarily target these because they offer the biggest margin for error.
trader's-equation.quote: The very best trades have either a 60 to 70 percent chance of making a reward that is at least as large as the risk or a 50 to 60 percent chance of making two or more times the risk. These are the trades that beginners should primarily take.

trader's-equation.teaching: You can never know the true probability of a trade with precision and have very little time to decide; when uncertain, default to assuming a 50% chance the limit order gets filled before the stop, or 60/40 if confident (often the real odds are even better than 60/40).
trader's-equation.quote: Since trading can be easier if you have only two choices, in general, if you are not certain, assume that there is a 50 percent chance that your limit order will be filled before your stop is hit. If you are confident about the trade, assume that the odds are 60-40 in your favor, although they are often higher.

trader's-equation.teaching: A 'best trade' does not require high probability alone — a ~50% probability setup can still be a best trade if the reward is at least twice the risk; the trader's equation has three variables (probability, reward, risk), not just win rate.
trader's-equation.quote: Even though selling in the middle of a trading range might have only about a 50 percent chance of success, if the reward is twice the size of the risk, it can be a best trade.

trader's-equation.teaching: On typical days there are usually five or more setups where probability is ≥60% and reward is at least as large as risk — these are the 'best trades' to prioritize.
trader's-equation.quote: There are usually five or more such setups on most days ... Remember, if traders think that a setup is probably good, they should assume that this means that the chance of success is at least 60 percent. If the reward is at least as large as the risk, this creates a positive trader's equation.

trader's-equation.teaching: When average daily range has compressed well below its recent average (e.g., 2 points vs. a typical 10-15), odds increase that the range will expand before the close, which by itself can be reason enough to take a modest-edge trade with strong reward:risk, even without a clear directional read.
trader's-equation.quote: the odds were high that today's range was going to double or triple before the close. The problem was that traders did not know if the breakout would be up or down, or both.

trader's-equation.teaching: Trading is a zero-sum game against smart, mostly-institutional competitors, so any edge that exists is inherently small and fleeting — a trader must be better than roughly half of all other participants (i.e., win the trader's-equation more than half the time) to profit, and any edge that grows too large gets arbitraged away by institutions before it can persist.
trader's-equation.quote: There is no easy set of reliable rules to make money as a trader, and everything is subjective. This is a zero-sum game with very smart players, so when an edge exists, it is small and fleeting. ... The edge can never get very large because institutions would take advantage of it as it was growing.

trader's-equation.teaching: A stated probability (e.g. 60%) is not fixed — it varies by context (pullback near support, scaling in with larger size, using a very wide stop) and can effectively become 80-90% in the right circumstances; being flexible about coexisting probabilities improves your chance of success.
trader's-equation.quote: 60 percent is 60 percent in only 90 percent of the cases and can be 90 percent sometimes and 10 percent at other times.

trader's-equation.teaching: Most countertrend setups fail and most with-trend setups succeed; trends constantly create great-looking countertrend setups and lousy-looking with-trend setups, but the math favors with-trend. Countertrend setups in strong trends almost always fail and become great with-trend setups, especially on the 1-minute chart.
trader's-equation.quote: Countertrend setups in strong trends almost always fail and become great with-trend setups.

trader's-equation.teaching: Until consistently profitable, take only trades where potential reward is at least as large as risk; you cannot risk 2 points to make 1 and expect profit unless right ~80% of the time, which very few traders are.
trader's-equation.quote: The chance of winning times your potential reward has to be significantly greater than the chance of losing times your risk.

trader's-equation.teaching: The trader's equation has three variables (probability, reward, risk); any setup with a positive expected result is a good trade, whether high-probability/reward-equals-risk or low-probability/huge-reward — or anything in between.
trader's-equation.quote: Any setup with a positive result is a good trade.

always-in.teaching: Always-in is the concept of treating yourself as forced to hold a position (long or short) at every moment; when confident which direction you'd choose if forced, the market is 'always-in' that direction. Almost all always-in trades require a spike in the trend direction before traders gain confidence.
always-in.quote: If you have to be in the market at all times, either long or short, this is whatever your current position is (always in long or always in short)... Almost all of these trades require a spike in the direction of the trend before traders will have confidence.

always-in.teaching: About 80% of reversal attempts within a trend fail and become flags/pullbacks rather than true reversals; smart traders fade the panic and trade with the still-intact trend.
always-in.quote: The market is always trying to reverse, but 80 percent of those reversal attempts fail and become bull flags.

always-in.teaching: After a strong break of a range/trend-line and follow-through trend bar, traders should look to trade only in the always-in direction (buy at market or on pullbacks, not fade).
always-in.quote: the bar 3 strong bull trend bar showed you that the always-in position was up and you should look to buy either at the market or above the bar 5 pullback... Always in... basically means that if you have to be in the market at all times, either long or short, the always-in position is whatever your current position is.

always-in.teaching: A single very strong reversal bar that closes beyond the closes and lows/highs of many prior bars can flip the always-in direction of the market outright, trapping the swing (strongest) participants who held through pullbacks and forcing them to exit at a loss, which itself fuels further movement.
always-in.quote: A single strong reversal that reverses many closes and lows...can flip the always-in direction.

always-in.teaching: Weak-looking signal bars are actually a structural ingredient of strong trends: both trapped countertrend traders and eager with-trend traders keep waiting for a 'perfect' setup that never comes, creating constant urgency and small-piece order flow that keeps the trend grinding.
always-in.quote: The weak signal bars are an important ingredient in strong trends...Both keep selling in small pieces all day long, just in case their perfect setup never comes...and it usually doesn't.

always-in.teaching: Entering late at market to match the swing-size position you would still be holding from a missed entry, with the same stop, is mathematically and psychologically identical to already holding that swing position — there is no rational reason to hesitate once always-in is clear.
always-in.quote: Logically, it doesn’t make any difference whether you are buying a swing-size portion now or holding a swing position from an earlier entry.

always-in.teaching: Treat any gap opening or large trend bar as a de facto one-bar trend/breakout — since breakouts commonly fail, be ready to fade it if a countertrend setup appears.
always-in.quote: It is helpful to consider a gap opening and any large trend bar to be effectively breakouts and each should be treated as if it is a one-bar trend, since breakouts commonly fail and you need to be prepared to fade them if there is a setup.

always-in.teaching: Only about 5% of bars are 'spike' bars where the vast majority of institutions are trading the same direction at once with enough size to overwhelm the opposition; the market rarely moves far unless this alignment happens.
always-in.quote: The only time that the vast majority of the institutions are on the same side is during the spike phase of a strong trend, and that occurs in fewer than 5 percent of the bars on the chart.

always-in.teaching: In a tight bull channel it is rarely wise to short below the low of a bar unless there has been a strong climactic reversal after a top-of-channel breakout, and even then it's often better to wait for a lower high after the sell-off before shorting.
always-in.quote: In a tight bull channel, it is rarely wise to short below the low of a bar unless there has been a strong climactic reversal after a breakout of the top of the channel.

always-in.teaching: Micro trend lines should only be used to find with-trend setups; once the larger trend reverses (e.g., after a trend line break and reversal from a higher high), then look for micro trend line countertrend setups in the new direction.
always-in.quote: It is critical to remember that micro trend lines should only be used to find with-trend setups. However, once the trend has reversed... you should be looking for micro trend line short setups.

always-in.teaching: A trend has a spike phase (steep, large trend bars, little overlap, small tails — where the market becomes clearly always-in) followed by a channel phase (more two-sided, larger pullbacks) that can last far longer than expected; eventually the market evolves into a trading range that is itself just a pullback on a higher time frame chart.
always-in.quote: This is the spike phase of a trend... The market eventually begins to have some two-sided trading in the form of pullbacks, and the trend then transitions into the channel phase... The trading range is simply a pullback on a higher time frame chart.

always-in.teaching: The market can flip to 'always-in long' within a bar or two of a strong reversal bar, prompting traders to swing (and even add to) their full position rather than just scalp.
always-in.quote: Within a bar or two after bar 11, traders saw the market as always-in long, and swung the remainder of their longs, and even added to them.

always-in.teaching: A trader can only be successfully long or flat in a bull trend, and short or flat in a bear trend; consistently trading against a clear trend is a losing strategy for almost everyone.
always-in.quote: A tiny fraction of traders have the ability to consistently make money by trading against a trend, and you should assume that you are not part of that group.

always-in.teaching: Markets overshoot toward 'excess' rather than stopping precisely at 'neutrality,' because neutrality is never clear in real time but excess (an obvious extreme/magnet) is — so reversals cluster at magnets/measured-move areas, not at a calculable fair-value midpoint.
always-in.quote: That clarity occurs at some magnet, and at that point everyone sees that the market has gone too far... it will usually overshoot it again because neutrality is never clear but excess is.

always-in.teaching: Every day begins as a trend-from-the-open day the moment the first bar's high or low is exceeded; whether that initial direction persists into a real trend depends on whether the breakout grows into a larger multi-bar spike.
always-in.quote: As soon as a bar moves above the high of the prior bar, the day is a trend from the open bull trend day, at least for that moment.

always-in.teaching: The very strongest trends generate reversal-looking patterns (wedges, small pullback flags) all day long that never quite trigger cleanly and almost always fail; when a wedge's correction is sideways instead of reversing, conclude the trend is very strong and stop looking for reversals.
always-in.quote: The very strongest trends tend to create reversal patterns all day long, but they somehow don't look just right... they constantly trap early bears into shorting.

always-in.teaching: A runaway trend (large trend bars, little overlap, small tails) is a breakout and a clear always-in flip; you must enter quickly even if it looks climactic/overdone, because it will likely continue further, and you should swing most of the position.
always-in.quote: There are large trend bars with little overlap and mostly small tails. This is a breakout and a clear always-in flip. You must enter quickly, even if the new trend looks climactic and overdone (and it is, but it will likely continue to get much more so!), and swing most of your position.

always-in.teaching: The always-in concept: at any moment you must mentally be long or short (never flat) — whichever position you'd confidently take now is the always-in direction, and almost all such conviction requires seeing a spike first.
always-in.quote: If at any time you are forced to decide between initiating a long or a short trade and are confident in your choice, then the market is in always-in mode at that moment.

always-in.teaching: The single most important recurring decision a trader makes, after the close of every bar, is whether there will be more buyers or sellers above and below the prior bar — this determines the always-in direction and usually implies a multi-bar move, not just a scalp.
always-in.quote: the single most important determination that a trader makes, and he makes this after the close of every bar, is whether there will be more buyers or sellers above and below the prior bar.

always-in.teaching: All pullbacks begin with a reversal pattern strong enough to entice countertrend traders in, but usually not strong enough to flip the always-in direction — so traders should not fade the trend on that reversal, but instead look to re-enter with the trend once the pullback shows signs of ending.
always-in.quote: Since the setup and the pullback are not strong enough to change the direction of the always-in trade, traders should not be looking for a countertrend trade.

always-in.teaching: As soon as the always-in direction flips (e.g., after a convincing spike), traders should enter at least a small position immediately (at market or on a tiny pullback) rather than waiting for a full pullback that may not come before price runs far away, because the directional probability at that point is already ~60%+.
always-in.quote: As a general rule, as soon as traders decide that there is now a bull trend underway, they should buy at least a small position at the market or on a tiny pullback... because the directional probability of an equidistant move is probably at least 60 percent.

always-in.teaching: When the trader's equation deteriorates through the life of a trade (still favorable but weaker), tighten stops or take smaller profit; if it goes negative, exit immediately at market even for a loss — the equation is recalculated every tick.
always-in.quote: The trader's equation of every trade changes with every tick...If it becomes negative, they should exit immediately at the market, even if that means taking a loss.

always-in.teaching: Do not trade Countertrend until the market has broken the trendline AND gone back to test the trend's extreme; the trend continues even after the trendline break until that test fails.
always-in.quote: You should not be trading countertrend until the mar­ket has enough strength to break the trendline. However, even then, you should not be entering countertrend until the market first goes back to test the extreme of the trend.

always-in.teaching: Once a trend is confirmed strong, no setup is needed to enter — you can enter at the market any time all day with a relatively small stop; the only function of a setup is to reduce risk.
always-in.quote: once you realize that the market is in a strong trend, you don't need a setup to enter. You can enter any time all day long at the market if you wish with a relatively small stop. The only purpose of a setup is to minimize the risk.

always-in.teaching: A false breakout of a swing low/high followed by another false breakout of the opposite side, confirmed by a subsequent break of the first level again, is a classic sequence that confirms directional control and sets up high-confidence trend entries.
always-in.quote: A false breakout of the low (Bar 6) followed by a false breakout of the high (Bar 7) confirmed by the Bar 8 breakout of the low again made the smart shorts feel pretty good at this point.

always-in.teaching: Trends commonly unfold in two legs: after a strong first countertrend leg, both bulls and bears expect a test of the old extreme to fail, causing With Trend traders to exit and creating a self-fulfilling Higher Low/Lower High second leg.
always-in.quote: this kind of trading goes on all day long in all markets and creates a lot of two-legged moves.

always-in.teaching: After any trendline break, expect a second leg because all traders are now aware of the possibility of a Higher Low/Lower High reversal; therefore generally avoid With Trend (old trend) entries until the second attempt at reversal fails.
always-in.quote: Any time there is a trendline breakout, the chances are high that there will be a second leg... In general, avoid a With Trend entry until after the second attempt fails.

always-in.teaching: An alternative to scalping/swinging is to try to stay 'Always In' the market most of the day, trading at least two contracts (scalp one, swing one), reversing size when the opposite signal triggers, and holding through pullbacks unless the protective stop is hit.
always-in.quote: A good alternative to scalping and occasionally swinging a portion of the trade is to try to stay in the market most of the day, exiting on the close.

always-in.teaching: A Tight Trading Range acts like Barb Wire — a magnet pulling breakouts back to its middle; don't trust breakouts from it, fade small bars at its extremes and wait for failed breakouts or Breakout Pullbacks instead.
always-in.quote: A Tight Trading Range is like Barb Wire, and it acts as a magnet, drawing breakouts back toward its middle.

always-in.teaching: Never enter countertrend unless the countertrend side has already demonstrated some ability to control the market (i.e., a genuine trendline break with strong momentum) — the trend is still assumed in force until price actually tests and confirms.
always-in.quote: you do not want to enter countertrend unless the countertrend traders have already shown some ability to control the market.

always-in.teaching: A trendline break by itself does not flip the trend; the prior trend (e.g., a bear) remains in force — and shorts remain the higher-probability side — until price actually tests and reverses at the trend's extreme.
always-in.quote: Remember, breaking the trendline does not tum the trend into a bull. It is still a bear until after the market tests the low so you should still be looking for shorts.

always-in.teaching: In a strong bull trend you don't need a formal setup to buy — you can buy at the market almost anywhere and make money — but waiting for setups lets you use a tighter, better-defined stop.
always-in.quote: In a strong bull, you do not need setups to buy. You can buy at the market anywhere and make money, but setups allow you to use tighter stops.

always-in.teaching: All patterns eventually fail, and even the failures can fail — a failed failure resumes in the original direction as a high-probability second entry.
always-in.quote: All patterns fail, and the failures often fail, but a failed failure is a second entry in the original direction and has a high probability of success.

always-in.teaching: Classify every bar and bar series as either trend or trading range; if trend, you must take every valid with-trend trade and are barred from taking any countertrend trade until you have been taking all the with-trend ones.
always-in.quote: Every bar and every series of bars is either a trend or a trading range. Pick one... If it does and you are looking to take any trade, you must take every With Trend trade. Never consider taking a Countertrend trade if you haven't been taking all of the With Trend trades.

always-in.teaching: With Trend direction should default to the direction of the most recent 5-minute trend signal, or, more simply, whichever side most of the last 10-20 bars sit on relative to the EMA.
always-in.quote: Countertrend: ...In general, the direction of the most recent 5-minute signal should be assumed to be the trend's direction. Also, if most of the past 10 or 20 bars are below the EMA, With Trend is likely on the sell side.

always-in.teaching: The always-in concept: assume you must be in the market either long or short at all times; your best-guess current stance (long or short) reveals the higher-probability direction. Almost all always-in trades require a spike in the trend direction before traders gain confidence.
always-in.quote: always in If you have to be in the market at all times, either long or short, this is whatever your current position is (always in long or always in short)... Almost all of these trades require a spike in the direction of the trend before traders will have confidence.

always-in.teaching: The always-in concept: if a trader had to be in the market at all times (long or short), a genuine reversal is marked by the point at which the majority of traders would flip their always-in position, and this almost always requires a real breakout.
always-in.quote: A reversal at some point makes the majority of traders believe that the always-in position has reversed, and this almost always requires some kind of breakout.

always-in.teaching: Buying pullbacks in bull trends / selling rallies in bear trends is statistically superior to countertrend scalping — more trades, higher win rate. Countertrend scalpers who get lucky picking exact tops/bottoms tend to scalp out early instead of riding the trend, so their edge doesn't compound ("death by mathematics").
always-in.quote: In general, buying pullbacks in bull trends and selling rallies in bear trends is a much better approach for most traders. There are far more trades, and the winning percentage is higher.

always-in.teaching: A break of a trend line signals a possible reversal, but if the market then rallies/sells off 5+ bars beyond the old extreme without stalling, treat the breakout as valid trend resumption and restart the reversal-search process from scratch.
always-in.quote: If there is a trend line break, but the move to a higher high goes five or more bars above the old bull high, traders usually should begin the process from the beginning.

always-in.teaching: Trends end via: (1) a countertrend move that breaks the trend line, then (2) a test of the final trend extreme (which may overshoot or undershoot); the test itself is frequently two-legged.
always-in.quote: Trends end with a reversal, which is a countertrend move that breaks the trend line, and then a test of the final trend extreme.

always-in.teaching: If an overshoot of the old trend extreme goes too far in too tight a channel, do not look for a reversal - the trend has likely resumed; restart the trend-line-break-and-test process.
always-in.quote: If the overshoot goes too far and is in too tight a channel, you should not be looking for a reversal. The trend has likely resumed.

always-in.teaching: Down spikes in bull markets and up spikes in bear markets are with-trend pullbacks that don't need to be tested (traders are eager to resume the trend); up spikes in bull markets and down spikes in bear markets are countertrend spikes that usually DO get tested because traders are less willing to believe a reversal will succeed.
always-in.quote: A spike pullback does not have to get tested since traders agree that the trend is resuming and they are eager to get on board, but a spike reversal usually gets tested because it is countertrend and traders are less willing to believe that a reversal will succeed.

always-in.teaching: In a strong bear (or bull) channel, smart traders buy only below prior bars, not above, because they are far more interested in shorting than buying — the with-trend side controls entries at the extremes.
always-in.quote: When the market is in a bear channel, smart traders are buying only below bars and not above; they are much more interested in shorting, and look to short above prior bars, not below.

always-in.teaching: A second high/low that fails as a low-2 (or high-2) short/long with a weak breakout above/below strongly suggests wedge-top/bottom formation; if the low-2 fails and breakout is strong, expect at least two more legs in the trend instead.
always-in.quote: They view the second high as a large low 2 short setup. If the low 2 fails and the breakout above the low 2 top is strong, the market will likely have at least two more legs up. If it is not strong, the market will probably form a wedge top.

always-in.teaching: Wedge reversal is a countertrend setup — general rule: wait for a second entry (e.g., lower high after a wedge top) unless the pattern is exceptionally strong, whereas wedge pullbacks (with-trend) are safe to enter on the first signal.
always-in.quote: It is important to remember that a wedge reversal is a countertrend setup. It is therefore usually better to wait for a second entry... unless you are trading a wedge pullback where you are entering in the direction of the larger trend. Then, entering on the first signal is a reliable approach.

always-in.teaching: Consecutive bull trend bars after a reversal convince traders the market is now always-in long, at which point they stop looking to fade the presumed bear flag and instead look for continuation setups.
always-in.quote: This convinced most traders that the market was now always-in long, and they were no longer looking for a breakout of a bear flag.

always-in.teaching: A strong bear trend bar that closes several ticks beyond a flag's extreme (a breakaway-gap-like close) is what convinces traders the market has flipped always-in in that direction and will have good follow-through.
always-in.quote: Bar 7 closed many ticks below the bar before it and therefore was an attempt to form a breakaway gap. Many traders shorted as soon as it closed... expecting that the market was flipping to always-in short.

always-in.teaching: Every failed bull setup is a sign of strength for bears and every failed bear setup is a sign of strength for bulls — failures often create excellent setups in the opposite direction because traders forced out are reluctant to re-enter the same way, making the market one-sided, and their exits with losses add fuel to the opposite move.
always-in.quote: Every failed bull setup is a sign of weakness by the bulls and strength by the bears... When a setup fails, it often creates an excellent setup for a trade in the opposite direction.

always-in.teaching: Some failures instead signal a strong trend and set up a WITH-trend trade: failure of price to dip below prior bar's low in a strong bull trend (or above prior high in strong bear trend) indicates urgency/strong trend; failure of a pullback to reach the moving average also indicates urgency; failure to reach a magnet often leads to a pullback then another attempt at the magnet.
always-in.quote: Failure to fall below the low of the prior bar in a strong bull trend or to rally above the high of the prior bar in a strong bear trend. This indicates urgency and a strong trend.

always-in.teaching: The single most reliable 'failure' is a flag within a trend: when a flag forms and countertrend traders bet it's a reversal, that's a low-probability bet because markets have inertia and trends resist reversal attempts; the failure of that countertrend bet (i.e., a with-trend continuation, e.g. a high 2 in the flag) is a great with-trend entry.
always-in.quote: The single most reliable failure is a flag in a trend. As a flag is forming, countertrend traders are hoping for a trend reversal, which is a low-probability bet because markets have inertia and trends resist reversal attempts.

always-in.teaching: The first hour or two of the day is the single most important trading period — the most likely time to find a trade offering 2-3x risk in reward, and also the easiest time to lose money if not careful.
always-in.quote: there is a tendency for the first hour or two to be the most likely time of the day to find a trade where you can make two to three times your risk. This is the most important time of the day.

always-in.teaching: Always-in is the discipline of asking, at any moment, 'if forced to be long or short right now, which would I choose?' — that answer is the always-in position, and it is different from simply asking whether the market is trending.
always-in.quote: If you had to be in the market at all times, either long or short, the always-in position is whatever your current position is.

always-in.teaching: When you cannot decide the always-in direction, the market is by definition in a trading range (uncertainty is the hallmark of a range), and the correct approach is to buy low / sell high rather than force a swing decision.
always-in.quote: If you simply cannot decide, then the market is in a trading range and you look to buy low and sell high.

always-in.teaching: The market perpetually tries to reverse and often 'almost flips' without follow-through; because roughly 80% of reversal attempts fail, with-trend traders should treat these near-misses as buying (or selling) opportunities rather than getting scared into premature countertrend entries.
always-in.quote: It is a costly mistake to keep betting that reversals will be successful, because 80 percent of them are not.

always-in.teaching: Confirmation that the always-in direction has flipped generally requires at least two consecutive strong trend bars in the new direction; the close of the second bar is the key tell (same-direction close = credible flip, doji/opposite close = weak, fade it instead).
always-in.quote: There usually has to have been at least two consecutive strong bull trend bars before most traders will believe that the always-in direction is long, and at least two consecutive strong bear trend bars before they will think that always in is short.

always-in.teaching: During the first hour, before a clear trend is established, the bar needed to flip sentiment is lower — just two consecutive trend bars of either color will make traders look for scalps in that direction.
always-in.quote: During the first hour, before a clear trend is established, two consecutive bull trend bars will make traders look for buy scalps, and two consecutive bear trend bars will make them look for sell scalps.

always-in.teaching: Always-in is a swing concept meant for trending conditions, not tight trading ranges; in a tight range it is better to scalp/fade extremes or wait for the breakout/failed breakout rather than try to call an always-in flip.
always-in.quote: Since this is a swing approach, traders should not use it when the market is in a tight trading range... they should either take the always-in trade that was present prior to the formation of the tight trading range and hold for a swing profit, or simply trade scalps with limit order entries, fading the extremes of the range.

always-in.teaching: A low 1 short is invalid (and likely to fail into a higher low) unless there was a strong bear spike AND the market is clearly already in a bear trend; shorting a low 1 immediately after a sell climax is specifically flagged as a mistake.
always-in.quote: Bar 9 was a bad low 1 short setup, and therefore a higher low was likely. The market had a strong bear spike, which is needed for a low 1, but it was not clearly in a bear trend, which is also needed before shorting a low 1. Also, a trader should not short a low 1 after a sell climax, and this was possibly one.

always-in.teaching: Big trend bars or consecutive trend bars in one direction almost always fail as sustained breakouts in the first hour — traders should wait for a confirmed breakout and a clear always-in market before switching to swing trading rather than chasing the initial move.
always-in.quote: They see large trend bars up or down and are afraid that they are missing a breakout, which almost always fails. They have to learn to wait for a confirmed breakout and a clear always-in market. Then they should switch to a swing approach and become willing to buy near the top when the market is always-in long, or sell near the bottom when it is always-in short.

always-in.teaching: Because the first hour offers the best combination of reward, risk, and probability, most traders should work hardest to catch swing trades in that window, and can even build a profitable practice trading only the first hour (or first two hours) at increased size once consistently profitable.
always-in.quote: In general, most traders should work very hard to take potential swing trades in the first hour or two because those trades have the best trader's equation combination of reward, risk, and probability... If you simply trade the first hour and increase your position size once you become consistently profitable, you might discover that the intensity is needed for only that hour.

always-in.teaching: A pair of consecutive strong trend bars (especially with large bodies and small tails) in the first hour is read by traders as evidence the market is 'always-in' in that direction, at least for a scalp, and this belief itself drives entries on the next with-trend pullback.
always-in.quote: Traders often look for a pair of consecutive bull trend bars in the first hour or so. If they are present, traders would see them as a sign that the market is always-in long, at least for a scalp.

always-in.teaching: Traders should only use limit (countertrend-style) orders to trade in the direction they believe the market is (or is about to become) always-in; using limit orders to fade a trend when you still believe the trend's always-in direction persists has a losing trader's equation.
always-in.quote: Traders should only use limit orders to trade in the direction of the trend...The probability of success is simply too low when using limit orders to trade countertrend...unless you are an exceptional profitable and experienced scalper.

always-in.teaching: Once a trader believes the market is always-in long, the highest-quality trade is almost always buying a pullback, especially near the moving average with a bull-bodied signal bar; multiple consecutive bull trend bars, a strong spike, and minimal closes below the moving average are cumulative evidence reinforcing the always-in-long read.
always-in.quote: Once a trader believes that the market is always-in long, the best trade is almost always buying a pullback, especially near the moving average and when the signal bar has a bull body.

always-in.teaching: After a strong bear (or bull) spike with a large trend bar and immediate follow-through, the market becomes 'always-in' in that direction for most traders, who then hold/short rallies (or buy dips) until an opposing always-in signal or a break of the spike's extreme.
always-in.quote: Once there was a large bear breakout bar and then the immediate follow-through at bar 12, the market was always-in short for most traders, and they would assume that it would stay that way unless there was an always-in buy signal or until the market went above the top of the bear spike.

always-in.teaching: There are no reliable countertrend patterns; never trade countertrend unless there has first been a strong break of a significant trend line and the entry is a reasonable always-in-reversal setup. Since 80% of reversals fail, whoever is on the other side of your countertrend entry is likely the smarter trader.
always-in.quote: There are no reliable countertrend patterns... Since 80 percent of reversals fail, who do you think is making the money?

always-in.teaching: Every bar and every series of bars is either a trend or a trading range — pick one, decide the always-in direction, and take every with-trend trade; never consider a countertrend trade if you haven't been taking all the with-trend trades.
always-in.quote: Every bar and every series of bars is either a trend or a trading range. Pick one.

always-in.teaching: A strong spike that breaks out of a trading range flips the market to always-in in the breakout direction; a subsequent strong opposite spike flips it the other way.
always-in.quote: which broke out of a trading range and turned the market into always-in long

math.teaching: Edges in the market are always small and fleeting because a profitable edge requires a counterparty, and the market is full of smart traders who won't let an edge persist or grow large.
math.quote: edge A setup with a positive trader's equation. The trader has a mathematical advantage if he trades the setup. Edges are always small and fleeting because they need someone on the other side, and the market is filled with smart traders who won't allow an edge to be big and persistent.

math.teaching: Scalping math is unfavorable: since profit target is often smaller than risk, a scalper needs to win at least 70% of the time, an unrealistic goal for most traders — trade only setups where reward is at least as great as risk unless extremely skilled.
math.quote: Since the profit is often smaller than the risk, a trader has to win at least 70 percent of the time, which is an unrealistic goal for most traders. Traders should take trades only where the potential reward is at least as great as the risk unless they are extremely skilled.

math.teaching: A 'failure' in the Emini often occurs even when the objective was close: a scalper's 4-tick target usually requires a move of 6 ticks (and similarly the market must go a tick beyond a limit order for it to be filled), and a 5-tick move against a signal bar that then reverses ('five-tick failure') is itself a common setup for a trade in the opposite direction.
math.quote: Currently, a scalper's target in the Emini of four ticks usually requires a six-tick move... five-tick failure A trade in the Emini that reaches five ticks beyond the signal bar and then reverses... It is often a setup for a trade in the opposite direction.

math.teaching: Institutional 4-tick scalping requires price to trade 6 ticks past the signal-bar extreme to fill (1 tick entry + 4 tick target + 1 tick to fill limit); a repeated 'five-tick failure' followed by heavy volume without a downtick often signals a real institutional buy expecting more than a scalp.
math.quote: If they just entered a buy, the market will usually have to move six ticks above the high of the signal bar... Sometimes when the market just keeps hitting five ticks but not six (a potential five-tick failure), there will suddenly be a trade of 250 Emini contracts and the price does not tick down.

math.teaching: Scalping profitably requires roughly a 70% win rate, which is essentially impossible for all but elite traders; beginners should plan to swing trade instead of scalp.
math.quote: This means that they have to win about 70 percent of the time, which is impossible for anyone except an extremely good trader.

math.teaching: Small pullback trend days keep gains modest even though the day is a clear trend: because bulls buy small positions all day (never chasing in a panic) and bears are never heavily short (so no big short-covering rally), the day often does not cover as many points as a more volatile trend day despite being 'the strongest' trend type.
math.quote: even though the day is a trend day, it often does not cover too many points and the bulls do not make a windfall proﬁt, even though they were on the right side of a strong trend

math.teaching: Scalping (target smaller than risk) mathematically requires an unrealistic ~70% win rate; traders should generally only take trades where potential reward is at least as large as risk unless extremely skilled.
math.quote: Since the profit is often smaller than the risk, a trader has to win at least 70 percent of the time, which is an unrealistic goal for most traders. Traders should take trades only where the potential reward is at least as great as the risk unless they are extremely skilled.

math.teaching: Martingale (double-up-after-a-loss) sizing is mathematically sound in theory but practically impossible: a modest losing streak (e.g., 8 losers) would require position sizes (256x) far beyond any trader's actual risk tolerance.
math.quote: if you are comfortable trading 10 contracts, but start with just one and plan to double up and reverse with each loss... eight consecutive losers would require 256 contracts! ... This is the inherent, insurmountable, mathematical problem with this approach.

math.teaching: A reasonable profit-target heuristic: expect the market to ultimately move about as far in your favor as the size of protective stop that was required to take the trade.
math.quote: A move equal to the size of the required protective stop: if an Emini trade required you to use a 12-tick protective stop to avoid getting stopped out, expect the move to ultimately reach 12 ticks in your favor.

math.teaching: 80% of breakout attempts out of trading ranges fail; only about 20% of any single breakout attempt succeeds, though the range does eventually break out (often, but not always, with the prior trend) roughly 60% of the time.
math.quote: A trading range is just a pullback that has lasted so long that it has lost its short-term predictive power, and 80 percent of the breakout attempts up or down will fail.

math.teaching: Directional probability of an equidistant move is ~50% in the middle of a range, rises toward 60%+ near the edges, and can reach 70-80% at the far edge of a range that follows a strong trend (asymmetric depending which side of the prior trend).
math.quote: Near the bottom of the range, that is reversed, and the directional probability favors a rally... instead of 60 percent in favor of a rally, maybe it is 70 to 80 percent.

math.teaching: A tight trading range caps the directional edge at roughly 51-55%, regardless of how good the setup looks, because the range itself signals the market has lost directional conviction.
math.quote: A tight trading range trumps everything... the probability of the breakout in either direction is never more than about 55 percent.

math.teaching: On a one-point scalp with a two-point stop, you need to win over 67% just to break even before commissions, and ~80% to be reliably profitable — a bar almost no trader can sustain.
math.quote: On one-point scalps, you have to win on over 67 percent of your trades just to break even.

math.teaching: Backtested 'high win-rate' patterns discovered by data mining are often statistical noise (like a coin landing heads 8/10 times by chance), not real edges — beware of overfitting historical distributions.
math.quote: if you test enough ideas or enough inputs in any system, you will discover that some have worked in eight out of 10 tests and others have failed in eight out of 10. That is just the distribution of outcomes under a bell curve and has nothing to do with actual likelihood.

math.teaching: Measured-move targets often work precisely because that distance is the institutional minimum needed for a positive trader's equation on the position — not because of magic technical significance.
math.quote: Why does a measured move target often work so precisely? Because it is the minimum needed by institutions to have a positive trader's equation, and they would not otherwise take the trade.

math.teaching: For the ES 5-minute chart with a 10-15 point average daily range, roughly 8 ticks works as a default stop on most days; scale stop and target sizes together (larger bars -> larger stop AND larger target) to preserve trader's equation.
