exhaustion.teaching: Unusually large trend bars in a trend can represent climactic exhaustion (last buyers/sellers exhausted), often followed by at least a two-legged correction; big breakout trend bars often fail on the very next bar, trapping traders, especially on quiet trading-range days.
exhaustion.quote: if a bar is unusually large, it often represents a climactic exhaustion... Big trend bars on breakouts often fail on the next bar, trapping traders into the wrong side of the market.

countertrend-precondition.teaching: You cannot sell in a strong bull (or buy in a strong bear) unless there has been a prior trendline break demonstrating some opposite-side strength first; countertrend trades need a prior trendline break plus a good signal bar — both are required.
countertrend-precondition.quote: You cannot sell in a strong bull if there has not been a prior bull trendline break.

climax.teaching: A climactic selloff/rally (e.g., 16 of 17 bars each making a lower high) is unsustainable and is usually followed by a two-legged correction lasting many bars (at least an hour on a 5-min chart).
climax.quote: A climax is usually followed by a two-legged correction that lasts for many bars (at least an hour on a 5-minute chart).

outside-bar-risk.teaching: It is almost always unwise to enter on the breakout of a 5-minute outside bar because the stop is too distant/risky; if you must, use a fixed money stop or reduce size. A 1-minute outside-bar breakout is more tradeable.
outside-bar-risk.quote: it is almost always unwise to enter on a breakout of a 5-minute outside bar, especially if the outside bar is large... because of the greater risk that the distant stop entails

decisiveness.teaching: When uncertain about an ambiguous bar (like an outside bar), the best decision is simply to wait for more price action rather than force a trade.
decisiveness.quote: The single most important thing to remember about outside bars is that whenever a trader is uncertain about what to do, the best decision is to wait for more price action to develop.

tools.teaching: Related ETFs (e.g., SPY vs SDS inverse) or alternate chart types can clarify ambiguous price action; if a pattern (e.g., bull flag) doesn't look right, check the inverse instrument's chart for confirmation before trading.
tools.quote: if it does, you would be wise not to buy the Emini flag and instead wait for more price action to unfold

second-entry-psychology.teaching: 'Good fill, bad trade' — if the market lets you in at a BETTER price than earlier entrants (e.g., a cheaper second entry), be suspicious you're reading the chart wrong and it may be a trap; most good second entries occur at the same or worse price.
second-entry-psychology.quote: Most of the time, a good fill equals a bad trade (and a bad fill equals a good trade!).

momentum-and-second-entries.teaching: When fading a move that already had 4+ strong trend bars (too much one-directional momentum), don't take the first countertrend signal — wait, let it fail, and take the second (or with a Wedge, third) attempt to reverse instead.
momentum-and-second-entries.quote: if one fails, assume that you are reading the market incorrectly and do not take a third entry unless it is a Wedge (a failed trend channel line breakout).

late-entry.teaching: It is never too late to enter a strong trend: entering late at market with the position size and stop you would still be holding from the original entry is mathematically identical to having held the original trade — hesitating due to 'it's already moved' is an emotional/logical error.
late-entry.quote: Entering late and using the original stop is absolutely identical to being long the swing portion of the original position, using the same protective stop.

breakouts.teaching: Treat any gap opening or large trend bar as a one-bar 'breakout' / one-bar trend — since breakouts commonly fail, be ready to fade them if a valid signal bar appears after sideways bars 'break' that one-bar trendline.
breakouts.quote: It is helpful to consider a gap opening and any large trend bar to effectively be breakouts and each one should be treated as if it is a one-bar trend, since breakouts commonly fail

dueling-lines.teaching: When a trendline and a trend channel line (drawn from opposite sides) both point to a reversal at the same area, this 'Dueling Lines' confluence strengthens the trade.
dueling-lines.quote: the Bar 6 short an example of a Dueling Lines trade (trendline and trend channel lines intersecting and in opposite directions)

outside-bars.teaching: An outside bar occurring mid-trading-range is meaningless for generating a breakout trade unless followed by a small fade bar near its high or low.
outside-bars.quote: If an outside bar is in the middle of a trading range, it is meaningless and should not be used to generate trades, unless it is followed by a small bar near the high or low of the outside bar, setting up a fade.

trend.teaching: In a strong trend, every tick is effectively a With Trend entry — you can enter at market at any point using swing size and a reasonable swing stop and expect profit, though risk/reward is worse than entering on pullbacks.
trend.quote: In a strong trend, every tick is a With Trend entry, so you can simply enter at the market at any point using your swing size and use a reasonable swing stop.

magnets.teaching: Chapter framed explicitly as background, not trading basis: magnets (Measured Moves, Fibonacci, prior failed-reversal signal-bar levels, trendlines, swing highs/lows, emotional round numbers, etc.) are guides to keep you trading With Trend until tested/overshot — do not fade them with limit orders, and do not go countertrend at a magnet without prior countertrend strength (e.g., a trendline break).
magnets.quote: When you become aware of a magnet, trade the market as if it is trending toward the magnet, and do not trade Countertrend until after the magnet is tested, unless there is a clear trendline break and then a test of the extreme.

trade-selection.teaching: Focus primarily on the best two to five entries each day (typically second entries at new swing highs/lows on non-trending days, ~80% of days, and With-Trend pullbacks on trend days); once consistently profitable, grow position size rather than adding lower-probability entries.
trade-selection.quote: A trader should focus primarily on taking only the best two to five entries each day...Once a trader is consistently profitable, the next goal should be to increase the position size rather than adding lower probability entries.

gray-zone/probability.teaching: Setups are never textbook-perfect; traders must operate in a probabilistic 'gray zone' where a close-enough pattern behaves similarly to the textbook version, and reliable fixed rules cannot exist in a zero-sum game because any consistently-working rule would be arbitraged away.
gray-zone/probability.quote: If a pattern is setting up and is not perfect but is reasonably similar to a reliable setup, it will likely behave similarly as well. Close is usually close enough... it is impossible to have a zero-sum game where rules consistently work.

day-typing.teaching: Identify early (often by the 3rd or 4th bar) whether the session is a trend-from-the-open day; a large trend bar dropping/rising far from the open on one of the first few bars is a strong tell, and confidence should build once price breaks below/above the first bar.
day-typing.quote: You would have suspected it by the third bar of the day (a large bear trend bar, dropping far from the open), and you would have been very confident by the time the market broke below bar 1.

breakout-pullback.teaching: All patterns can fail, and failures can themselves fail — when a failure fails, it creates a breakout pullback in the original direction with a high probability of success.
breakout-pullback.quote: All patterns fail and the failures often fail, and when they do, they create a breakout pullback in the original direction and have a high probability of success.

beginner-method.teaching: Beginners should start on the 5-minute chart, entering on pullbacks with stop orders: buy above a bull bar at the moving average in a bull trend, short below a bear bar at the moving average in a bear trend; scale out at a limit near the prior trend extreme and move the stop to breakeven on the remainder.
beginner-method.quote: When the market is in a bull trend, look to buy above a bull bar at the moving average.

trend-following.teaching: Buy low, sell high — except in a clear, strong trend, where you buy high-2 setups even at the day's high in a bull trend (or sell low-2 setups in a bear trend). But the market is in a trading range most of the time, so before buying high ask whether the pattern truly matches an established strong bull trend pattern; if not, don't buy high even if momentum looks great.
trend-following.quote: Buy low and sell high, except in a clear and strong trend... In a bull trend, buy high 2 setups even if they are at the high of the day.

trading-range.teaching: When you (as a trader) feel uncertain/confused, the market is likely in a trading range — only buy low/sell high or scalp; if uncertain, prefer shorting after 5-10 bars up and buying after 5-10 bars down, since most breakout attempts fail.
trading-range.quote: When a trader is uncertain or confused, the market is in a trading range and he should only buy low and sell high.

time-of-day.teaching: The first hour is usually the easiest time to make money (larger swings, fewer doji bars) but also easiest to lose money because of overconfidence and rule-breaking; swing setups in the first hour typically have under 50% win rate but reward at least twice the risk. Experienced traders can scalp.
time-of-day.quote: The first hour is usually the easiest time to make money... The first hour is the easiest time to lose money as well.

signal-bar.teaching: The best signal bars are trend bars in the direction of the trade; doji bars are one-bar trading ranges and usually terrible signal bars — you'll usually lose buying above or selling below a trading range.
signal-bar.quote: The best signal bars are trend bars in the direction of your trade... You will usually lose if you buy above a trading range or sell below one.

scalping.teaching: You won't make consistent money trading countertrend scalps — risk is usually as large as the profit target, requiring about six winners to recover four losses; realistically scalp only if you can win 60%+ of the time, and beginners should scalp only with the trend.
scalping.quote: You will not make consistent money until you stop trading countertrend scalps.

always-in-reversal.teaching: You will not make money trading reversals until you wait for a break of a significant trend line and then a strong reversal bar on a test of the trend's extreme.
always-in-reversal.quote: You will not make money trading reversals until you wait for a break of a significant trend line and then for a strong reversal bar on a test of the trend's extreme.

deliberate-practice.teaching: To truly learn price action, print out the 5-minute chart every day (and stock charts if trading stocks) and annotate every setup you see, doing this for years until you can instantly read any chart.
deliberate-practice.quote: Print out the 5 minute Emini chart every day... and write on the chart every setup that you see.

two-legs.teaching: Always look for two legs; when the market tries something twice and fails both times, that is a reliable signal it will likely succeed doing the opposite.
two-legs.quote: When the market tries to do something twice and fails both times, that is a reliable signal that it will likely succeed in doing the opposite.

loss-avoidance.teaching: Finding winners is easy; avoiding losers is the key skill, since a few losers can ruin the day. Common losers: weak doji signal bars in the middle of the range, entering a reversal too early (wait for the second entry when in doubt), taking 1- or 3-minute countertrend reversal entries out of trend denial (which fail and become great with-trend setups), and following a credentialed TV expert's buy/sell call.
loss-avoidance.quote: The key to success is avoiding the losers.

scalping-vs-swing.teaching: Do not scalp when you should swing, and vice versa. Until consistently profitable, keep trading simple — swing just 1-3 trades a day and don't scalp, since successful scalping requires risking about as much as the target, needing a >60% win rate.
scalping-vs-swing.quote: Do not scalp when you should swing, and do not swing when you should scalp.

trend-bias.teaching: After a losing month, avoid trading reversals; if 7 of the past 10 bars are mostly above the moving average, only look to buy (not short), and vice versa below.
trend-bias.quote: If you lost money last month, do not trade any reversals.

motivation.teaching: Mastering the skills of trading can make more money than imaginable and provide the ability to live your dreams.
motivation.quote: If you perfect the skills of trading, you can make more money than you could ever have imagined possible.
